### Original:
Figuring the nature of the times deceased; The which observed, a man may prophesy, With a near aim, of the main chance of things As yet not come to life; which in their seeds And weak beginnings lie intreasured. Shakespeare, 2 Henry IV., III. i. They pass through whirl-pools, and deep woes do shun, Who the event weigh, 'ere the action's done. Webster, Duchess of Malfi, II. 4. PREFACE The following was at first intended to be no more than an attempt to foresee the probable trend of mechanical invention and scientific discovery during the present century. But as the work took shape it was seen to involve a certain amount of what may be called moral conjecture, since the material progress of the new age could not very well be imagined without taking into account its mental characteristics. In these expectations of an optimist, a great ethical improvement of the civilised human race has been anticipated, and a rate of progress foreseen which perhaps no previous writers have looked for. Both in regard to moral development and material progress, it has been the aim of the author to predict nothing that the tendencies of existing movement do not justify

### Plain English:
# Rewritten Passage

By understanding what times are like now, a person can make good guesses about what will happen next. Things that haven't happened yet are already starting to form. (Shakespeare, 2 Henry IV., III. i.)

People who think things through before they act can avoid big problems. (Webster, Duchess of Malfi, II. 4.)

PREFACE

This book started as a simple attempt to guess what inventions and scientific discoveries would probably happen during this century. But as I wrote it, I realized I needed to make guesses about people's values and thoughts too. I couldn't imagine how technology would change without also thinking about how people would change mentally.

I'm an optimist. So I predicted that people would become more ethical and moral. I also predicted that progress would happen faster than any writer before me has suggested. Whether I'm talking about how people will change morally or how technology will advance, I've tried only to predict things that make sense based on what's already happening today.

Price $1. _PREFACE_ There is no difficulty in the learned writing for the learned, but it is extremely difficult to compose a work for the instruction of the ignorant. The more comprehensive and exact knowledge the writer has of his subject the more arduous is the effort to express his thoughts in such simplicity as will make it understandable to those who have little or no knowledge of the subject he treats. This is doubly so when the subject is one like sailing--an art whose language is wholly technical and almost totally divorced from the common expressions of life. It is impossible to translate sea language into land language; nor is it possible to explain the conditions and operations of the art without employing sea terms. In this work I have endeavored to avoid as far as is possible the employment

### Plain English:
HANDLING. By Thomas Fleming Day. Price $1. ON YACHT SAILING. By Thomas Fleming Day. Price $1. ON MARINE MOTORS AND MOTOR LAUNCHES. By E. W. Roberts, M. E. Price $1. ON YACHT ETIQUETTE. Second Edition Revised. By Captain Patterson. Price $1. SOUTHWARD BY THE INSIDE ROUTE. Reprint from THE RUDDER. HINTS TO YOUNG YACHT SKIPPERS. By Thomas Fleming Day. Price $1. PREFACE It's not hard for smart people to write for other smart people. But it's really hard to write something that will teach people who don't know much about a subject. The more you know about something, the harder it is to explain it in a simple way that beginners can understand. This is even more true with sailing. Sailing has its own special language that's totally different from how people talk about normal things. You can't just translate sailor words into regular words. And you can't explain how sailing works without using sailor language. In this work, I tried to use as few special sailing terms as possible.

### Original:
fiery-tongued eloquence and full-breasted chivalry which had in turns thrilled and overawed freemen at the polls were no less fervid and embattled before a jury. Yet the Colonel was counsel for two or three pastoral Ditch companies and certain bucolic corporations, and although he managed to import into the simplest question of contract more or less abuse of opposing counsel, and occasionally mingled precedents of law with antecedents of his adversary, his legal victories were seldom complicated by bloodshed. He was only once shot at by a free-handed judge, and twice assaulted by an over-sensitive litigant. Nevertheless, it was thought merely prudent, while preparing the papers in the well known case of “The Arcadian Shepherds' Association of Tuolumne versus the Kedron Vine and Fig Tree Growers of Calaveras,” that the Colonel should seek with a shotgun the seclusion of his partner's law office in the sylvan outskirts of Rough and Ready for that complete rest and serious preoccupation which Marysville could not afford. It was an exceptionally hot day. The painted shingles of the plain wooden one-storied building in which the Colonel sat were warped and blistering in the direct rays of the fierce, untempered sun. The tin sign bearing

### Plain English:
The Colonel was a passionate and powerful speaker who impressed voters. He was just as fierce and intense when speaking to a jury in court. He worked for a few small companies and business groups. In his cases, he would often insult the other lawyer and bring up old legal cases about his opponent's past. He rarely won cases that ended in violence. Only once did a judge shoot at him, and twice angry people who were suing took swings at him. But people thought it was smart for him to get away for a while. He was working on a big case between two groups of farmers. So the Colonel took a shotgun and went to his partner's law office in the woods near a small town called Rough and Ready. He needed to rest and focus completely. The town of Marysville couldn't give him that peace. It was extremely hot that day. The wooden building where the Colonel was had one story. Its painted shingles were warped and blistering from the intense sun. There was a tin sign on the building.

### Original:
Mr. Bright's theory of the public good will have no difficulty in acknowledging the clearness of his diction, the skill with which he arranges his arguments, the vigour of his style, the persuasiveness of his reasoning, and above all, the perfect candour and sincerity with which he expresses his political convictions. It seems likely that the course of events in this country will lead those, who may desire to possess influence in the conduct of public affairs, to study the art of public speaking. If so, nothing which can be found in English literature will aid the aspirant after this great faculty more than the careful and reiterated perusal of the speeches contained in these volumes. Tried indeed by the effect produced upon any audience by their easy flow and perfect clearness, or analysed by any of those systems of criticism which under the name of 'rhetoric' have been saved to us from the learning of the ancient world, these speeches would be admitted to satisfy either process. This is not the occasion on which to point out the causes which confer so great an artistic value on these compositions; which give them now, and will give them hereafter, so

### Plain English:
People who agree with Mr. Bright's ideas about what's good for society will easily see that he writes clearly. His arguments are well organized. His writing is strong and persuasive. His reasoning convinces you to believe what he's saying. Most importantly, he seems honest and genuine when he talks about his political beliefs. It looks like people in this country who want to have power and influence will need to learn how to speak well in public. If that's true, there's nothing in English writing that will help someone learn public speaking better than reading the speeches in these books over and over. You can judge these speeches by how well they work on an audience because they flow easily and are crystal clear. You can also analyze them using the old rules about good writing that we learned from ancient times. Either way, these speeches pass the test. This is not the right time to explain why these speeches are so well-written as art. But they have special value now and will continue to have it in the future.

### Original:
stand in the centre of an extensive demesne, once richly wooded, and within which, formerly spread the placid waters of a small but handsome lake, since drained. The grounds have been almost entirely deprived of their ancient timber, but still retain some traces of their former beauty. The remains of this once noble mansion, of which our engraving represents the rere, consist of a massive keep, which, with outworks long since destroyed, formed the ancient fortress: attached to this is the mansion built in the Italian style, the front of which is surmounted by a heavy and richly-moulded cornice. Of this part of the building (apparently erected about the beginning of the last century) nothing but the outer walls remain, and the interior space, once formed into ample hulls and chambers, has been converted into a flower garden. It would perhaps be impossible now to determine with any degree of certainty the age to which the original erection of this castle should be referred, its ancient architectural peculiarities having been completely destroyed in the endeavour to make it harmonize with the buildings of more recent erection, which have been appended to it, and the property having changed masters so often;

### Plain English:
The building stands in the middle of a large piece of land that used to be heavily forested. There was once a small, attractive lake on the property, but it was drained long ago. Most of the old trees are gone, but you can still see some signs of how beautiful it once was. The ruins of this once-impressive mansion show the back of the building in our picture. What's left includes a thick stone tower that was part of the original fortress, along with other structures that have since been destroyed. A mansion built in Italian style was added to it. The front of this part has a large, fancy stone trim at the top. This section was probably built around the start of the last century. Only the outer walls of this part survive. The inside, which once had large halls and rooms, is now a flower garden. It's probably impossible to say exactly when the original castle was first built. The old architectural features were completely removed to make it match newer additions that were attached later. Also, the property has changed owners so many times.

### Original:
SCREENS. From earliest times the numerous iron mines which exist in Spain, especially in the Cantabrian provinces, have been worked, and their presence has developed in that country excellent objects of art in metal at all times; but owing to the perishable character of iron, the slight intrinsic value of the material, and the little care taken of such fabrics, examples of very early specimens, with the exception of a few interesting ones which have reached us from the Spanish Arabs, have disappeared. The most interesting examples of Moorish manufacture which have survived are some iron keys of most delicate tracery. Their perfect state of preservation shows that they were used only as symbols of cities or fortresses, and, on given occasions, offered to kings or great people, and even in the present day in Spain this ceremony is kept up, and a key signifying the freedom of the palace, is offered to the foreign princes who stay at the royal residence in Madrid. In a similar manner, as far back as the middle ages, keys have been presented to Spanish sovereigns on occasions of their visits to such towns as Toledo and Seville; and a ceremony of swearing them

### Plain English:
SCREENS. Spain has had many iron mines for a very long time, especially in the Cantabrian provinces. These mines helped Spain make great metal art. But iron doesn't last long. It's not worth much money. People didn't take care of these things. So most old iron objects have been lost. Only a few items from Spanish Arabs survived. The best Moorish iron objects that are still around are some keys with beautiful designs. They look like they were never actually used. This tells us they were just symbols of cities or fortresses. They were given to kings or important people on special occasions. Spain still does this today. When foreign princes visit the royal palace in Madrid, they get a key that represents freedom of the palace. In the Middle Ages, Spanish kings were given keys when they visited towns like Toledo and Seville. This was a ceremony to show that the towns were loyal to the king.

### Original:
138 _FOREWORD._ _In ransacking old court records, newspapers, diaries and letters for the historic foundation of the books which I have written on colonial history, I have found and noted much of interest that has not been used or referred to in any of those books. An accumulation of notes on old-time laws, punishments and penalties has evoked this volume. The subject is not a pleasant one, though it often has a humorous element; but a punishment that is obsolete gains an interest and dignity from antiquity and its history becomes endurable because it has a past only and no future. That men were pilloried and women ducked by our law-abiding forbears rouses a thrill of hot indignation which dies down into a dull ember of curiosity when we reflect that they will never be pilloried or ducked again._ _An old-time writer dedicated his book to "All curious and ingenious gentlemen and gentlewomen who can gain from acts of the past a delight in the present days of virtue, wisdom and the humanities." It does not detract from the good intent and complacency of these old words that the writer lived in the days when the pillory, stocks and whipping-post

### Plain English:
I've been looking through old court documents, newspapers, diaries and letters to research colonial history books. I found a lot of interesting stuff that I never used in those books. I collected so many notes about old laws, punishments and penalties that I decided to write this book about them. The subject isn't fun to read about, though sometimes it's funny. But old punishments are easier to think about because they don't happen anymore. They're just history now. When you hear that men were put in pillories and women were dunked in water as punishment, it makes you angry at first. But then you feel curious instead, knowing it will never happen again. An old writer once dedicated his book to "all curious and clever gentlemen and ladies who can enjoy learning from the past and feel better about how moral and wise we are now." Those nice words still make sense, even though this writer lived in a time when the pillory, stocks and whipping-post

The contraption has a small wooden tail like a rudder in the back

### Plain English:
(B) says that all reports about flying discs should be investigated by field offices. Before we got these instructions, two flying disc reports came to our attention. The first report came in on July 7, 1947. The details are in a letter we sent to the Bureau on July 8, 1947. It was called "Flying Discs or Saucers, Miscellaneous, Telephone Call from Mr. Fletcher at the Bureau at 8:30 a.m., 7-7-47." We didn't investigate that report. The second report came to us at 1:20 p.m. on July 11, 1947. It was from ____ who runs the Civil Air Patrol of Wisconsin. The Civil Air Patrol is part of the Army Air Forces. ____ called from Black River Falls, Wisconsin. He told us that someone found a disc-shaped object on July 10, 1947. The disc was nineteen inches across. A city electrician named ____ found it on the Jackson County fairgrounds near Black River Falls, Wisconsin, around 3:30 p.m. The disc might have been made of cardboard covered with silver airplane paint. It had a small wooden tail like a rudder on the back.

### Original:
We entered. "Take me," I said, "to the battle-pictures, so that I may study my country's glories." "Right!" he answered, and with a promptitude that does him immense credit, he brought me out into a huge arena in the open air with seats all round it, a grand stand, and crowds of spectators. The performance in the arena so deeply interested me that I forgot all about the pictures. I saw at once what it was. Detachments of our citizen soldiers were going through ambulance drill. The sight was one which appealed to our common humanity. My daring, dangerous Yeomanry days rose up again before me, and I felt that if ever I had had to bleed for my QUEEN I should not have bled untended. Even my companion, a scoffer, who had never risen above a full privacy in the Eton Volunteers, was strangely moved. There were, I think, ten detachments, each provided with a stretcher and a bag containing simple surgical appliances. All that was wanted to complete the realism of the picture was the boom of the cannon, the bursting of shells, and the rattle of musketry. In imagination I supplied them, as I propose to do,

### Plain English:
We went inside. "Take me," I said, "to the pictures of battles, so I can learn about my country's great victories." "Sure!" he answered. He quickly took me to a huge outdoor arena with seats all around it, a main stand, and lots of people watching. I got so interested in what was happening in the arena that I forgot all about the pictures. I could see right away what it was. Groups of our soldiers were practicing how to help wounded people. It was something that touched everyone's hearts. My old days as a soldier came back to me. I felt that if I ever had to get hurt fighting for my queen, I wouldn't have been left alone to suffer. Even my friend, who usually made fun of things and had only been a low-ranking soldier in his school military group, was moved by what he saw. There were about ten groups, each with a stretcher and a bag of basic medical supplies. The only things missing to make it feel totally real were the sound of cannons, exploding shells, and the sound of guns. In my head I imagined these sounds, and I plan to continue doing so,

good, traitors, heady, high-minded, lovers of pleasure more than lovers of God; having a form of godliness but denying the power thereof.--2 Tim. 3: 1-5. The Savior once began his instructions with these words, "This day is this Scripture fulfilled." They seem to be an appropriate introduction to our lesson upon this occasion. What is the religion of thousands? They were made the special objects of God's favor in their infancy (?), were christened in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Spirit (?), were dedicated to God and his service by their parents (?), who, for them, took a solemn vow to forsake the devil and all his works, the vain pomp and glory of the world, with all covetous desires, to forsake, also, all the carnal desires of the flesh, and not to follow or be led by them. It is said that the christened took this vow when they were children, and understood it not; when they became men they understood it about as well as when they were children. But in all candor, I confess that I never could believe they took this vow; their sponsors took it upon themselves

### Plain English:
Good people, traitors, stubborn, proud, people who love fun more than they love God. They act like they're religious, but they don't really believe in God's power.--2 Tim. 3: 1-5. Jesus once started teaching by saying, "This Scripture is coming true today." These words seem like a good way to start our lesson right now. What is the religion of thousands of people? When they were babies, they were chosen to receive God's special favor (?). They were baptized in the name of the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit (?). Their parents dedicated them to God and promised to serve him (?). Their parents took a serious promise to turn away from the devil and everything he does. They promised to turn away from wanting fancy things and worldly glory. They also promised to turn away from physical desires and not to follow them. When these children were baptized, they took this vow, but they didn't understand it. When they grew up, they understood it about as well as they did when they were children. To be honest, I never believed they really took this vow themselves. Their sponsors took the vow for them.

### Original:
Appleton Press, New York, U. S. A. PREFACE This book is intended primarily for use as a text-book in schools and colleges. I have therefore given more dates and more details about the lives of authors than are in themselves important, because dates are convenient aids to memory, as they enable the learner to connect his new knowledge with historical facts he may have learned before, while biographical details help to endow authors with something of concrete personality, to which the learner can attach what he learns of their literary and intellectual activity. Extracts from Latin authors are given, with few exceptions, in English translation. I considered the advisability of giving them in Latin, but concluded that extracts in Latin would probably not be read by most young readers, and would therefore do less good than even imperfect translations. Moreover, the texts of the most important works are sure to be at hand in the schools, and books of selections, such as Cruttwell and Banton’s _Specimens of Roman Literature_, Tyrrell’s _Anthology of Latin Poetry_, and Gudeman’s _Latin Literature of the Empire_, are readily accessible. I am responsible for all translations not accredited to some other translator. In making my translations,

### Plain English:
Appleton Press, New York, U.S.A. PREFACE

This book is meant to be used as a textbook in schools and colleges. So I included more dates and more information about authors' lives than might seem necessary. This is because dates help people remember things. They let students connect what they're learning to history they already know. Biographical details also make authors seem more like real people. This helps students understand what those authors wrote and thought.

Most of the passages from Latin authors are given in English translation. I thought about whether I should give them in Latin instead. But I decided that most young readers probably wouldn't read Latin passages. So even bad translations would be better than that. Besides, the main works are available in schools anyway. There are also books with selections like Cruttwell and Banton's _Specimens of Roman Literature_, Tyrrell's _Anthology of Latin Poetry_, and Gudeman's _Latin Literature of the Empire_. These books are easy to find. I did all the translations myself, unless I say someone else translated them. When I made my translations,

PREFACE _In the Boys' Golden West Series I have done my best to present to its readers the West that I knew as a boy._ _Frontier days were made up of many different kinds of humans. There were men who were muddy-bellied coyotes, so low that they hugged the ground like a snake. There were girls whose cheeks were so toughened by shame as to be hardly knowable from squaws. There were stoic Indians with red-raw, liquor-dilated eyes, peaceable and just when sober, boastful and intolerant when drunk. And then there were those White Men, those moulders, those makers of the great, big open-hearted West, that had not yet been denatured by nesters and wire fences, men to whom a Colt gun was the court of last appeal and who did not carry a warrant in their pockets until it was worn out, men who faced staggering odds and danger single-handed and alone, men who created and worked out and made an Ideal Civilization,--a country where doors were left unlocked at night and the windows of the mind were always open,--men who were always kind to the weak and unprotected, even if they did

### Plain English:
# Plain English Version

All Rights Reserved. Printed in the U.S.A.

PREFACE

In the Boys' Golden West Series, I have tried my best to show readers the West that I knew when I was a boy.

The frontier had many different kinds of people. Some men were sneaky and mean. They were so low that they crawled on the ground like snakes. Some girls had faces so hardened by shame that you could barely tell them apart from Native American women. There were Native Americans who had red, swollen eyes from drinking. When they were sober, they were fair and peaceful. When they were drunk, they were loud and rude. 

And then there were the White Men who shaped the West. These were the men who built the big, open, generous West before it was changed by settlers and barbed wire fences. These men used their guns as their law. They didn't need official papers to do what they thought was right. They faced huge danger and odds all by themselves. They created a perfect civilization. It was a place where people left their doors unlocked at night and their minds were always open. These men were always kind to weak and helpless people, even if they

It seems that each general on the Union side, who fought a battle, and writes an article for the aforesaid magazine, admits that his battle was the one which did the business. On the Confederate side, the generals who write articles invariably demonstrate that they everlastingly whipped their opponents, and drove them on in disorder. To read those articles it seems strange that the Union generals who won so many decisive battles, should not have ended the war much sooner than they did, and to read the accounts of battles won by the Confederates, and the demoralization that ensued in the ranks of their opponents, it seems marvellous that the Union army was victorious. Any man who has followed these generals of both sides, in the pages of that magazine, must conclude that the war was a draw game, and that both sides were whipped. Thus far no general has lost a battle on either side, and all of them tacitly admit that the whole thing depended on them, and that

### Plain English:
# Plain English Rewrite

I've noticed that both Union and Confederate generals write about "decisive battles" and say they saved the country. Each Union general who fought a battle and wrote an article for the magazine claims his battle was the one that won the war. Confederate generals who write articles always say they completely defeated their enemies and made them run away in chaos. When you read these articles, it's strange. If Union generals won so many decisive battles, the war should have ended much sooner. But when you read about all the battles the Confederates won and how badly they hurt their enemies, it's amazing that the Union army actually won the whole war. If you read what these generals from both sides write in the magazine, you have to think the war was a tie and that both sides lost. So far, no general on either side admits to losing a single battle. All of them secretly say that everything in the war depended on them, and that

### Original:
emperor, who at his pleasure made kings and destroyed kingdoms, the ancient eastern part of the Continent; like mummies which preserve but the semblance of life, was gradually tumbling to pieces, and getting parcelled out amongst bold adventurers who skirmished over its ruins. Without mentioning local revolts which produced only short-lived struggles and trifling changes, of administration, such as that of Djezzar Pacha, who refused to pay tribute because he thought himself impregnable in his citadel of Saint-Jean-d'Acre, or that of Passevend-Oglou Pacha, who planted himself on the walls of Widdin as defender of the Janissaries against the institution of the regular militia decreed by Sultan Selim at Stamboul, there were wider spread rebellions which attacked the constitution of the Turkish Empire and diminished its extent; amongst them that of Czerni-Georges, which raised Servia to the position of a free state; of Mahomet Ali, who made his pachalik of Egypt into a kingdom; and finally that of the man whose, history we are about to narrate, Ali Tepeleni, Pacha of Janina, whose long resistance to the suzerain power preceded and brought about the regeneration of Greece. Ali's own will counted for nothing in this important movement. He foresaw it, but

### Plain English:
The emperor could make kings and destroy kingdoms whenever he wanted. The old eastern part of the continent was like dead bodies that only looked alive. It was slowly falling apart as bold adventurers fought over what was left. There were small local rebellions that didn't last long and didn't change much. For example, Djezzar Pacha refused to pay taxes because he thought his fortress was too strong to attack. Passevend-Oglou Pacha stood on the walls of Widdin to protect the Janissaries against new military rules that Sultan Selim made. But there were bigger rebellions too. These attacked the Turkish Empire itself and made it smaller. Czerni-Georges led a rebellion that made Serbia a free country. Mahomet Ali took over Egypt and turned it into a kingdom. Then there was Ali Tepeleni, the Pacha of Janina. His story is what we're about to tell. He fought against the Turkish rulers for a long time. This fight helped Greece become free again. But Ali didn't plan for this to happen on purpose. He saw it coming, but

### Plain English:
These hymns were made mainly for Indians who couldn't read. They had to memorize them. But once they learned to read, they sang them in English. You'll see that sometimes two syllables get sung to one note. For example, in No. 9, the word "nika" is sung to the first note of "Happy Land." In No. 2, in the first line, "skookum" is sung to one note. The main problem I found with making hymns in this language is that a lot of words have two syllables. Most of these words have the stress on the second syllable. This makes it almost impossible to write hymns in long, common, or short meters. In the second edition, I added one hymn in each of three languages: Skokomish, Nisqually, and Clallam. I also added a medley that uses all four languages. M. E. ### Original:
Ludwig Illustrated by PAUL ORBAN _The line between noble dreams and madness is thin, and loneliness can push men past it...._ Onward sped the _Wanderer_, onward through cold, silent infinity, on and on, an insignificant pencil of silver lost in the terrible, brooding blackness. But even more awful than the blackness was the loneliness of the six men who inhabited the silver rocket. They moved in loneliness as fish move in water. Their lives revolved in loneliness as planets revolve in space and time. They bore their loneliness like a shroud, and it was as much a part of them as sight in their eyes. Loneliness was both their brother and their god. Yet, like a tiny flame in the darkness, there was hope, a savage, desperate hope that grew with the passing of each day, each month, and each year. And at last.... "Lord," breathed Captain Sam Wiley. Lieutenant Gunderson nodded. "It's a big one, isn't it?" "It's a big one," repeated Captain Wiley. They stared at the image in the _Wanderer's_ forward visi-screen, at the great, shining gray ball. They stared hard, for it was like an enchanted, God-given fruit handed them on a star-flecked platter of midnight.

### Plain English:
Ludwig illustrated by Paul Orban. The line between great dreams and insanity is thin. Loneliness can push people over it.

The Wanderer kept going. It moved through cold, silent space. On and on it went. The silver rocket was tiny and lost in the terrible black darkness. But the darkness was not the worst part. The worst part was how lonely the six men felt inside the rocket. They lived in loneliness like fish live in water. Their lives went around and around in loneliness like planets move through space. They carried their loneliness like a heavy blanket. It was part of them like their eyes were part of them. Loneliness was both their brother and their god. But there was also hope. It was small, like a tiny flame in the dark. It was a wild, desperate hope. It got stronger with every day, every month, and every year. Finally it happened.

"Lord," said Captain Sam Wiley under his breath.

Lieutenant Gunderson nodded.

"It's a big one, isn't it?" he asked.

"It's a big one," Captain Wiley said again.

They looked at the picture on the Wanderer's front screen. They saw a huge, shiny gray ball. They stared hard at it. It looked like a beautiful, perfect gift placed in front of them on a plate of stars and night sky.

PREFACE Mythology, since it began to receive a scientific handling at all, has been treated as a subordinate branch of history or of ethnology. The "science of religion," as we know it in the works of Burnouf, Müller, and others, is a comparison of systems of worship in their historic development. The deeper inquiry as to what in the mind of man gave birth to religion in any of its forms, what spirit breathed and is ever breathing life into these dry bones, this, the final and highest question of all, has had but passing or prejudiced attention. To its investigation this book is devoted. The analysis of the religious sentiment I offer is an inductive one, whose outlines were furnished by a preliminary study of the religions of the native race of America, a field selected as most favorable by reason of the simplicity of many of its cults, and the absence of theories respecting them. This study was embodied in "The Myths of the New World; a Treatise on the Symbolism and Mythology of the Red Race of America" (second

Until recently, mythology was studied as a minor part of history or the study of different cultures. The "science of religion," as shown in the works of Burnouf, Müller, and others, compares different religions and how they developed over time. But there's a deeper question that matters more. What makes people create religion in the first place? What keeps religion alive? This biggest and most important question hasn't received much serious attention. This book focuses on answering it.

I studied religion by looking at real examples and building up from there. I started by studying the religions of Native Americans. I chose this group because their religions were simpler and people hadn't yet created many theories about them. pale-faced little adult standing five foot five in his boots. Educated at a small private school in the suburbs of London, his record for conduct was practically spotless and he once took a prize for Divinity; further, to the surprise and relief of his preceptors, he managed to scrape through the Senior Cambridge Local Examination before he was transferred to a desk in the office of a London insurance company. His preceptor-in-chief, in a neatly-written certificate, assured his future employers that they would find him painstaking and obedient—and William, for the first six years of his engagement, lived up to the character given him. His mother, a sharp-eyed, masterful woman, had brought him up to be painstaking and obedient; it might be said with truth that as long as she lived he did not know how to be otherwise. It is true he disliked his office superiors vaguely, for the restrictions they placed upon his wishes—just as, for the same reason, he vaguely disliked his mother; but his wishes being indeterminate and his ambition non-existent, his vague dislike never stiffened into active resentment. It would seem that the supreme effort of passing his Cambridge Local had left him mentally exhausted

### Plain English:
A pale-faced young man who was five foot five in his boots. He went to a small private school in the suburbs of London. His behavior record was almost perfect. He even won a prize for Divinity. His teachers were surprised and happy when he managed to pass the Senior Cambridge Local Examination before he got transferred to work at a desk in a London insurance company. His main teacher wrote a neat letter to his future employers. He said they would find him hard-working and obedient. William did act this way for the first six years of his job. His mother was a sharp-eyed, controlling woman. She had raised him to be hard-working and obedient. As long as she was alive, he didn't know how to be any other way. It's true he didn't really like his office bosses. They placed limits on what he wanted to do. In the same way, he didn't really like his mother either. But because his wishes weren't clear and he had no real ambitions, his vague dislike never turned into real anger. It seems like the hard work of passing his Cambridge Local exam left him mentally tired.

the British Army, which serves everywhere, does everything, undergoes every kind of climate, and gains contact with every class of people. In this respect, the British soldier enjoys a distinct advantage over the soldiers of continental armies; he has a chance of seeing the world. India, Africa, Egypt, the West Indies, Mauritius, and the Mediterranean stations are open to him, and by the time he leaves the service he has at least had the opportunity of becoming cosmopolitan in his tastes and ways--of becoming a man of larger ideas and better grasp on the problems of life than were his at the time when he took the oath and passed the doctor. Of that phase, more anon. It is of little use, in the present state of the British Army, to attempt to define its extent or composition, for it is in such a state of flux that the numbers of battalions, regiments, and batteries of a year ago are as obsolete as the Snider rifle. ### Plain English:
The British Army works everywhere and does everything. Its soldiers experience every kind of weather and meet all kinds of people. This gives British soldiers an advantage over soldiers in other countries. They get to see the world. India, Africa, Egypt, the West Indies, Mauritius, and the Mediterranean are places where British soldiers can be sent. By the time a soldier leaves the army, he has had the chance to become more worldly. He has learned new ways and picked up bigger ideas about life than he had when he first joined up and passed his medical exam. I'll talk more about that later. Right now, it's hard to say exactly how many soldiers are in the British Army or how it's organized. The numbers keep changing. What was true a year ago is already out of date, like old rifles. In the past, there were 157 infantry battalions, 31 cavalry regiments, and about 180 horse and field artillery batteries. There were also about 100 companies and 9 mountain batteries of Royal Garrison Artillery.

PREFACE.

It shows something important about America. America has never built a real navy fleet. We have everything we need to do it. We just need to want to do it. A fleet only needs one leader. ### Plain English:

When people stop being angry and prejudiced about what happened, and start thinking clearly, these future historians will be able to study the causes, how the war was fought, and what came from it better than people living at that time could. The main reason he wrote this book was to fix wrong ideas that people were spreading about what the Confederate States and their government did and wanted. People kept using wrong words like "rebellion" and "treason." They kept saying the South was attacking the United States. Because of this, people who didn't understand how the Union worked and what powers the states had were tricked into thinking the Confederate States were like provinces that had turned against their government. They are the scourges that lash our minds in modern days, lash them into confusion, perplexity, despair. I have never been troubled by them before. Why should I be troubled by them now? And the absurdity of Professor Black's is surely obvious. A child would laugh at it. Yes, a child! I have never been a diary writer. To read, as a man, the _menu_ that I ate through as a boy, the love-story that I was actor in, the tragedy that I brought about, the debt that I have never paid--how could it profit me? ### Plain English:
RETURN OF THE SOUL By Robert S. Hichels 1896 "I have been here before, but I cannot remember when or how!" Rossetti. I. Tuesday Night, November 3rd. Theories! What good are they? They mess up our minds these days. They confuse us. They make us upset. I never cared about them before. Why should I care now? And Professor Black's theory is obviously stupid. A child would laugh at it. Yes, a child! I have never kept a diary. I never understood why people would sit down late at night and write down every small thing that happened to them in a hidden book. People say it's fun to read what you wrote years later. To read, as a grown man, about the food I ate as a kid, the girlfriend I had, the bad things I caused, the money I never paid back—how would that help me? Keeping a diary always seemed like just another bad thing in life. But now I have a hidden book too, like everyone else.

PREFACE

In 1919, the City of New York started a textile school. They asked me to teach a knitting class. I was very busy with my manufacturing work, but I decided to give up some of my time for this teaching job. I thought it was important to help fill a real need in the industry. There were no good textbooks available, especially for beginners. So I had to write my own material to teach my students. This book contains what I created. It covers different types of flat latch needle machines. I plan to write more books after this one about circular latch needle machines, spring needle machines, and other types. I also want to cover what these machines make. The chapters in this book were first published in the knitting section of TEXTILE WORLD magazine. But I think they will be more useful now that they are revised and put together in this book form. One of the biggest needs for

For it was as "The Man without a Country" that poor Philip Nolan had generally been known by the officers who had him in charge during some fifty years, as, indeed, by all the men who sailed under them. ### Plain English:
It was May 11th: Philip Nolan." I noticed it because I was stuck at an old mission house in Mackinac. I was waiting for a boat from Lake Superior that never showed up. While I waited, I read everything I could find. I even read the death and marriage announcements in the newspaper. I'm good at remembering names and faces. As you read this story, you'll understand why I needed to remember Philip Nolan. Hundreds of readers would have stopped and paid attention if the officer from the ship had announced it differently: "Died, May 11th, THE MAN WITHOUT A COUNTRY." That's what people called Philip Nolan. The officers who watched him for about fifty years knew him by that name. So did all the sailors who worked under them. I bet there are plenty of men who had a drink with him once every two weeks during a three-year voyage. They never knew his real name was Nolan. They didn't even know if he had a name at all.

They age in character and physique, and after the reconnoitre there is a pathetic consciousness of the grudging confessions which time has inscribed on the monumental palimpsest. My meeting with Bentham after a severance of years was bleak with this pathos. But he was gay as ever, and better dressed than he used to be in the old art school days, with a self-respecting adjustment of hat and necktie that had been unknown in Bohemia; for he was no longer a boy, but a man, and a noted one, and fortune had stroked him into sleekness. The gender of success must be feminine: she is so capricious. Hitherto her smiles have been for veterans grown hoary in doing; now she opens her arms for youngsters grown great merely by daring. Bentham, it must be owned, had dared uncommonly well, and success had pillowed his head in her lap while she twined the bay with her fingers. But lines round his mouth and fatigued cynicism on the eyelids betrayed the march of years, and, more, the thinker, who, like most thinkers, plumbs to exhaustion in a bottomless pit. For

Ted Garvin, C. Markus and PG Distributed Proofreaders EPOCHS OF ANCIENT HISTORY * * * * * THE GRACCHI MARIUS AND SULLA BY A.H. BEESLEY WITH MAPS 1921 PREFACE It would be scarcely possible for anyone writing on the period embraced in this volume, to perform his task adequately without making himself familiar with Mr. Long's 'History of the Decline of the Roman Republic' and Mommsen's 'History of Rome.' To do over again (as though the work had never been attempted) what has been done once for all accurately and well, would be mere prudery of punctiliousness. But while I acknowledge my debt of gratitude to both these eminent historians, I must add that for the whole period I have carefully examined the original authorities, often coming to conclusions widely differing from those of Mr. Long. And I venture to hope that from the advantage I have had in being able to compare the works of two writers, one of whom has well-nigh exhausted the theories as the other has the facts of the subject, I have succeeded in giving a more consistent and faithful account of the leaders and legislation of the revolutionary era than has hitherto been written.

### Plain English:
Ted Garvin, C. Markus and PG Distributed Proofreaders EPOCHS OF ANCIENT HISTORY * * * * * THE GRACCHI MARIUS AND SULLA BY A.H. BEESLEY WITH MAPS 1921 PREFACE

Anyone writing about this time period would find it almost impossible to do a good job without reading Mr. Long's 'History of the Decline of the Roman Republic' and Mommsen's 'History of Rome.' I'm not going to pretend that I'm redoing work that's already been done well. But I want to say that I owe thanks to both of these great historians. However, for everything in this book, I have carefully read the original sources. Many times, I came to different conclusions than Mr. Long did. I'm hoping that by comparing the work of two writers—one who has studied all the theories and one who has studied all the facts—I have been able to write a better and more accurate account of the leaders and laws during this revolutionary period than anyone has written before.

Gentleman’s Magazine from 1731 to 1868 furnished most useful and excellent notices of deceased worthies; then there appeared for one year only, The Register and Magazine of Biography, the first volume by Mr. Thompson Cooper, F.S.A., the second by Mr. Edward Walford, M.A. After this period there was a lull and biography was for some time at a discount; gradually however _The Times_, which hitherto had paid little attention to the subject, commenced inserting numerous obituary notices, and this fashion gradually increased, until at the present day there are few numbers of that paper which do not contain interesting memoirs. Other periodicals followed suit, and now the majority of the daily and weekly journals not only give lives but many of them well engraved portraits. In the meantime there also came out various books on biography, such as Men of the Time, Men of Mark, Eminent Women Series, English Men of Action, English Men of Letters, English Worthies, Great Artists, Great Writers, Memorable Men of the Nineteenth Century, Men worth Remembering, The Biograph and others, while various improved biographical dictionaries, more especially the Dictionary of National Biography, still in progress, were produced. But gradually, _The Times_ newspaper started paying attention. It began printing lots of obituaries. This trend grew and grew. Now almost every issue of The Times has interesting life stories. Other newspapers copied this idea. Today most daily and weekly newspapers not only write about people's lives but also print good portraits of them. During this time, many biography books came out too. These included Men of the Time, Men of Mark, Eminent Women Series, English Men of Action, English Men of Letters, English Worthies, Great Artists, Great Writers, Memorable Men of the Nineteenth Century, Men worth Remembering, The Biograph, and others. An only son, closely associated from boyhood with him, his chief of staff in the Civil War, and always the recipient of his counsel and confidence, the opportunities thus given me to know his sentiments and characteristics, and to witness so many of his actions, plainly augment the duty of making his record more widely known. In these pages, setting aside, as far as possible, the bias of filial respect and affection, I seek to simply narrate the actual facts of his life. Since beginning this work in 1877, I have been greatly assisted by data furnished by many of General Stevens's contemporaries, former brother officers, and associates in the public service, many of whom have now passed on. I render my grateful thanks to them for such

### Plain English:
1900, by Hazard Stevens All Rights Reserved THIS RECORD IS DEDICATED TO THE YOUNG MEN OF AMERICA PREFACE For many years I have wanted to write this book about General Stevens's life. I wanted to do this for two reasons. First, I wanted to honor his memory. Second, I wanted to show young men in America what a great life he lived. Maybe they could learn from his example. I am his only son. I have known him my whole life. I was his chief of staff during the Civil War. He always gave me advice and trusted me. Because of this, I know him well. I understand his beliefs and what he was like. I saw many of his actions myself. This is why I feel I have to tell his story to more people. In this book, I try to put aside my feelings as his son. I just want to tell what actually happened in his life. I started writing this in 1877. Many people helped me. They gave me information about General Stevens. These people knew him when he was alive. Some were his officers during the war. Some worked with him in the government. Many of them have died now. I want to thank them for helping me with this book.

Henry Bunbury, and represented the county of Suffolk, as his uncle, Sir Thomas Charles Bunbury, had done before him. His descendants still occupy the mansion and estates in Suffolk, where they have been, and are still, the great benefactors to the poor, and the parish of Great Barton near Bury St. Edmund's. But we have to speak more particularly of Henry Bunbury, Esq. and his talents. To this day, his accurate delineations of the political and social customs of the age he lived in, and of the characters who came under his observation, are remarkable for their truthful force. It is very seldom that men of high life and good education, possess the artistic power of graphic delineation: at least, we have but few amateur delineators who can stand the test of the invidious sneers and jeers of those empty possessors of wealth and station, who consider themselves degraded even by the acquaintance of an artist, a poet, or a literary character. ### Plain English:
Henry Bunbury represented Suffolk County, just like his uncle Sir Thomas Charles Bunbury had done before him. His family still lives in the mansion and owns the estates in Suffolk. They have always helped poor people in the area, especially in the parish of Great Barton near Bury St. Edmund's. But let's talk more about Henry Bunbury himself and what he was good at. Even today, people think his drawings are amazing. He drew pictures of how people lived and acted during his time. He was really accurate and honest in his work. It's rare for rich, well-educated people to be good at drawing and describing things the way Henry was. Most amateur artists can't handle the mean comments and jokes from wealthy people who think it's embarrassing to even know an artist, a poet, or a writer. But if a man lives a good life and follows God's laws, he doesn't need to care what other people say about him or make fun of him. He can do what Henry Bunbury did when people talked to him like this.

AUSTIN FREEMAN PREFACE In writing the following story, the author has had in view no purpose other than that of affording entertainment to such readers as are interested in problems of crime and their solutions; and the story itself differs in no respect from others of its class, excepting in that an effort has been made to keep within the probabilities of ordinary life, both in the characters and in the incidents. Nevertheless it may happen that the book may serve a useful purpose in drawing attention to certain popular misapprehensions on the subject of finger-prints and their evidential value; misapprehensions the extent of which may be judged when we learn from the newspapers that several Continental commercial houses have actually substituted finger-prints for signed initials. The facts and figures contained in Mr. Singleton's evidence, including the very liberal estimate of the population of the globe, are, of course, taken from Mr. Galton's great and important work on finger-prints; to which the reader who is interested in the subject is referred for much curious and valuable information. It was noon on a perfect summer day. Paul Mario was lying on a white deck-chair on the terrace under green sun blinds. He had just finished lunch and was looking out at the view. He could see sloping grass, rose gardens, and old brick steps worn down by time. They zigzagged down to the fir trees. Beyond that, a river wound through green banks toward a distant town. A lark sang beautifully. It flew higher and higher, singing praise to the sun. Swallows that had recently flown over southern Europe glided around Hatton Towers. They made shapes in the air. Young birds chirped from nests under the roof edges. Climbing roses had busy bees working on them. Their hard work was a lesson for selfish people. Near Mario's feet, ants swarmed over something that was still moving.

# Chapter I

It was spring in the California Sierra mountains. The sky was as blue as it could be. The golden sun made the huge forests glow with life and beauty. Ridge after ridge, the mountains went down on one side until far away they became flat and turned into the boring Sacramento Valley. Down there it was already summer, and the fields were hot and brown. On the other side, ridge after ridge of mountains went up higher and higher, becoming more impressive and rocky. On their tops, you could see bright white snow across the blue valleys. Winter still held on tight at the high places where it was seven thousand feet up.

So winter, spring, and hot, fruit-filled summer all existed next to each other. They touched across the seventy miles between the icy top of the Sierra and the hot lowlands. In this area, about a mile up in the thin air, was a ridge made of bare rock and tall stone points along the top. Pine trees and cedar trees covered its sides. The afternoon sun hit the big bronze tree trunks and made them bright.

The aim of this book is to present a complete and entertaining account of Grecian and Roman mythology in such a manner that the student will appreciate its great influence upon literature and art. These myths, an inexhaustible fund of inspiration for the poets and artists of the past, have also inspired many noted modern works. To impress this fact forcibly upon the student, appropriate quotations from the poetical writings of all ages, from Hesiod's "Works and Days," to Tennyson's "Œnone," have been inserted in the text, while reproductions of ancient masterpieces and noted examples of modern painting and sculpture are plentifully used as illustrations. The myths are told as graphically and accurately as possible, great care being taken, however, to avoid the more repulsive features of heathen mythology; and when two or more versions of the same myth occur, the preference has invariably been given to the most popular, that is to say, to the one which has inspired the greatest works. Both the Latin and the Greek forms of proper names are given, but the Latin names are usually retained throughout the narrative, because more frequently used in poetry and art. ### Original:
_Lord Lyttelton’s Monody on his Wife_. PREFACE The prevailing taste of the public for anecdote has been censured and ridiculed by critics who aspire to the character of superior wisdom; but if we consider it in a proper point of view, this taste is an incontestable proof of the good sense and profoundly philosophic temper of the present times. Of the numbers who study, or at least who read history, how few derive any advantage from their labours! The heroes of history are so decked out by the fine fancy of the professed historian; they talk in such measured prose, and act from such sublime or such diabolical motives, that few have sufficient taste, wickedness, or heroism, to sympathize in their fate. Besides, there is much uncertainty even in the best authenticated ancient or modern histories; and that love of truth, which in some minds is innate and immutable, necessarily leads to a love of secret memoirs and private anecdotes. We cannot judge either of the feelings or of the characters of men with perfect accuracy, from their actions or their appearance in public; it is from their careless conversations, their half-finished sentences, that we may hope with the greatest

### Plain English:
**Lord Lyttelton's Monody on his Wife**. PREFACE

Some critics have made fun of people for liking anecdotes. They think this taste is a sign of bad judgment. But if you think about it the right way, this taste actually shows that people today have good sense and think carefully about things. Many people read history, but not many actually learn anything from it. The historians dress up their heroes too much with fancy writing. The heroes speak in perfect sentences and act for reasons that are either noble or evil. Most people can't really feel connected to these heroes because they're not like them. Also, even the best history books aren't always accurate. People who love the truth want to read private letters and personal stories instead. We can't really understand what people are actually like just by watching them in public or seeing their actions. We learn the most about people from their casual conversations and their unfinished sentences.

### Plain English:
She had cards from dressmakers, cards from corset-makers, and cards from hairdressers lying on her sitting-room table. But she had never seen a card like this one. It was pink, but it looked more like a visiting card than an advertisement for a business. She picked it up because she was curious. It said "Madame Cagliostra" on it. Underneath the name were the words "Fortune Teller." In the corner, in small black letters, was an address: "5, Rue Jolie, Montmartre." A fortune-teller's card? How strange! Sylvia was a pretty, rich, and idle woman, and like many women like her, she was quite superstitious. _graphic layout by:_ Chris Christopher 1981 CONSERVATION ARCHAEOLOGY _Archaeology_[1] has a number of popular stereotypes usually involving expeditions to remote parts of the Earth in search of ancient tombs, lost cities or long-extinct races of Man. The _archaeologist_ is seen working a “dig” for years, looking for bits of bone or stone of little importance to anyone but other scientists. In reality, however, archaeology departs from this picture considerably. Many modern archaeologists work in their own communities on projects that include things familiar to most of us. The scope of their studies may range from 10,000 year old American Indian _sites_ to early twentieth century farms. Excavations are carried out with the aid of tools, including small dental instruments, large earth-moving machines, and electronic computers. Often, archaeologists do not dig at all, but gather information from maps, photographs, written histories, and living informants. In fact, more time by far is spent working on _artifacts_ in a laboratory, and especially in writing reports of excavations, than is spent in the field. Even more surprising, many archaeologists today work in cooperation with private and governmental agencies to protect archaeological remains, as required by state and federal laws. A specialized field of archaeology,

### Plain English:
Graphic layout by: Chris Christopher 1981 CONSERVATION ARCHAEOLOGY Archaeology has a lot of popular stereotypes. People usually think of it as going on expeditions to remote parts of the Earth to search for ancient tombs, lost cities, or extinct human races. The archaeologist is seen working a "dig" for years, looking for bits of bone or stone that seem unimportant to anyone except other scientists. In reality, though, archaeology is very different from this picture. Many modern archaeologists work in their own communities on projects about things that are familiar to most of us. Their studies might range from American Indian sites that are 10,000 years old to early twentieth century farms. Excavations use many kinds of tools. These include small dental instruments, large earth-moving machines, and electronic computers. Often, archaeologists don't dig at all. Instead, they gather information from maps, photographs, written histories, and people they talk to. In fact, they spend much more time working on artifacts in a laboratory. They also spend a lot of time writing reports about excavations. They spend less time actually working in the field. Even more surprising is that many archaeologists today work with private and government agencies to protect archaeological remains. This is required by state and federal laws. ### Plain English:
# Plain English Version

This Bristol reprint is dedicated to anyone who cares about good character, deep thinking, or beautiful writing. "He was protected from the world's slow corruption." PREFACE. People might wonder why Dr. Bliss's edition of the Microcosmography needs a preface. The answer is that it doesn't really need one. It would be hard to find a more well-researched, complete, or well-done edition of this favorite book. Almost everything that helps you understand the text is here. Almost everything about Bishop Earle that could make us like him more is included here. (There's nothing known that would make us dislike him.) And you end up liking the editor too because of how he did the work. It's not just his high standards that make this true—standards that might have impressed Mark Pattison—but also the hard, caring work you can see throughout. Like all true love, whether it's for people or books, this kind of care doesn't skip the difficult parts. Also, Dr. Bliss deserves our respect because he makes fun of himself. He compares himself to Earle's "critic," who makes books huge and thick by adding so many comments. This joke about himself isn't meant to be taken as genuine criticism.

As the mills were always busy, Saunders was always busy, and it was no place for a dog to be around, among the screeching saws, the thumping, wet logs, and the spurting sawdust. So the big bitch, with fiery energy thrilling her veins and sinews and the restraint of a master's hand seldom exercised upon her, practically ran wild. Hunting on her own account in the deep wilderness which surrounded Brine's Rip Settlement, she became a deadly menace to every wild thing less formidable than a bear or a bull moose, till at last, in the early prime of her adventurous career, she was shot by an angry game warden for her depredations among the deer and the young caribou. & CO. 1906 PREFACE In the following account of the life and work of that "hero and type of the intellectual energy of the eighteenth century"--the "honest heretic"--Joseph Priestley, I have, to a considerable extent, made the subject of it tell his own story. After Priestley's death there was found among his papers a short autobiography, dealing with the main events of his life up to the time of his settlement in America. This was subsequently published, with additions and explanatory notes, by his eldest son. Of this biography I have made full use, considering it, of course, as the best authority on the matters to which it refers. For the account of the Warrington Academy, with which institution Priestley was connected for some years, and which connection profoundly affected his career, I am mainly indebted to Mr Henry A. Bright's paper in the _Transactions of the Historic Society of Lancashire and Cheshire_, 1858-59. The Yates papers in the possession of the Royal Society have also afforded me much assistance, and have been freely drawn upon. I am also indebted to the late Mr Henry Carrington Bolton's collection for certain letters and for information concerning the Lunar Society of Birmingham.

### Plain English:
& CO. 1906 PREFACE

This book tells the story of Joseph Priestley. People called him a "hero" and a symbol of smart thinking in the 1700s. He was an "honest heretic"—someone who questioned what people believed. I let Priestley tell much of his own story.

After Priestley died, people found a short autobiography in his papers. It covered the main events of his life until he moved to America. His oldest son published it later and added notes to explain things. I used this biography a lot because it's the best source for information about his life.

For information about Warrington Academy, where Priestley worked for several years, I mainly used a paper by Henry A. Bright. It was published in the Transactions of the Historic Society of Lancashire and Cheshire in 1858-59. This job at the academy changed the direction of his career.

I also got help from the Yates papers. The Royal Society has these papers and they gave me lots of useful information. I used them freely throughout my research.

I also want to thank Henry Carrington Bolton's collection. It gave me certain letters and information about the Lunar Society of Birmingham.

These memories had faded away, but now they suddenly felt real again. She seemed to hear them shouting from their school days. All this is not to say that the epic is the oldest form of literary expression, but it is the expression of the oldest literary ideas, for, even when the epic is not at all primitive in form, it deals essentially with elemental moods and ideals. Epical poetry is poetic not because it is metrical and conformative to rhythmical standards,--though it usually is both,--but it is poetry because of the high sweep of its emotional outlook, the bigness of its thought, the untamed passion of its language, and the musical flow of its utterance. Here, then, we have a veritable source book of the oldest ideas of the race; but not only that--we are also led into the penetralia of the earliest thought of many separate nations, for when the epic is national, it is true to the earliest genius of the people whose spirit it depicts. is intended for the work of fourth and fifth semester German in College (or third and fourth year High School), pedagogic considerations imposed certain limitations not only as to individual poems but also as to poets. Thus I felt that I must exclude Novalis, Hölderlin, Brentano, Annette von Droste, Nietzsche and Dehmel. My standard of difficulty--aside from matters purely linguistic--was: Could a similar poem in English be read and appreciated by the same class of students? Moreover I tried out in a class of fourth semester German all poems that seemed to offer special difficulties and have made use of the experience thus acquired. WRITER TO THE READER: "Those who know will not tell; those who tell do not know." The old saying applies to woman in to-day's literature. Women writers when they write of women, evade and conceal and palliate. Ancestral reticences, sex loyalties, dissuade the pen. Men writers when they write of women, do so without comprehension. Men understand women only as women choose to have them, with one exception, the family physician. He knows. He sees through the body to the soul. But he may not tell what he sees. Professional honour binds him. Only through the unaccustomed medium of fiction and out of the vatic incense-cloud of pseudonymity may he speak the truth. Being a physician, I must conceal my identity, and, not less securely, the identity of those whom I picture. There is no such suburb as Dorrisdale ... and there are a score of Dorrisdales. There is no such family as the Fentrisses ... and there are a thousand Fentriss families. For the delineation which I have striven to present, honestly and unreservedly, of the twentieth century woman of the luxury-class I beg only the indulgence permissible to a neophyte's pen. I have no other apologia to offer.

### Plain English:
WRITER TO THE READER: "People who really know something won't talk about it. People who do talk about it don't really know." This old saying is true about women in today's books. When women writers write about women, they hide things and make them seem better than they are. Old family habits and loyalty to other women stop them from writing the truth. When men write about women, they don't really understand them. Men only know what women let them know. There's one exception: the family doctor. He knows. He sees past the body to the real person. But he can't tell what he knows. His job won't let him. Only by writing fiction and hiding who he really is can he tell the truth. Because I'm a doctor, I have to hide who I am. And I also have to hide who the people in my story really are. There is no suburb called Dorrisdale. But there are many places like Dorrisdale. There is no family called the Fentriss family. But there are thousands of families like the Fentriss family. I tried to write honestly about the women of the wealthy class in the twentieth century. I'm asking you to be patient with me as someone who is new at writing. That's all I have to say.

This is a book about Christmas stories and legends for boys and girls. It was put together by Elva S. Smith from the Carnegie Library in Pittsburgh and Alice I. Hazeltine from the Public Library in St. Louis. It has pictures from famous paintings. It came out in 1915.

INTRODUCTION In the south of France during summer, small children and poor old people who can't do hard work search dried-up river beds for "gold dust." It sparkles in the sun, and the water carries it away as it flows. These poor people and children collect the gold dust that God put in those hidden rivers. We should do the same thing with the advice and lessons that God has spread almost everywhere. They sparkle and inspire us for a moment, then disappear. We're left wishing we had saved those thoughts. Who hasn't felt this way?

Dedication.

My Dear Lady Franklin,

There is no one I would rather dedicate my journal to than you. I originally wrote it for you, and now it is being printed for you to read. I owe a lot of thanks to our mutual friend, Sherard Osborn. He was kind enough to help get it ready for printing. I couldn't have done that work so soon after coming home. He also pointed out some things I left out and some parts that would have been confusing to regular readers. I tried to fix some of these problems, but because time was running short, the book still has many of its original mistakes, just like when you read it as a handwritten copy. Even so, this book gives me a good chance to tell you how happy I am that I was able to help accomplish something that meant so much to you.

No page of history is more crowded with thrilling interest than that which records the uprising of the Hungarians, in 1848-49, in a gallant attempt to recover their constitutional rights. The events of that stirring period, even when related by the sober pen of the annalist, read more like romance than reality; and thus they cannot fail to lend themselves admirably to the purposes of historical fiction. More than one of that brilliant series of novels with which the genius of Hungary's greatest story-writer has enriched the literature, not of his own country merely, but of the world, takes its theme from those memorable scenes in which the author himself played no unimportant part. Into none of these fascinating romances has the writer succeeded in crowding so much of the life and colour, of the heroism and self-sacrifice, the triumph and the despair, of that national convulsion, as into the pages of "The Baron's Sons" ("_A Kőszívű Ember Fiai_," literally, "The Sons of the Stony-hearted Man"). Especially effective is his description of the historic flight over the Carpathians of the two hundred and twenty hussars who, at the outbreak of the Revolution, deserted the Austrian army and hastened to their

### Plain English:
# Plain English Rewrite

The Hungarian uprising of 1848-49 is one of the most exciting moments in history. The Hungarians fought to get back their constitutional rights. The events of that time are so dramatic that they read like fiction, even when historians tell them in a straightforward way. This makes them perfect for historical novels. Hungary's greatest writer wrote several brilliant novels based on those events. He even lived through some of them himself. His novel "The Baron's Sons" captures more of the real life, heroism, sacrifice, victories, and defeats of that time than any of his other books. One especially powerful part describes when two hundred twenty Hungarian soldiers deserted the Austrian army at the start of the Revolution. They fled across the Carpathian Mountains to join their own people.

AND ANNE SCARBOROUGH PREFACE The subject of the supernatural in modern English fiction has been found difficult to deal with because of its wealth of material. While there has been no previous book on the topic, and none related to it, save Mr. C. E. Whitmore's work on _The Supernatural in Tragedy_, the mass of fiction itself introducing ghostly or psychic motifs is simply enormous. It is manifestly impossible to discuss, or even to mention, all of it. Even in my bibliography which numbers over three thousand titles, I have made no effort to list all the available examples of the type. The bibliography, which I at first intended to publish in connection with this volume, is far too voluminous to be included here, so will probably be brought out later by itself. It would have been impossible for me to prosecute the research work or to write the book save for the assistance generously given by many persons. I am indebted to the various officials of the libraries of Columbia University and of New York City, particularly to Miss Isadore Mudge, Reference Librarian of Columbia, and to the authorities of the New York Society Library for permission to use

### Plain English:
# Rewritten Passage

BY ANNE SCARBOROUGH - INTRODUCTION

The supernatural in modern English fiction is hard to write about because there's so much of it. No one has written a book about this topic before. The only related work is C. E. Whitmore's book on supernatural elements in tragic plays. But there are thousands and thousands of stories that include ghosts or psychic events. It's impossible to talk about all of them. It's impossible to even mention all of them. My bibliography has over three thousand titles in it. Even so, I didn't try to list every single example of this type of story. I first wanted to publish the full bibliography with this book. But it's way too long to fit here. I'll probably publish it separately later. I couldn't have done the research or written this book without help from many people. I want to thank the librarians at Columbia University and the New York City libraries. I especially want to thank Miss Isadore Mudge, who is the reference librarian at Columbia. I also want to thank the people in charge of the New York Society Library for letting me use their books.

### Plain English:
# Plain English Version

This work is respectfully dedicated to useful friends who have participated with me in many of these scenes. 

PREFACE

I wrote this book because I wanted to do several things. I wanted to spread the influence of pure and beautiful art. I wanted to promote a perfect system of what is truly beautiful when creating a picture or scene. I wanted to help people notice and appreciate the grace and elegance in everyday objects. I wanted to encourage everyone to love beautiful things.

Art should not only exist in an artist's studio. Beauty should be in every home. Art should inspire every person. Art doesn't get tired. It's not limited to one place on earth. It doesn't belong to just one group of people. Beautiful forms are still beautiful, whether they're in a simple cottage or a fancy palace. A perfect picture will be recognized and appreciated no matter where it's shown or who made it. In short, nature is still nature. The feeling of poetry is similar wherever it shows up.

Braces: surround simultaneous notes in a chord {a c' e'} Accidentals: [f++] = F double-sharp [a+] = A sharp [c=] = C natural [e-] = E flat [d--] = D double-flat In the main text, accidentals are written out in full, as [natural], A[flat], G[sharp]. One table uses [#] for [sharp]. Accents and marcato: denoted by > and ^ before a note. Time signatures: [4/4], [6/8], etc. [C] or [C/4] = C-shaped [4/4] time. [C|] or [C/2] = C-shaped [2/2] time. [O] = A circle [O.] = A circle with a dot in the center [C.] = A broken circle (C-shaped) with a dot in the center [G:] = Treble clef ([G8:] = Treble clef 8va bassa) [F:] = Bass clef ([F8:] = Bass clef 8va bassa) Rhythms (A trailing . represents a dotted note): [L] = Longa [B] = Brevis [S] = Semibrevis [1] = Whole-note (Semibreve) [2] = Half-note (Minim) [4] = Quarter-note (Crotchet) [8] = Eighth-note (Quaver) [16] = Sixteenth-note (Semiquaver) Lyrics and Labels: words aligned with the notes begin [W: ...] Breves and macrons, used to denote short and long stresses in poetry are denoted ['] and [-] respectively.

### Plain English:
# How to Read Music Notation

Notes in chords are surrounded by braces like this: {a c' e'}

Sharp and flat symbols work like this:
- [f++] means F double-sharp
- [a+] means A sharp
- [c=] means C natural
- [e-] means E flat
- [d--] means D double-flat

In the main text, these are written out as words: natural, A flat, G sharp. One table uses [#] for sharp.

Accents are shown with > and ^ symbols before a note.

Time signatures look like [4/4] or [6/8].

Special symbols for time:
- [C] or [C/4] is regular 4/4 time
- [C|] or [C/2] is 2/2 time
- [O] is a circle
- [O.] is a circle with a dot in the middle
- [C.] is a broken circle with a dot in the middle

Clefs are shown like this:
- [G:] is treble clef
- [G8:] is treble clef one octave lower
- [F:] is bass clef
- [F8:] is bass clef one octave lower

Note lengths use these symbols:
- [L] = longa
- [B] = brevis
- [S] = semibrevis
- [1] = whole note
- [2] = half note
- [4] = quarter note
- [8] = eighth note
- [16] = sixteenth note

A dot after a note makes it dotted (longer).

Words that go with notes start with [W: ...]

Short and long stresses in poetry are shown with ['] and [-].
