PREFACE Jean Henri Fabre, author of the long series of "Souvenirs Entomologiques" from which these studies are taken, was a French school-teacher and scientist whose peculiar gift for the observation and description of insect life won for him the title of the "insects' Homer." A distinguished English critic says of him, "Fabre is the wisest man, and the best read in the book of nature, of whom the centuries have left us any record." The fact that he was mainly self-taught, and that his life was an unending struggle with poverty and disappointment, increases our admiration for his wonderful achievements in natural science. A very interesting account of his early years, given by himself, will be found in Chapter XVII of this volume. The salaries of rural teachers and professors were extremely small in France during the last century, and Fabre, who married young, could barely support his large family. Nature study was not in the school curriculum,

### Plain English:
J. HENRI FABRE

Selections from Alexander Teixeira de Mattos' Translation of Fabre's "Souvenirs Entomologiques"

RETOLD FOR YOUNG PEOPLE BY LOUISE SEYMOUR HASBROUCK

ILLUSTRATED BY ELIAS GOLDBERG

NEW YORK DODD, MEAD AND COMPANY 1917

COPYRIGHT, 1917, By DODD, MEAD & COMPANY, Inc.

PREFACE

Jean Henri Fabre was a French teacher and scientist who wrote a long series of books called "Souvenirs Entomologiques." These studies come from those books. Fabre was really good at watching insects and writing about them. People called him the "insects' Homer" because of this talent. An important English critic said about him, "Fabre is the wisest man. He knows more about nature than anyone in recorded history."

Fabre taught himself most of what he knew. His whole life was hard. He struggled with money and disappointment. This makes his achievements in science even more amazing.

Fabre himself tells an interesting story about his early years. You can read it in Chapter XVII of this book. In France during the last century, teachers and professors did not make much money. Fabre got married young. He had a large family and barely had enough money to take care of them. Schools did not teach nature study back then.

### Plain English:
He roared for the second time. His wife opened the kitchen door and looked at him. "If I have to order you to come take off my boots again, I'm going to take half a crown off your allowance tomorrow," Mr. Baynes said very clearly. She didn't say anything. "The best thing to do," he went on, "is to write it all down so we both understand what's happening. We need a system of fines, like they have in any good business. Go get the pen, ink, and paper." "Why don't you get it yourself?" He stared at her in shock. He searched his pockets and found a small notebook and a short pencil. "I'm going to stay calm with you," he said carefully, "because as far as I can tell, you've lost your mind right now. You're going to be sorry about this when you come back to your senses. Okay, let's make a list. 'For not answering when called, one shilling.'" He wrote this carefully on a page and looked at it like he was pleased with himself.

A careful selection of illustrations has been made from examples of established authenticity, the majority of which are to be seen, either in the Museums to which reference is made, or by permission of the owners; and the representations of the different "interiors" will convey an idea of the character and disposition of the Furniture of the periods to which they refer. These illustrations are arranged, so far as possible, in chronological order, and the descriptions which accompany them are explanatory of the historical and social changes which have influenced the manners and customs, and directly or indirectly affected the Furniture of different nations. An endeavour is made to produce a "panorama," which may prove acceptable to many, who, without wishing to study the subject deeply, may desire to gain some information with reference to it generally, or with regard to some part of it, in which they may feel a particular interest. It will be obvious that within the limits of a single volume of moderate dimensions it is impossible to give more than an outline sketch of many periods of design and taste which deserve far

### Plain English:
We have reliable information about this up to now. We picked out good pictures from examples we know are real. You can see most of them in the museums mentioned, or you can ask the owners to see them. The different room pictures show you what the furniture was like and how it was arranged in each time period. We put the pictures in time order as much as we could. The writing that goes with them explains the history and social changes that affected how people lived and acted. This also changed the furniture in different countries. We tried to create a "tour" of furniture history. This will help people who don't want to study the subject deeply but just want to learn about it in general. Or they might just want to know about one part they care about. It's clear that one book can't cover everything. Many time periods had interesting design and style that deserve much more explanation than we can give here.

### Original:
_Times_ newspaper of Monday, 1st July, 18–, there appeared a notice of Mr. White’s last novel. The notice—for one cannot dignify with the name of review an article which did not exceed a quarter of a column—contained the following sentence:— “Mr. White’s novels appear to us to lack but one element. Having achieved that one thing needful, Mr. White at once and without cavil takes his place in the first rank of modern novelists. In one word, Mr. White must learn to study Human Nature from the life. His characters are too often evolved from his inner consciousness, and as beings thus produced are apt to be wanting in backbone, it is not surprising that many of this popular author’s works are weak and flabby—shadows without substance—pictures without colour. If Mr. White were to give one-half of the time to the study of the men and women by whom he is surrounded, which he gives to the elaboration of plot and the cultivation of style, we do not know that there is any seat in the republic of letters which we would deny him.” Mr. White was a timid gentleman, with thin reddish hair—a very tall forehead and weak eyes.

### Plain English:
In the _Times_ newspaper on Monday, July 1st, there was a short notice about Mr. White's latest novel. The notice was really just a quarter of a column long—you wouldn't call it a proper review. It said this: "Mr. White's novels are missing just one thing. If he could fix that one problem, he would be one of the best modern writers right away. Here's what he needs to do: Mr. White needs to study real people and real life. His characters usually come from his own imagination instead. When characters are made up like that, they tend to feel flat and weak. That's why so many of his popular books feel empty and lifeless—like shadows with no substance, or pictures with no color. If Mr. White spent half the time studying the real men and women around him that he spends on his plots and writing style, we think there would be no limit to how successful he could be." Mr. White was a shy man. He had thin reddish hair, a very tall forehead, and weak eyes.

of ancient tales which have given pleasure to story-lovers of all centuries from the eighth onward, I feel that some explanation of my choice is necessary. Men's conceptions of the heroic change with changing years, and vary with each individual mind; hence it often happens that one person sees in a legend only the central heroism, while another sees only the inartistic details of mediæval life which tend to disguise and warp the heroic quality. It may be that to some people the heroes I have chosen do not seem heroic, but there is no doubt that to the age and generation which wrote or sang of them they appeared real heroes, worthy of remembrance and celebration, and it has been my object to come as close as possible to the mediæval mind, with its elementary conceptions of honour, loyalty, devotion, and duty. I have therefore altered the tales as little as I could, and have tried to put them as fairly as possible before modern readers, bearing in mind the altered conditions of things and of intellects to-day. In the work of selecting and retelling these stories I have to acknowledge with most hearty thanks the help and advice

But they were definitely heroes to the people who lived back then. Those people thought these heroes were worth remembering and talking about. I've tried to understand how medieval people thought about things like honor, loyalty, and duty. Because of this, I didn't change the stories much. I've tried to present them fairly to modern readers. I've kept in mind that people today think differently than they did back then. I had to choose which stories to include and how to tell them again. I want to say thank you for the help and advice I received.

* * ACROSS THE PLAINS TO CALIFORNIA IN 1852 FROM THE LITTLE WABASH RIVER IN ILLINOIS TO THE PACIFIC SPRINGS OF WYOMING JOURNAL OF MRS. LODISA FRIZZELL EDITED FROM THE ORIGINAL MANUSCRIPT IN THE NEW YORK PUBLIC LIBRARY BY VICTOR HUGO PALTSITS, KEEPER OF MANUSCRIPTS EDITOR'S NOTE This simple narrative journal was written at Cañon Creek in the Sierra Nevada Mountains of California, in the middle of December, 1852, by Mrs. Lodisa Frizzell, who, with her husband, Lloyd Frizzell, and their four sons, set out on April 14th, of that year, from their unnamed home, not far from Ewington, Effingham County, Illinois, on the upper reaches of the Little Wabash River, on an overland journey to California. The journal records her observations and experiences from the Little Wabash, across Illinois and Missouri, to St. Louis and St. Joseph, and over the St. Joseph and Oregon Trails to the Pacific Springs, in Fremont County, Wyoming. Here, at the continental divide and at the halfway point of her journey, the journal ends, on June 26th, or the seventy-fourth day out. It was nearly seven months later, in her snowbound quarters of the Sierra Nevadas, that she busied herself with its composition from

### Plain English:
Mrs. Lodisa Frizzell traveled across the plains to California in 1852. She kept a journal of her trip from the Little Wabash River in Illinois to the Pacific Springs in Wyoming. This is her story.

Mrs. Frizzell wrote this journal in December 1852. She was in the Sierra Nevada Mountains in California at that time. She had started her journey on April 14th of that year. She traveled with her husband Lloyd and their four sons. They left from their home near Ewington in Effingham County, Illinois. The home was near the Little Wabash River.

The journal tells what she saw and what happened to her. She traveled from the Little Wabash River across Illinois and Missouri. She went through St. Louis and St. Joseph. Then she followed the St. Joseph and Oregon Trails. Her journey ended at Pacific Springs in Fremont County, Wyoming. This was at the continental divide. It was also the halfway point of her trip. The journal stops on June 26th. That was the seventy-fourth day of her journey.

It was almost seven months later when she actually wrote this journal down. She was stuck in the snow in the Sierra Nevada Mountains at that time.

COMPANY PHILADELPHIA NEW YORK Printed in U.S.A. "Of _Brownys and of Bogillis Full this Buke_." --GAVIN DOUGLAS PREFACE There are, roughly speaking, two distinct types of Scottish Fairy Tales. There are what may be called "Celtic Stories," which were handed down for centuries by word of mouth by professional story-tellers, who went about from clachan to clachan in the "Highlands and Islands," earning a night's shelter by giving a night's entertainment, and which have now been collected and classified for us by Campbell of Isla and others. These stories, which are also common to the North of Ireland, are wild and fantastic, and very often somewhat monotonous, and their themes are strangely alike. They almost always tell of some hero or heroine who sets out on some dangerous quest, and who is met by giants, generally three in number, who appear one after the other; with whom they hold quaint dialogues, and whom eventually they slay. Most of them are fairly long, and although they have a peculiar fascination of their own, they are quite distinct from the ordinary Fairy Tale. These latter, in Scotland, have also a character of their own, for there is no country where the existence

### Plain English:
COMPANY PHILADELPHIA NEW YORK Printed in U.S.A. "This Book is Full of Brownies and Ghosts." --GAVIN DOUGLAS PREFACE There are basically two different kinds of Scottish fairy tales. The first kind are "Celtic Stories." People told these stories by mouth for hundreds of years. Professional storytellers traveled from small village to small village in the Scottish Highlands and Islands. They would tell stories at night in exchange for food and a place to sleep. Now people like Campbell of Isla have written down and organized these stories. You also find these stories in Northern Ireland. They are wild and strange. Many of them are pretty boring because the plots are all similar. They usually tell about a hero or heroine who goes on a dangerous adventure. They meet giants—usually three of them—one after another. The hero talks to these giants in weird ways and then kills them. Most of these stories are quite long. They are interesting in their own way, but they are different from regular fairy tales. In Scotland, regular fairy tales are special too. There is no other country where these kinds of stories exist in the same way.

_Art of Interesting_. The following chapters represent the reactions of the writer to literature both as composed today and as taught in our schools. Any active mind, bewildered by the ceaseless experimenting in literature and education, and not satisfied with a passive acceptance of even excellent critics, is necessarily forced back upon first principles. Such a mind will not yield to the despair of skepticism, that there are no first principles, nor to the despair of agnosticism, that there may be such principles but we cannot know them, nor yet to the despair of pragmatism, that we must wait and see whether the human race ages from now will give us assurance that there really are principles of art because the last man has seen that these principles have been found to work up to the moment prior to which he joined Tutankhamen. Art, just as morals and pure science, differs entirely from the natural sciences, which are generalizations based upon acquired information and must change as long as the information upon which they are based can be modified and enlarged. But where, as in art or pure science, principles are based on final truths, the principles have also a

### Plain English:
Art of Interesting. The chapters that follow show how the writer feels about literature as it is written today and as it is taught in schools. Any smart person who is confused by all the constant changes in literature and education, and who doesn't just accept what even good critics say, has to go back to basic ideas. Such a person won't give up and say there are no basic ideas. They won't give up and say there might be basic ideas but we can't know them. They also won't give up and say we just have to wait and see if people in the future will prove that real principles of art exist. Art is different from natural science in the same way that morals and pure science are different. Natural science is made up of general rules based on facts we've learned. These rules have to change when the facts change. But when basic ideas are based on deep truths, like they are in art or pure science, then those basic ideas don't change either.

in cities and towns, and at work which keeps them nearly all day in-doors. Many hours are devoted for days and years, under careful teachers, and many millions of dollars are spent annually, in educating the mind and the moral nature. But the body is allowed to grow up all uneducated; indeed, often such a weak, shaky affair that it gets easily out of order, especially in middle and later life, and its owner is wholly unequal to tasks which would have proved easy to him, had he given it even a tithe of the education bestowed so generously in other directions. Not a few, to be sure, have the advantage in youth of years of active out-door life on a farm, and so lay up a store of vigor which stands them in good stead throughout a lifetime. But many, and especially those born and reared in towns and cities, have had no such training, or any equivalent, and so never have the developed lungs and muscles, the strong heart and vigorous digestion--in short, the improved tone and strength in all their vital organs--which any sensible plan of body-culture, followed up daily, would have secured. ### Plain English:
People in cities and towns work indoors almost all day. Schools spend years teaching students and spend millions of dollars every year on their minds and their character. They break down easily, especially when people get older. These people can't do jobs that would have been easy if they had taken better care of their bodies from the start. Some young people are lucky. They grow up on farms and do outdoor work. This makes them strong and healthy for their whole lives. But many people, especially those born in cities and towns, never get this kind of training. They never develop strong lungs and muscles. They don't get a strong heart or good digestion. They miss out on the strength and energy that comes from taking care of your body every day. It doesn't matter.

She thinks it's because everyone is nice, and she doesn't wonder why they smile. The other girl isn't as pretty. She's twenty years old and taller and paler, and she doesn't have as bright a face. But she's definitely not boring or ordinary. Both girls are dressed exactly the same way. They wear brown dresses that aren't fancy or expensive, with little cloaks that match perfectly, and bonnets with ribbons and pink rosebuds that are exactly the same on both. They each have little paper packages in their hands, and they look about the same size.

PREFACE
This book explains how to do enameling in a practical way. Even if you know nothing about enameling, you can learn the basic ideas by studying this book. Then, with some practice, you can make really good enameled pieces. This is a fun and interesting type of jewelry making.

INTRODUCTION
Enamel is a shiny coating that you put on metal. It works the same way that glazes work on pottery, glass, and other ceramics. This book focuses on hard enamel. This is different from soft enamels like paints and lacquers. Hard enamels are made from glass mixed with different metals. These metals create different colors. You heat the mixture to a very high temperature. When it cools down, it becomes very hard. Hard enamel sits between glass and pottery. The clear types look just like glass to most people. The white types look like pottery. There are many other colors too.

I don't really care if naturalists mess up the language, but I really do hate how basic and shallow their ideas are. They've turned our literature into nothing but materialism—and they act like democracy in art is great! Look, their theory is weak, and their strict little system kills everything that makes art alive. They only care about dirty stuff and the body. They ignore anything magical and reject anything beyond what we can physically sense. I don't think they would understand what you meant if you told them that real artistic curiosity starts exactly where our senses stop. You might shrug, but tell me—what has naturalism actually done to solve life's real problems? When you need to examine something deep in the soul—or even just a small pimple—naturalism can't help. All it cares about is "appetite and instinct," and it just gets stuck in routines and confusion. Naturalism only deals with stuff below the stomach. It's like a hernia clinic that just gives your soul a support belt! I'm telling you, Durtal, it's fake medicine dressed up as real help, and that's not even the worst part. This gross naturalism celebrates how messed up modern life is and kisses up to our very American ways.

### Plain English:
BATTLESHIP BOYS TO THE RESCUE

"This is the famous Bois de Boulogne, Sam."

"The what?"

"Bois de Boulogne. It's one of the most popular places to drive in Paris."

"Huh!" grunted Sam Hickey. "That sounds like some kind of sausage to me. Why do they name their streets like that in Paris?"

"All the names in this great, fun city mean something," answered Dan Davis. "This park has the same name. It used to be full of dangerous robbers way back in the fourteen hundreds."

"Robbers!" exclaimed the red-haired boy. "Yes."

"Are they still here?"

"No. Napoleon got rid of them. We'll be at the Arch soon. The French people call it Arc de Triomphe."

"They do?"

"Yes."

"Just like that?"

"Of course."

"I bet there isn't a single French person who would understand you if you said that name. I don't know French, but if that's French, give me plain American English. Are you sure there are no robbers left in that sausage place?"

"Bois de Boulogne, Sam," corrected Dan. "No. There are no robbers here. You don't need to be scared."

"Scared! What do you think I am, Dan?"

of light, color, lighting, and vision is a firmly entrenched conviction of the inadequacy of physical measurements as a means for representing what is perceived. Physical measurements have supplied much of the foundation of knowledge and it is not a reflection upon their great usefulness to state that often they differ from the results of intellectual appraisal through the visual sense. In other words, there are numberless so-called visual illusions which must be taken into account. All are of interest; many can be utilized; and some must be suppressed. Scientific literature yields a great many valuable discussions from theoretical and experimental viewpoints but much of the material is controversial. The practical aspects of visual illusions have been quite generally passed by and, inasmuch as there does not appear to be a volume available which treats the subject in a condensed manner but with a broad scope, this small volume is contributed toward filling the gap. The extreme complexity of the subject is recognized, but an attempt toward simplicity of treatment has been made by confining the discussion chiefly to static visual illusions, by suppressing minor details, and by subordinating theory. In other words, the intent has been to emphasize experimental

### Plain English:
Most people believe that measuring light, color, lighting, and vision with numbers isn't enough. You need to pay attention to what your eyes actually see. Physical measurements have helped us learn a lot, but they often don't match what we actually perceive with our eyes. In other words, there are many visual illusions we have to consider. Some are interesting, some can be useful, and some need to be ignored. Scientists have written a lot about visual illusions from different angles, but many of their ideas disagree with each other. People haven't paid much attention to the practical side of visual illusions. Since there doesn't seem to be a book that covers this topic in a simple but complete way, this book tries to fill that gap. The subject is very complicated, but this book tries to keep things simple. It focuses mainly on static visual illusions, leaves out small details, and doesn't focus on theory. In other words, the goal is to focus on experiments.

Even in our own country, which people think is great, hardworking honest men often lose hope because they can't find jobs or food. However, occasional riots and protests don't really show how poor a country really is. Underneath the nice appearance of society, there are huge numbers of people who are suffering and miserable. These people suffer quietly without complaining, but they are a real danger to everyone's safety. Extreme poverty often leads to crime and prostitution, which cause lots of other bad things. Overcrowding, not having enough food, and dirty living conditions cause diseases that spread beyond poor neighborhoods and infect rich people's homes too. Modern society is like a beautiful palace built in a poisonous swamp that fills the air with deadly fumes. No matter how clever builders and engineers are, they can't protect people in this situation. In the same way...

His face was long and thin like a horse's, and nobody who knew him ever remembered seeing him smile. He wasn't the weirdest person nature ever made, but he was different from most people because he really believed in Destiny with a capital D. Even though bad things kept happening to him, he always believed that one day he would find what he was meant to do and become famous. Finally, he did. And that's the story of Knucklebone Smith. The Pluto Lamp is from the early days of space travel. It's harder to explain but easier to believe. Spacemen knew about it as well as they knew about Rafferty.

Links to LEO, DICDATA, and MR HONEY are kept up. Mr Honey's services are free to help teach business language in English and German.

History and Philosophy

This dictionary started when England wanted to join the European Community. The BBC, British Council, the Department of Education, and Oxford University Press worked together. They wanted to figure out how to teach business English to future leaders in Europe. I was one of the few teachers in London who taught business English back then. The project contacted me. At that time, I had to choose between a career in data processing or teaching business English at college. I chose to do both. As a college teacher, I decided to use multimedia to teach useful business English. From my real work with different classes and different types of students, I created word lists and dictionaries. I learned from A.S. Hornby, who taught at University College London. He believed in practical thinking. I made sure to include lots of useful examples of how to actually use the words. The indexed word lists in the smaller versions—and in my opinion even better—the large sequential word lists...

### Plain English:
# THE BEGINNINGS OF NEW ENGLAND

This book was made by Charles Franks and PG Distributed Proofreaders.

**THE BEGINNINGS OF NEW ENGLAND: OR THE PURITAN THEOCRACY IN ITS RELATIONS TO CIVIL AND RELIGIOUS LIBERTY**

By John Fiske

"The Lord Christ intends to achieve greater matters by this little handful than the world is aware of."
—Edward Johnson, _Wonder-Working Providence of Zion's Saviour in New England_, 1654

1892

**To my dear classmates: Benjamin Thompson Frothingham, William Augustus White, and Frederic Cromwell. I dedicate this book to you.**

**PREFACE**

This book is based on lectures I gave at Washington University in St. Louis in May 1887. I was there as a University Professor of American History. I gave the same lectures again in June in Portland, Oregon. After that, I gave these lectures—either all of them or some of them—in many cities. These cities included Boston, Newton, Milton, Chelsea, New Bedford, Lowell, Worcester, Springfield, and Pittsfield in Massachusetts. Also Farmington, Middletown, and Stamford in Connecticut. Also New York, Brooklyn, and Tarrytown in New York. Also Philadelphia and Ogontz in Pennsylvania. Also Wilmington in Delaware. Also Chicago in Illinois. Also San Francisco and Oakland in California.

In this summary of how New England was settled, I have purposely left out many details.

### Plain English:
By July, art season in London was dying down. By the end of August, most English artists had left the city. Some of the best ones went to places near Welsh mountains and English farms to find quiet cottages and beautiful fields. From the Scottish border all the way to the Shetland Islands, artists were everywhere on the hills. You could find them in every part of England—in the cornfields, by the lakes, in Welsh valleys, and along streams. The same thing was happening on the continent too. Artists and hobbyists were scattered all over the place. Smith was working in the Italian countryside where it was 95 degrees, and he would relax with a pipe at a café when the sun went down. There were hundreds of other people like him doing the same thing in different places. In the art galleries in Florence and Rome, there was no more room for easels. In Switzerland and Savoy, little white tents and sun umbrellas covered the mountainsides. You could see Brown coming down a mountain ridge from Flegère, carrying all his art supplies on his back like a carpenter's toolbox, after spending the day sketching the tall mountains above.

### Plain English:
The committees that check family trees for different types of cattle made it hard to publish the first book of records from the Association of Breeders of Purebred Cattle earlier than this. The committees wanted to include as many cattle as possible. They also wanted to help as many breeders as they could. So they kept their books open for a long time. It took a long time to figure out some family trees that were unclear. The committees had to write lots of letters to the owners and breeders to get the information they needed. This made it take longer to finish the book. We have been very careful to make sure the records are correct. We only included family trees that are definitely real. We believe we did this well. ### Plain English:
# THE ADVENTURES AND VAGARIES OF TWM SHÔN CATTI

This book is about life in Wales. It includes poems too. It was written by T. J. Llewelyn Prichard. It was printed in Aberystwyth by John Cox in 1828.

Here is a poem about Twm Shôn Catti:

In Ystrad Fîn this year, there is great noise and crying. People are weeping and wailing. Fear and sadness are everywhere. Lightning strikes the mountain. Big stones melt like lead. All of nature shakes and trembles. People are scared of Twm Shôn Catti and his loud shout.

## CHAPTER 1

This chapter is about why Twm Shôn Catti is so famous in Wales. His character is like Robin Hood and other famous outlaws. Some people have told false stories about him in a book called the "Innkeeper's Album." A play was also made from these false stories. Different towns claim he was born in their town. But this book will tell the true story of where he came from and who his parents were.

NEW YORK. 1875. In reprinting the following articles, by Miss Octavia Hill, of London, it is not proposed that the system described is, in all its details, applicable to this country. The conditions of poverty in America and in England differ widely. The Municipal forms of Government there are very unlike our own. But the broad truths of humanity are the same everywhere, and the principles which underlie Miss Hill's efforts, those of helping the poor towards self-support and self-respect; of bringing to their aid an unwearied patience, a true sympathy, and a large hope; of loving them and trusting them; above all, of realizing that they and we are children of One Father; these principles are applicable to all countries and to every people. NEW YORK. 1875. We are reprinting these articles by Miss Octavia Hill from London. We are not saying that everything she describes will work exactly the same way here in America. Poverty is different in America than it is in England. Our government works differently too. But people are the same everywhere. The ideas behind Miss Hill's work are the same everywhere too. She wants to help poor people support themselves and feel good about themselves. She shows them patience, real caring, and hope. She loves them and trusts them. Most of all, she knows that poor people and rich people are all children of the same God. These ideas work in every country for every group of people. LOUISA LEE SCHUYLER, President, State Charities Aid Association. NEW YORK, April 14, 1875. HOUSES FOR POOR PEOPLE IN LONDON. November 1, 1866. Many people care about where poor people live. * * ACROSS THE PLAINS TO CALIFORNIA IN 1852 FROM THE LITTLE WABASH RIVER IN ILLINOIS TO THE PACIFIC SPRINGS OF WYOMING JOURNAL OF MRS. LODISA FRIZZELL EDITED FROM THE ORIGINAL MANUSCRIPT IN THE NEW YORK PUBLIC LIBRARY BY VICTOR HUGO PALTSITS, KEEPER OF MANUSCRIPTS EDITOR'S NOTE This simple narrative journal was written at Cañon Creek in the Sierra Nevada Mountains of California, in the middle of December, 1852, by Mrs. Lodisa Frizzell, who, with her husband, Lloyd Frizzell, and their four sons, set out on April 14th, of that year, from their unnamed home, not far from Ewington, Effingham County, Illinois, on the upper reaches of the Little Wabash River, on an overland journey to California. The journal records her observations and experiences from the Little Wabash, across Illinois and Missouri, to St. Louis and St. Joseph, and over the St. Joseph and Oregon Trails to the Pacific Springs, in Fremont County, Wyoming. Here, at the continental divide and at the halfway point of her journey, the journal ends, on June 26th, or the seventy-fourth day out. It was nearly seven months later, in her snowbound quarters of the Sierra Nevadas, that she busied herself with its composition from

### Plain English:
Mrs. Lodisa Frizzell traveled across the plains to California in 1852. She kept a journal during her trip. It started at the Little Wabash River in Illinois and ended at Pacific Springs in Wyoming.

This journal was found in the New York Public Library. Victor Hugo Paltsits edited it for publication.

Here's what happened: Mrs. Frizzell, her husband Lloyd, and their four sons left home on April 14th, 1852. They lived near Ewington in Effingham County, Illinois, on the Little Wabash River. They decided to travel overland to California.

The journal shows what she saw and experienced. She wrote about traveling from the Little Wabash River across Illinois and Missouri. Then she went to St. Louis and St. Joseph. From there, she followed the St. Joseph Trail and the Oregon Trail. She ended at Pacific Springs in Fremont County, Wyoming. This was the halfway point of her journey and where the continental divide is. The journal stops on June 26th after seventy-four days of travel.

Mrs. Frizzell actually wrote down this journal later. It was almost seven months after she started. She wrote it while stuck in the Sierra Nevada Mountains in California during the middle of December, 1852.

Yet the writer of these sketches of noted women has prepared for just such a phase of American life, which makes her work now appear a prophecy of the future as well as a summary of past events. "The Women of the Revolution" having been published in the middle of the century, the material for these biographical sketches was collected while some men and women were still living who could recall the faces and figures of the statesmen and soldiers of the Revolutionary struggle. PREFACE

This book explains what we know about astronomy today in a way that regular people can understand. Many people think you need to study really hard to learn anything useful about astronomy. They think you have to learn a completely new way of thinking. But the way we study the stars and planets uses the same kind of thinking we use in everyday life. People often get the wrong idea because of mathematics. But math is just a tool.

It is true freedom. If you get to a place any other way, you arrive too fast. You miss thousands of small good things waiting for you along the road. If you drive a car, you have to worry about so many things. The horses' legs are eight of the most important. If you ride a bike--but who that loves nature would ride a bike? And motors? The point of my trip wasn't to get somewhere. It was about the journey itself.

PREFACE Some fifteen years ago the late Dr. James Bain, Librarian of the Toronto Public Library, urged me to write my reminiscences. He knew that, as one of the founders of the Canada First party, as Chairman of the Organising Committee of the Imperial Federation League in Canada, then President of it, and after its reorganisation, under the name of the British Empire League in Canada, still President, I had much private information, in connection with the struggle for Imperial Unity, that would be of interest to the public. He was therefore continually urging me to put down my recollections in order that they should be preserved. I put the matter off until the year 1899, when I was retired from the command of my regiment on reaching the age limit. I then wrote my military recollections under the title _Soldiering in Canada_.

### Plain English:
COLONEL GEORGE T. DENISON is the President of the British Empire League in Canada. He wrote books called "Modern Cavalry," "A History of Cavalry," and "Soldiering in Canada."

This book was published by Macmillan and Co. in London, Toronto, and New York in 1909.

PREFACE

About fifteen years ago, Dr. James Bain ran the Toronto Public Library. He asked me to write down my memories. He knew I had been one of the people who started the Canada First party. I was also in charge of organizing the Imperial Federation League in Canada. Later, I became its President. When the group changed its name to the British Empire League in Canada, I stayed as President. Dr. Bain knew I had lots of private information about the fight for Imperial Unity that people would want to read about. He kept asking me to write down what I remembered so the information would not be lost.

I waited a long time to do this. In 1899, I retired from running my military regiment because I was too old. That is when I finally wrote down my military memories. I called the book "Soldiering in Canada."

"Well, the ship will get to Queenstown next Friday," said Mrs. Sinclair. "Then it will take six or seven days for her letter to get back to us. I guess we have to wait ten more days, Neddie. But I feel just as bad as you do about Fleetwing, dear." "Yes, mother," said Ned. He felt a little better because she was sympathetic.

### Plain English:
At the west end of Grove Street in Carlingford stood a red brick building on the run-down side of the street. It had a pointed roof with a strange little tower on top that looked like a handle you could use to pick up the whole building. This was Salem Chapel, the only non-Church of England place of worship in Carlingford. The chapel sat on a narrow piece of land. There were small houses next to it on both sides, and they had gardens. But the chapel's yard had no flowers and looked dark and sad. At the back were a few scattered gravestones—plain slabs where English families wrote their grief. On both sides of the little chapel were small detached houses, each two stories tall, each with a small flower garden out front. They were clean, respectable, and modest. These houses gave most of the people who went to the Chapel. Across the street were bigger houses that turned their backs to the street and had stairs at the windows. They didn't pay much attention to the humble non-Church of England people. Maybe twice in winter, the Miss Hemmings came by—two kind religious women. They had been close to the old rector—the Low-Church rector who had been in charge.

The first feature determines the worth of the several philosophical systems in the history of mankind, being the gist of that which will last, and giving them strength and backbone. The second one, however, appeals powerfully to the sentiments of those who are imbued with the same spirit and thus constitutes its immediate acceptability; while the ethics of a philosophy becomes the test by which its use and practicability can be measured. What we need is truth; our fundamental emotion must be truthfulness, and our ethics must be a living of the truth. Truth is not something that we can fashion according to our pleasure; it is not subjective; its very nature is objectivity. But we must render it subjective by a love of truth; we must make it our own, and by doing so our conduct in life will unfailingly adjust itself. Rules of conduct need a strong foundation. The first feature decides how good different philosophical systems are throughout history. It's what lasts and makes them strong. The second feature appeals to people who believe in the same things. This makes people accept it right away. The ethics of a philosophy shows whether it actually works in real life. The author wants to bring these three features together. The first one should guide the second one and support the third one. What we really need is truth. Our basic feeling must be about being truthful. And we must live according to the truth. Truth isn't something we can make up however we want. It's not just our opinion. Truth is objective by nature. But we can make it personal by loving truth. We can make it our own. When we do this, our behavior in life will naturally adjust itself. Old philosophies made personal opinion too important. Because of this, every philosopher created his own philosophy. Each one tried to be unique.

These observations were made about three years ago, since which period, the spirit of architectural improvement has been fast extending from public buildings to individual mansions. Among the latter, the renovation or encasement of Apsley House, at Hyde Park Corner, with a fine stone front, is entitled to foremost notice. The basement story is rusticated, and the principal front has a handsome pediment supported by four columns of the Corinthian order. A bold cornice extends on all sides, which are decorated at the angles with Corinthian pilasters. The whole has an air of substantial elegance, and is in extremely good taste, if we except the door and window cases, which we are disposed to think rather too small. ### Plain English:
A man feels curious to see his palace. When they show it to him, it turns out to be just a plain brick house that's forty or fifty feet wide. People made these comments about three years ago. Since then, people have started improving the way buildings look. They're not just fixing up public buildings anymore — they're also making their homes look nicer. One big example is Apsley House at Hyde Park Corner. They covered it with a nice stone front, and this is the most important change to talk about. Benjamin Wyatt, Esq. designed it, and it's built in the Palladian style. The bottom floor has a rough stone texture. The main front has a nice triangle roof held up by four big columns. A strong stripe runs around all the sides. It has decorative Corinthian pillars at the corners. The whole thing looks impressive and elegant, and it's in great taste, except maybe the doors and windows are a bit too small. The Piccadilly side is enclosed with a fancy bronze fence between decorated pillars. This continues the classical look of the gates to Hyde Park and the amazing entrance to the Royal Gardens across the street.

The landscape has lots of rock formations and ridges that stop suddenly at a mountain range near Mexico. Erosion has carved out giant arroyos and huge gorges all over the area. The Chisos Mountains have tall, sharp peaks that blend with the desert, plains, and valleys in Big Bend National Park. This creates a wild mix of really different and beautiful landscapes. To get to Big Bend from the north, you go through Marathon. You leave U.S. Highway 90 and take State Road 51 for about forty miles to reach Persimmon Gap at the Park boundary. Big Bend is about 330 miles west of San Antonio and 220 miles southeast of El Paso. Alpine is the western entrance to Big Bend and is 118 miles from the Basin of the Chisos Mountains in the center of the Park. The Rio Grande River curves around the Park, and the Park sits right across the river from Mexico. The geography on the Mexican side affects how this area looks. On the east side, the Sierra del Carmen Range rises up above Boquillas Canyon. It looks beautiful behind the mesquite and cactus plants.

I The End of the Honeymoon

It was definitely a strange house. Even through the heavy storm, they could see how weird it looked as they got down from the coach. Dorothy laughed happily. She didn't care that her husband's umbrella was dripping water on her neck. "It's such a nice old place," she said. "I already love it!" Lightning flashed for a second and made the odd windows look like they were on fire. Then everything went dark again. Harlan tried to answer, but thunder crashed and the heavy wheels turned on the gravel road too loudly. "Don't stop," yelled the driver. "I'll come back tomorrow for my money. Good luck to you—and the Jack-o'-Lantern!" "What did he mean?" asked Dorothy, shaking the water out.

I brought up Leonidas Polk, the last warrior Bishop, baptizing his fellow generals by candlelight. "Romance," I said, "was there with the grey and blue soldiers just as much as it was with knights or crusaders." The Scholar was cutting a piece of raw leather. He looked up at me with his intelligent eyes. "Don't you guys ever think that we're all confused about what things mean? We look for romance in nice, fancy places. But when it shows up in wild, untouched places, we're shocked. Any idiot would know that's where it naturally belongs." He picked up a burning stick to light his pipe again. "The things we call aristocracies"

### Plain English:
A few actors who survived the wars and poverty joined together under Sir William Davenant at the Red Bull Theatre. At the same time, a bookseller named Rhodes set up the Cockpit in Drury Lane. He started a company with completely new actors. This happened in 1659. Rhodes's two apprentices, Betterton and Kynaston, became the stars. These two companies later combined and became known as the Duke's Company. Around the same time, a man named Killigrew gathered some of the old actors still alive. They were given the title "King's Company" or "His Majesty's Servants." The Drury Lane Company still has this title today because they inherited it from Killigrew. He built and opened the first theatre in Drury Lane in 1663. In 1662, Sir William Davenant got permission to build "the Duke's Theatre" in Little Lincoln's Inn Fields. He opened it with a play called "the Siege of Rhodes," which he wrote himself. The company performed there until 1671. Then another "Duke's Theatre" was built in Dorset Gardens by Sir Christopher Wren. It was built in a similar style to the one in Lincoln's Inn.

modest scientist, the good and great man, whose life-story I have attempted to tell in the following pages. A life-story such as that of Michael Faraday is both easy and difficult to tell--it is easy in that he passed a simple and unadventurous life; it is difficult, partly, perhaps, for the same reason, and partly because the story of his life-work is a story of the wonderful advance made in natural science during the first half of the present century. Any detailed account of that scientific work would be out of place in a biography such as the present, which aims at showing by the testimony of those who knew him and by an account of his relations with his fellow-men, how nobly unselfish, how simple, yet how grand and useful, was the long life of Michael Faraday. Besides this, we are shown--how many an illustrious name in the bede-roll of our great men brings it to mind--that with an enthusiastic love for a particular study, and unflagging perseverance in pursuance of it, the most adverse circumstance of birth and fortune may be overcome, and he who has striven take rank among the great and good whose names adorn the

### Plain English:
Michael Faraday was a modest scientist and a good man. I have tried to tell his life story in the pages below. His life story is both easy and hard to tell. It is easy because he lived a simple life without much adventure. It is hard for the same reason, and also because his scientific work happened during a time when science was making huge advances. I cannot explain all of his scientific discoveries in detail in a biography like this one. Instead, I want to show through the words of people who knew him how unselfish, simple, yet great and useful his life was. This book also shows something important. Many great names in history teach us that if you really love what you study and never give up, you can overcome any bad luck or poor circumstances you were born into. Even someone with a difficult start in life can become great and good.

Of all the towns in Ontario, we are sure that not one has a history as eventful, as old, or as interesting as Niagara. At different times, it was a center for government, education, the military, and business. Enemy soldiers occupied it once. Another time it was burned to the ground. Now it is a quiet summer resort with many historical sites. It has wide streets with old elm trees and amazing views of the lake and river. The members of the newest of these Historical Societies feel proud. They are able to give the public a story that has not been told before — the story of the Taking of Fort George. The story is told in a clear way. It is fair and unbiased. It shows deep research. It is a story about difficult times. When told this way, it will help people of all ages and all backgrounds. We are asking for a kind welcome for our first work. We are sending it out to the public. We hope it will help prove that

### Plain English:
# To David Whitelaw: A Memory of Our Long Friendship

My dear David,

It's been more than ten years since we first met in that small studio way up in a tall London building. A lot has happened to both of us since then.

We have so many memories together, don't we? Late nights and hungry mornings back when we were young and struggling to make it as artists. We saw so many different people come and go. We had a lot of experiences. Some of us didn't make it. Others failed and are still hanging around the poor areas of Fleet Street and the Strand. There was one guy who became really successful and did amazing things before he died. You know the saying—even a strong pitcher breaks eventually if you keep using it.

There's another person who is slowly but surely becoming famous.

And the rest of us have done something, even if we haven't done everything we wanted to yet. At least we've gotten past the first part of the climb. We feel good and ready to go higher.

The fog is swirling down in the valleys where we came from. But we can already see ahead.

THE LAND OF CONTENT

It was early spring. The day was almost over. The sun and warmth had made shy flowers bloom along the roads. White cherry blossoms from farms were falling to the ground like late snow. The setting sun was shining on the open space at the top of the mountain. This was where the ridge-road and the turnpike met.

The ridge-road was just a small mountain path. It wound through woods and went up and down through valleys. The mountain people had worn these paths into existence over time. The turnpike was an old main road that connected North and South. It threaded through cities, villages, and farms like trophies on a chain.

The shy, winding mountain road only saw the doctor's car pulled by a horse named White Rosy. It was exciting to see groups of armed, quiet men who walked on it at night or sneaked across it during the day. But on the pike, coaches and cars pounded and rolled constantly. A generation or so earlier, an army had marched north on it.

MILITARY TERMS.

_Abatis._--Trees that are cut down with their branches sharpened. They are used to block roads or placed in front of forts for protection.

_Advance._--The part of an army that is ahead of the rest.

_Aides-de-camp._--Officers chosen by generals to help them with military work.

_Ambulances._--Vehicles used to carry sick and hurt people.

_Battery._--A group of one or more guns. A full battery of field guns has six cannons.

_Battalion._--A military unit made up of two or more companies, but smaller than a regiment.

_Bombardment._--Firing bullets or shells at a fort or dirt fortification.

_Canister._--A metal tube filled with iron balls. When the gun fires, the tube breaks open and spreads the iron balls over a large area.

THE JEALOUSY OF LE BARBOUILLÉ *** Produced by Delphine Lettau THE JEALOUSY OF LE BARBOUILLÉ. (LA JALOUSIE DU BARBOUILLÉ.) Among the small farces said to have been sketched by Molière during his stay in the provinces, two only which seem genuine have come down to us, and have been published for the last thirty years with his comedies. These are, 'La Jalousie du Barbouillé,' and 'Le Médecin Volant.' Molière has made use of the former in the third act of the comedy called 'George Dandin.' Molière acted the part of Le Barbouillé. ### Plain English:
# THE JEALOUSY OF LE BARBOUILLÉ

This is a short funny play. People think Molière wrote it when he was traveling around the countryside. Only two plays like this have survived, and they've been printed with his other comedies for the last thirty years. The two plays are "The Jealousy of Le Barbouillé" and "The Flying Doctor." Molière used the first one later in a play called "George Dandin." Molière himself played the character Le Barbouillé.

## THE CHARACTERS:

- LE BARBOUILLÉ (a husband)
- ANGÉLIQUE (his wife)
- VALÈRE (a man in love with Angélique)
- THE DOCTOR
- CATHAU (Angélique's maid)
- GORGIBUS (Angélique's father)
- VILLEBREQUIN
- LA VALLÉE

## SCENE 1: LE BARBOUILLÉ SPEAKS

Everyone has to agree that I am the unluckiest man alive! I have a wife who makes my life miserable. Instead of making me happy and doing things the way I want them, she makes me angry at her twenty times a day. She won't stay home. Instead, she's always going out, eating fancy meals, and spending time with people I don't even know. Oh, poor me!

My cheeks are soft like apple petals and my eyes are like the sea or sky. They only see my messy braids. They don't know I wear a crown with turquoises and beryls in my curled-up hair. They don't know about the dangerous adventures I have on land and sea. Pages, courtiers, knights, and squires all bow down to me. Proud ships with silk sails do what I tell them to do. All these happy things, they can't know about them. They only see a little girl when I'm studying, working, or playing.

### Plain English:
I'm writing from a hidden place where all the big and small drama of life happens inside my own mind. From struggling with my own thoughts and feelings, I've come to understand the world in new and surprising ways. That's why I'm writing again about things that smarter people have already written about. I hope I can add something new and fresh, even if it's small, based on what I've learned from my own life and the lives of my people. Between serious thinking, I've tried to add some poetry. These poems honor beauty, but they're not good enough to stand by themselves. Now that I'm done, I'm confused about whether I meant the ideas to be serious or just pretty—or the pretty to be serious—or why the book ends playfully instead of ending with hard facts. But that's always how life is, isn't it? Some of the things I wrote here were published before in other places. ### Plain English:
The palace wasn't just where the leaders lived. At different times in history, it was also the place where the government met, where courts held trials, where people were locked up in prison, and where people were executed. Because it combines all these things in one building, and because of its amazing architecture and history, it might be the most interesting old building that still exists today. A writer named Taine wrote about Italy and described what it's like to see the Ducal Palace for the first time. He said it's like a beautiful jewel that makes everything around it look less important. He wrote that the architecture is unlike anything else you've ever seen. Everything about it is new and different. When you look at it, you feel pulled away from regular, normal design. You realize there's a whole world of building styles outside of the Classic and Gothic forms that we always use. You understand that people can invent things in unlimited ways. Just like nature, human creativity can break all the rules and make something perfect that looks nothing like what we're taught to follow. Everything you think you know about building design gets turned upside down. And you see something surprising and amazing—eastern design style putting full shapes on empty spaces, instead of the normal way of putting empty spaces on full shapes. Strong stone columns hold up a second level.

### Plain English:
I'm going to write about Richard Hooker's life. He's the author of five, maybe more, of the eight books called "The Laws of Ecclesiastical Polity." I've taken on this job, but I don't really want to do it. I know it's going to be hard work, especially at my age. I'll have to search, think about, and figure out everything important about him. I never met him when he was alive. So I have to look back to when he died—that was sixty-four years ago—and even go back fifty more years before that to learn about his childhood and youth. I need to find information and details that might help make my writing better, or that might even be necessary to finish what I started.

I know this work will be difficult. I also know that people will probably criticize me no matter what. I can't protect myself from that with good intentions and hard work alone—times are different now than they used to be. So I'm asking my readers to hold off on judging me until I explain some of my reasons. I hope these reasons will help them understand why I did things the way I did.

memoirs will not be written by my own hand. I have no skill of pen and ink, and any relation of length would be beyond my genius. its predecessor, "Ragged Dick," was contributed as a serial story to the "Schoolmate," a popular juvenile magazine published in Boston. The generous commendations of the first volume by the Press, and by private correspondents whose position makes their approval of value, have confirmed the author in his purpose to write a series of stories intended to illustrate the life and experiences of the friendless and vagrant children to be found in all our cities, numbering in New York alone over twelve thousand. In the preparation of the different volumes, the requisite information will be gathered from personal observation mainly, supplemented, however, by facts communicated by those who have been brought into practical relations with the class of children whose lives are portrayed. The volumes might readily be made more matter-of-fact, but the author has sought to depict the inner life and represent the feelings and emotions of these little waifs of city life, and hopes thus to excite a deeper and more widespread sympathy in the public mind, as well as to exert a salutary influence upon the class of whom he is writing, by setting before them inspiring examples of what energy, ambition, and an honest purpose may achieve,

To write these books, the author will mostly use what he sees himself. He will also get information from people who work with these children. The books could be written in a more straightforward way, but the author wants to show what these children think and feel. He wants people to care more about these kids. He also wants to help the children by showing them examples of what hard work, big dreams, and doing the right thing can do.

all the joys in life, none is greater than the joy of arriving on the outskirts of Switzerland at the end of a long dusty day's journey from Paris. ### Plain English:
Of all the good things in life, nothing is better than arriving at the edge of Switzerland after a long, hot day of traveling from Paris. Someone who really loves great experiences will never take the train to Basle at night. He wants to deal with the hot sun and the boring French countryside—the slow rivers and endless rows of poplar trees—because it's worth it for the cool evening and getting closer to the great Alps, which he'll see at the end of the day. Around Mulhausen, he starts to notice the landscape changing. The fields get bigger and start rolling up and down, with clear, fast-moving streams running through them. Swiss thistles grow green by the rivers and farm buildings. Pine trees start to cover the sides of gently rising hills. Now the sun has gone down, and stars come out—first the bright one, then all the smaller ones. He feels it—yes, there's no doubt now—that special, wonderful fresh air that always blows from snowy mountains and meadows with streams that never dry up. The last hour is incredibly enjoyable. When he gets to Basle, he can barely sleep all night because he keeps hearing the fast-moving Rhine River underneath.

### Original:
by Sir Walter Scott, Bart. AUTHOR'S INTRODUCTION The scene of this romance is laid in the fifteenth century, when the feudal system, which had been the sinews and nerves of national defence, and the spirit of chivalry, by which, as by a vivifying soul, that system was animated, began to be innovated upon and abandoned by those grosser characters who centred their sum of happiness in procuring the personal objects on which they had fixed their own exclusive attachment. The same egotism had indeed displayed itself even in more primitive ages; but it was now for the first time openly avowed as a professed principle of action. The spirit of chivalry had in it this point of excellence, that, however overstrained and fantastic many of its doctrines may appear to us, they were all founded on generosity and self denial, of which, if the earth were deprived, it would be difficult to conceive the existence of virtue among the human race. Among those who were the first to ridicule and abandon the self denying principles in which the young knight was instructed and to which he was so carefully trained up, Louis XI of France was the chief. That sovereign

### Plain English:
This story is set in the 1400s. Back then, two things were falling apart. The first was the feudal system. It had been the backbone of how countries defended themselves. The second was the spirit of chivalry. It was like the soul that kept the feudal system alive and working.

But people were starting to ignore and abandon both of these things. The people doing this were more selfish and greedy. They only cared about getting things for themselves. They didn't care about anything else.

Selfish people had always existed, even in ancient times. But now, for the first time, people were openly saying that being selfish was okay. They were making it their main rule for how to live.

Chivalry had one really good thing about it. Some of its rules might seem weird and over the top to us today. But they were all based on being generous and making sacrifices for others. Without these values, it would be hard to imagine virtue and goodness existing in the human race at all.

Some people were the first to mock and give up these self-sacrificing principles. Young knights used to be taught these principles and trained to follow them carefully. The biggest person who did this was King Louis XI of France.

Author of books about singing and voice training

1902

"When you see something new in art, or hear an idea in philosophy you've never heard before, don't be quick to laugh at it, deny it, or argue against it. Maybe the problem is with you—who knows?"

INTRODUCTION.

Hello to my readers again through this book. Thank you for all the kind things you said about my earlier books. You've been wonderful to me—my students, the critics, and everyone in the singing world. I appreciate it so much.

To those of you who understood and liked what I taught in my last book about position and action in singing, this new book will help you even more. To all my other readers, if you didn't completely understand or like what I taught before about energy and power in singing—about the movements I showed you in my last book—this book will explain it better.

### Plain English:
My real name is Pirrip. That's what my father's family name was, according to his tombstone and my sister Mrs. Joe Gargery. She married the blacksmith. I never saw my father or mother. I never even saw a picture of them. There were no photographs back then. So when I was a kid, I made up ideas about what they looked like based on their tombstones. The shape of the letters on my father's tombstone made me think he was a square, heavy, dark man with curly black hair. The words on my mother's tombstone said "Also Georgiana Wife of the Above." From reading that, I got the silly idea that my mother had freckles and was always sick. Next to their grave were five small stone markers in a row. They were for my five little brothers who died. They died very young, before they could really start living. Because of those five tombstones, I got a weird belief that all my brothers had been born lying on their backs with their hands in their pockets. And I thought they never took their hands out the whole time they were alive. We lived...

# PROLOGUE: MONS, SEPTEMBER 1572

It was almost two hours before sunrise on a hot night in early September. The thin moon had disappeared behind clouds in the west a long time ago. Nothing made a sound in the flat land around the city that was being attacked. To the south, the campfires from Alva's army had gone out one by one. Here, the only sound was the steady footsteps of the guards walking back and forth on their patrol. To the north, Orange and his small group of men were trying to get Alva to fight them. The men were not trained, had no money, and didn't want to follow orders. Orange was depending on God to help him win. But things were even quieter in that direction. The guards were tired and didn't care. They fell asleep while they were supposed to be watching. The soldiers were angry and almost ready to rebel. The only thing keeping them working was Orange's strong personality.

I. "YOU CALLED ME, AND I CAME HOME TO YOUR HEART." Another revolution of the social wheel. Summer was over, and Twickenham, Richmond, Bath, and Tunbridge Wells were deserted for the new squares and narrow streets between Soho and Hyde Park Corner. The theatres in Drury Lane and Lincoln's Inn were open every night, the opera-house in the Haymarket was crowded, and drums and assemblies, concerts and quadrille-parties, filled the very air with excitement. 'Twas said the young people were younger than they used to be, and all the old had grown young. The new reign began in a blaze of gaiety; King and Queen, flushed with the sense of power, delighted to occupy the first place after having so long held the second rank; conscious, too, of a handsome exchequer, and a clever minister who could change stones into gold; at peace with other nations, and with leisure to enjoy themselves. The King had only one objection to London, and that extended to the whole of his British dominions. He would rather have been in Hanover. It needed all her Majesty's subtlety, all Lady Suffolk's subservient devotion, all Walpole's strenuous arguments, to keep him contented at St. James's or

Parties, concerts, and dances happened everywhere. People said young people seemed even younger than before, and old people seemed young again. The new king and queen were having a great time. They were happy to finally be in charge after waiting so long. They had lots of money and a smart minister who seemed to make money appear from nowhere. They weren't at war with anyone, so they had time to have fun. But the King had one problem. He didn't like London much, or anywhere else in Britain really. He wanted to be in Hanover instead. The Queen had to use all her skills. Lady Suffolk had to be extra loyal. Walpole had to argue hard. They all worked together just to keep the King happy at his palace.

## PREFACE

This is a first sketch of a system about right and wrong. It's different from most other books on this topic because I left out the old arguments that people always have. I tried to study morality directly. I wanted to understand its ideas and rules by looking at how people actually live.

I did this for two reasons. First, I think ethics classes are stuck because they spend too much time on old theories and arguing about them. They don't use real life examples, where the answers are actually obvious. Second, I want to connect what people learn about ethics in school to how they think about real problems in their daily lives. Morality is clearly the most important and urgent thing we can study. I want to show that it actually matters.

pharmaceutical education in this country has been comparatively slow. The history of pharmacy in this country may be divided into three periods: (1) The pioneer period during the sixteenth century, when there were no physicians, and the general storekeeper who sold dry goods, groceries, books and paints, also sold medicines and prescribed them; (2) the colonial period during the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, when educated physicians from abroad emigrated to the colonies and prescribed as well as dispensed medicines; (3) the college period, or the period of organization and development, during the nineteenth century, when pharmacy became a distinct profession and business, and colleges of pharmacy were established, these numbering nearly 100 at the present time. We are now entering, after nearly a century, upon the fourth period, namely, that of standards in education, and we are endeavoring to fix the position of pharmacy among the other professions. The problem of pharmaceutical education involves two phases, namely (1) that of the natural ability and preliminary qualifications of the applicants for entrance to the colleges and schools of pharmacy, and (2) that of the character and kind of instruction that shall be given by a recognized teaching institution in pharmacy. Not

### Plain English:
Pharmacy education in this country has grown pretty slowly. The history of pharmacy here can be split into three periods: (1) The pioneer period in the 1500s. At that time, there were no doctors. General store owners sold dry goods, groceries, books, and paints. They also sold medicines and told people what to take. (2) The colonial period in the 1600s and 1700s. Educated doctors came from other countries and moved to the colonies. They told people what medicines to take and also handed out the medicines themselves. (3) The college period, or the period of organization and development, in the 1800s. Pharmacy became its own profession and business. Colleges of pharmacy were set up. There are almost 100 of them now. We are now starting a fourth period after almost a century. This is the period of standards in education. We are trying to figure out where pharmacy fits among other professions. Pharmacy education has two big problems to solve: (1) We need to look at the natural ability and early qualifications of people who want to get into pharmacy colleges and schools. (2) We need to decide what kind of teaching should happen at pharmacy schools that are recognized as real teaching institutions.

To that end each structure is shown in detail as much as possible, and the parts separated so as to give a clear insight of the different functions, all of which are explained by original drawings specially prepared to aid the reader. MOTORS To the boy who wants to know the theory and the practical working of the different kinds of motors, told in language which he can understand, and illustrated with clear and explicit drawings, this volume will be appreciated. It sets forth the groundwork on which power is based, and includes steam generators, and engines, as well as wind and water motors, and thoroughly describes the Internal Combustion Engine. It has special chapters on Carbureters, Ignition, and Electrical systems used, and particularly points out the parts and fittings required with all devices needed in enginery. It explains the value of compounding, condensing, pre-heating and expansion, together with the methods used to calculate and transmit power. Numerous original illustrations.

### Plain English:
This book helps you understand how each part of a machine works. It shows you why each part is made the way it is. It also explains the good and bad points of different types. Each structure is shown in detail. The parts are separated so you can see how each one works. Original drawings help explain everything.

This book is for anyone who wants to learn about motors. It explains how different kinds of motors work in simple language. The drawings are clear and easy to understand. The book teaches you the basics of how power works. It covers steam engines, water motors, wind motors, and gas engines. There are special sections on carburetors, ignition systems, and electrical systems. The book explains all the parts and tools you need for engines. It also explains how to combine different parts, how to cool things down, how to heat things first, and how to expand things. It shows you how to figure out power and move it from place to place. The book has lots of original drawings to help you learn.

I am somewhat reluctant to see it so placed, because it has nothing whatever to do with the story which is told in the following pages, beyond being a faithful likeness of the author who is responsible for this, and many other previous books which have had the good fortune to meet with a friendly reception from the reading public. ### Plain English:
This year, 1906, is the picture at the front of this book. I don't really want it there, because it has nothing to do with the story in the pages that follow. The only reason it's here is because it looks like me, the person who wrote this book and many others that people have liked. Also, I don't think my picture will actually interest my readers. I've always believed that what an author really is comes through in their writing, not in a photograph. But here's the thing. Fake stories and lies about me have been spread around in Britain, the colonies, and America by certain cheap magazines and newspapers. They've done this on purpose to try to turn my readers against me. Because of this, my publishers think it's a good idea to show a real picture of what I actually look like now.

### Plain English:
I have a soul. I sing softly to myself about him. His angel-like pride beats all other pride. He brings together high and low things. He treats beggars and kings as equals, like how the sky treats everything equally." --_Wasson_ PREFACE

We have known the simple facts of Nathaniel Hawthorne's life for a long time. From 1835 onward, you can find them easily in his Note-books. These came from his diary. Before that time, we learned about his life mainly from two of his close friends, Horatio Bridge and Elizabeth Peabody. George P. Lathrop first organized and published these memories in 1872. Twelve years later, Julian Hawthorne published a more complete biography. However, he chose not to say what he thought about his father's talent or to examine his father's writing in a critical way. This book was written to fill that gap. At the same time, I want to give credit to the people who came before me. They did good work by helping people learn about Hawthorne.

Number Seven last week, a curious state of affairs was found: the prisoners below mining the ore as usual, the overseer dead, and every indication of some stark drama having taken place. In the study of the overseer's house one man was found dead, apparently by his own hand, and beside him on the desk was a hastily scribbled document which is herewith published._ * * * * * We hated Marnick. Because he was an Earthman and because he laughed, we hated him. Awake and asleep, at our daily drudge of labor and in the throes of sluggish nightmare, with a fierce tenacity from the very depths of our souls--those of us who still had souls--we hated him. And there was not a man among us who had not sworn to kill him if given the chance, who did not dream of being _the one_. For we knew that some day it was going to happen. But when? It seemed impossible. Daily that is what I thought as I trudged wearily to my place in B-Tunnel two miles below. We were forty men against him, Martians and Earthmen alike. Once there had been Venusians here, too, but they died

### Plain English:
Last week at Number Seven, something weird happened. The prisoners were working below, mining ore like they always do. But the overseer was dead. Everything showed that something terrible had taken place. In the overseer's house, in his study, they found one man dead. It looked like he had killed himself. On his desk next to him was a note that someone wrote quickly. Here is what it said.

We hated Marnick. We hated him because he was from Earth and because he laughed. Whether we were awake or asleep, whether we were working hard or having bad dreams, we hated him. We hated him with all our strength, from deep inside ourselves—those of us who still had any humanity left. Every single man among us had promised to kill him if we got the chance. Every man dreamed about being the one to do it. We knew that someday it would happen. But when? It seemed like it would never happen. That's what I thought every day as I walked tired down to my spot in B-Tunnel, two miles below the surface. There were forty of us against him. Some of us were from Mars and some were from Earth. There used to be people from Venus here too, but they all died.

### Plain English:
We've included basic instructions that would help someone new to embroidery teach themselves. However, learning without actual hands-on practice will always be incomplete and disappointing. So we've mainly tried to answer questions we get all the time. We wanted to help people who can't take lessons in person and have to figure things out on their own without a professional embroiderer to guide them. At the same time, the rules and directions we give here can help people who have taken classes remember details they might forget after their lessons end. These details matter a lot for good embroidery. We've described the most useful stitches and explained when to use them for different types of work. We've listed the different materials that work best as backgrounds for embroidery, and the threads and other supplies that people use most often. We've also included practical tips on how to use these materials in the best and cheapest ways. Finally, we've given clear instructions on how to stretch, frame, and clean finished embroidery pieces.

MARY J. SAFFORD. PREFACE. Nearly all the more recent romances and dramas, whose scene is laid in classic times, depict the period of the great rupture between Paganism and Christianity. This is true of “Hypatia,” “Fabiola,” “The Last Days of Pompeii,” “The Epicureans,” “The Emperor and The Galilean,” “The Last Athenian,” and many other works. The cause of this coincidence is not difficult to understand; for a period containing such strong contrasts invites æsthetic treatment. The present tales derive their material from a different, but no less interesting epoch. They give pictures of the flowering of Hellas, the distant centuries whose marvellous culture rested solely on the purely human elements of character as developed beneath a mild and radiant sky. Yet it required a certain degree of persistence to procure this material. When we examine the Greek writers to find descriptions of the _men_ of
