### Plain English:
Several books have been published over the last ten or twenty years. One important one is Paul's 'Principles of Language History.' We rewrote that work to use it as a basis for this book. We changed it as much as we needed to for each topic. One of the authors already translated Professor Paul's book into English. But we felt that translation didn't make this book unnecessary. Students who read these pages should still study Paul's original work. It's important to explain how this book is like Paul's book and how it's different. We followed Paul's way of organizing the material very closely. Our chapters match his chapters in number, order, and topic. Most of the ideas in this book come from Paul. Most of the arguments are his too. This is true even in the few cases where we might disagree with Paul. For example, we might have different opinions about how sound changes work and what kind of laws they follow. We also follow the order that Paul uses when he presents his ideas.

### Original:
GEORGE SAINTSBURY LONDON G. BELL AND SONS, LTD. NEW YORK: HARCOURT, BRACE AND CO. 1922 PREFACE When my publishers were good enough to propose that I should undertake this book, they were also good enough to suggest that the Introduction should be of a character somewhat different from that of a school-anthology, and should attempt to deal with the Art of Letter-writing, and the nature of the Letter, as such. I formed a plan accordingly, by which the letters, and their separate Prefatory Notes, might be as it were illustrations to the Introduction, which was intended in turn to be a guide to them. Having done this with a proper _Pourvu que Dieu lui prête vie_ referring to both book and author, I thought it well to look up next what had been done in the way before me, at least to the extent of what the London Library could provide me in circumstances of enforced abstinence from the Museum and from "Bodley." From its catalogue I selected a curious eighteenth-century _Art of Letter Writing_, and four nineteenth and earliest twentieth century books--Roberts's _History of Letter Writing_ (1843) with Pickering's ever-beloved title-page and his beautiful clear print; the _Littérature Epistolaire_

### Plain English:
GEORGE SAINTSBURY LONDON G. BELL AND SONS, LTD. NEW YORK: HARCOURT, BRACE AND CO. 1922 PREFACE

My publishers asked me to write this book. They also asked me to write an introduction that was different from what you'd find in a school book of letters. They wanted me to explain what letter-writing is and what makes a letter special. So I made a plan. The letters in this book and their notes would work like examples that show what the introduction says. The introduction would help you understand the letters.

I hoped everything would go well for both the book and for me. Then I decided to look at other books that people had written about this topic before. I could only use what the London Library had. I couldn't go to the Museum or to Bodley library because of the situation at the time. I looked at their list of books and picked out an unusual book about letter-writing from the 1700s. I also found four books from the 1800s and early 1900s. One was Roberts's History of Letter Writing from 1843. It had a beautiful title page and clear printing by Pickering. The other was called Littérature Epistolaire.

light of the table-lamp, softened and enriched by its mosaic shade, gave an appearance of added opulence to the already handsome appointments of the room. The little table-clock ticked merrily from half-past eleven to a quarter to twelve. Into the cozy, bookish atmosphere of the novelist's study penetrated the muffled chime of Big Ben; it chimed the three-quarters. But, with his mind centered upon his work, Leroux wrote on ceaselessly. An odd figure of a man was this popular novelist, with patchy and untidy hair which lessened the otherwise striking contour of his brow. A neglected and unpicturesque figure, in a baggy, neutral-colored dressing-gown; a figure more fitted to a garret than to this spacious, luxurious workroom, with the soft light playing upon rank after rank of rare and costly editions, deepening the tones in the Persian carpet, making red morocco more red, purifying the vellum and regilding the gold of the choice bindings, caressing lovingly the busts and statuettes surmounting the book-shelves, and twinkling upon the scantily-covered crown of Henry Leroux. The door bell rang. Leroux, heedless of external matters, pursued his work. But the door bell rang again and continued to ring. “Soames! Soames!” Leroux raised his voice

### Plain English:
The light from the table lamp came through its colored glass shade. It made the nice things in the room look even fancier. The small clock on the table ticked away. It was almost midnight. The sound of Big Ben chiming came through the walls of the writer's study. It was three-quarters past the hour. But the writer, Leroux, kept working. He didn't stop.

Leroux was a strange-looking man. His hair was messy and patchy. It made his forehead look less striking. He wore a baggy, dull-colored robe. He looked more like someone who should be working in a tiny attic than in this big, fancy room. The soft light hit all the expensive old books on the shelves. It made the Persian carpet look deeper. It made the red leather bindings seem redder. It made the gold on the book covers shine brighter. The light touched the statues on top of the shelves. It reflected off the top of Leroux's head, where his hair was thin.

The doorbell rang. Leroux was focused on his work. He didn't pay attention. The doorbell rang again. It kept ringing. "Soames! Soames!" Leroux called out loudly.

### Plain English:
You followed my instructions well about how you used it. You're the one who worked so hard to figure out the places he went through. And you're the one who most importantly gave me such a strong feeling of who Frank really was. It feels like I must have known him really well, even though we never actually met. Those changes that happened to him were really strange, it's true. I guess even with all our modern psychology today, we still use the old religious words for these kinds of changes—cleaning up your soul, getting enlightened, and becoming one with God. But religious teachers tell us that this mysterious thing Catholics call God's Grace doesn't erase who someone naturally is. Instead, it makes their natural character stronger and changes it into something better. I think Frank had this same element in him—the same basic thing inside—that made him do those weird things with bread and butter.

### Original:
guess what I have for you." Hands behind him, Henry Allen stood looking amusedly down at his daughter. Stretched full length in a gaily striped hammock swung between two great trees, her gray eyes dreamily turned toward the distant mountain peaks, Jane Allen had not heard her father's noiseless approach over the closely clipped green lawn. At sound of his voice, she bobbed up from the hammock with an alacrity that left it swaying wildly. "Of course I was dreaming, Dad," she declared gaily, making an ineffectual grab at the hands he held behind him. "No fair using force," he warned, dexterously eluding her. "This is a guessing contest. Now which hand will you choose?" "Both hands, you mean thing!" laughed Jane. "I know what you have in one of them. It's a letter. Maybe two. Now stand and deliver." "Here you are." Obligingly obeying the imperative command, Mr. Allen handed Jane two letters. "Oh, joy! Here _you_ are!" Jane enveloped her father in a bear-like hug, planting a resounding kiss on his sun-burnt cheek. "Having played postman, I suppose my next duty is to take myself off and leave my girl to her letters," was his affectionately smiling comment.

### Plain English:
"Guess what I have for you." Henry Allen stood with his hands behind his back, looking down at his daughter with a smile. Jane Allen was lying in a colorful striped hammock that hung between two big trees. She was looking off at the mountains far away and didn't hear her dad walking quietly across the grass. When he spoke, she jumped up so fast the hammock started swinging back and forth. "Of course I was daydreaming, Dad," she said happily. She tried to grab his hands, but he wouldn't let her. "No fair using force," he said, moving his hands away. "This is a guessing game. Which hand do you pick?" "Both hands, you're being mean!" Jane laughed. "I know what you have. It's a letter. Maybe two letters. Come on, give them to me." "Here you go." Mr. Allen gave Jane two letters. "Yes!" Jane gave her father a big hug and kissed his sunburned cheek. "I guess now that I've brought the mail, I should go and let you read your letters," he said with a smile.

PUBLICATION NUMBER 107-8 WILLIAM ANDREWS CLARK MEMORIAL LIBRARY UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, LOS ANGELES 1964 INTRODUCTION John Oldmixon's _Essay on Criticism_, like his _Reflections on Dr. Swift's Letter to the Earl of Oxford, about the English Tongue_,[1] provides evidence to support Dr. Johnson's description of its author as a "scribbler for a party," and indicates that Oldmixon must have been devoted to gathering examples of what appeared to him to be the good and bad in literature. The story of the appearance of the _Essay on Criticism_ in 1728 should begin in 1724, when Oldmixon published in one volume his _Critical History of England, Ecclesiastical and Civil_. Dr. Zachary Grey's criticism of this book was answered by Oldmixon in 1725 in _A Review of Dr. Zachary Grey's Defence of our Ancient and Modern Historians_. In 1726 a two-volume edition of the _Critical History of England_ appeared with the 1725 edition of the _Review of Dr. Zachary Grey's Defence_ appended to the first volume. In the preface to the second volume of the _Critical History_ Oldmixon referred to the _Essay on Criticism_, stating that it was ready

### Plain English:
# REPRINT SOCIETY JOHN OLDMIXON AN ESSAY ON CRITICISM (1728) _INTRODUCTION_ BY R. J. MADDEN, C.S.B. PUBLICATION NUMBER 107-8 WILLIAM ANDREWS CLARK MEMORIAL LIBRARY UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, LOS ANGELES 1964 INTRODUCTION

John Oldmixon wrote an _Essay on Criticism_. He also wrote _Reflections on Dr. Swift's Letter to the Earl of Oxford, about the English Tongue_.[1] These works show what Dr. Johnson said about him. Johnson called Oldmixon a "scribbler for a party." His writings show that Oldmixon spent time collecting examples of good and bad literature.

The _Essay on Criticism_ came out in 1728. To understand this, we need to go back to 1724. That year, Oldmixon published his _Critical History of England, Ecclesiastical and Civil_ in one book. Dr. Zachary Grey criticized this book. Oldmixon wrote back in 1725. He published _A Review of Dr. Zachary Grey's Defence of our Ancient and Modern Historians_. In 1726, a new two-volume version of the _Critical History of England_ came out. The 1725 _Review of Dr. Zachary Grey's Defence_ was added to the first volume. In the preface to the second volume, Oldmixon mentioned the _Essay on Criticism_. He said it was ready.

Daughter--Mdlle. de la Meure Marries--My Despair and Jealousy--A Change for the Better In the beginning of March, 1757, I received a letter from my friend Madame Manzoni, which she sent to me by a young man of good appearance, with a frank and high-born air, whom I recognized as a Venetian by his accent. He was young Count Tiretta de Trevisa, recommended to my care by Madame Manzoni, who said that he would tell me his story, which I might be sure would be a true one. The kind woman sent to me by him a small box in which she told me I should find all my manuscripts, as she did not think she would ever see me again. I gave Tiretta the heartiest of welcomes, telling him that he could not have found a better way to my favour than through a woman to whom I was under the greatest obligations. “And now, that you may be at your ease with me, I should like to know in what manner I can be of service to you?” “I have need of your friendship, perhaps of your purse, but at any rate of your protection.” “You have my friendship

### Plain English:
My Daughter Gets Married--I Feel Upset and Jealous--Things Get Better

In early March 1757, I got a letter from my friend Madame Manzoni. She sent it with a young man who looked nice and acted like he was from a rich family. From his accent, I could tell he was from Venice. He was a young count named Tiretta de Trevisa. Madame Manzoni had told him to come see me. She said he would tell me his story, and that it would be true. She also sent me a small box with him. In it, she said, I would find all my writings. She told him that she didn't think she would ever see me again. I welcomed Tiretta warmly. I told him that he couldn't have done anything better to get on my good side than to come from a woman I owed so much to. "Now, so you can feel comfortable with me, I want to know how I can help you." "I need your friendship. Maybe I need money from you too. But I definitely need your protection." "You have my friendship."

INTRODUCTION This Supplement is designed to supply a double need: it furnishes an analytical index to the entire series of twenty volumes; and it affords a great deal of additional information, bearing on the subject-matter of these volumes, but which from its very nature it was impossible to incorporate in the text. This additional information includes biographical sketches of the characters mentioned in each volume; similar sketches of prominent Canadians who for one reason or another do not appear in any of the twenty volumes; and brief descriptions of wars, battles, treaties, and political and other events having a vital bearing on the history of Canada. References have been added, wherever necessary, to the principal sources which the student may consult for further information. The whole has been thrown into one alphabetical arrangement, and it constitutes, to a large extent, a dictionary of Canadian history. To satisfy further the desire of those who, after reading the foregoing volumes, find it profitable to investigate more fully certain lines of inquiry suggested by the narratives, it has been thought advisable to add a list of manuscript sources from which new material may be

### Plain English:
CO., LIMITED 1912 COPYRIGHT, 1911. COPYRIGHT IN GREAT BRITAIN. INTRODUCTION This book is made to do two things. First, it gives you an index for all twenty books in this series. Second, it includes a lot of extra information about the topics in those books. This extra information couldn't fit in the actual books. The extra information has short life stories of the people mentioned in each book. It also has short life stories of important Canadians who aren't in any of the twenty books. You'll also find short descriptions of wars, battles, treaties, and important political events that shaped Canadian history. We added references to show you where to find more information on each topic. Everything is organized in alphabetical order, like a dictionary. This makes it work like a dictionary of Canadian history. We also added a list of old documents and papers. Students who read the first twenty books might want to learn more about certain topics. These documents can give you new material to study for those topics.

NEW YORK. 1875. In reprinting the following articles, by Miss Octavia Hill, of London, it is not proposed that the system described is, in all its details, applicable to this country. The conditions of poverty in America and in England differ widely. The Municipal forms of Government there are very unlike our own. But the broad truths of humanity are the same everywhere, and the principles which underlie Miss Hill's efforts, those of helping the poor towards self-support and self-respect; of bringing to their aid an unwearied patience, a true sympathy, and a large hope; of loving them and trusting them; above all, of realizing that they and we are children of One Father; these principles are applicable to all countries and to every people. LOUISA LEE SCHUYLER, President, State Charities Aid Association. NEW YORK, _April 14, 1875_. COTTAGE PROPERTY IN LONDON. _November 1, 1866._ The subject of dwellings for the poor is attracting so much attention, that an account of a small attempt to improve them may be interesting to many readers, especially as the plan adopted is one which has answered pecuniarily, and which, while it might be undertaken by private individuals without much risk, would bring them

### Plain English:
NEW YORK, 1875. We are reprinting these articles by Octavia Hill from London. We're not saying that everything she describes will work exactly the same way here in America. Poverty is different in America than in England. The way our governments work is different too. But people are people everywhere. The ideas behind what Miss Hill does are the same everywhere. She wants to help poor people take care of themselves and feel good about themselves. She shows them patience, real kindness, and hope. She cares about them and believes in them. Most importantly, she knows that we're all equal. These ideas work for every country and every group of people. LOUISA LEE SCHUYLER, President, State Charities Aid Association. NEW YORK, April 14, 1875. COTTAGE PROPERTY IN LONDON. November 1, 1866. People are talking a lot about homes for poor people. So we thought you might like to hear about a small project to make these homes better. This is especially true because the plan we used actually made money. Private people could do this without much risk, and it would help them.

### Original:
The house and place had about them that characteristic appearance of abundance and slovenly neglect which is, unfortunately, almost peculiar to our country. The house was a long slated one, and stood upon a little eminence, about three or four hundred yards from the highway. It was approached by a broad and ragged boreen or mock avenue, as it might be called, that was in very good keeping with the premises to which it led. As you entered it from the road, you had to pass through an iron gate, which it was a task to open, and which, when opened, it was another task to shut. In consequence of this difficulty, foot passengers had made themselves a way upon each side of it, through which they went to and came from the house; and in this they were sanctioned by the example of the family themselves, who, so long as these side paths were passable, manifested as much reluctance to open or close the gate as any one else. The month was May; and nothing could be more delightful and exhilarating than the breeze which played over the green fields that were now radiant with the light which was

### Plain English:
The house and the land around it looked like they had plenty of money but weren't well taken care of. This was pretty common in our country. The house was long with a slate roof. It sat on a small hill, maybe three or four hundred yards away from the road. You got to it by walking down a wide, messy path or driveway. The path was in bad shape, just like the house and land around it. When you came from the road, you had to go through an iron gate. Opening the gate was hard, and closing it was just as hard. Because of this, people started making their own path on each side of the gate instead. They used these side paths to go to and from the house. Even the family who lived there did the same thing. As long as the side paths were easy to walk on, they didn't want to open or close the gate either. It was May. The wind blowing over the green fields was really nice and made you feel good.

This was in 1659, when Rhodes's two apprentices, Betterton and Kynaston, were the stars. These companies afterwards united, and were called the Duke's Company. About the same time, Killigrew, that eternal caterer for good things, collected together a few of the old actors who were honoured with the title of the "King's Company," or "His Majesty's Servants," which distinction is preserved by the Drury Lane Company, to the present day, and is inherited from Killigrew, who built and opened the first theatre in Drury Lane, in 1663. In 1662, Sir William Davenant obtained a patent for building "the Duke's Theatre," in Little Lincoln's Inn Fields, which he opened with the play of "the Siege of Rhodes," written by himself. ### Plain English:
A few actors who survived the wars and poverty came together under Sir William Davenant at the Red Bull Theatre. At the same time, a bookseller named Rhodes fixed up the Cockpit in Drury Lane. He started a new company there with performers who had never acted before. This happened in 1659. Two of Rhodes's young apprentices, Betterton and Kynaston, became the stars. Later, these two companies joined together and became known as the Duke's Company. Around the same time, a man named Killigrew brought together some of the old actors. They were called the "King's Company" or "His Majesty's Servants." The Drury Lane Company still uses this title today because they got it from Killigrew. He built the first theatre in Drury Lane and opened it in 1663. In 1662, Sir William Davenant got permission to build "the Duke's Theatre" in Little Lincoln's Inn Fields. He opened it with a play called "the Siege of Rhodes," which he wrote himself. The company performed there until 1671. Then another "Duke's Theatre" was built in Dorset Gardens. Sir Christopher Wren designed it. It looked similar to the one in Lincoln's Inn.

PREFACE

In the Boys' Golden West Series, I tried my best to show readers the West that I knew when I was a boy.

The frontier had all kinds of people. There were men who were sneaky and bad. They were so low they crawled on the ground like snakes. There were girls who had been through so much shame that you could barely tell them apart from Native American women. There were Native Americans who were quiet and had red, swollen eyes from drinking. When they were sober, they were fair and peaceful. When they were drunk, they were loud and mean. And then there were the White Men who built the West. These men shaped and created the big, open West before it was ruined by farmers and fences. Their guns were their law, and they didn't need official papers to act. They faced huge dangers alone and didn't back down. They built an ideal civilization—a country where people left their doors unlocked at night and kept open minds—men who were always kind to the weak and helpless, even if they

THE BELGIAN ARMY'S ACTIVITIES SINCE THE BATTLE OF THE YSER. Everyone knows how severely the Belgian Army was tested in the initial stages of the campaign. Caught unawares by the war while in the midst of re-organisation, it had to struggle alone, for long weeks on end, against forces greatly superior in both numbers and equipment, suddenly hurled against it in accordance with a deliberate and carefully planned scheme of attack. Yet the Belgian Army bravely faced the enemy, grimly determined to fulfil its duty to the last, and at once aroused enthusiasm by its heroic resistance at Liège, from August 8 onwards, to the onset of several army corps. On the 12th the troops emerged victoriously from the bloody engagements at Haelen; and not till the 18th, and then only to escape being overwhelmed by the ever-rising flood of invasion, did the Belgian Army abandon its positions at La Gette and fall back on Antwerp, the national stronghold in which would be concentrated the whole of the country's powers of opposition. With the exception of Chapter II, which deals with the origin of the Polynesian people, there is hardly an incident in the whole book that has not come within the scope of my own personal experience. Hence this is essentially a travel narrative. I have confined myself to the task of interpreting the problems of the Pacific in the light of the episodes of everyday life. Wherever possible, I have tried to let the incident speak for itself, and to include in the picture the average ideals of the various races, together with my own impressions of them and my own reflections. The field is a tremendous one. It encompasses the most important regions that lie along the great avenues of commerce and general intercourse. The Pacific is a great combination of geographical, ethnological, and political factors that is extremely diverse in its sources. I have tried to discern within them a unit of human commonality, as the seeker after truth is bound to do if his discoveries are to be of any value. But the result has been an unconventional book. For I have sometimes been compelled

It is only a sketch--a sketch of the problems which the war created or rendered pressing--of the conditions under which they cropped up; of the simplicist ways in which they were conceived by the distinguished politicians who volunteered to solve them; of the delegates' natural limitations and electioneering commitments and of the secret influences by which they were swayed; of the peoples' needs and expectations; of the unwonted procedure adopted by the Conference and of the fateful consequences of its decisions to the world. In dealing with all those matters I aimed at impartiality, which is an unattainable ideal, but I trust that sincerity and detachment have brought me reasonably close to it. Having no pet theories of my own to champion, my principal standard of judgment is derived from the law of causality and the rules of historical criticism. The fatal tactical mistake chargeable to the Conference lay in its making the charter of the League of Nations and the treaty of peace with the Central Powers interdependent. For the maxims that underlie the former are irreconcilable with those that should determine the latter, and the efforts to combine them must, among other

### Plain English:
We have records of what people said and debated. This is just an outline. It covers the problems that the war created or made urgent. It shows the conditions that caused these problems. It explains how famous politicians tried to solve them in simple ways. It looks at the delegates' weaknesses and their promises to voters. It describes the hidden forces that influenced them. It shows what people needed and wanted. It talks about the unusual way the Conference worked and how its decisions changed the world. When I wrote about all this, I tried to be fair. Being completely fair is impossible, but I tried my best to be honest and stay neutral. I don't have my own theories to push. Instead, I judged things based on cause and effect and the rules of how historians should work. The Conference made one big tactical mistake. It tied the League of Nations charter to the peace treaty with the Central Powers. They depended on each other. But the ideas behind the League don't match the ideas that should guide the peace treaty. Trying to combine them would have to lead to problems.

members of the newspaper and printing trades in Liverpool were desirous of forming a regiment composed of men connected with those businesses. A meeting was held in the Liverpool Town Hall, and the scheme was so well received that steps were taken towards the formation of a corps. Sanction was obtained, and on the 21st February, 1861, the officers and men of the new unit took the oath of allegiance at St. George's Hall. Thus came into being the 80th Lancashire Rifle Volunteers, and on the 2nd April, 1863, the 73rd Battalion of the Lancashire Rifle Volunteers was amalgamated with it. In the early days of its existence the new unit attended reviews and inspections at Mount Vernon, Newton-le-Willows and Aintree. Some time afterwards it was renumbered the 19th Lancashire Rifle Volunteers. Later--in 1888--it became the 6th Volunteer Battalion of The King's (Liverpool Regiment). The early parades of the Regiment took place at Rose Hill Police Station, and the Corn Exchange, Brunswick Street, until Headquarters were established at 16, Soho Street. ### Plain English:
People who worked in newspapers and printing in Liverpool wanted to start their own military group. They held a meeting at Liverpool Town Hall. Everyone liked the idea, so they started to make it happen. They got permission, and on February 21st, 1861, the officers and soldiers took an oath at St. George's Hall. This is how the 80th Lancashire Rifle Volunteers was created. On April 2nd, 1863, another group called the 73rd Battalion of the Lancashire Rifle Volunteers joined with it. At first, the group did training exercises and inspections at Mount Vernon, Newton-le-Willows, and Aintree. Later, it was given a new number: the 19th Lancashire Rifle Volunteers. Then in 1888, it became the 6th Volunteer Battalion of The King's (Liverpool Regiment). At the start, the group trained at Rose Hill Police Station and the Corn Exchange on Brunswick Street. Then they moved their main office to 16 Soho Street. The people who joined in these training sessions did great work. Because of them, a proper military unit was formed. Men were trained for fighting.

### Original:
OTHER STUDIES BY ADMIRAL SIR CYPRIAN BRIDGE, G.C.B. PREFACE The essays collected in this volume are republished in the hope that they may be of some use to those who are interested in naval history. The aim has been to direct attention to certain historical occurrences and conditions which the author ventures to think have been often misunderstood. An endeavour has been made to show the continuity of the operation of sea-power throughout history, and the importance of recognising this at the present day. In some cases specially relating to our navy at different periods a revision of the more commonly accepted conclusions--formed, it is believed, on imperfect knowledge--is asked for. It is also hoped that the intimate connection between naval history in the strict sense and military history in the strict sense has been made apparent, and likewise the fact that both are in reality branches of the general history of a nation and not something altogether distinct from and outside it. In a collection of essays on kindred subjects some repetitions are inevitable, but it is believed that they will be found present only to a moderate extent in the following pages. My nephew, Mr. J. S. C.

### Plain English:
OTHER STUDIES BY ADMIRAL SIR CYPRIAN BRIDGE, G.C.B. PREFACE

The essays in this book are being published again. We hope they will help people who are interested in naval history. The author wants to point out certain historical events and situations that he thinks people have often gotten wrong. He tries to show how sea-power has been important throughout history, and why we should understand this today. In some cases about our navy at different times, he is asking people to reconsider what most people believe—beliefs that he thinks are based on incomplete information. He also wants to show that naval history and military history are closely connected. They are both really just parts of a country's overall history, not separate from it. When you collect essays on similar topics, some ideas will be repeated. But the author believes there are not too many repetitions in this book. My nephew, Mr. J. S. C.

PREFACE.

I'm finishing the last volume of these Illustrations. I want to say how happy I am that people have liked this work. It's been popular both in this country and in Europe. Some smart writers have complained about books like this one that cover lots of different animals. Here are their complaints. First, these books talk about many different animals but don't finish telling the whole story about any one type. Second, the authors say they're only writing about new or unknown animals. But they actually include lots of animals that you can read about in regular nature books. Third, putting out new discoveries this fast is unfair to scientists who spend their whole careers studying just one type of animal. But these complaints don't have good answers. First of all, these mixed animal books should really be thought of as pictures...

George Wither (1588-1667) _The praises of poetry have been often sung in ancient and in modern times; strange powers have been ascribed to it of influence over animate and inanimate auditors; its force over fascinated crowds has been acknowledged; but before Wither, no one ever celebrated its power at home, the wealth and the strength which this divine gift confers upon its possessor. Fame, and that too after death, was all which hitherto the poets had promised themselves from this art. It seems to have been left to Wither to discover that poetry was a present possession, as well as a rich reversion, and that the Muse has a promise of both lives,--of this, and of that which was to come._--CHARLES LAMB. William Browne (1591-? 1645) _I feel an envious touch, And tell thee Swain: that at thy fame I grutch, Wishing the Art that makes this Poem shine, And this thy Work (wert not thou wrongèd) mine._ GEORGE WITHER: _To the Author_ [_of Britannia's Pastorals_]. ### Plain English:
Ben Jonson praised poetry. He said to look at the work of Breton.

George Wither lived from 1588 to 1667. People have always talked about how great poetry is. They said it had strange powers. They said it could influence people and even things that aren't alive. They said it had power over crowds that loved it. But before Wither, nobody ever talked about what poetry could do for the person writing it. Nobody talked about the wealth and strength it gives to the poet. Before Wither, poets only hoped for fame after they died. That was all they wanted from writing poetry. Wither figured out something new. He realized that poetry gives you good things right now. It's not just something that helps you after you're dead. The Muse gives rewards in this life and in the next life too.

Charles Lamb wrote this.

William Browne lived from 1591 to around 1645. George Wither wrote to Browne about his work. Wither said he felt jealous. He told Browne that he envied his fame. He wished that the skill that made Browne's poem so good was his own skill instead. He wished that Browne's work was his work. Browne had not been treated fairly, Wither said, but Wither still wanted what Browne had.

Contents include a prefatory note. There is a section on Nicholas Breton called "A Sweet Pastoral." There is also a section called "Aglaia: a Pastoral."

PREFACE

Some railroads are like some people. They go through good times and bad times. The Rome, Watertown & Ogdensburgh railroad was one of these. During its forty years, it experienced everything a railroad could. Sometimes it was one of the best railroads around. Sometimes it was one of the worst.

The author tried to tell the true facts in this book. He wrote without anger. In fact, he still likes the old R. W. & O. railroad. He tried to be fair when he wrote about what happened. These events are now part of history.

The author hopes you will be kind to this book. He hopes you will think about it carefully and be patient with it. He looks forward to hearing from you.

These books about old London are coming out at a perfect time. Everyone in England is thinking about this amazing look at London's past. London is the most important city in the empire.

The Pageant will be beautiful, grand, educational, and interesting. But really, London doesn't need a Pageant to show how great it is. London is already like a Pageant by itself. A ceaseless and relentless hand is laid on almost every animal to provide food for human beings. Nothing that lives or grows is missed by man in his search for food to satisfy his appetite. Animal food will be useful in emergency, also at other times; still, plant substance is more favorable to health, endurance, and power of mind. ### Plain English:
He was born in Essex in 1771. He died of heart disease on February 22, 1845, when he was seventy-four years old. His father was a smart man who moved around a lot. When he settled in England, he spent his money by buying about nineteen different properties, changing them, ruining them, and then selling them. His mother came from a French family in Languedoc. They had moved to England because of the Revocation of the Edict of Nantes. Sydney Smith's grandfather on his mother's side could not speak English. Sydney Smith said some of his happy personality came from his French blood. He had three brothers. His oldest brother Robert, called Bobus, went to Eton. There he helped Canning, Frere, and John Smith write the school magazine called the Microcosm. Later at Cambridge, Bobus became known as a great Latin scholar. Sydney Smith first went to school in Southampton, and then to Winchester, where he became captain of the school. He then spent six months in Normandy to improve his French before going to New College, Oxford. After he got a job as a fellow there, his father left him to take care of himself.

by David Widger A GHETTO VIOLET By Leopold Kompert From “Christian and Leah.” Translated by A. S. Arnold. 1869 Through the open window came the clear trill of a canary singing blithely in its cage. Within the tidy, homely little room a pale-faced girl and a youth of slender frame listened intently while the bird sang its song. The girl was the first to break the silence. “Ephraim, my brother!” she said. “What is it, dear Viola?” “I wonder does the birdie know that it is the Sabbath to-day?” “What a child you are!” answered Ephraim. “Yes, that 's always the way; when you clever men can't explain a thing, you simply dismiss the question by calling it childish,” Viola exclaimed, as though quite angry. “And, pray, why should n't the bird know? The whole week it scarcely sang a note: to-day it warbles and warbles so that it makes my head ache. And what's the reason? Every Sabbath it's just the same, I notice it regularly. Shall I tell you what my idea is? “The whole week long the little bird looks into our room and sees nothing but the humdrum of work-a-day life. To-day it sees the bright

### Plain English:
A canary was singing loudly and clearly through an open window. It was in a cage. In a small, neat room, a thin girl and a young man were listening carefully to the bird sing. The girl spoke first and broke the silence.

"Ephraim, my brother!" she said. "What is it, dear Viola?" he asked.

"I wonder if the bird knows that today is the Sabbath?"

"You're so silly!" Ephraim answered.

"That's what you always do," Viola said, sounding angry. "When you smart men can't explain something, you just call it childish. But why shouldn't the bird know? All week long it barely sings. Today it sings and sings so much it gives me a headache. Why? Every Sabbath it's the same. I notice it every time. Do you want to know what I think?

The little bird looks into our room all week and only sees regular, boring work. Today it sees something bright.

BISHOP BUTLER. {vii} PREFACE This little book is the preliminary sketch of a system of ethics. Its form differs from that of most contemporary books on the subject because of the omission of the traditional controversies. I have attempted to study morality directly, to derive its conceptions and laws from an analysis of life. I have made this attempt because, in the first place, I believe that theoretical ethics is seriously embarrassed by its present emphasis on the history and criticism of doctrines; by its failure to resort to experience, where without more ado it may solve its problems on their merits. But, in the second place, I hope that by appealing to experience and neglecting scholastic technicalities, I may connect ethical theory with every-day reflection on practical matters. Morality is, without doubt, the most human and urgent of all topics of study; and I should like, if possible, to make it appear so. The references which I have embodied in the

was a time in the history of our country when the stoutest hearts were filled with despair. The defeat of General Gates, followed by the overrunning of the Carolinas, the treachery of Benedict Arnold, and the general bad condition of our army, did not, certainly, tend to cheer those thousands of noble souls earnestly praying for the success of the American cause. It is of that period, of that darkest hour, which precedes the day, that we purpose to write. Toward the close of a long, disagreeable day, two women sat by the fireside of a dwelling some fifteen or eighteen miles from Charleston. The two presented a contrast, indeed, even though their features were alike. One was an elderly woman, with hair sprinkled with threads of gray, though she yet retained much of her early beauty. The other was a young girl, whose age could not have exceeded nineteen. Although not absolutely beautiful, there was that about her which made her fascinating. With features finely molded, and a graceful carriage, her figure was a model of physical grace and perfectness. Her hair was of that golden hue, so seldom seen save in poet’s dreams. Her voice was as musical

### Plain English:
There was a time in our country's history when even the bravest people felt hopeless. General Gates had been defeated. The British had taken over the Carolinas. Benedict Arnold had betrayed us. Our army was in bad shape. All of this made thousands of people who believed in America feel scared and sad. We're going to write about that time. It was the darkest moment before things got better. Late in a long, unpleasant day, two women sat by a fire in a house about fifteen or eighteen miles from Charleston. The two women looked very different from each other, even though they had similar faces. One was an older woman with gray hair mixed in. But she was still beautiful. The other was a young girl, probably nineteen years old. She wasn't absolutely beautiful, but there was something about her that was attractive. Her face was nicely shaped. She stood and moved with grace. She had the perfect body. Her hair was a golden color that you almost never see except in poems. Her voice was pretty to listen to.

### Plain English:
By July, the art season in London was ending. By the end of August, most English artists had left the city. Some of the best ones went to Wales and the English countryside to paint. They wanted to find quiet places with cottages and open fields. Artists spread out everywhere. They went to the hills between Scotland and the Shetland Islands. They painted in the cornfields and by the lakes in England. They worked in the valleys and mountain streams of Wales. On the continent, artists and people who just liked art were everywhere too. There was a guy named Smith working across the countryside with temperatures at 95 degrees. He would relax with a pipe at a café when the sun went down. Hundreds of other artists were doing the same thing all over Europe. In Florence and Rome, the galleries had so many artists painting that they couldn't fit any more easels. In Switzerland and Savoy, you could see small white tents and umbrellas all over the mountainsides. You might see a guy named Brown coming down a mountain path from Flegère. He had all his painting stuff strapped to his back like a toolbox, after spending the whole day sketching the tall mountains called the Aiguilles.

Vandewater Street, N.Y. * * * * * TO THE PRACTICAL CULTIVATORS OF THE SOIL, The True Lords of the Manor, THIS VOLUME IS DEDICATED, BY THEIR SINCERE FRIEND, THE AUTHOR. * * * * * PREFATORY NOTE TO THE READER. If "he who causes two blades of grass to grow where but one grew before, is a benefactor of his race," he is not less so who imparts to millions a knowledge of the methods by which it is done. The last half century has been the era of experiments and writing on the cultivation of the soil. The result has been the acquisition of more knowledge on the subjects embraced, than the world had attained in all its previous history. That knowledge is scattered through many volumes of numerous periodicals and books, and interspersed with many theories, and much speculation, that can never be valuable in practice. In the form in which it is presented, it confuses, rather than aids, the great mass of cultivators. Hence the prejudice against "_book-farming_." Provided established facts only are presented, they are none the worse for being printed. The object of this volume is to condense, and present in an intelligible form,

### Plain English:
Vandewater Street, N.Y. * * * * * TO THE PRACTICAL FARMERS WHO WORK THE LAND, The True Lords of the Manor, THIS BOOK IS DEDICATED, BY THEIR SINCERE FRIEND, THE AUTHOR. * * * * * A NOTE TO THE READER. If "someone who makes two blades of grass grow where only one grew before is helping humanity," then so is someone who teaches millions how to do it. The last fifty years have been a time of experiments and writing about farming. The result is that we know more about these subjects than the world ever knew before. But that knowledge is spread across many magazines and books. It's mixed with theories and guesses that don't actually work in real farming. The way it's presented just confuses farmers instead of helping them. That's why people don't trust "book farming." Real facts don't get worse just because they're printed. The goal of this book is to take all that information and present it in a way that's easy to understand.

### Plain English:
The development of Israelite religion is a problem that is not covered in this book (pages 4 and 114 and following). This book is based on the Amarna tablets, Egyptian records, and recent excavation results. The material has been understood using comparative religion. This method was used because scholars disagree about Old Testament theology and modern research. The evidence presented is accurate and fair. It was difficult to write this because no one has written a comprehensive treatment of this subject before. However, the list of sources at the end shows that the most important materials only recently became widely available. Light from the full moon filtered through the large window which ran the entire length of the boys sleeping quarters. Twenty cots filled the dormitory, and all but one held its sleeper. Dark-haired Garry Coleman was standing beside his cot, quietly dressing. Every now and then he would cast an anxious glance toward the darkened door at the end of the dormitory. Above all, he must not disturb the charge-of-quarters, or all would be lost. As he sat on the edge of the cot to put on his shoes, Garry heard a squeak from one of the cots. He stiffened, his heart thumping fearfully. Then Garry breathed easily. He saw that it was only Patch, who occupied the bunk next to his. "Hey, Garry, where are you going?" Patch asked interestedly. Patch was short and towheaded. He was Garry's best friend, and so Garry did not mind telling him. "I'm going to the spaceport and watch the _Orion_ blast off for the Von Braun Space Station. Want to go?" "Sure thing!" Patch said. "You'll have to take the

Introduction

It would have been a serious mistake not to write down stories about the Christian faith and bravery of the good men who served in every part of the military during the war in South Africa. These men worked hard to help their fellow soldiers. Many soldiers came to believe in God because of their work. Men who were hurt in battle or in hospitals were comforted. Some men even found peace with God before they died. It would have also been a mistake not to tell the story of the chaplains and volunteers who worked so hard to help others. Parts of this scene will soon make it look like something from history. Future generations will be excited to learn about how people actually lived back then, which they'll call the "old days." The colorful small buses called "coucous" that used to sit on the Place de la Concorde and block the Cours-la-Reine had been around for a hundred years. There were still lots of them in 1830. But by 1842, you barely saw them anymore. The only time you'd find them was for special events in the suburbs, like the water fountain shows at Versailles. In 1820, the different famous areas around Paris didn't all have regular coach services. But the Touchard family—a father and son—had gotten the right to run all travel and transportation to all the big towns within forty-five miles. Their business was a really successful operation on rue du Faubourg-Saint-Denis. Even though they had old rights, even though they worked hard and had money and all the benefits of a big organized company, their coaches faced tough competition from the coucous for trips that were fifteen or twenty miles away. ## PREFACE

This book is not a complete guide to how children think. Instead, it covers certain parts of children's minds that I have noticed and found interesting. I tried to write these studies in a way that is correct but also easy for regular people to read. I wanted to reach parents and young teachers, not just people who study psychology.

SUMMARY

LECTURE I: Where Browning got his ideas as a teacher. How the five poems in this course connect to each other. "Caliban upon Setebos"—where it came from and what people said about it. What Caliban is like. Compare this with Shakespeare's Caliban. Breaking down the poem:

(i) The introduction (lines 1-23).
(ii) What Caliban thinks about Setebos.
(_a_) Where Setebos lives (lines 24-25).
(_b_) Setebos created all living and non-living things (lines 26-55).
(_c_) Why Setebos created things: to please himself or just for fun (lines 55-84, 170-199).
(_d_) When creatures pray to Setebos, they don't know if he'll answer because he's unpredictable (lines 85-97).
(_e_) Setebos's main trait—he has power but uses it however he wants (lines 98-126, 200-240).
(iii) "The Quiet" and what Caliban thinks about evil (lines 127-141, 246-249). Other ideas in the poem about: _A._ Sacrifice. _B._ Life after death. _C._ Why God might have needed to become human, based on the bad qualities given to "the Quiet."

LECTURE II: CLEON

"Cleon." Compare it with "Caliban": (i) how the characters change; (ii) what they have in common. What the Greeks thought about life and what influenced Cleon. Breaking down the poem. Part I: Introduction and description (lines 1-42). Part II: All the things Cleon is good at show that people have been getting better over time.

J. SYMES. INTRODUCTION. George Payne Rainsford James, Historiographer Royal to King William IV., was born in London in the first year of the nineteenth century, and died at Venice in 1860. His comparatively short life was exceptionally full and active. He was historian, politician and traveller, the reputed author of upwards of a hundred novels, the compiler and editor of nearly half as many volumes of letters, memoirs, and biographies, a poet and a pamphleteer, and, during the last ten years of his life, British Consul successively in Massachusetts, Norfolk (Virginia), and Venice. Scott, on whose style he founded his own, encouraged him to persevere in his career as a novelist; Washington Irving admired him, and Walter Savage Landor composed an epitaph to his memory. He achieved the distinction of being twice burlesqued by Thackeray, and two columns are devoted to an account of him in the new "Dictionary of National Biography." Each generation follows its own gods, and G. P. R. James was, perhaps, too prolific an author to maintain the popularity which made him "in some ways the most successful novelist of his

### Plain English:
George Payne Rainsford James was born in London in 1800 and died in Venice in 1860. He was the official historian for King William IV. His life was short but very busy and full of activity. He was a historian, a politician, and a traveler. He wrote over a hundred novels. He also put together and edited almost fifty books of letters, memoirs, and biographies. He was a poet and wrote pamphlets too. For the last ten years of his life, he worked as the British Consul in three different places: Massachusetts, Norfolk in Virginia, and Venice. He was friends with many important people of his time. Scott, whose writing style James copied, told him to keep writing novels. Washington Irving liked his work. Walter Savage Landor even wrote an epitaph for him after he died. Thackeray made fun of him in his writing twice. James gets two whole columns in the new "Dictionary of National Biography." Every generation likes different writers. James may have written too many books too quickly to stay popular. He was one of the most successful novelists of his time.

Once he got to his office—which he called "Dental Parlors" on his sign—he took off his coat and shoes and unbuttoned his vest. He filled his small stove with coke and then lay back in his operating chair by the window. He read the newspaper, drank his beer, and smoked his big pipe while his food digested. He was full, tired, and warm. After a while, he drank so much beer and got so hot from the room, the cheap tobacco, and his heavy meal that he fell asleep. Late in the afternoon, his canary bird in its gold cage above his head started to sing. He woke up slowly and finished the rest of his beer, which was flat and stale by then. He took his concertina down from the bookcase, where it sat next to seven books called "Allen's Practical Dentist," and played about six very sad songs on it. But long before the red man, even long before the growth of the present forests, there lived an ancient race, whose origin and fate are surrounded with impenetrable darkness. The remains of their habitations, temples and tombs, are the only voices that tell us of their existence. Over broad areas, in the most fertile valleys, and along the numerous tributaries of the great rivers of the central and western portions of the United States, are to be found these wonderful remains, of the existence and origin of which, even the oldest red man could give no history. Following in the track of these ancient tumuli, which have been raised with some degree of order and sagacity, we are

SMYTH, M.A., B.Sc., Ph.D. When the first European settlers came to North America and started clearing the forests, they thought the Native American tribes were the original people of this continent. But long before Native Americans existed, and even before the current forests grew, there was an ancient group of people. We don't know where they came from or what happened to them. The only proof they lived here is what's left of their homes, temples, and burial sites. These amazing remains can be found across large areas. They're in the most fertile valleys and along the many rivers in the central and western United States. Even the oldest Native Americans couldn't tell any stories about who these people were or where they came from. These ancient mounds were built in an organized and smart way. If we follow them and study them, we can learn more.

Its literature, which extends over two centuries, is voluminous, but of little value to the practical scientific worker, since hardly two descriptions can be found which agree. The present work is intended to supply this want, by giving, in a tabulated form, a concise account of the doctrines, rites, ceremonies, and customs, together with the technical and theological terms, of the Muhammadan religion. will, I know, permit me to address you these essays which are more the product of your erudition than of my enthusiasm. With the motives of their appearance you are familiar. We have wondered together that a society so avid of experience and enlargement as is ours, should ignore the chief expression of its closest neighbour, its highest rival and its coheir in Europe: should ignore, I mean, the literature of the French. We have laughed together, not without despair, to see the mind of England, for all its majesty and breadth, informed at the most critical moments in the policy of France by such residents of Paris as were at the best fanatical, at the worst (and most ordinary) corrupt. Seeing around us here a philosophy and method drawn from northern Germany, a true and subtle sympathy with the Italians, and a perpetual, just and accurate comment upon the minor nationalities of Europe, a mass of recorded travel superior by far to that of other countries, we marvelled that France in particular should have remained unknown. We were willing, in an earlier youth, to read this riddle in somewhat crude solutions. I think we have each of us arrived,

### Plain English:
I know you'll let me share these essays with you. They come more from what you know than from what I care about. You know why they're being published. We've both noticed that our society loves new experiences and wants to grow. But it ignores the most important thing from its closest neighbor. It ignores our biggest rival and our equal in Europe. I mean, it ignores French literature. We've laughed about this together, though we were also sad about it. England's mind is smart and broad. But when it comes to important decisions about France, it only listens to people living in Paris. And those people are either crazy or corrupt. Most of them are corrupt. Around us here, I see ideas and methods from northern Germany. I see real understanding of Italian culture. I see constant, fair, and accurate writing about smaller European countries. We have more books about travel than any other country. So it's strange that France has stayed unknown. When we were younger, we had simple answers to this puzzle. I think we've both come to understand it better now.

memory of the past. It refers to an incident which occurred only a few years after the beginning of the present century, when I was a boy at the burgh school of Peebles, a small town on the Tweed. The school in its way had a somewhat superior reputation, and drew to it pupils from a distance of several miles around. Trudging in all weathers, the children of farmers and ploughmen came to be educated along with boys and girls belonging to the town. Whatever they were, all were treated alike, and the intermingling of classes was never found to be in any respect disadvantageous; on the contrary, there sprung up agreeable acquaintanceships between the town and country boys that were mutually useful and agreeable. Among the crowd of country lads who thronged in daily, there was one I have some cause to remember. His name was Rob Graham. I will try to give a picture of Rob. ### Plain English:
This is a memory from a long time ago. It happened just a few years after 1900, when I was a student at a school in Peebles, a small town. The school had a good reputation and students came from miles around to go there. Kids from farms and their parents' workers walked to school in any weather. They studied alongside kids from the town. Everyone was treated the same way. Rich and poor kids mixed together, and it worked out fine. In fact, town kids and country kids became friends and helped each other out. One country boy stood out to me. His name was Rob Graham. Let me describe him. Picture a strong twelve-year-old boy with a solid build. In summer he went barefoot and bare-legged, with thick red hair and a big forehead that made you think he was smart. Rob's face was calm.

People who care about fashion would come home with nothing but makeup, wrinkles, and candy to show their friends. Naples is close to Rome, which might make Naples seem less interesting. Naples doesn't have buildings, sculptures, or paintings that are as good as Rome's. But Naples is still one of the most beautiful and enjoyable cities in the world. Nothing is more unusual than how Naples looks from any direction you view it from. Naples sits on the south and east side of some hills. It curves around a gulf that is 16 miles wide and 16 miles long. The locals call this basin the Crater. The city looks like it crowns this amazing basin. One part of the city rises toward the west like an amphitheater, on the hills of Pausilippo, St. Ermo, and Antiguano. The other part stretches toward the east over flatter land. Villas follow one after another very quickly from the Magdalen Bridge.

It all looked cheap and ugly now, he thought. It had no character. Yet new ideas kept coming. Democracy was relentless and messy, and it was still growing.

We stated our views moderately, not denying that in some points it might be possible to effect a salutary change; but utterly deprecating the enforcement of a bill which was so constructed as to uproot and destroy the ancient consuetudinal law of the kingdom, to strike a heavy and malignant blow at morality and religion, and which, moreover, was regarded by the people of Scotland with feelings of unequivocal disgust. Popularity, it was quite clear, could never be gained from persisting in a measure so unpalatable to the whole community; nor had England, save in the matter of Gretna-green marriages, any visible interest in the question. Our Whig leaders, or at least some of them, wanted to change Scotland's marriage laws. We shared our thoughts in a reasonable way. We admitted that some changes might be good. But we strongly opposed this bill because it would destroy Scotland's old marriage traditions. It would hurt morality and religion. Plus, Scottish people hated it. People from all political parties and religions agreed on this. They all disliked the bill. We thought that once people stopped the bill, it would be dropped completely. No one would support it because everyone in Scotland hated it. England didn't really care about this issue either, except for marriages at Gretna Green. It's possible that a few politicians believed their own ideas were better than what the Scottish people wanted. That's the kind of mistake proud people sometimes make.

MANGASARIAN I may be doing you an injustice, Bertie, but it seemed to me in your last that there were indications that the free expression of my religious views had been distasteful to you. ### Plain English:
Is Life Worth Living Without Immortality?

I might be wrong about this, Bertie, but your last letter made it seem like you didn't like me sharing my religious views. I don't mind if you disagree with me. But if you don't want to have an open and honest conversation about religion—the thing people should be most honest about—then I'd be really disappointed. 

Free-thinkers have a problem in regular society. If they share their unusual opinions, people think it's rude. But people who disagree with them can say whatever they want without anyone complaining. There was a time when it took guts to be a Christian. Now it takes guts not to be.

Is life worth living? If you're healthy, then yes, it definitely is. In a way, even asking this question means something is wrong.

PREFACE. In the same week, and within three days of the same date, I received from three Judges of the Supreme Court, of three States, the request that I would record my remembrances of the men and things I had known for fifty years. The gentlemen making this request were Joseph Henry Lumpkin, of Georgia; William L. Sharkey, of Mississippi, and James G. Taliaferro, of Louisiana. From Judge Sharkey the request was verbal; from the other two it came in long and, to me, cherished letters. All three have been my intimate friends--Lumpkin from boyhood; the others for nearly fifty years. Judge Lumpkin has finished his work in time, and gone to his reward. Judges Sharkey and Taliaferro yet live, both now over seventy years of age. The former has retired from the busy cares of office, honored, trusted, and beloved; the latter still occupies a seat upon the Bench of the Supreme Court

### Plain English:
This book is dedicated to my nephew, Ovid Garten Sparks, and to Colonel Thomas Hardeman of Macon, Georgia. I am dedicating it to them as their older relative. I hope that when I am gone, they will read this book and remember my love for them.

PREFACE

Within three days of each other, I got requests from three Supreme Court judges from three different states. They wanted me to write down what I remember about the people and events I have known for the past fifty years. The judges were Joseph Henry Lumpkin from Georgia, William L. Sharkey from Mississippi, and James G. Taliaferro from Louisiana. Judge Sharkey asked me in person. The other two wrote me long letters that I treasure. All three have been my close friends. I have known Lumpkin since I was a boy. I have known the other two for almost fifty years. Judge Lumpkin finished his work and passed away. Judges Sharkey and Taliaferro are still alive. Both are over seventy years old now. Sharkey retired from his job and is respected and loved by everyone. Taliaferro still works as a judge on the Supreme Court.

INTRODUCTION In southern France during the summer, children and poor old people who can't do hard work search dried-up river beds for "golden dust"—tiny bits of gold that sparkle in the sun and get carried away by the water when it flows. These poor people and children collect gold dust from those hidden rivers. We should do the same thing with all the helpful advice and teachings that God has scattered almost everywhere. These ideas sparkle and light us up for a moment, then they're gone. We're left wishing we had written them down and kept them. Who hasn't felt this way?

THE BENEFIT OF THINKING.

Even the worst and the best people in the world sometimes stop and think. A bad person doesn't have good feelings. He usually lets his emotions control him. He ignores what's good about morals and religion. But sometimes he sees how different he is from other people. This makes him stop and think for a little while. Also, our bodies and minds are connected. Sometimes how we feel physically makes us stop and think whether we want to or not. This seems like nature's way of helping us. It's a good thing. People who think a lot get better and smarter. So it makes sense that God gave us bodies that can change and grow.

both these aspects of the Northern capital which is altogether absent from the sparkling beauty of the city on the Isis, and from the genius of the place. The impression that Oxford makes upon those who, familiar with her from early years, have learnt to know and love her in later life is remarkable. Teeming with much that is ancient, she appears the embodiment of youth and beauty. Exquisite in line, sparkling with light and colour, she seems ever bright and young, while her sons fall into decay and perish. "Alma Mater!" they cry, and love her for her loveliness, till their dim eyes can look on her no more. And this is for the reason that the true lovableness of Oxford cannot be learnt at once. As her charms have grown from age to age, so their real appreciation is gradual. Not that she cannot catch the eye of one who sees her for the first time, and, smiling, hold him captive. This she can do now and then; but even so her new lover has yet to learn her preciousness. It is worth while to try to understand what are the charms that have grown with her growth.

### Plain English:
Both of these things are completely missing from the beautiful city on the Isis, and from the special feeling of the place. The impression that Oxford makes on people who have known it since they were young and have grown to love it later in life is really striking. It's full of old things, but it looks like it represents youth and beauty. It's beautiful to look at, bright with light and color, and it always seems fresh and young, while the people who studied there get old and die. "Dear Oxford!" they cry, and they love it because it's so lovely, until they can't see it anymore with their tired eyes. And this happens because you can't understand what makes Oxford so lovable all at once. Just like its good qualities have grown over hundreds of years, you have to learn to appreciate them slowly. It's not that Oxford can't catch the eye of someone visiting for the first time and keep them interested. It can do that sometimes. But even then, the new person visiting still has to learn how special it really is. It's worth trying to understand what good things have grown as Oxford has grown.

one of the discoveries of the War. For years, it seems, he has been writing poetry, but it is only recently that an inapprehensive country has awakened to the fact. Now he is taking his rightful place among our foremost singers. What William Barnes was to Dorset, what T. E. Brown was to the Manx people--this is Mr. Gilbert to the folk of his native county of Lincoln. He has interpreted their lives, their sorrows, their aspirations, with a surprising fidelity. Mr. Gilbert never loses his grip upon realities. One feels that he knows the men of whom he writes in their most intimate moods; knows, too, their defects, which he does not shrink from recording. There is little of the dreamy idealism of the South in the peasant people of Lincolnshire. The outwardly respectable chapel-goer who asks himself, in a moment of introspection But why not have a good time here? Why should the Devil have all the beer? is true to type. But he has, too, his softer moods. Fidelity in friendship, courage, resource and perseverance--these are typical of the men of the Fens. ### Plain English:
He was discovered during the war. For years he wrote poetry, but people didn't notice until recently. Now he's becoming one of the best poets in the country. William Barnes wrote about Dorset, and T. E. Brown wrote about the Isle of Man. Gilbert does the same thing for Lincoln, his home county. He writes about the people there—their lives, their sadness, what they hope for—and he gets it right. Gilbert always stays true to real life. You can tell he knows the men he writes about really well. He knows their bad sides too, and he isn't afraid to write about them. The peasants in Lincolnshire aren't dreamy or fake. They're practical people. A man might go to church on Sunday and seem respectable, but then think to himself: Why shouldn't I have fun? Why should the Devil get all the beer? That's realistic. But these men have good sides too. They're loyal to their friends, brave, clever, and never give up. That's what the men of the Fens are like.

To Morley Roberts

Thanks to the editors of the English Review, New Age, and Colour.

### Original:
these, for sixty years, from 1830 to 1890,[1] two poets sat, till their right to these lofty peaks became unchallenged. Beneath them, during these years, on the lower knolls of the mount of song, many new poets sang; with diverse instruments, on various subjects, and in manifold ways. They had their listeners; the Muses were also their visitants; but none of them ventured seriously to dispute the royal summits where Browning and Tennyson sat, and smiled at one another across the vale between. Both began together; and the impulses which came to them from the new and excited world which opened its fountains in and about 1832 continued to impel them till the close of their lives. While the poetic world altered around them, while two generations of poets made new schools of poetry, they remained, for the most part, unaffected by these schools. There is nothing of Arnold and Clough, of Swinburne, Rossetti or Morris, or of any of the others, in Browning or Tennyson. There is nothing even of Mrs. Browning in Browning. What changes took place in them were wrought, first, by the natural growth of their own character; secondly, by the natural development of their art-power;

### Plain English:
For sixty years, from 1830 to 1890, two poets sat at the top. No one challenged their place there. Below them, many new poets sang on the lower slopes. They had people who listened to them. The Muses visited them too. But none of these new poets tried seriously to fight for the top spots where Browning and Tennyson sat. They smiled at each other across the valley between them.

Both poets started at the same time. The new and exciting world that opened up around 1832 pushed them forward. This kept pushing them until they died. The poetry world changed around them. Two generations of new poets created new types of poetry. But Browning and Tennyson mostly stayed the same. They didn't follow Arnold and Clough, Swinburne, Rossetti, Morris, or any of the others. Even Browning wasn't influenced by Mrs. Browning. The changes that did happen in them came from two things. First, their own character grew naturally. Second, their ability to make art developed naturally.

### Plain English:
Captain Nicholas Joel was standing on the front bridge of his ship. He looked out through the thick, slotted windows made of quartz. He could see them coming. A jeep was bouncing wildly as it came out of the forest. Now they were driving fast across the hard, yellow sand of the desert. They were heading straight for the ship that had brought them here three weeks ago. Captain Joel watched them. He could see how excited they were. They were kicking up clouds of yellow dust behind them. He knew what they would tell him before he even used his radio. "We found it again!" they would say. He turned away from the windows. February 13, 1883. His father was a Leipsic city official, who gave his son the benefit of the illustrious Thomas School, preparatory to a university career. The latter, however, was not of much advantage to him, as young Wagner devoted himself mainly to musical studies. He led a theatre orchestra in Magdeburg, then in Königsberg, then in Riga. From the latter place he went, in 1839, to Paris, where he completed "Rienzi" and the "Flying Dutchman," in 1841. The latter was suggested by a gale which Wagner experienced during a short voyage. "Rienzi" was first brought out at Dresden in 1842, and led to Wagner's appointment as orchestra leader in Dresden, where he brought out his "Tanhäuser" in 1845. In 1849 Wagner had to leave Germany for political reasons, and went to Switzerland, where "Lohengrin" was finished and the tetralogy of the "Nibelung" was begun. Wagner then lived in Italy, Vienna, and Paris, where "Tanhäuser" met with a disastrous presentation in 1861, and led accidentally to the following pages. February 13, 1883. His father was an official in the city of Leipzig. He sent his son to the famous Thomas School to prepare him for university. But young Wagner didn't really care about university. Instead, he focused on music. He led orchestras in theaters in Magdeburg, then Königsberg, then Riga. In 1839, he moved to Paris. There he finished two operas called "Rienzi" and "The Flying Dutchman" in 1841. He got the idea for "The Flying Dutchman" from a bad storm he went through on a short boat trip. "Rienzi" was first performed in Dresden in 1842. This got Wagner a job as the orchestra leader in Dresden. He performed his opera "Tannhäuser" there in 1845. In 1849, Wagner had to leave Germany for political reasons and moved to Switzerland. There he finished "Lohengrin" and started working on a big group of four operas called "The Ring of the Nibelung." Later, Wagner lived in Italy, Vienna, and Paris. His "Tannhäuser" had a terrible performance in Paris in 1861, which led to the pages that follow. In 1864, Wagner became close friends with Louis II, the young King of Bavaria. Louis II supported Wagner's work. Wagner performed "Tristan" in 1857, "The Mastersingers" in 1868, and "Rhinegold" in 1853.

It is only a sketch--a sketch of the problems which the war created or rendered pressing--of the conditions under which they cropped up; of the simplicist ways in which they were conceived by the distinguished politicians who volunteered to solve them; of the delegates' natural limitations and electioneering commitments and of the secret influences by which they were swayed; of the peoples' needs and expectations; of the unwonted procedure adopted by the Conference and of the fateful consequences of its decisions to the world. In dealing with all those matters I aimed at impartiality, which is an unattainable ideal, but I trust that sincerity and detachment have brought me reasonably close to it. Having no pet theories of my own to champion, my principal standard of judgment is derived from the law of causality and the rules of historical criticism. The fatal tactical mistake chargeable to the Conference lay in its making the charter of the League of Nations and the treaty of peace with the Central Powers interdependent. For the maxims that underlie the former are irreconcilable with those that should determine the latter, and the efforts to combine them must, among other

### Plain English:
I kept records of what people said and debated. This is just an overview. It covers the problems the war created or made urgent. It shows the situations these problems came from. It explains how the politicians who wanted to fix them thought about it—in simple ways. It talks about the delegates' real limitations. It covers what deals they had made before coming. It shows what secret influences changed their minds. It discusses what people needed and wanted. It explains the unusual way the Conference worked and how its decisions affected the world. I tried to be fair when writing about all this. Perfect fairness is impossible, but I tried to be honest and objective. I don't have my own theories I'm trying to promote. Instead, I based my judgments on cause and effect and the rules of historical study. The Conference made a big tactical mistake. It connected the League of Nations charter to the peace treaty with the Central Powers. But the ideas behind the League don't work with the ideas that should be in the peace treaty. Trying to combine them must have caused problems.

His wild and reckless behavior had been calmed down by the years in a way I wouldn't have thought possible. He had become serious instead of the cheerful and funny person he used to be. He always used to say that there was no ghost or supernatural event, no matter how real it seemed, that couldn't be explained by natural causes. While we were in college, something happened that many people thought was a ghost. It took place in an empty house on a remote plantation in a nearby county. Nobody could figure out what was going on, and the newspapers wrote about it. It reminded people of the famous Cock Lane ghost and other well-known hauntings. Groups of people went to investigate but couldn't solve the mystery. Stokeman bet someone a box of cigars that he could go alone and prove it was fake. He did it. He not only scared away the "ghost," but he caught whatever was causing the trouble and brought it to town as proof that his theory was right and to show how brave he was. This made him really famous in our small community.
