THE FIRST EDITION. Popular lectures, owing to the knowledge they presuppose, and the time they occupy, can afford only a _modicum_ of instruction. They must select for this purpose easy subjects, and restrict themselves to the exposition of the simplest and the most essential points. Nevertheless, by an appropriate choice of the matter, the _charm_ and the _poetry_ of research can be conveyed by them. It is only necessary to set forth the attractive and the alluring features of a problem, and to show what broad domains of fact can be illuminated by the light radiating from the solution of a single and ofttimes unobtrusive point. Furthermore, such lectures can exercise a favorable influence by showing the substantial sameness of scientific and every-day thought. The public, in this way, loses its shyness towards scientific questions, and acquires an interest in scientific work which is a great help to the inquirer. The latter, in his turn, is brought to understand that his work is a small part only of the universal process of life, and that the results of his labors must redound to the benefit not only of himself and a few of his associates, but to that of the

### Plain English:
THE FIRST EDITION. Popular lectures can only teach a small amount because of what they assume you already know and how much time they take. They have to pick easy topics and focus on the simplest and most important ideas. But if you choose the right subjects, these lectures can show the beauty and interest of research. You just need to show the cool and exciting parts of a problem. You need to show how many big areas of knowledge can be explained by solving one small point. Also, these lectures can help by showing that science and everyday thinking are basically the same. When people see this, they stop being nervous about science. They start to care about scientific work, which really helps scientists. Scientists also learn that their work is just one small part of how life works in general. They learn that what they discover helps not just themselves and a few colleagues, but everyone.

### Plain English:
A Canadian soldier who was hurt at Ypres is speaking.

So this green land is England! We saw it smiling and shining in our dreams when we were young. It was a land we dreamed about with love. Now we come here with different eyes than we had back then. We come to find shelter in her strong arms.

We come from fights that were hard and lasted a long time. We want to see stars shining on the streets of villages. We want to watch a country that is calm in the middle of summer. It is a soft land, dressed in August but cold. It seems dull to us Canadians who know the sun. We know it as a red thing that moves across a bright blue sky.

We walk around slowly and smoke. We play cards. We wait, wanting to leave again. Sometimes two of us, with the other soldiers here, go quiet. Only two of us left. Three started out, but Edward died in the woods at Julien.

Sometimes the two of us go silent. Then we look up. We try to see in other people's eyes if they understand what we now know. We learned it in the fields of France. During those terrible nights, some of us heard something strange or saw something we can't explain.

Then we look back at that strange new time when everything changed for us suddenly.

Shortridge is associate curator of cultural history in the United States National Museum, Smithsonian Institution._ Perhaps the modern tendency to idealize progress has been responsible for the neglect of Italian harpsichords and virginals during the present day revival of interest in old musical instruments. Whatever laudable traits the Italian builders may have had, they cannot be considered to have been progressive. Their instruments of the mid-16th century hardly can be distinguished from those made around 1700. During this 150 years the pioneering Flemish makers added the four-foot register, a second keyboard, and lute and buff stops to their instruments. However, the very fact that the Italian builders were unwilling to change their models suggests that their instruments were good enough to demand no further improvements. Anyone who has heard a properly restored Italian harpsichord or an accurately

### Plain English:
Europeans didn't have the familiar second keyboard and range of stops they were used to. This paper describes typical Italian harpsichords from the Hugo Worch Collection in the United States National Museum in detail with pictures. The author also explains why Italian harpsichords have some confusing differences in keyboard ranges and string lengths. John D. Shortridge is an associate curator of cultural history at the United States National Museum, part of the Smithsonian Institution. Today, people might ignore Italian harpsichords and virginals because of how we tend to think progress is always good. The Italian builders may have had good qualities, but they weren't trying to make new improvements. Their instruments from the mid-1500s look almost the same as ones made around 1700. During those 150 years, the Flemish makers made big changes. They added a four-foot register, a second keyboard, and lute and buff stops to their instruments. But the fact that Italian builders didn't want to change their designs actually suggests their instruments were already so good they didn't need to be improved. Anyone who has heard a properly fixed-up Italian harpsichord or an accurate one knows this is true.

and house wiring. This lets a farm mechanic install his own electrical system without paying an expert. With modern tools that most farmers can afford, making electricity becomes easy and automatic. You just need a reliable power source—like a water wheel, a gasoline engine, or a windmill. The water wheel works best for making electricity on farms that are far from towns. Since water power is wasted on thousands of farms across the country, several chapters cover this topic. They explain how to build weirs and other simple tools to measure how much water flows in streams. There are charts and formulas that anyone who knows basic math can use to figure out how much power falling water can create under different conditions. There are also diagrams showing how to build dams, walls, channels, and pipes using materials you probably already have on your farm. That small stream that waters the farm animals often has enough power to supply electricity to the farmhouse itself.

to me dearer Friends than you!_ The Humorous Ghost INTRODUCTION The humorous ghost is distinctly a modern character. In early literature wraiths took themselves very seriously, and insisted on a proper show of respectful fear on the part of those whom they honored by haunting. A mortal was expected to rise when a ghost entered the room, and in case he was slow about it, his spine gave notice of what etiquette demanded. In the event of outdoor apparition, if a man failed to bare his head in awe, the roots of his hair reminded him of his remissness. Woman has always had the advantage over man in such emergency, in that her locks, being long and pinned up, are less easily moved--which may explain the fact (if it be a fact!) that in fiction women have shown themselves more self-possessed in ghostly presence than men. Or possibly a woman knows that a masculine spook is, after all, only a man, and therefore may be charmed into helplessness, while the feminine can be seen through by another woman and thus disarmed. The majority of the comic apparitions, curiously enough, are masculine. You don't often find women wraithed in smiles--perhaps because

to me dearer Friends than you!_ The Funny Ghost INTRODUCTION The funny ghost is clearly a modern invention. In old stories, ghosts took themselves seriously. They expected people to be respectful and scared when they showed up to haunt them. If a mortal saw a ghost come into a room, they were supposed to stand up. If they were slow to do it, their body would shake and remind them what they should do. If a ghost appeared outside, a man was supposed to take off his hat in respect. If he didn't, his hair would stand on end and make him remember. Women had an easier time than men in these situations. This is because women's hair is long and pinned up, so it doesn't move as easily. This might explain why women in stories seem calmer around ghosts than men do. Or maybe women know that a male ghost is still just a man, so they can charm him into being helpless. And maybe a woman can see through a female ghost and make it powerless. Strangely, most funny ghosts in stories are male. You don't often see women ghosts laughing—maybe because

The muzzles flamed as he spoke: And the shameless soul of a nameless man Went up in the cordite-smoke. _THE EYE AND THE TRUTH_ Up from the fret of the earth-world, through the Seven Circles of Flame, With the seven holes in Its tunic for sign of the death-in-shame, To the little gate of Valhalla the coward-spirit came. Cold, It crouched in the man-strong wind that sweeps Valhalla’s floor; Weak, It pawed and scratched on the wood; and howled, like a dog, at the Door Which is shut to the souls who are sped in shame, for ever and evermore: For It snuffed the Meat of the Banquet-boards where the Threefold Killers sit, Where the Free Beer foams to the tankard-rim, and the Endless Smokes are lit.... And It saw the Nakéd Eye come out above the lintel-slit. And now It quailed at Nakéd Eye which judges the naked dead; And now It snarled at Nakéd Truth that broodeth overhead; And now It looked

### Plain English:
The sun rose. He was going to die in disgrace. The gun bolts locked into place. The rifles were ready. The cold metal rested against even colder skin. The sight lined up on his chest. "Fire!" yelled the Sergeant-Major. The guns went off as he spoke. And a nameless man's shameless soul went up in the smoke.

A coward's spirit came up from the earth world. It had seven holes in its clothes as a sign of shameful death. It came to the little gate of Valhalla. It was cold and weak in the strong wind that blows across Valhalla's floor. It scratched and clawed at the wooden door and howled like a dog. The door was shut to souls that died in shame, forever and ever. It could smell the meat on the banquet tables where the great warriors sit. It could see the free beer and the endless cigarettes. And then it saw the Naked Eye come out from above the door opening. Now it was scared of the Naked Eye that judges the dead. Now it growled at the Naked Truth that hung above. And now it looked...

### Plain English:
This book is based on writings by Schindler, Ries, Wegeler, Marx, and Thayer. It's written for people who don't have a lot of time to study Beethoven deeply, but want to learn more about him than what you'd get from a concert program. The few letters in this book have been translated to match how Beethoven actually wrote them. His writing style was simple, emotional, and unique, but not fancy. PREFACE TO THE SECOND EDITION.

The first version of this book sold out a few months after it came out. People have asked me many times to print it again, but I said no because I wanted to write a bigger, more complete book about Beethoven instead. But I still don't have enough free time to do that.

BEESLEY WITH MAPS 1921 PREFACE It would be scarcely possible for anyone writing on the period embraced in this volume, to perform his task adequately without making himself familiar with Mr. Long's 'History of the Decline of the Roman Republic' and Mommsen's 'History of Rome.' To do over again (as though the work had never been attempted) what has been done once for all accurately and well, would be mere prudery of punctiliousness. But while I acknowledge my debt of gratitude to both these eminent historians, I must add that for the whole period I have carefully examined the original authorities, often coming to conclusions widely differing from those of Mr. Long. And I venture to hope that from the advantage I have had in being able to compare the works of two writers, one of whom has well-nigh exhausted the theories as the other has the facts of the subject, I have succeeded in giving a more consistent and faithful account of the leaders and legislation of the revolutionary era than has hitherto been written.

### Plain English:
Ted Garvin, C. Markus and PG Distributed Proofreaders EPOCHS OF ANCIENT HISTORY * * * * * THE GRACCHI MARIUS AND SULLA BY A.H. BEESLEY WITH MAPS 1921 PREFACE

Anyone writing about this time period needs to read Mr. Long's 'History of the Decline of the Roman Republic' and Mommsen's 'History of Rome.' If I just copied what they already did well, that would be pointless. I want to thank both of these famous historians for their help. But I also read all the original sources myself for this whole period. I often came to different conclusions than Mr. Long did. I had the advantage of reading both writers. One of them covered all the different theories about what happened, and the other covered all the facts. Because of this, I think I've written a better and more accurate account of the leaders and the laws from this revolutionary time than anyone has written before.

THE CAXTON MEMORIAL. When a few people want the same thing and really want to get it done, they usually succeed. "The Caxton Memorial" wants to honor the first person who printed books in English. If a person's best memory is their own work, we need to know what books they made. Most books that Caxton printed came from French books. Many of them he probably translated himself. He clearly wrote the introductions himself.

Scene: A room in Juliet's flat. In the back center is a fire. To the right of the fire is a chair. To the left is an easy chair and a small table. Two envelopes and a new novel are on the table.

JULIET sits in the easy chair, looking at the fire.

JULIET (dreamily): Hans Andersen, when he was old and weak, said that his life had been like a fairy tale. That's what mine is! Can you believe it? I became famous in just one week because of my first book! Would it have caused such a big fuss if I had written it under my real name? Who knows? But when you write a really mean description of someone you love, you can't admit you did it. And when a book has a man's name on it...

The sky goes away from those weird admissions we call days. Again, your tiny fighting blue things try to stop life from spinning around. They want to find something permanent within all the blurry moving stuff. Broken pieces of a soul are shocked at how small they've become. They're not sure they're even still alive. They ask to see proof with their own eyes. But when they remember how their life used to be—before they were forced down to earth and made so small, stuck with one sad problem—their blue color turns into big daydreams. These daydreams push past the locked-up prison around them. On your soft, unfinished face, where bad thoughts stir in curves that shake into strange shapes, darkness sometimes pushes in. It hits secret, untouched places under your eyes and where your thin nostrils stop. This darkness bends with helpless messages. It's like history warning a world that is shown through one face combined together. You mutter with your face asking for more space because it has seen everything. # HISTORY OF PHOENICIA by George Rawlinson

First published in 1889 by Longmans, Green, and Co.

George Rawlinson was a professor of ancient history at Oxford University and a member of the Church of England.

This book is dedicated to the leaders and scholars of Oxford University. This is the last book written by the author while he was a professor. He wrote it to show his respect and thanks.

October 1, 1889

# NOTE FROM THE PERSON WHO PREPARED THIS TEXT

The original book had special letters and characters that don't work in all computer systems. We tried to use similar letters instead. Some things, like Hebrew writing, are missing. The version on Windows computers uses special font characters. These might not show up correctly in other versions or with different fonts.

Greek text is shown in brackets {} using standard English spellings. Special marks above letters have been removed. Phoenician or other similar languages have been replaced with {...}. The many drawings and maps from the original book are not included here.

His successors have now the pleasure of making a reprint, believing the subject to be of as much interest today as it was twenty years ago._ THE IMPORTANCE OF THE PROOF-READER In preparing a work for the press, the author, the compositor, and the proof-reader are the three factors that enter into its construction. We will, however, treat more especially of the last-named in connection with the first. The true proof-reader should not only be a practical printer, but he should be a lover of literature, familiar with the classics of all languages, with the results accomplished by science, and indeed with every subject that concerns his fellow-men. When an author prepares a work for the press, he often uses many abbreviations, his capitalization is frequently incorrect, his spelling occasionally not in accordance either with Worcester or Webster, his punctuation inaccurate, his historical and biographical statements careless, and his chirography frequently very bad. In such cases the proof-reader is sorely tried; and unless he is a man of much patience, well versed in

### Plain English:
1922

John Wilson wrote this paper. He used to own The University Press. He died in 1903. His company now wants to print it again. They think people will find this topic just as interesting today as they did twenty years ago.

THE IMPORTANCE OF THE PROOF-READER

Three people help make a book: the author, the person who sets the type, and the proof-reader. We will focus mostly on the proof-reader and the author.

A good proof-reader needs to be more than just a printer. He should love reading. He should know famous books in many languages. He should understand science. Really, he should know about everything that affects people's lives.

When authors write books, they often use shortcuts. They don't always capitalize words correctly. Their spelling doesn't always match the dictionary. Their punctuation is wrong. Their facts about history and people are sloppy. Their handwriting is often hard to read.

This makes the proof-reader's job very hard. He needs to be patient. He needs to know a lot about different subjects.

### Plain English:
There's been a revival of interest in Shakespeare lately, but it's not as popular as it used to be. Back when the New Shakespeare Society was really active in England, and people in Germany were arguing about whether Shakespeare was great or not, things were different. People like Mr. Fleay and Mr. Spedding were studying the rhythms in Shakespeare's plays. Mr. Spedding was challenging Dr. Furnivall's ideas about when Shakespeare wrote his plays. Dr. Furnivall was disagreeing with what Mr. Swinburne had said. Mr. Halliwell-Phillipps got angry and asked Mr. Browning, who led the Society, to control Dr. Furnivall. We younger people watching all this felt like we were seeing real literary history being made. It was as exciting as when earlier scholars had dealt with Mr. Collier's corrected version of Shakespeare's works, which ended badly. Then interest in Shakespeare died down for a while. This happened partly because people got interested in the Browning Society instead. It seemed like people went back to thinking like they did in 1850, when Mr. Phillipps had written that more and more people were starting to believe something, and some writers had encouraged this idea.

Look at all the changes happening everywhere. From the east side of London to the west side. Men, women, kids, buildings, signs, and clothing. The government, theater, business, tastes, moods, and feelings. Everything is changing everywhere you look. The court, the city, the countryside - it's all different or getting different.

The streets used to be paved with stones. And riding in a taxi would shake you around so much it hurt your bones. When lovers wanted to be together, they had to be careful. They took a real risk if they tried to kiss. Just one little kiss - the girl would say - oh no! And as they got close - the taxi would bump and throw them around. Poor Sylvia got bruised, and Damon got a black eye.

Now people with weak stomachs ride around in taxis. And fat guys sleep through the whole ride without getting bounced around. The fancy gentleman from the old days has completely changed. Everything about him is different now. Back then, he wore a big blonde wig that went down over his shoulders.

It was strange because her eyes were dark. They were large and black, full of intelligence and learning, but they looked sad and proud. She stood in front of her friend, tall and thin. A small smile was on her lips.

PREFACE

This was written in Cambridge, Mass., on March 1, 1869.

Since I published the first edition of this guide for people who want to teach kindergarten, I went to Europe. I studied kindergartens in Hamburg, Berlin, and Dresden. Froebel started these schools, and his best students ran them. This study made me even more sure about something I learned from reading Froebel's "Essay on the Education of the Human Race." I believe nothing would help our country more than spreading kindergartens everywhere. They would support our public schools like a strong foundation. They would give our national education a good moral base, make it more thorough, and make it more practical. But I also learned that no book could teach someone how to be a good kindergarten teacher. His mother, now dead, had designed and planted it with loving care. In the spring, and particularly on hot summer evenings when the moonlight patched the garden with silver, it was his favorite spot, the place where he liked to sit alone, smoking and dreaming. Admiring college girls had sometimes told him that he was a combination of Abe Lincoln and Lord Byron. That pleased him, though in his heart he knew it really wasn't very important. He was finished with his studies now, ready for the world of achievement. His father, a retired Professor of Ethnology, had arranged the financing of an exploring expedition. Alice--George's twin sister, who from birth had been blind--was going to visit distant relatives.

Aulus Gellius,[2] who gives a more circumstantial detail of this uncommon law, affirms the penalty to be "no less than confiscation of all the effects, and banishment of the delinquent." Cicero mentions the same law to his friend Atticus,[3] and even makes the punishment capital, though he resolves at the same time not to conform to it under his present circumstances, unless his friend should advise him to the contrary. Which of these relators has given us the real penalty annexed to this law by Solon, is scarce worth our inquiry. But I cannot help observing, that strange as this law may appear at first sight, yet if we reflect upon the reasons of it, as they are assigned by Plutarch and A. Gellius, it will not appear unworthy of that great legislator. The opinion of Plutarch is; "that Solon intended no citizen, as soon as ever he had provided for the security of his own private affairs, should be so unfeeling with respect to the publick welfare, as to affect a brutal insensibility,[4] and not to sympathize with the distress and calamities of his country: but that he should

### Plain English:
should stay neutral and not take sides with either group." Aulus Gellius gives more details about this unusual law. He says the punishment was "at least taking away all the person's stuff and forcing them to leave the country." Cicero mentions this same law to his friend Atticus. He even says the punishment could be death. But at the same time, he says he won't follow this law right now unless his friend tells him he should. We don't really need to worry about which of these writers got the real punishment right. But I think it's worth noticing that even though this law seems weird at first, if you think about why Plutarch and Aulus Gellius say Solon made it, it doesn't seem like a dumb law at all. Plutarch's idea is: "Solon didn't want any citizen to just focus on their own life and stop caring about what happens to their country. He didn't want them to act like they don't care about their country's problems and suffering. Instead, he wanted them to care about what happens to their country."

The Casa Grande ruin, situated near Gila river, in southern Arizona, is perhaps the best known specimen of aboriginal architecture in the United States, and no treatise on American antiquities is complete without a more or less extended description of it. Its literature, which extends over two centuries, is voluminous, but of little value to the practical scientific worker, since hardly two descriptions can be found which agree. The variations in size of the ruin given by various authors is astonishing, ranging from 1,500 square feet to nearly 5 acres or about 200,000 square

CASA GRANDE RUIN By Cosmos Mindeleff

INTRODUCTION. LOCATION AND CHARACTER.

The Casa Grande ruin is near the Gila river in southern Arizona. It's probably the most famous Native American building in the United States. Any book about old American buildings has to include a description of it. People have written about it for two hundred years, but most of it isn't very useful to real scientists. That's because almost no two people describe it the same way. The size estimates are crazy different. Some say it's 1,500 square feet. Others say it's almost 5 acres, which is about 200,000 square feet.

### Plain English:
# How the Rabbit Caught the Sun in a Trap - An Omaha Myth

There was a rabbit. He lived with his grandmother. This is what happened.

Every morning, the rabbit would go hunting. One day, he noticed something strange. There was a very long footprint on the ground. He had never seen anything like it before.

The rabbit thought about it. He said to himself, "I should follow this track and see what made it."

The next morning, he got up early and went out again. He followed the same trail. When he got home, he had something to tell his grandmother.

He said, "Grandmother, I found something. There is a creature out there. I have never seen its footprint before. I am going to catch it."

His grandmother said, "Grandson, be careful."

### Plain English:
You need Mohlon yarn in color #11 and a 36 inch circular needle. You also need 4 stitch holders. Here are the color options:

1. 305 Blond Sand, 313 Tan Heather, 318 Acorn
2. 306 Salmon Glow, 313 Tan Heather, 310 Indo Brown
3. 307 Green Spray, 305 Blond Sand, 320 Moss Green
4. 322 Cranberry, 312 Dark Oxford, 311 Light Oxford
5. 308 Black, 311 Light Oxford, 301 White
6. 321 Light Navy, 309 Arctic Blue, 302 Star Bright

The gauge is 3 stitches per 1 inch.

Here's how to make it: Use one strand of each of three colors the whole time. Start at the collar edge with three strands of yarn and cast on 47 (51-55) stitches. Don't join them together. Work in rows like this:

Row 1: Knit 1, then purl 1, knit 1. Keep doing this pattern across the whole row. Repeat Row 1 to make the Seed Stitch Pattern. Keep working in Seed stitch for 4 (4½-5) inches.

Now divide the stitches for raglan shaping like this:

Row 1: For the left front, work in Seed Stitch across the first 9 stitches (this is the front band). Knit the next 4 (5-6) stitches and put a marker on the needle. For the left sleeve, knit the next 4 stitches and put a marker on the needle. For the back, knit the next 13 (15-17) stitches and put a marker on the needle. For the right sleeve, knit the next 4 stitches.

to the teachers of rural schools, to the public spirited school boards, individually and collectively, and to the leaders of rural communities and of social centers generally. I have tried to avoid the two extremes which Guizot says are always to be shunned, viz.: that of the "visionary theorist" and that of the "libertine practician." The former is analogous to a blank cartridge, and the latter to the mire of a swamp or the entangled underbrush of a thicket. The legs of one's theories (as Lincoln said of those of a man) should be long enough to reach the earth; and yet they must be free to move upon the solid ground of fact and experience. Details must always be left to the _person_ who is to do the work, whether it be that of the teacher, of the farmer, or of the school officer. I am aware that there is a veritable flood of books on this and kindred topics, now coming from the presses of the country. My sole reasons for the publication of the present volume are the desire to deliver the message which has come to fruition in my mind, and the hope that it may

### Plain English:
I'm writing this for the teachers in rural schools, for school boards that care about their communities, and for leaders in rural towns and community centers. I tried to avoid two problems that Guizot said we should always avoid. First, there's the "visionary theorist" — someone with big ideas that don't work in real life. Second, there's the "libertine practician" — someone who just does whatever without thinking. The first is like firing a blank bullet. The second is like getting stuck in a swamp or tangled bushes. Your theories need to be practical enough to actually work in the real world. Lincoln said it well — your ideas need legs long enough to touch the ground. But those legs also need to move freely on solid facts and real experience. The people actually doing the work — teachers, farmers, school officers — they should decide the details. I know there are tons of books coming out about this topic right now. I'm publishing this book because I have an important message to share that I've been thinking about. I hope it will be helpful.

Though perhaps misunderstood, reviled and even persecuted while living, the apotheosis is almost sure to come after death: and the victim of yesterday's mob, raised to the state of an Intercessor in Heaven, is besought with prayer and tears, and placatory penances, to mediate with God for the pardon of human sin. This is a mean and vile trait of human nature, the proof of ignorance, selfishness, brutal cowardice, and a superstitious materialism. It shows the base instinct to put down and destroy whatever or whoever makes men feel their own imperfections; with the alternative of ignoring and denying these very imperfections by turning into gods men who have merely spiritualised their natures, so that it may be supposed that they were heavenly incarnations and not mortal like other men. This process of euhemerisation, as it is called, or the making of men into gods and gods into men, sometimes, though more rarely, begins during the life of the hero, but usually after death. The true history of his life is gradually amplified and decorated with fanciful incidents, to fit it to the new character which has been posthumously given him. Omens and portents are now made

### Plain English:
for a god. People often misunderstand these people, hate them, and even hurt them while they're alive. But after they die, they almost always get treated like gods. The person that everyone attacked yesterday becomes someone in heaven that people pray to and cry to. They ask this person to talk to God and ask him to forgive human sins. This is a bad and disgusting part of human nature. It proves we're ignorant, selfish, cowardly, and superstitious. It shows we want to destroy anyone or anything that makes us realize we're not perfect. Or we ignore our own problems by turning people into gods. We pretend these people were sent from heaven, not just regular humans like everyone else. This process is called making men into gods and gods into men. Sometimes it starts while the person is still alive, but usually it happens after they die. People take the real story of their life and add made-up details to match the new god-like image people have given them. Now people add in signs and warnings that never really happened.

### Plain English:
# Rewritten Passage:

People want to know what Indian magic tricks are really like and how they're done. Indian magicians do lots of different tricks. The most common ones are the mango tree trick, the basket trick, the water bowl trick, the dry sand trick, the rope trick with a cut-up body, levitation, snake charming, and being buried alive. People talk a lot about Indian magic and what these performers can do. So it makes sense to look at these tricks carefully. We should figure out if they're really using supernatural powers. Are they doing things that can't be explained by science that we know about in the West?

The Mango Tree Trick

Let me start with the famous mango tree trick. It's probably the most well-known trick that Indian magicians do. First, I'll describe what it looks like to someone watching it for the first time. Then I'll explain how it's really done. Here's how the trick usually goes: The magician comes out wearing almost no clothes. He just has a small piece of cloth around his waist. It's so small that you can clearly see there's nothing hidden in it.

### Plain English:
His ship was leaving the coaling station at Cape Verde Islands when his wife suggested the name "Percival." This made him stop the ship and write a letter saying he didn't like the name. He wanted to name his son "Donald" instead. He thought "Percival" was a weak English name and didn't want to give the McKenzie family name a bad reputation. "I won't have any Percy McKenzies in my family," he wrote. To keep his wife happy, he said she could call the boy Percival too, but Donald had to come first. So the boy was named Donald Percival McKenzie. This name was written in the official birth register for Glasgow, Scotland, in 1876. Even though that was the official name, his mother only called him Percival and dropped the "Donald" when she talked about him or spoke to him. It was only when Captain McKenzie came home for short visits between his sailing trips that the boy went by his full name.

The above cottage stands in the grounds of Grove House, adjoining the churchyard of Old Windsor. It was built under the superintendent taste of the Princess Elizabeth,[1] second sister of the present King, and now known as the Landgravine of Hesse Homburg. To the decoration of this cottage the Princess paid much attention: it is quite in the _ornée_ style; and its situation is so beautiful as to baffle all embellishment. Grove House, the seat of Lady Dowager Onslow, of whom the Princess purchased the whole property, was built by Mr. Bateman, uncle to the eccentric Lord Bateman. This gentleman made it a point in his travels to notice everything that pleased him in the monasteries abroad; and, on his return to England, he built this house; the bedchamber being contrived, like the cells of monks, with a refectory, and every other appendage of a monastery; even to a cemetery, and a coffin, inscribed with the name of a supposititious ancient bishop. Some curious Gothic chairs, bought at a sale of the curiosities in this house, are now at Strawberry

### Plain English:
Cottages are different from the artificial luxury and showy decoration of royal palaces. The cottage above is in the grounds of Grove House. Grove House is next to the church at Old Windsor. Princess Elizabeth built it. She is the second sister of the current King. People now call her the Landgravine of Hesse Homburg. The Princess spent a lot of time decorating the cottage. It is very fancy and well-designed. The location is so beautiful that it doesn't need any extra decoration. Grove House was the home of Lady Dowager Onslow. The Princess bought the whole property from her. Mr. Bateman built Grove House. He was the uncle of the strange Lord Bateman. When Mr. Bateman traveled, he looked at everything he liked in old monasteries. When he came back to England, he built this house like a monastery. The bedrooms were made to look like monks' rooms. There was a dining hall and other parts of a monastery. There was even a cemetery and a coffin with the name of a fake old bishop written on it. Some unusual Gothic chairs were sold from this house. They are now at Strawberry.

So sings the poet, and so agrees the world. Humiliating as it is to make the confession, it is undeniably true. "Men and Dress are all women think about," cry the lords of creation in their unbounded vanity. And again, we must submit--and agree--to the truth of the accusation; at any rate, in nine cases out of ten. Fortunately I am a tenth case; at least, I consider myself so. I don't dispute the "dress" imputation. I am very fond of dress. Nearly as fond of it as the twenty-year old youth, and saying that, I allow a good deal. But very few of my thoughts are given to the creature "man"! I do not think him worth it. As my old nurse used to say, "I never 'ad no opinion of the sex!" Do not conclude, however, that because of my statement that I am a disappointed, soured old maid, for I am nothing of the sort. I am on the right side of twenty-five, and I have never been crossed in love; indeed, I have never even experienced the tender passion, and only write from my observations of other people; thus taking a perfectly neutral ground in speaking

### Plain English:
The poet sings about this, and the whole world agrees. It's embarrassing to admit, but it's true. "Women only think about men and clothes," say the arrogant men. And we have to admit they're right—at least nine times out of ten. I'm lucky. I'm that one exception. Or at least, I think I am. I won't deny that I like clothes. I'm really into fashion. Almost as much as a twenty-year-old guy, and that's saying something. But I don't spend much time thinking about men. I don't think they're worth it. My old nurse used to say, "I never thought much of men!" Don't think I'm some bitter, unhappy old woman though. I'm not. I'm under twenty-five, and I've never had my heart broken. Actually, I've never even been in love. I'm just writing about what I see in other people. That way, I can stay completely neutral when I talk about this.

The mistake of all is that, misled perhaps by Walter Scott and Macaulay, they have looked solely to the Commonwealth period. The custom was then in its decay. I have to thank several clergymen for giving me extracts from the registers and records under their care. A stranger to them, I felt some diffidence in making my requests. AUTHOR. PIAZZA TO THE THIRD VOLUME. There can really be no excuse for this last book of trite and beautiful sayings. I do not attempt, in any way, to palliate this great wrong. I would not do so even if I had an idea what palliate meant. It will, however, add one more to the series of books for which I am to blame, and the pleasure of travel will be very much enhanced, for me, at least. There is one friend I always meet on the trains when I travel. He is the news agent. He comes to me with my own books in his arms, and tells me over and over again of their merits. He means it, too. What object could he have in coming to me, not knowing who I am, and telling me of their great worth? Why would he talk that way to me if he did not really feel it? That is one reason I travel so much. When 1 get gloomy and heartsick, I like to get on a train and be assured once more, by a total stranger, that my books have never been successfully imitated. Long, long ago on a frozen, shiny ice field, Ung was an artist. He made a picture out of snow. He shaped it to look like a man from his tribe. He was happy while he worked, whistling and singing as he used his fingers to shape the snow. Read the Story of Ung!

His tribe loved the snow figure. Hundreds of people came to see it. They touched it and smelled it. They said, "This really looks like a man! This is how we hold our spears. This is how we wear our war belts. It looks just like us. Ung is great and deserves honor!"

Later, on another frozen ice field near caves in a place called Dordogne, Ung became an artist again. This time he carved pictures on bone. He made a lot of them.

(KIPLING)

Cartoonists were making art way back in ancient times.

(All Rights Reserved.) *THE SECRET OF THE EARTH.* *PROLOGUE.* When Dirk Waaijen, master of the Voorne, was five days out from the island of Celebes, a strange thing happened. For nearly a week the Dutchman had idled along with a cargo of cocoa, jaggaree, trepang, some Manado coffee, a few bags of nutmegs and other products of the Archipelago, but without an incident worth logging; when suddenly, an odd looking cask, with mast and streamer, was seen floating in the waters ahead, and all hands became alive with excitement. A couple of burly fellows hauled the barrel upon the deck, with the expectation of a prize, but were discomfited on finding that it contained only some cotton cloth, carefully wrapped about a dirty water-stained document, written in a language which no one could understand. Even Captain Waaijen himself was unable to read a word of it, despite his wise look, and the volumes of smoke which he blew from time to time over the packet. Had he confided in me, his only English speaking

### Plain English:
"The Ghost of Guir House." Published by F. TENNYSON NEELY in London and New York. Copyright 1899 in the United States and Great Britain by F. TENNYSON NEELY. (All rights reserved.) THE SECRET OF THE EARTH. PROLOGUE. Captain Dirk Waaijen was in charge of the ship called the Voorne. Five days after leaving the island of Celebes, something weird happened. For almost a week, the Dutch captain had been sailing slowly with a load of cocoa, sugar, sea cucumber, some coffee from Manado, bags of nutmeg, and other goods from the islands. Nothing interesting had happened until suddenly the crew spotted a strange barrel floating in the water ahead. It had a mast and a flag on it. Everyone got excited. Two big sailors pulled the barrel onto the deck. They hoped it would contain something valuable. But they were disappointed when they opened it. Inside was just some cotton cloth wrapped carefully around a dirty, water-stained piece of paper. The paper had writing on it in a language nobody could read. Even Captain Waaijen couldn't read it. He looked smart and smoked a lot while he studied it. But he couldn't figure out what it said. If he had asked me, his only crew member who spoke English...

### Original:
unseen things." Among friends, parting for a lengthy spell has its disadvantages. They age in character and physique, and after the reconnoitre there is a pathetic consciousness of the grudging confessions which time has inscribed on the monumental palimpsest. My meeting with Bentham after a severance of years was bleak with this pathos. But he was gay as ever, and better dressed than he used to be in the old art school days, with a self-respecting adjustment of hat and necktie that had been unknown in Bohemia; for he was no longer a boy, but a man, and a noted one, and fortune had stroked him into sleekness. The gender of success must be feminine: she is so capricious. Hitherto her smiles have been for veterans grown hoary in doing; now she opens her arms for youngsters grown great merely by daring. Bentham, it must be owned, had dared uncommonly well, and success had pillowed his head in her lap while she twined the bay with her fingers. But lines round his mouth and fatigued cynicism on the eyelids betrayed the march of years, and, more, the thinker, who, like most thinkers, plumbs to exhaustion in a bottomless pit. For

### Plain English:
things you can't see." When friends don't see each other for a long time, it's hard. They change how they look and act. When you see them again, it's sad because you notice all the ways time has changed them. When I saw Bentham again after years apart, it felt sad. But he was still happy and fun, and he dressed better than he used to in art school. His hat and tie looked nice and clean, which never happened back then. He wasn't a kid anymore — he was a successful adult. Success had made him look good. Success is like a woman, I guess, because she's so unpredictable. She used to help people who had been working hard for years. Now she helps young people who succeed just by being brave. Bentham was definitely brave, and it paid off. Success was good to him. But I could see lines around his mouth and tiredness in his eyes. He looked older. And you could tell he was a thinker who worked so hard he was worn out.

### Plain English:
He had noticed many strange coincidences and lucky accidents. This was true for his own cases and also for cases that the police worked on. He knew police officers pretty well and was friendly with them. He told me lots of stories about weird things that happened to Scotland Yard officials. For example, Inspector Nettings spent many months looking for a man that the American Government wanted. In the end, he found the man completely by accident. A phone call went to the wrong person and that's how he found out. The man had been living next door to him the whole time. Neither of them knew the other was there. There was also another inspector whose name I don't remember. He got very little information about a man he was supposed to find. He thought it would be really hard to locate him. He walked down the stairs of his office where he got his instructions. At the bottom of the stairs, he literally tripped over the man. The man was bent down tying his shoelace right there by the door. There were also other cases where when something big happened.

There was no beaten path, nor any mark of former human visitation; and the way was over an endless heaping of tumbled fragments that rolled or turned beneath the foot. Sometimes a mass dislodged would clatter down with hollow echoings;—sometimes the substance trodden would burst like an empty shell….Stars pointed and thrilled; and the darkness deepened. “Do not fear, my son,” said the Bodhisattva, guiding: “danger there is none, though the way be grim.” Under the stars they climbed,—fast, fast,—mounting by help of power superhuman. High zones of mist they passed; and they saw below them, ever widening as they climbed, a soundless flood of cloud, like the tide of a milky sea. Hour after hour they climbed;—and forms invisible yielded to their tread with dull soft crashings;—and faint cold fires lighted and died at every breaking. And once the pilgrim-youth laid hand on a something smooth that was not stone,—and lifted it,—and dimly saw the cheekless gibe of death. “Linger not thus, my son!” urged the voice of the teacher;—“the summit that we must gain is very far away!” On through the dark they climbed,—and felt continually beneath them the soft

### Plain English:
"you." It was getting dark as they climbed. There was no real path. No one had been here before. The ground was covered with broken rocks that moved under their feet. Sometimes rocks would fall and make hollow sounds. Sometimes what they stepped on would crack like an empty shell. Stars shone brightly. The darkness got deeper. "Don't be scared, my son," said the Bodhisattva as he led the way. "There's no danger, even though this is hard." Under the stars they climbed fast, really fast. They climbed using superhuman power to help them. They passed through thick clouds. Below them they could see a huge sea of white clouds getting wider as they climbed higher. They climbed for hours and hours. Invisible things fell apart under their feet with soft, dull crashes. Faint cold lights came and went every time something broke. Once the young pilgrim touched something smooth that wasn't stone. He picked it up. In the dim light, he saw a skull without a face. It was death. "Don't stop now, my son!" said the teacher's voice. "The top we need to reach is still very far away!" They kept climbing through the dark. They kept feeling soft things under their feet.

There was a savage conflict and turmoil in the air, whence issued, nevertheless, a note as of victory. The blasts were those of spring, and were instinct with life,--they heralded a resurrection. The mountains were still half buried in snow, and the ancient stronghold that looked down from their heights upon the valley towered above snow-laden pines. It was one of those gray, rock-crowning castles that were formerly the terror of the surrounding country, and are now for the most part deserted and forgotten, with naught but ruins to tell of ancient splendour. This, however, was not the case in this instance: the Counts von Steinrück carefully preserved the cradle of their race from decay, although otherwise they cared very little for the old pile, secluded as it was from the world in the depths of the mountains. In the hunting season only, when there was usually an arrival of guests, life and bustle awoke the echoes within its ancient walls. This year was an exceptional one, however. Guests, it is true,

### Plain English:
The winds woke up the earth. They roared over the fields and plains. They beat their wings around the tall mountain peaks. They stirred up the sea. There was a wild fight happening in the air. But it sounded like victory was coming. These were spring winds. They brought life with them. They meant things were coming back to life. The mountains still had snow on half of them. An old castle sat on top of the mountains. It looked down at the valley below. Tall pine trees covered in snow surrounded it. This was the kind of gray stone castle that used to scare people in the area. Now most castles like this were empty and forgotten. Only ruins were left to show they used to be great. But this castle was different. The Counts von Steinrück kept their family's old castle in good shape. They didn't really care about it much though. It was far away in the mountains, cut off from everything else. The castle only came alive during hunting season. That's when guests would come visit. Then the old walls would echo with noise and activity. But this year was different. Guests did come, it's true.

STREET 1880. _The right of translation is reserved._ PREFACE TO THE SECOND EDITION. The present edition of this Essay is substantially a reproduction of the first. It is possible, indeed, and I hope probable, that the fruits of nine years’ more experience and study would have manifested themselves in some marked improvements upon the former work had I rewritten or recast the whole of it. But after mature consideration it did not seem to me that the defects of my original attempt were sufficient to warrant such an expenditure of time and toil. I have therefore contented myself with carefully revising the text and references, and making here and there a few slight alterations in the way either of addition or omission. these, for sixty years, from 1830 to 1890,[1] two poets sat, till their right to these lofty peaks became unchallenged. Beneath them, during these years, on the lower knolls of the mount of song, many new poets sang; with diverse instruments, on various subjects, and in manifold ways. They had their listeners; the Muses were also their visitants; but none of them ventured seriously to dispute the royal summits where Browning and Tennyson sat, and smiled at one another across the vale between. Both began together; and the impulses which came to them from the new and excited world which opened its fountains in and about 1832 continued to impel them till the close of their lives. While the poetic world altered around them, while two generations of poets made new schools of poetry, they remained, for the most part, unaffected by these schools. There is nothing of Arnold and Clough, of Swinburne, Rossetti or Morris, or of any of the others, in Browning or Tennyson. There is nothing even of Mrs. Browning in Browning. What changes took place in them were wrought, first, by the natural growth of their own character; secondly, by the natural development of their art-power;

### Plain English:
For sixty years, from 1830 to 1890, two poets sat at the top. Their right to be there was never questioned. Below them, during those years, many new poets sang on the lower parts of the mountain of song. They had different instruments. They wrote about different subjects. They wrote in different ways. People listened to them. The Muses visited them too. But none of them seriously tried to challenge Browning and Tennyson at the top. Those two smiled at each other from across the valley. Both poets started at the same time. The new, exciting world that opened up around 1832 pushed them forward their whole lives. The poetry world changed around them. Two generations of new poets created new styles. But Browning and Tennyson stayed mostly the same. They didn't follow Arnold and Clough. They didn't follow Swinburne, Rossetti, or Morris. They didn't follow any of the others. Even Browning wasn't influenced by Mrs. Browning. The changes that did happen to them came from two things. First, their own character grew naturally. Second, their skill at writing poetry developed naturally.

She was a good mother. She wanted her children more than anything else to become followers of Christ. Charles was a pleasant child, but he needed a new heart, like all children do, no matter how nice they are. When he was young, he felt this need. When he was about seven years old, a child from a missionary family died. This made him think about death. He heard a sermon that made him pray a lot for his sins to be forgiven. The Holy Spirit was working on him. He soon gave in to God's power and trusted in Jesus. He started a new life. Prayer made him happy. The Bible was very important to him. He wanted to do the right thing and make everyone around him happy. Charles's friends couldn't help but love him because he was always kind. Some of his friends were also religious young people like him. Whenever they spent time together, they would pray before they said goodbye. One time, when one of his good friends was about to leave, Charles said, "Let's not say goodbye until we pray together." So they went.

### Plain English:
The news started about God becoming human and the Virgin Mary's Son. Then some shepherds who were watching their flocks heard about it. An angel told them: "Listen, I have good news about a Saviour being born. This news is for you and for everyone on earth. Today God kept his promise. Today a Saviour was born—Christ the Lord. You'll find him in David's city, shepherds. He's the one everyone said would come to save people. He's wrapped in cloths, this holy baby. He's lying in a manger—that's how you'll know it's him." The angel spoke, and right away a group of angels from heaven joined in. They sang songs of joy like nothing anyone had heard before. They sang about God's saving love. Heaven rang out with their praise! They sang about God's glory. They sang about peace on earth and people being kind to each other. The shepherds ran straight to Bethlehem to see what God had done. These simple, honest shepherds, with pure hearts, went to find the baby. Let's follow this baby too. He lost something for us, and we need to follow him. He went from a poor manger to dying on a cross. We should follow his example, with God's help, until we get back what humans lost in the beginning. Then we can hope to be with the angels in heaven and sing.

fought again. Money? No credit even. Trade was at a standstill, yet the court was uproariously gay. Money and credit--sinews of pleasure as well as business--having flitted from lively Paris, you might suppose that the wheels of Society would cease to turn--that the flower-decked car of gilded Juggernaut would come creeping to a standstill. Not yet. Impelled by the impulse of its own velocity, it continued to crush on awhile. Those who knew were numbed by the chill shadow of the inevitable, or rendered callous by the knowledge of their helplessness. Those who were deaf and blind groped blissfully on in their lighthearted ignorance. Selfish all, depraved most, the blue-blooded sang in merry chorus, "Let us eat and drink that the worms may grow fat on us." Not so the gaunt crowds whose blood was but mud and water. As their long-suffering ancestors had monotonously done, they groaned in unison, crying to God for death, as the only release from misery. What if whole villages were decimated by famine? What if plague and starvation stalked through the towns? My lords and my ladies cared not, for they were poised too high to see. Were the grovelling creatures slaves or insects?

### Plain English:
They fought again. Money? There was no credit. Trade had stopped completely. But the court was having a wild party. Money and credit were what made fun and business work. Since they had left Paris, you might think that society would break down. You might think the fancy parade would stop. But it didn't. The party just kept going on its own. People who knew what was happening felt numb and scared. People who knew how helpless they were didn't care anymore. People who didn't know anything just kept partying without worry. They were all selfish, and most were corrupt. The rich people sang, "Let's eat and drink while we can." But the poor people were suffering. Just like their ancestors before them, they cried together to God for death, because death was the only escape from their pain. So what if whole villages were starving to death? So what if disease and starvation were everywhere? The rich lords and ladies didn't care. They were too high up to see what was happening. To them, poor people were just slaves or bugs.

AND ANNE SCARBOROUGH PREFACE The subject of the supernatural in modern English fiction has been found difficult to deal with because of its wealth of material. While there has been no previous book on the topic, and none related to it, save Mr. C. E. Whitmore's work on _The Supernatural in Tragedy_, the mass of fiction itself introducing ghostly or psychic motifs is simply enormous. It is manifestly impossible to discuss, or even to mention, all of it. Even in my bibliography which numbers over three thousand titles, I have made no effort to list all the available examples of the type. The bibliography, which I at first intended to publish in connection with this volume, is far too voluminous to be included here, so will probably be brought out later by itself. It would have been impossible for me to prosecute the research work or to write the book save for the assistance generously given by many persons. I am indebted to the various officials of the libraries of Columbia University and of New York City, particularly to Miss Isadore Mudge, Reference Librarian of Columbia, and to the authorities of the New York Society Library for permission to use

### Plain English:
AND ANNE SCARBOROUGH PREFACE

Writing about the supernatural in modern English fiction is hard because there is so much of it. No one has written a book about this topic before. The only related book is Mr. C. E. Whitmore's work on _The Supernatural in Tragedy_. But there are thousands of fiction books that include ghosts or psychic stuff. It's impossible to talk about all of them. Even in my list of over three thousand books, I didn't try to include every example. The full list is way too long to put in this book. I'll probably publish it separately later. I couldn't have done this research or written this book without help from many people. I want to thank the librarians at Columbia University and the New York City libraries. ### Plain English:
This book is dedicated to Gladys Goddard, who has been a friend to many boys and girls.

The author wants to thank Houghton, Mifflin and Company for letting her use the poem "Vantage" by Josephine Preston Peabody in this book. She also thanks Margaret Sherwood for letting her use her poem "Indian Summer."

Books for girls are meant to spark ideas and feelings. Their real value is in how they inspire readers. Some girls who read this story will really love beautiful words. This book includes those poems for readers like that.

Schools sometimes teach students that English literature is old and dead, like Latin. But there are writers alive right now who are "musicians of the word." Because they are alive, they can touch our hearts in ways that dead writers cannot.

If this book helps some girl discover "Singing Leaves," or the nice stories in "Daphne" and "King Sylvaine and Queen Aimée," then Catherine Smith and her friends will have done their job.

INTRODUCTION The interest and importance of the so-called Albigensian Heresy[1] lie in the fact that while it bears "a local habitation and a name," its actual habitation was not local, and its name is misleading. Its origin must be traced back to pre-Christian Ages, and its fruits will remain for ages to come. Its current title is inexact and incomplete; _inexact_, because Albi was not the _fons et origo_ of a movement which, although it took deepest root in Southern France, was sporadic throughout Central and Western Europe; _incomplete_, because the movement was not one heresy, but many, defying rigid classification, heterogeneous, self-contradictory, yet united in opposition to the Church of Rome. It is a mere accident of history that the name is derived from Albi, for Albi was but one, and that by no means the most important town infected. Long-accepted standards are being questioned and hitherto unchallenged rules of conduct submitted to searching criticism. History shows us that our present social system is only a phase in human development, and we turn to a study of the past, confident that a clearer insight into the social standards and habits of life prevalent in past ages will aid us in a better estimation of the relative importance of those factors of change we find around us to-day. MANKIND IN THE MAKING By H. G. Wells

PREFACE

I want to explain what this book is about so there's no confusion. This book is connected to an earlier book called _Anticipations_, and together with that book and a short pamphlet called "The Discovery of the Future," it presents an overall theory about how society develops and how people should act in society and politics. This book tries to handle social and political questions in a new way. It starts from a new angle. It looks at all of society and politics as parts of one big system that keeps changing. It puts all social and political activities in a specific place within that system. I want readers to pay special attention to this new way of thinking and looking at things. The two books and the pamphlet together should be seen as an attempt to explain these ideas clearly. The writer admits he started this work mainly for himself.

Produced by David Widger

# THE MARTINS OF CRO' MARTIN

TO THE REVEREND MORTIMER O'SULLIVAN, D.D.

I didn't ask permission to dedicate this book to you. That's because I didn't want you to be responsible for any opinions in it. I also didn't want to use your famous name to make my own simple work look better. But I can't stop myself from putting your name in this book. I've put a lot of thought and work into it. It shows how much I respect and like you. Your friendship is the thing I'm most proud of.

Yours truly,
CHARLES LEVER
Casa Cappoli, Florence
May, 1856

### Plain English:
TUNE IX.—UNDER THE OAK-TREE X.—FLEUR'S WEDDING XI.—THE LAST OF THE OLD FORSYTES

THE FORSYTE SAGA—VOLUME III. By John Galsworthy

WAKING UP

Through the big skylight that lit up the hallway at Robin Hill, the July sun at five o'clock shone right where the wide stairway turned. In that bright light stood little Jon Forsyte, wearing a blue linen suit. His hair was shining. His eyes had a frown under them. He was trying to figure out how to go downstairs. This would be the last time before his mom and dad came home in the car. Should he go four steps at a time, then five at the bottom? That was boring. Should he slide down the handrail? But how? On his feet first? Really boring. On his stomach, sideways? That was weak. On his back with his arms stretched out on both sides? That was not allowed. On his face, head first, in a way nobody else had ever done? That was the reason for the frown on little Jon's face in the sunlight. It was the summer of 1909. The people who wanted to make English easier to speak didn't know about little Jon. If they had, they would have wanted him to help them.

### Plain English:
There's a lot of sad literature out there right now. Young people who weren't very talented came back from the war saying they were "fed up." That's basically what was wrong with them. The talented ones are giving more details now. They say something like a web is covering the sky. Or the sun has become something ugly. That's about as detailed as you can expect a talented person to explain their pain, even if they really do feel it. Disappointment has happened before, though. Around 1880, most of the best art was sad and complaining. Burne-Jones's work was always troubled. Matthew Arnold's poems were like a well-behaved, sad song. Rossetti was full of lost love and feeling out of place. But you could tell their sad view of the world was just an act for the public. At home, Burne-Jones was a happy, red-faced man. Arnold went to a lot of fancy dinners. Rossetti loved bacon and eggs and simple things. Today's sad young artists aren't as good at this act. But they do make you feel like something important to them really does seem broken.

### Plain English:
MORE GENERAL READING. _Pimlico, Aug. 22, 1807._ _Bailey & Macdonald, Printers, 3, Harris's Place, Pantheon, Oxford-Street._ THE *First Book* OF THE METAMORPHOSES OF OVID. I want to sing about bodies that change into different shapes. God, help me! These changes came from you. Inspire me to write. Keep my whole song connected. It goes from the old chaos and mess all the way to now, when the world is happy under Augustus. At first there was no earth or sea. There was no blue sky above everything. Nothing was separated or organized. All of nature looked like one dead, shapeless thing. We can call it chaos. The elements were all fighting with each other in confusion. The sun hadn't started moving across the sky yet. The moon hadn't lit up the night. No round earth hung in the sky, balanced by its own weight. There was no sky. The ocean hadn't wrapped itself around the land. The earth was soft and mixed with water and air. The air was thick and dark. The water wasn't flowing right. All the elements crashed into each other in a mess. Cold fought with heat. Wet fought with dry. Light, heavy, hard, and soft all battled each other. The world started to rise up.

"God, it's so nice to look at her, the graceful, good and beautiful one! Because of all the great things she has, everyone is ready to praise her. Who could ever get tired of her? Her beauty is always fresh and new. God, it's so nice to look at her, the graceful, good and beautiful one! CHARLES D'ORLÉANS NEW YORK E. P. DUTTON AND COMPANY 1913

PREFACE

Christina of Denmark is famous because of Holbein's well-known portrait in the National Gallery. The great Court painter was sent to Brussels by Henry VIII to paint a picture of the Emperor's niece. He did great work. He painted her really well. He showed her "very good face," her clear brown eyes with their honest look, the smile on "her beautiful red lips," and her thin fingers with their nervous grip. Brantôme and the queen Catherine de' Medici thought her hands were "the most beautiful hands in the world." In an amazing way, he caught the special charm of the young Duchess's personality. He made it feel real and alive.

### Plain English:
# Power and Money

If someone only cares about power and money, and thinks only about themselves, they will lose their good reputation while they're still alive. When they die, they will be forgotten and nobody will care. They'll turn into dust, just like they came from dust. Nobody will cry for them. Nobody will honor them. Nobody will even sing about them.

# What Smart People Think Today

An old business man talks about how much better the world is now compared to seventy years ago. Lewis Carroll gives advice about reading good books. Rudyard Kipling explains what literature really is. Richard Mansfield says that all men are actors. A professor gives reasons why we should change how we spell words. Helen Keller describes how terrible it is to be blind. Other important people also share their opinions.

# The Real Truth About "The Good Old Days"

Stephen A. Knight is an old cotton factory owner. He talks about what work and pay were like seventy years ago.

The more you learn about life back in the "good old days," the more grateful you'll be that you live now instead of then. Life was really hard for American workers back then. People made very little money. It was almost impossible to get an education. People didn't have many comforts compared to what we have today.

### Plain English:
This book came from a 1962 publication. We looked hard but couldn't find proof that anyone renewed the copyright. We fixed some small spelling and typing mistakes without mentioning them. * * * * * "Mark Phillips" is actually two writers: Randall Garrett and Laurence M. Janifer. They made up this pen name from their middle names (Philip and Mark). They came up with it soon after they met at a science-fiction convention. Both guys were drunk at the time, which explains a lot. Only one of them has ever gotten sober. The two writers argue constantly about which one it is. They've been working together for a while now. They came up with a weird way to write: Mr. Garrett writes all the verbs, adverbs, and interjections. Mr. Janifer writes all the nouns, pronouns, and adjectives. They make decisions together about conjunctions. If they tie, they replay the whole thing at Fenway Park in Boston early the next year. BRAIN TWISTER took fifteen years to make. Only three of those days were spent actually writing it. When they finished the book, both authors were happy and relaxed together in the pleasure of

### Plain English:
"I told her that at the door, but she made it clear she had come a long way and wouldn't leave without seeing the Doctor. She told the carriage driver to come back for her in about two hours because she didn't want to miss her train." "Where did you leave her? Not in that cold, dark living room, I hope?" "She sat down on one of the chairs in the hallway, and I left her there." "That's not very welcoming! Do you want her to freeze? Go ask her to come into the library where the fire is." As Hannah left the room, Mrs. Lindsay stood up and added two pieces of oak wood to the pile of coals burning in the fireplace. Then she carefully moved a silver teapot and a covered dish with the pastor's dinner farther away from the flames. "Come in, madam. I promise nobody will bother you. Miss Elise says she only wants to see the Doctor." Hannah brought the visitor inside and stood at the door, making a signal to her boss, who stopped and stared curiously at the stranger wrapped up in heavy clothes with her face covered by a veil.

### Plain English:
# Plain English Version

New York: T. J. Hurley, Printer, Harmony Hall Building. 1895. Introduction.

Not long after 1492, when the Italian explorer Christopher Columbus and his Spanish crew first discovered America, Europeans who came after him started finding huge buildings with beautiful architecture all over Mexico, Guatemala, and Yucatan. When they conquered Peru, they found man-made roads and water systems. These could only have been built by people who knew advanced science and how to live in a civilized way. No people living on this continent at that time could have done this kind of work. It amazed the world.

Since these discoveries were first made, smart people and dreamers have tried to figure out when these monuments were built and who built them. People have suggested that Norwegians and Welsh people did the work. Our own Donnelly has changed where historians say humans first started. Instead of saying it was in the valley of the Tigris and Euphrates rivers, he says it was somewhere else.

### Plain English:
# Plain English Version

Editor Whiz-Bang, Robbinsdale, Minnesota

Dear Sir,

On September 27th, our train #12 stopped at Robbinsdale for 37 minutes. We were loading what they said was 36,000 pounds of mail. We need to find a way to handle this much mail without slowing down trains too much. Can you please tell us this information:

First, how often do you publish the magazine?

Second, what days or dates do you normally mail it out?

Third, how much does each issue weigh, or how many copies do you send out?

If you give us this information, we can work out a special plan to get the magazines to the cities faster. We might even leave an extra baggage car at Robbinsdale the day before you need to send out the magazine.

Sincerely,
M. J. Woulfe

This letter says a lot! If all our Winter Annuals had been loaded at the same time, Captain Billy would have needed an entire train just for mail. I sing about a dream I had, then about love—hot, burning love—and I end by calling to the forest spirit, because that's what I want. It's warm and quiet. Birds chirp now and then, so softly it sounds like whispering. I kind of expect to see a mountain goat with long horns curling back from its head peek out from between the birch trees. The sun shines through the leaves that are moving, and it throws little spots of light that dance and twinkle on the pine needles. I close my eyes. A big bright shape appears.

OF ANNY AND TO “TELL TRUE” INTRODUCTION In the sense of requiring elucidation or apology, this novel needs no introduction. The young lady who wrote it about two years ago, when she was eighteen, has already abandoned this work to publishers and other grown-ups, and with admirable professional good sense, is working upon fresh enterprises. In this, indeed, she is a genuine artist. Nothing is more clear from her correspondence with the writer of this introduction, than that she is, without ever becoming conscious of the fact, a genuine artist. Speaking of the intellectuals who occasionally impinge upon the family circle she says: “They have a horrid habit of---- oh, I can’t spell it, but it means pulling their minds to pieces and finding out how they are made, and they do that with their emotions, too.” Nothing of the sort is to be found in this tale of eastern England during the Restoration. And yet, while we may accept the unusual spectacle of a modern schoolgirl writing a red-blooded adventure story and privately poking fun at psychoanalysts and their dupes, we are justified in a certain curiosity as to the genesis of such a book. That curiosity the introduction

### Plain English:
# Annie and "Telling the Truth" - Introduction

This novel doesn't need an introduction to explain itself or say sorry for anything. The young woman who wrote it two years ago, when she was eighteen, has already given it to publishers and moved on to new projects. She's smart about her work. In fact, she's a real artist. Her letters to me make this clear. She's a real artist, even though she probably doesn't realize it. When she talks about the smart people who sometimes visit her family, she says: "They have this annoying habit of—oh, I can't spell it, but it means they take their minds apart to see how they work, and they do the same thing with their feelings too." You won't find anything like that in this story set in eastern England during the Restoration period. It's strange to see a teenage girl today write an exciting adventure story and secretly make fun of psychoanalysts and the people who believe in them. So it makes sense that people want to know where a book like this comes from. This introduction tries to answer that question.

UNIVERSITY PREFACE It has long been held that the laws of the Israelites, as revealed by God to Moses, by him embodied in the books of the Pentateuch and since preserved by the zealous care of the Jewish people, are incomparable. Accordingly they have been adopted professedly by most Christian nations and were early accepted by our own king Alfred[1] as the basis of the law system of this our land. We live in an age of devotion to comparative methods, when it is an article of faith to hold that the most fruitful means to attain a clear understanding of the exact nature of anything is to compare it with its like. This comparative method forms a large part of modern scientific research and, with proper safeguards and reserves, has become a favourite weapon of literary research into the history of human institutions. Long ago, as it seems to us, SIR HENRY MAINE used it[2] when he wrote his _History of Early Law_. As a consequence of his investigations and those of many who have followed in his footsteps, the Science of Comparative Law has grown up. All the great law systems of the world have been classified and

### Plain English:
People have always believed that the laws of the Israelites—the laws God gave to Moses, which Moses wrote down in the first five books of the Bible, and which Jewish people have carefully kept safe—are the best laws ever made. Most Christian countries have adopted these laws on purpose. King Alfred of England also used them as the foundation for English law long ago. Today, we believe in comparing things to understand them better. We think the best way to really understand something is to look at how it compares to similar things. Scientists use this method all the time. Writers who study history also use it to understand how human society developed. A long time ago, Sir Henry Maine used this method when he wrote his book called _History of Early Law_. Because of what Maine discovered, and because many other people followed his ideas, a whole new subject was created called Comparative Law. People have now organized and studied all the major law systems in the world.

I should write these reminiscences, mainly because it seems to me that circumstances have thrown my life along such lines that I really have been more than any other man at the centre of the growth of golf--a growth out of nothingness in England, and of relative littleness in Scotland, fifty years ago, to its present condition of a fact of real national importance. ### Plain English:
I should write down these memories. The main reason is that my life has put me right in the middle of golf's huge growth. Fifty years ago, golf was basically nothing in England and very small in Scotland. Now it's really important to the country. I saw golf start up in England at Westward Ho! I watched it grow at Hoylake and later at Sandwich. I was on the committees that created the Amateur Championship, the International Match, and the Rules of Golf Committee. I was Captain of the Royal North Devon, Royal Liverpool, Royal St. George's, and Royal and Ancient Clubs, plus many others. Because of these jobs, I was able to watch every step of golf becoming popular. I'm not mentioning these positions to brag. I'm just showing that probably no one else has had the same chances. I should also explain that when I call golf a "fact of national importance," I'm not exaggerating.
