FEAR AND all day long, the city sounds like an injured animal in a cave that is drowning. Days keep coming one after another, hitting it over and over again. Something invisible and sad releases a flood that goes beyond all limits. The old city lies on the ground and roars as it feels water from a huge lake reach it from far away. All it can do now as the water rises is listen and hear the waves crash like thunder through the broken streets. It hears sounds echoing in the quiet spaces between crashes.

WAKING UP

WHEN I woke up, the light from the lake was shaking on the wall. The sunshine came across the room in bright waves, back and forth. A big furry bee was hanging over the flowers in the window. His body was covered in black fur, and the sound he made was annoying.

### Plain English:
The websites LEO, DICDATA, and MR HONEY are linked together. Mr Honey's services are free and not for profit. They help teach business language in both English and German.

History and Philosophy

This dictionary started when England wanted to join the European community. The BBC, British Council, Department of Education, and Oxford University Press worked together. They wanted to find a way to teach business English to future leaders in Europe. I was one of the few teachers in London who taught business English back then. So I got involved with the project. At that time, I had to choose between a career in computers or teaching business English at a college. I decided to do both. As a teacher, I wanted to use multimedia to teach practical business English. From my work with different classes and groups, I created word lists and dictionaries based on what students actually needed. I learned from A.S. Hornby, a professor at University College London. He believed in teaching with lots of real examples. So I made sure my dictionaries had many useful examples. The word lists in the shorter versions are organized in order.

# The Story Begins

It was two hours before sunrise on this hot night in early September. The thin crescent moon had disappeared behind clouds in the west long ago. Nothing made a sound across the flat land around the city under attack. To the south, the campfires of Alva's army had gone out one by one. The only noise was the guards walking back and forth on their patrol routes. To the north, Orange and his small group of men—who were not trained, not paid, and not happy about following orders—were trying hard to get his powerful enemy to fight a real battle. They were hoping God would help them win. It was quieter there. The guards were tired and didn't care. They had fallen asleep while on duty. The soldiers were already angry about the situation. They only kept doing their jobs because of the strong leader they followed.

### Plain English:
# THE CAPTAIN OF THE GRAY-HORSE TROOP

I. A CAMP IN THE SNOW

Winter in the high parts of the Bear Tooth Range is beautiful but empty. Snow covers everything and the sky is bright blue. Nothing moves. Nothing makes a sound. Only small rabbits come out at the openings of their holes. They go back down into their homes in the rocks. The mountains look like big white domes. During the day, the sun hits them. At night, stars shine above them. The evergreen trees look like they're being crushed under all the snow. The streams are frozen and silent. Only the wind blows through this empty space. The wind never stops. It's cold and sharp like betrayal. It wants more and more like a fire that can never be full. But it's still beautiful. It's especially beautiful at dawn when the shadows are deep purple. The tops of the mountains are bright and glowing. The slopes on the east side look like roads covered with crushed diamonds. It's also beautiful at sunset when the high peaks look like burning markers showing the way to heaven. The lakes...

### Plain English:
William T. Adams wrote under the pen name "Oliver Optic." When he died, he left his last book unfinished. This book was supposed to be the final one in the "Blue and Gray--On Land" series. Many people asked for someone to finish it and publish it. Edward Stratemeyer, who wrote the popular "Old Glory" series about the Spanish-American War, agreed to complete it. He did a great job finishing the story the way Adams probably wanted it to end. The book series stayed consistent all the way through.

The publishers want to honor Adams' memory. For many years, he only gave his books to this one publisher. No other publisher ever got his work. This new book is the one hundred and eighth book published under the name "Optic." Adams stayed loyal to this publisher through good times and bad times. In return, his books sold over two million copies.

Downie surveyed him through his double eyeglass, thinking that Sharkley was laughing covertly at him; but no such thought was hovering in the mind of that personage, who was not much of a laugher at any time, save when he had successfully outwitted or jockeyed any one. ### Plain English:
"You expect me to believe this made-up story that you invented just to get money out of me!" exclaimed Downie Trevelyon. He was lying back in a fancy leather chair and playing with his expensive gold eye-glass and silk ribbon. He sounded angry and superior. "It's not a made-up story, my lord. It's about a real ship that sank—the steamer Admiral from Montreal," replied Sharkley quietly and seriously. Downie looked at him through his eye-glass. He thought Sharkley might be secretly laughing at him. But Sharkley wasn't the type to laugh much. He only laughed when he had tricked or outsmarted someone. He looked very uncomfortable. He sat on the edge of a fancy chair with brass nails. He held his tall shiny hat on his knees. His long, bare, dirty-looking fingers moved nervously on the hat's crown. He kept looking back and forth between Downie and Lady Lamorna. She was watching him like he was some kind of strange curiosity.

Shortridge is associate curator of cultural history in the United States National Museum, Smithsonian Institution._ Perhaps the modern tendency to idealize progress has been responsible for the neglect of Italian harpsichords and virginals during the present day revival of interest in old musical instruments. Whatever laudable traits the Italian builders may have had, they cannot be considered to have been progressive. Their instruments of the mid-16th century hardly can be distinguished from those made around 1700. During this 150 years the pioneering Flemish makers added the four-foot register, a second keyboard, and lute and buff stops to their instruments. However, the very fact that the Italian builders were unwilling to change their models suggests that their instruments were good enough to demand no further improvements. Anyone who has heard a properly restored Italian harpsichord or an accurately

### Plain English:
Europeans liked these instruments, but they didn't have the extra keyboard and sound controls that European harpsichords had. This paper describes Italian harpsichords from the Hugo Worch Collection in the United States National Museum in detail. The author also explains why Italian harpsichords had different keyboard sizes and string lengths. John D. Shortridge wrote this paper. He works as an associate curator of cultural history at the United States National Museum at the Smithsonian Institution. Today, people might ignore Italian harpsichords and virginals because we tend to think new things are always better. The Italian builders who made these instruments were not interested in making them more modern. Their instruments from the middle 1500s looked almost the same as the ones they made around 1700. During those 150 years, Flemish builders from other parts of Europe added new features to their harpsichords. They added a four-foot register, a second keyboard, and other sound controls. But the fact that Italian builders didn't want to change their designs shows something important. It shows that their instruments were already so good that they didn't need to be improved. If you listen to a properly restored Italian harpsichord or an accurate one, you understand this.

that I have said to you about poetical Translations, and the resemblance there is between _Virgil's_ and _Milton's_ Versification, and some other Matters of the same nature. To begin with the Business of Translation. Whoever sits down to translate a Poet, ought in the first place to consider his Author's peculiar _Stile_; for without this, tho' the Translation may be very good in all other respects, it will hardly deserve the Name of a Translation. The two great Men amongst the Antients differ from each other as much in this particular as in the Subjects they treat of. The Stile of _Homer_, who sings the Anger or Rage of _Achilles_, is _rapid_. The Stile of _Virgil_, who celebrates the Piety of _Æneas_, is _majestick_. But it may be proper to explain in what this Difference consists. The Stile is _rapid_, when several Relatives, each at the head of a separate Sentence, are governed by one Antecedent, or several Verbs by one Nominative Case, to the close of the Period. Thus in _Homer_: "Goddess, sing the pernicious Anger of _Achilles_, which brought infinite Woes to the _Grecians_, and sent many valiant Souls of Heroes to Hell, and gave their Bodies to

### Plain English:
I've talked to you about poetry translations. I've also mentioned how Virgil and Milton write their verses in similar ways. There are other things like this too. Let me start with translation.

When someone decides to translate a poet, they need to think about how that poet writes. This is the most important thing. Even if the translation is good in every other way, it won't really be a translation if you don't get this right. The two great ancient poets wrote very differently from each other. They didn't just write about different things. They also had different styles.

Homer wrote about Achilles' anger. His style is fast and quick. Virgil wrote about Aeneas and how good he was. His style is grand and impressive. But I should explain what I mean by this difference.

A style is fast and quick when you use many relative clauses. Each one starts a new sentence. But they all connect back to one main word. Or you have many verbs. They all have the same subject. The sentence keeps going to the end. Here's how Homer does it: "Goddess, sing about the terrible anger of Achilles. It caused endless pain to the Greeks. It sent many brave heroes to Hell. It gave their bodies to..."

This may account for the saying that "history is a lie agreed to;" for it needs to be argus-eyed to give us any adequate idea of the truth; and while the writer of the following sketches does not aspire to the rank of a historian, he has been induced to print them for two or three reasons. First, because urged to by friends; and secondly, because of the unique condition of American frontier life that is so rapidly passing away forever. One may read Macaulay, Froude, Knight, and, in fact, a half-dozen histories of England, and then sit down to the gossipy sketches of Sidney culled from Pepys's, Evelyn's, and other diaries, and get a truer view of English life than in all the great histories combined. It would be impossible to give even the slightest sketch of a country so large as ours for a single decade in many volumes; although, in one sense, we are more homogeneous than many suppose. There was a greater difference in two counties in England before the advent of the railways

### Plain English:
People used to think history had two eyes. But now we know it has at least three. Archaeology is the third eye. That's why people say "history is a lie that everyone agrees on." You need to look at everything carefully to get close to the truth. The person who wrote these sketches is not trying to be a real historian. But he decided to print them anyway for a couple of reasons. First, his friends asked him to. Second, life on the American frontier is disappearing fast, and it's unique. You could read a bunch of history books about England by Macaulay, Froude, and Knight. Then you could read some gossipy sketches about Sidney from old diaries by Pepys and Evelyn. You would understand English life better from those diaries than from all those big history books put together. It would be impossible to write even a short description of a country as big as ours for just one ten-year period. It would take many books. But in some ways, we are more alike than people think. Before trains were invented, two counties in England were more different from each other.

### Plain English:
During my West African film trip in June 1913, I decided I wanted to make movies about native dramas. I wanted to film them in real places where they actually happened. Paradise Of God. By Joseph Bates New Bedford: Press Of Benjamin Lindsey. 1846. PREFACE In presenting the following subject to the consideration of whom it may concern, I would here state that the two leading motives which have actuated and guided me through this absorbing subject has been--first, the truth of God to encourage and strengthen the true-believer. Second, to correct, or "rebuke" the spiritual views, (may I not say of almost all Christendom,) in respect to the appearing and kingdom of our Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ. Twenty-one years observation and experience, but more especially the last seven, in pursuit of this object, has taught me that truth is the only thing that can save the soul. But the great mass of the professed Christian world seem to pay no more regard to it than their great _Predecessor_, who said unto the Saviour "what is truth?" when he had just said to him that he "came into the world to bear witness unto the truth, and every one that is of the truth, heareth my voice." Jesus in his last prayer for his disciples asks the Father to sanctify them through the truth. "Thy word is truth." _St. John._

### Plain English:
Paradise of God. By Joseph Bates. New Bedford: Press of Benjamin Lindsey. 1846.

PREFACE

I'm sharing this subject with whoever wants to read it. I want to explain why I wrote this book. I had two main reasons. First, I wanted to show God's truth so it would help and strengthen people who truly believe in God. Second, I wanted to correct wrong spiritual ideas. I think almost all Christian churches have wrong ideas about when Jesus will come back and about his kingdom.

I've studied this topic for twenty-one years. The last seven years especially taught me something important. The only thing that can save a person's soul is truth. But most Christians don't seem to care much about truth. They're like an important person from the past who asked Jesus "what is truth?" This was strange because Jesus had just told him that he came into the world to tell people about the truth. Anyone who believes in the truth listens to what Jesus says.

Near the end of his life, Jesus prayed for his followers. He asked God to make them holy through the truth. Jesus said "Your word is truth."

Additions and a new introduction by the translator.

LONDON: Printed for Edward Valentine, at the Queen's Head against St. Dunstan's Church, Fleet Street, 1721.

Translated by the author of the Account of Denmark.

**THE BOOKSELLER TO THE READER**

This translation of the famous Hotoman's Franco-Gallia was written in 1705. It was first published in 1711. The author was far away from London at that time. The publisher decided not to print the introduction that came with the original book. But since this piece is useful for English readers to read, and especially important right now, I decided to finally share it with the public. I'm sure people who love their country will want to read it.

**THE TRANSLATOR'S INTRODUCTION**

Many books and papers have been published since the recent Revolution. They explain why the English people acted the way they did at that important time. They show what our real rights and freedoms are. They explain how our government is built. This system isn't just ours. It belongs to almost all countries.

### Plain English:
# Plain English Version

The Philippines has amazing people and strange things that are just as interesting. A new author who really knows the country well wrote stories about it. The stories are simple but very powerful. They show you what the Philippines is really like in a dramatic way. There are pirates, almost naked native people, pearls, creatures that are part man and part ape, huge volcanoes with clouds and weird legends around their tops, unusual animals and birds, and strange people too. There are pythons and ropes covered in blood, palm trees that get pushed by soft breezes one moment and knocked down by storms the next. No stage has ever had a more amazing scene with so many interesting characters.

The stories by Sargent Kayme are very good and don't make things up. You can check this by reading letters from newspaper reporters, soldiers, or travel books about the Philippines. Maybe the strangest thing about these stories is the anting-anting. It's both a mysterious power that protects the person who owns it and an object that shows this protection. No weirder magical charm has ever been found in folklore. It could be a button, a coin, or a piece of something.

Third Edition

PREFACE TO THE FIRST EDITION

In this book about a region the author knows very well, he has collected some local points of interest. He has tried to show what life is like in South Lancashire right now. He includes descriptions of the scenery and information from the local history that relates to his topic. He gathered what he could find available to him. He is willing to let readers who know the area decide if the book is helpful or interesting. He admits that this book is incomplete and jumps around a lot compared to all the interesting things Lancashire really has to offer. However, he believes that what he has included is worth reading.

Though perhaps misunderstood, reviled and even persecuted while living, the apotheosis is almost sure to come after death: and the victim of yesterday's mob, raised to the state of an Intercessor in Heaven, is besought with prayer and tears, and placatory penances, to mediate with God for the pardon of human sin. This is a mean and vile trait of human nature, the proof of ignorance, selfishness, brutal cowardice, and a superstitious materialism. It shows the base instinct to put down and destroy whatever or whoever makes men feel their own imperfections; with the alternative of ignoring and denying these very imperfections by turning into gods men who have merely spiritualised their natures, so that it may be supposed that they were heavenly incarnations and not mortal like other men. This process of euhemerisation, as it is called, or the making of men into gods and gods into men, sometimes, though more rarely, begins during the life of the hero, but usually after death. We pretend they were special and sent from heaven, not just regular people like everyone else. This happens sometimes while someone is still alive, but usually after they die. People start adding fake stories to what really happened in their lives. They make up signs and miracles to match their new god-like status. Omens and portents are now made.

PHIL, THE FIDDLER By Horatio Alger, Jr. PREFACE Among the most interesting and picturesque classes of street children in New York are the young Italian musicians, who wander about our streets with harps, violins, or tambourines, playing wherever they can secure an audience. They become Americanized less easily than children of other nationalities, and both in dress and outward appearance retain their foreign look, while few, even after several years’ residence, acquire even a passable knowledge of the English language. In undertaking, therefore, to describe this phase of street life, I found, at the outset, unusual difficulty on account of my inadequate information. But I was fortunate enough to make the acquaintance of two prominent Italian gentlemen, long resident in New York--Mr. A. E. Cerqua, superintendent of the Italian school at the Five Points, and through his introduction, of Mr. G. F. Secchi de Casale, editor of the well-known Eco d’Italia--from whom I obtained full and trustworthy information. A series of articles contributed by Mr. De Casale to his paper, on the Italian street children, in whom he has long felt a patriotic and sympathetic interest, I have found of great service, and I freely acknowledge that, but for the

### Plain English:
PHIL, THE FIDDLER By Horatio Alger, Jr. PREFACE

Some of the most interesting street children in New York are young Italian musicians. They walk around the streets playing harps, violins, or tambourines wherever they can find people to listen. These kids don't become American as quickly as children from other countries. They keep their Italian style of dress and look foreign. Even after living here for several years, most of them don't learn much English at all. So when I decided to write about this part of street life, I ran into a big problem. I didn't know enough about it. But I got lucky. I met two important Italian men who had lived in New York for a long time. One was Mr. A. E. Cerqua. He runs the Italian school at Five Points. Through him, I also met Mr. G. F. Secchi de Casale. He edits a well-known newspaper called Eco d'Italia. Both of these men gave me real, accurate information. Mr. De Casale had written articles for his newspaper about Italian street children. He cares about them and has followed their situation closely. These articles helped me a lot. I want to say that I couldn't have written this without their help.

In his selection, only those units which had shown marked ability making them worthy of distinction were chosen, the majority having seen actual service either in the Spanish-American War or during the more recent border troubles. ### Plain English:
This book was put together by Lieutenant Harold Stanley Johnson from the 151st Field Artillery (1st Minnesota). It was printed by Eaton & Gettinger, Inc. at 263 Ninth Avenue in New York.

FOREWORD

After spending three weeks at our training camp, I noticed that officers and soldiers really wanted to get to know the people they worked with. I realized that personal contact was important for us to work well together and coordinate all the different parts of the military into one efficient unit. So I decided to make this roster, or list of people. I couldn't have done this without help from Major-General William A. Mann, Colonel Douglass MacArthur, the Statistics Department, and the officers at Division headquarters.

The Rainbow Division got its name from Secretary of War Baker. He called it that because the official Forty-second Division had organizations from twenty-six different states. These states covered the entire country from coast to coast and from Maine all the way to the Gulf of Mexico. Baker only picked military units that had proven they were really good at what they did. Most of these units had already fought in the Spanish-American War or had dealt with recent trouble on the border. To be part of this group is an honor.

### Plain English:
There are some unclear things in the myths and worship of Attis, Adonis, Osiris, Dionysus, Demeter, and Virbius that need to be explained. People believed the corn-spirit could take the form of many different animals. These include wolves, dogs, hares, roosters, geese, cats, goats, cows, oxen, bulls, pigs, and horses. People thought the corn-spirit lived in the corn in one of these animal forms. They believed it was caught or killed when they cut the last bundle of grain. As workers cut the corn, the animal runs away from them. If a worker gets sick while working in the field, people say the corn-spirit punished him because he disturbed it by accident. They might say "The Rye-wolf got him" or "the Harvest-goat pushed him." The person who cuts the last corn or ties the last bundle gets a name based on the animal. They might be called the Rye-wolf or the Rye-sow or the Oats-goat. Sometimes they keep this name for a whole year. The wide ocean presents features well suited to tempt the imagination to stray into the wildest flights. Its vastness; the difficulty of exploring even a small portion of its surface, as well as its enormous depths; its capacity for containing the strangest and most gigantic objects that fancy can picture: these are attributes of the mighty deep that have ever attracted the attention and prompted the weird imaginings of man. It is a curious fact that recent scientific research has revealed the existence in the sea, at the greatest depths, of most minute and wonderfully formed organisms, the beauty and rarity of which necessarily secure our admiration; but instances of animals of enormous size being met with beyond those already known, are few and far between. This created the 80th Lancashire Rifle Volunteers. Then on April 2nd, 1863, another group called the 73rd Battalion of the Lancashire Rifle Volunteers joined with them. At first, the new group went to training events and inspections at Mount Vernon, Newton-le-Willows, and Aintree. Later, they changed its number to the 19th Lancashire Rifle Volunteers. Because of what they did, a real trained military group was created, and men learned how to be soldiers.

### Plain English:
28. PRINTED FOR THE AUTHOR. 1854. A NOTE TO THE PUBLIC.

In a book I wrote recently about German literature, I told readers that I planned to explain something that happened to me. I said this event was why I had to leave my job studying, and that this was unfair to the people I worked for. Now I'm keeping that promise. I'm sharing some of the letters I wrote over the past year. I'm doing this for my friends and for anyone who cares about school and what's right. I could have included more letters from before and after this time. But I didn't want to write a whole book. I just wanted to tell the short story of a few pages about what happened to me at a big school in the city. I want to bring this private problem to public attention because I couldn't fix it in private. My main goal was to protect my good name, my job reputation, and my basic rights as a thinking person and a teacher.

National Park Handbooks, compact introductions to the great natural and historic places administered by the National Park Service, are designed to promote understanding and enjoyment of the parks. Each is intended to be informative reading and a useful guide before, during, and after a park visit. More than 100 titles are in print. This is Handbook 112. You may purchase the handbooks through the mail by writing to Superintendent of Documents, U.S. Government Printing Office, Washington, DC 20402. About This Book Great Smoky Mountains National Park straddles the North Carolina-Tennessee border and encompasses the climax of the Appalachian Mountain System. Major attractions are the mountains themselves, the preserved structures and lore of mountain folklife, stupendous displays of flowering plants and shrubs, fall colors, wild animals, superb hiking opportunities, and gorgeous rivers, streams, and waterfalls. This handbook is published in support of the National Park Service’s management policies and interpretive programs at the park. Part 1 gives a brief introduction to what you may find in a leisurely visit to the park; Part 2 outlines the natural history of the mountains and their valleys; and Part 3 presents concise travel guide and reference materials. Library of Congress Cataloging in Publication Data

### Plain English:
National Park Handbooks are short books that introduce you to the big natural and historic places run by the National Park Service. They help you understand and enjoy the parks better. Each book is meant to be interesting to read and useful before, during, and after you visit a park. There are more than 100 of these books available. This is book number 112. You can buy the handbooks by mail. Write to the Superintendent of Documents, U.S. Government Printing Office, Washington, DC 20402.

Great Smoky Mountains National Park sits on the border between North Carolina and Tennessee. It covers the highest part of the Appalachian Mountain System. The main things to see are the mountains themselves, old buildings and stories about how mountain people lived, lots of flowers and shrubs, fall leaves that change color, wild animals, great hiking trails, and beautiful rivers, streams, and waterfalls. This handbook supports how the National Park Service manages the park and teaches visitors about it.

The first part gives you a quick look at what you can see during a relaxed visit. The second part explains the natural history of the mountains and valleys. The third part gives you travel tips and useful information to look up.

How far our object has been attained of course our readers must judge. Nothing has been intentionally concealed; and all that is asserted is the result of minute observation, close application, and an extended continuous experience from childhood. We pretend not to infallibility, and are not so sanguine as to declare our views the most perfect that can be attained. But we can so far say, that the practice here recommended has been found very successful. Some very probably may be disappointed in not having the means of propagating as clearly delineated as those of culture; but to have entered into all the minutiæ connected therewith, would have formed materials for two volumes

### Plain English:
Readers and amateur flower lovers kept asking us for a practical book on growing flowers. So we finally decided to write one. This book is simple and straightforward. It's a plain guide to growing flowers, which is becoming more popular. You'll notice right away that we're not trying to sound fancy. The instructions are as simple as possible. We organized everything as clearly as we could. Our only goal is to make this book actually useful. Only you can judge if we succeeded. We haven't hidden anything on purpose. Everything we say comes from watching carefully, working hard, and having lots of experience since we were kids. We're not claiming to be perfect. We don't think our ideas are the best possible. But we can say that the methods we recommend really work well. Some people might wish we explained how to grow new plants as clearly as we explained how to care for them. But if we had covered all those details, we would have needed two books instead of one.

By L. B. Seeley and Sons, Fleet Street. 1828. Mills, Jowett, and Mills, Printers, Bolt Court, Fleet Street.

DEDICATION

To the Right Reverend Charles Richard, Lord Bishop of Winchester.

My Lord,

I decided to ask if I could dedicate this small book to you because I believe its ideas match what your church teaches. I also think the ideas in this book agree with religious truth, which is the standard we should use to judge all theology and morality.

I want to publish this book to help young people understand the Bible better. I'm grateful that you approved of it. I want to thank you for supporting someone like me.

With great respect,

Your servant,

The Author

August 18th, 1828

PREFACE

A preface is expected in books. Most readers don't really care why an author decided to write something, but an introduction is usually needed anyway. This small book took me a long time to work on.

& CO. 1906 PREFACE In the following account of the life and work of that "hero and type of the intellectual energy of the eighteenth century"--the "honest heretic"--Joseph Priestley, I have, to a considerable extent, made the subject of it tell his own story. After Priestley's death there was found among his papers a short autobiography, dealing with the main events of his life up to the time of his settlement in America. This was subsequently published, with additions and explanatory notes, by his eldest son. Of this biography I have made full use, considering it, of course, as the best authority on the matters to which it refers. For the account of the Warrington Academy, with which institution Priestley was connected for some years, and which connection profoundly affected his career, I am mainly indebted to Mr Henry A. Bright's paper in the _Transactions of the Historic Society of Lancashire and Cheshire_, 1858-59. The Yates papers in the possession of the Royal Society have also afforded me much assistance, and have been freely drawn upon. I am also indebted to the late Mr Henry Carrington Bolton's collection for certain letters and for information concerning the Lunar Society of Birmingham.

### Plain English:
& CO. 1906 PREFACE

This book tells the life story of Joseph Priestley. People called him a "hero" and said he was the best example of smart thinking in the 1700s. They also called him the "honest heretic." In this book, I let Priestley tell much of his own story.

After Priestley died, people found a short autobiography in his papers. It covered the main events of his life until he moved to America. His oldest son later published this autobiography and added notes to explain things. I used this autobiography a lot because it's the best source for information about Priestley's life.

For information about Warrington Academy, where Priestley worked for several years, I mostly used a paper by Henry A. Bright. It was published in the Transactions of the Historic Society of Lancashire and Cheshire in 1858-59. Priestley's time at this school was very important to his career.

The Yates papers at the Royal Society also helped me a lot, and I used them freely. I also used letters and information from the late Henry Carrington Bolton's collection. This helped me learn about the Lunar Society of Birmingham.

Its purpose is to bring men courage and resolution, to cheer them, to fire them with new confidence when they grow dispirited, to strengthen their faith that THINGS CAN BE DONE. It is better for this purpose than the entire works of any one poet, for it takes the cream of many and has greater diversity than any one writer can show. It is made up chiefly of very recent poems--not such as were written for anthologies of poetical "gems," but such as speak directly to the heart, always in very simple language, often in the phrases of shop or office or street. Included, however, with the poems of the day are a few of the fine old pieces that have been of comfort to men through the ages. ### Plain English:
Distributed Proofreaders: IT CAN BE DONE - POEMS OF INSPIRATION collected by Joseph Morris and St. Clair Adams

FOREWORD

This is a book of poems meant to inspire people. It's meant to give people courage and help them stay strong. It cheers them up and makes them feel confident again when they're feeling down. It helps them believe that things can actually get done.

This book works better than reading all the poems by just one poet. That's because it takes the best poems from many different poets. This gives you more variety than any single writer could give you.

Most of the poems in here are new ones. They weren't written just to be put in fancy poetry books. Instead, they speak straight to your heart. They use very simple words. Many of them use the language you hear in shops, offices, and on the street.

But the book also includes some really good old poems. These are poems that have made people feel better for hundreds of years.

Besides the poems, the book also has things to help you understand and enjoy them better. Each poem has a short introduction. These introductions work like the music a piano player starts with before a singer begins to sing. They set up the right feeling and help you understand what the poem is about.

### Plain English:
The sound of their master's whip. The canal worker was short and ugly, with thick red hair and freckled skin. His nose was swollen from drinking, and it hung down over his upper lip. A jagged scar ran straight across his chin from ear to ear. He got this scar from a razor when he was a boy. When he got emotional, his throat would tighten up and shake, making his words hard to understand. His red flannel shirt was patched with lighter colored pieces. It hung open and showed his dark, rough skin underneath. "Git up! Git up!" he said with a stutter. For a few minutes the boat moved quietly, except for the sound of water splashing and the mules' feet hitting the narrow path by the river. From a small room at one end of the boat came a woman's voice singing. It was a young girl's voice that went up and down but had no real tune or rhythm. Then the mules suddenly stopped. "Whoa there!" the man yelled. "Get me a drink, Scraggy," he shouted, "and put a lot of whisky in it—you hear me?" His head moved forward as he gave the order. He held the reins in his left hand and turned to face forward.

modest scientist, the good and great man, whose life-story I have attempted to tell in the following pages. A life-story such as that of Michael Faraday is both easy and difficult to tell--it is easy in that he passed a simple and unadventurous life; it is difficult, partly, perhaps, for the same reason, and partly because the story of his life-work is a story of the wonderful advance made in natural science during the first half of the present century. Any detailed account of that scientific work would be out of place in a biography such as the present, which aims at showing by the testimony of those who knew him and by an account of his relations with his fellow-men, how nobly unselfish, how simple, yet how grand and useful, was the long life of Michael Faraday. Besides this, we are shown--how many an illustrious name in the bede-roll of our great men brings it to mind--that with an enthusiastic love for a particular study, and unflagging perseverance in pursuance of it, the most adverse circumstance of birth and fortune may be overcome, and he who has striven take rank among the great and good whose names adorn the

### Plain English:
Michael Faraday was a modest scientist and a good, great man. I have tried to tell his life story in the pages that follow. His life story is both easy and hard to tell. It is easy because he lived a simple life without much adventure. It is hard partly for that same reason, and partly because his life's work shows the amazing progress that science made in the first half of this century. A detailed account of all his scientific work would not fit in a biography like this one. This book aims to show, through the words of people who knew him and through stories about how he treated others, how selfless he was. It shows how simple yet grand and useful his long life was. This book also shows something important that many great men prove. When you love a subject deeply and work hard at it without giving up, you can overcome any bad luck about where you were born or how much money you had. You can become one of the great and good people whose names are remembered.

THUMB MARK BY R. AUSTIN FREEMAN PREFACE In writing the following story, the author has had in view no purpose other than that of affording entertainment to such readers as are interested in problems of crime and their solutions; and the story itself differs in no respect from others of its class, excepting in that an effort has been made to keep within the probabilities of ordinary life, both in the characters and in the incidents. Nevertheless it may happen that the book may serve a useful purpose in drawing attention to certain popular misapprehensions on the subject of finger-prints and their evidential value; misapprehensions the extent of which may be judged when we learn from the newspapers that several Continental commercial houses have actually substituted finger-prints for signed initials. The facts and figures contained in Mr. Singleton's evidence, including the very liberal estimate of the population of the globe, are, of course, taken from Mr. Galton's great and important work on finger-prints; to which the reader who is interested in the subject is referred for much curious and valuable information. In conclusion, the author desires to express his thanks to his friend Mr. Bernard E. Bishop for the assistance

### Plain English:
THUMB MARK BY R. AUSTIN FREEMAN PREFACE

I wrote this story just to entertain readers who like crime stories and how crimes get solved. The story is like other crime stories, except I tried to make it realistic. The characters and events are things that could actually happen in real life.

But this book might also help fix some wrong ideas people have about fingerprints and how useful they are as evidence. People are confused about fingerprints more than you might think. Newspapers have reported that some European businesses have actually started using fingerprints instead of signed initials.

The facts and numbers in Mr. Singleton's part of the story come from Mr. Galton's important book about fingerprints. If you want to learn more about fingerprints, you should read that book. It has lots of interesting and helpful information.

I want to thank my friend Mr. Bernard E. Bishop for helping me with this book.

AND ABUSE OF THE CHURCH BELLS. It has fallen to the writer’s lot, in the Divine dispensation, to be entrusted with the care, or joint care, of very many parishes in various parts of England: and he knows not any one external matter, common to them all, and to the neighbourhoods surrounding them, which has caused him more pain than the ordinary use, and the almost utter neglect for their own proper purposes, of the Church Bells. Indeed, so much is the proper use of these holy instruments of edification (for such they really are) generally lost sight of, that among all the New Churches which have been builded during the last few years, scarcely any have more than _one_ Bell; a greater number being considered a vain superfluity, a kind of ecclesiastical luxury—or, by deeper thinkers, a link between the Church and the _world_ (and that often in its fiercest contentions, vainest hours, and most carnal aspect) which we may well be rid of. In our older Churches, the position of the Belfry (on the floor of the Church, immediately communicating with the Nave, generally laid entirely open to it, often, too, having no other entrance, and not unfrequently

### Plain English:
# Misuse and Neglect of Church Bells

The writer has been put in charge of many parishes in different parts of England. Of all the problems he has seen in these places, nothing has upset him more than how church bells are being used and ignored. Church bells are supposed to serve an important purpose. They are meant to be holy tools. But people have forgotten why they're really there. This is so bad that new churches being built today have hardly any bells at all. Most people think having multiple bells is wasteful and unnecessary. Some people even think bells connect the church to the outside world in bad ways—through fights, empty pleasures, and physical desires. We should get rid of them, these people say.

In older churches, the bell tower is usually on the ground floor. It opens directly into the main part of the church. There's often no other way to get into it. Sometimes the tower is completely open to the rest of the church.

When Ulenspiegel heard this news, he told Lamme: "The Duke is calling for the Prince of Orange, his brother Ludwig, De Hoogstraeten, Van den Bergh, Culembourg, de Brederode, and other friends of the Prince. EDITION The first edition seems to have fulfilled a need for a general text-book on the subject of bacteriology. The original method of presentation is preserved. The text-book idea is adhered to, so that the individual instructor may have full liberty to expand on topics in which he is especially interested. A number of illustrations have been added, the text has been improved in many instances by the addition of further explanatory matter and the most recent general advances in the Science. Examples are the System of Classification of the Society of American Bacteriologists, which is used throughout the text, their Key to the Genera of Bacteria, a discussion of the H-ion concentration method of standardization, the selective action of anilin dyes, the mechanism of entrance of pathogenic organisms into the body, a more detailed explanation of the origin of antibodies, the nature of antigens and a table of antigens and antibodies. Professor Vera McCoy Masters has assisted in the revision by aiding in the preparation of manuscript and the reading of proof and in the making of the index, for which services the author’s thanks are hereby expressed. C. B. M. Columbus, Ohio, 1921. PREFACE TO FIRST EDITION An

### Plain English:
The first edition met the need for a basic textbook about bacteriology. The way it was written in the original version is still used here. It's still written as a textbook so that teachers can add more details about topics they care most about. We added more pictures. We improved the text in many places by adding more explanations and including the newest discoveries in science. Some examples are the way American bacteriologists classify bacteria, which we use throughout the book. We also added their guide to identifying different types of bacteria. We included information about measuring acid levels to standardize things. We talked about how certain dyes affect bacteria. We explained how disease-causing organisms get into the body. We gave a more detailed explanation of where antibodies come from. We described what antigens are. And we added a table showing antigens and antibodies. Professor Vera McCoy Masters helped with this new version by preparing the written material and checking it for errors. She also made the index. The author thanks her for this help. C. B. M. Columbus, Ohio, 1921.

cottages, and the natural enjoyment of rural life from the artificial luxury--the painted pomp and idle glitter of regal state. The above cottage stands in the grounds of Grove House, adjoining the churchyard of Old Windsor. It was built under the superintendent taste of the Princess Elizabeth,[1] second sister of the present King, and now known as the Landgravine of Hesse Homburg. To the decoration of this cottage the Princess paid much attention: it is quite in the _ornée_ style; and its situation is so beautiful as to baffle all embellishment. Grove House, the seat of Lady Dowager Onslow, of whom the Princess purchased the whole property, was built by Mr. Bateman, uncle to the eccentric Lord Bateman. This gentleman made it a point in his travels to notice everything that pleased him in the monasteries abroad; and, on his return to England, he built this house; the bedchamber being contrived, like the cells of monks, with a refectory, and every other appendage of a monastery; even to a cemetery, and a coffin, inscribed with the name of a supposititious ancient bishop. Some curious Gothic chairs, bought at a sale of the curiosities in this house, are now at Strawberry

### Plain English:
Cottages and the simple pleasure of country life are different from the fancy luxury of royal palaces with their decorations and showy displays. The cottage above is located on the grounds of Grove House. It sits next to the church in Old Windsor. Princess Elizabeth built it. She is the second sister of the current King. She is now called the Landgravine of Hesse Homburg. The Princess spent a lot of time decorating this cottage. It is decorated in a fancy style. The location is so nice that it doesn't need any extra decoration. Grove House belongs to Lady Dowager Onslow. The Princess bought the whole property from her. Mr. Bateman built Grove House. He is the uncle of the strange Lord Bateman. When Mr. Bateman traveled, he noticed things he liked in old monasteries. When he came back to England, he built this house based on those ideas. The bedrooms were made to look like the rooms where monks lived. It had a dining hall and everything else you would find in a monastery. It even had a cemetery and a coffin with the name of a fake ancient bishop written on it. Some interesting Gothic chairs were bought from a sale of items in this house. They are now at Strawberry.

MORE GENERAL PERUSAL. _Pimlico, Aug. 22, 1807._ _Bailey & Macdonald, Printers, 3, Harris's Place, Pantheon, Oxford-Street._ THE *First Book* OF THE METAMORPHOSES OF OVID. From bodies various form'd, mutative shapes My Muse would sing:--Celestial powers give aid! From you those changes sprung,--inspire my pen; Connect each period of my venturous song Unsever'd, from old Chaös' rude misrule, Till now the world beneath Augustus smiles. While yet nor earth nor sea their place possest, Nor that cerulean canopy which hangs O'ershadowing all, each undistinguish'd lay, And one dead form all Nature's features bore; Unshapely, rude, and Chaos justly nam'd. Together struggling laid, each element Confusion strange begat:--Sol had not yet Whirl'd through the blue expanse his burning car: Nor Luna yet had lighted forth her lamp, Nor fed her waning light with borrowed rays. No globous earth pois'd inly by its weight, Hung pendent in the circumambient sky: The sky was not:--Nor Amphitrité had Clasp'd round the land her wide-encircling arms. Unfirm the earth, with water mix'd and air; Opaque the air; unfluid were the waves. Together clash'd the elements confus'd: Cold strove with heat, and moisture drought oppos'd; Light, heavy, hard, and soft, in combat join'd. Uprose the world's great

### Plain English:
# The First Book of Ovid's Metamorphoses

More General Reading. _Pimlico, August 22, 1807._ _Bailey & Macdonald, Printers, 3, Harris's Place, Pantheon, Oxford Street._

My poem is about bodies that change into different forms. I want to sing about these changes. Gods, please help me! You caused these changes, so inspire my writing. Connect all the parts of my brave song together. Take me from the old chaos and disorder, all the way to now when the world is at peace under Augustus.

Before the earth and sea were in their places, and before the blue sky hung above everything, nothing had a clear shape. Everything was one dead, shapeless form. Chaos is the right name for it.

The elements were all mixed together and fighting. They caused strange confusion. The sun had not yet driven his burning chariot across the sky. The moon had not yet turned on her lamp or borrowed light from the sun. The earth was not a solid ball hanging in the sky. There was no sky yet. The sea goddess had not wrapped her arms around the land.

The earth was not firm. Water and air mixed together. The air was dark. The water was not flowing right. The elements all crashed into each other in confusion. Cold fought with heat. Water fought with dryness. Light, heavy, hard, and soft things all fought each other. The world began to rise up.

requires the additional caloric of a multitude, and a public debate; a third needs an antagonist, or a hot indignation; a fourth needs a revolution; and a fifth, nothing less than the grandeur of absolute ideas, the splendors and shades of Heaven and Hell. But because every man is an orator, how long soever he may have been a mute, an assembly of men is so much more susceptible. The eloquence of one stimulates all the rest, some up to the speaking point, and all others to a degree that makes them good receivers and conductors, and they avenge themselves for their enforced silence by increased loquacity on their return to the fireside. The plight of these phlegmatic brains is better than that of those who prematurely boil, and who impatiently break the silence before their time. Our county conventions often exhibit a small-pot-soon-hot style of eloquence. We are too much reminded of a medical experiment, where a series of patients are taking nitrous-oxide gas. Each patient, in turn, exhibits similar symptoms,--redness in the face, volubility, violent gesticulation, delirious attitudes, occasional stamping, an alarming loss of perception of the passage of time, a selfish enjoyment of his sensations, and loss

### Plain English:
Some people need lots of food to get fired up, and a public argument to talk. Another type needs someone to fight with, or to get really angry. Another needs a revolution. And another needs nothing less than big, important ideas—the good and bad sides of heaven and hell. But because every person can talk, no matter how quiet they usually are, a group of people gets excited easily. When one person talks well, it gets everyone else going. Some people start talking too, and everyone else gets ready to listen and pass along what they hear. Then when they go home, they make up for staying quiet by talking even more. People who stay calm are better off than people who get too excited too fast and start talking before they should. County meetings often have this problem. We see too many people acting like they're on nitrous oxide gas. Each person takes a turn and shows the same signs—a red face, talking a lot, big hand movements, wild behavior, sometimes stomping, losing track of time, only thinking about how good they feel, and losing awareness of other people.

I well remember the interest excited among the learned Hindus of Calcutta by the publication of the Sarva-darsana-samgraha of Mádhava Áchárya in the Bibliotheca Indica in 1858. It was originally edited by Pandit Ísvarachandra Vidyáságara, but a subsequent edition, with no important alterations, was published in 1872 by Pandit Táránátha Tarkaváchaspati. The work had been used by Wilson in his "Sketch of the Religious Sects of the Hindus" (first published in the Asiatic Researches, vol. xvi., Calcutta, 1828); but it does not appear to have been ever much known in India. MS. copies of it are very scarce; and those found in the North of India, as far as I have had an opportunity of examining them, seem to be all derived from one copy, brought originally from the South, and therefore written in the Telugu character. Certain mistakes are found in all alike, and probably arose from some illegible readings in the old Telugu original. I have noticed the same thing in the Nágarí copies of Mádhava's Commentary on the Black Yajur Veda, which are current in the North of India. As I was at that time the Oriental Secretary of the Bengal Asiatic Society, I was naturally attracted

### Plain English:
I clearly remember how excited the educated Hindus in Calcutta were when the Sarva-darsana-samgraha by Mádhava Áchárya was published in the Bibliotheca Indica in 1858. Pandit Ísvarachandra Vidyáságara first edited it, but then Pandit Táránátha Tarkaváchaspati published another edition in 1872 that didn't have any major changes. Wilson had used this work for his "Sketch of the Religious Sects of the Hindus" (first published in the Asiatic Researches, volume 16, Calcutta, 1828). But it doesn't seem like the book was ever really well-known in India. Hand-written copies of it are very hard to find. The ones I've seen in northern India all seem to come from one copy that originally came from the South. That's why they're written in Telugu characters. All these copies have the same mistakes in them. These mistakes probably happened because the old Telugu original was hard to read. I've noticed the same problem in the copies of Mádhava's Commentary on the Black Yajur Veda that are used in northern India. At that time, I was the Oriental Secretary of the Bengal Asiatic Society, so I was naturally drawn to this.

### Plain English:
She looked at the man on her right, then at the older man on her left. "You're both really kind, and I'm very grateful for everything you've done. But please stop arguing about this. It just hurts and makes us all tired. Please eat something, Mr. Trivett. And can I get you another cup of tea, Mr. Fenmarch?" Mr. Fenmarch on her left handed over his cup and sighed. He was a pale, grayish man with thin, short hair all over his face. His eyes were a dull blue and didn't show much expression. "As your lawyer, dear Olivia," he said, "I can only do what you tell me to do. But as a friend of your family, I'm going to give you some advice anyway." "Why the heck didn't your father set up a trust to control your money until you were twenty-five, at least?" said Mr. Trivett on her right. He was pouring himself whisky and soda. The table was covered with a green cloth and had papers and snacks all over it. "Why the heck——" he started again after taking a big gulp. "Yes, it's really bad luck," said Mr. Fenmarch, interrupting his friend. "And what are you going to do with yourself when you're all alone in the world?"

### Plain English:
Near the end of Louis XV's reign, he bought a job as major of the Gate Guards. He lived until 1813. He was lucky enough to avoid the dangers of the Revolution. Here's how it happened. Late in 1790, he went to Martinique, where his wife had business interests. He left a man named Mathias to take care of his property in Gascogne. Mathias was a notary's clerk who supported the new ideas coming from the Revolution. When the Comte came back, his property was still there and in good shape. This worked out because Mathias combined the practical Gascon style with the Norman style. Madame de Manerville died in 1810. The Comte had wasted money when he was young. Now, like many old men, he thought money was more important than it really is. He became stingy and miserly. He didn't realize that when parents are cheap with money, their kids end up spending it all. So he gave his son almost nothing. His son was his only child. Paul de Manerville came home from college in Vendome in 1810. He lived under strict rules.

Our Little Japanese Cousin

LOTUS BLOSSOM is the sweetest little girl in the world. Actually, I mean in the Eastern world, because she lives far away across the Pacific Ocean, on one of the beautiful islands of Japan. Lotus Blossom is very pretty. She has a round face with clear, yellow skin. Her teeth look like little pearls. Her black hair is cut straight across her forehead and braided in the back. No one curls it or uses a hot iron on it. Her mom would think that was messy. Lotus Blossom does not smile much, but she is always happy. She cannot remember ever crying in her whole life. Why would she cry? Her mom and dad are always kind and want to play with her. She is never made to go to bed alone in the dark.

Introduction

A few years ago, my job as a teacher kept me in a small town in the middle of a prairie province during the week. ### Plain English:
This play was registered with Congress in 1859 by Lester Wallack at the District Court for the Southern District of New York. It was published by Samuel French at 122 Nassau Street in New York City, upstairs.

Here are the characters and the actors who played them:

Manuel, Marquis de Champcey—played by Lester Wallack
Doctor Desmarets, formerly of the French Army—played by Brougham
M. de Bevannes, a man of the world—played by Walcot
Gaspar Laroque, an old man who used to be a ship captain—played by Dyott
Alain, a trusted servant—played by Young
M. Nouret, a Notary—played by Levere
Yvonnet, a Breton Shepherd—played by Baker
Henri—played by Oliver
Louis—played by Coburn
Madame Laroque, Gaspar's daughter-in-law—played by Mrs. Vernon
Marguerite, her daughter—played by Mrs. Hoey
Mlle Helouin, a Governess—played by an actress
Madame Aubrey, a relative of the Laroque family—played by Mary Gannon
Louise Vauberger, who used to be Manuel's nurse and now runs a boarding house—played by Mrs. Walcot
Christine, a Breton peasant girl—played by Fanny Reeves
There are also guests, servants, and peasants.

The play takes place in Paris during the first act, and then in the province of Brittany. The characters wear modern clothes. Robert Stoepel wrote and arranged the opening music, background music, and songs.

The play is called "A Poor Young Man."

Scene 1 shows a simple room with a table, chairs, an armchair, a desk, and a side table. There's a door in the middle. Madame Vauberger is peeking in.

At the time of Mr. Lincoln's death, I determined to write his history, as I had in my possession much valuable material for such a purpose. I did not then imagine that any person could have better or more extensive materials than I possessed. I soon learned, however, that Mr. William H. Herndon of Springfield, Ill., was similarly engaged. There could be no rivalry between us; for the supreme object of both was to make the real history and character of Mr. Lincoln as well known to the public as they were to us. He deplored, as I did, the many publications pretending to be biographies which came teeming from the press, so long as the public interest about Mr. Lincoln excited the hope of gain. Out of the mass of works which appeared, of one only--Dr. Holland's--is it possible to speak with any degree of respect. Early in 1869, Mr. Herndon placed at my disposal his remarkable collection of materials,--the richest, rarest, and fullest collection it was possible to conceive. Along with them came an offer of hearty co-operation, of which I have availed myself so extensively, that no art of mine would serve to conceal it. Added to

### Plain English:
When Mr. Lincoln died, I decided to write his life story. I had a lot of good information to do it. I thought I had the best materials anyone could have. But then I found out that William H. Herndon from Springfield, Illinois was working on the same thing. We didn't compete with each other. We both wanted the same goal—to tell people the true story of who Mr. Lincoln really was. We both hated all the fake biographies that kept coming out just because people wanted to make money off Lincoln's name. Most of these books were bad. Only Dr. Holland's book was worth reading. In early 1869, Herndon let me use his huge collection of materials. It was the best, rarest, and most complete collection anyone could have. He also offered to help me, and I used his help so much that I can't hide it.

I. "YOU CALLED ME, AND I CAME HOME TO YOUR HEART." Another revolution of the social wheel. Summer was over, and Twickenham, Richmond, Bath, and Tunbridge Wells were deserted for the new squares and narrow streets between Soho and Hyde Park Corner. The theatres in Drury Lane and Lincoln's Inn were open every night, the opera-house in the Haymarket was crowded, and drums and assemblies, concerts and quadrille-parties, filled the very air with excitement. 'Twas said the young people were younger than they used to be, and all the old had grown young. The new reign began in a blaze of gaiety; King and Queen, flushed with the sense of power, delighted to occupy the first place after having so long held the second rank; conscious, too, of a handsome exchequer, and a clever minister who could change stones into gold; at peace with other nations, and with leisure to enjoy themselves. The King had only one objection to London, and that extended to the whole of his British dominions. He would rather have been in Hanover. It needed all her Majesty's subtlety, all Lady Suffolk's subservient devotion, all Walpole's strenuous arguments, to keep him contented at St. James's or

### Plain English:
"You called me, and I came home to your heart." Life was changing again. Summer ended, and people left places like Twickenham, Richmond, Bath, and Tunbridge Wells. They moved to the fashionable new areas between Soho and Hyde Park Corner. The theatres on Drury Lane and Lincoln's Inn were full every night. The opera house in the Haymarket was packed. Parties, dances, concerts, and fancy ball events were happening everywhere. People said young people seemed even younger than before, and older people were acting young too. The new king and queen were having a great time. They had just taken power and loved being in charge for the first time. They were happy and excited. The country had money, a smart minister who could make anything happen, peace with other countries, and free time to enjoy life. But the King had one big problem with London. He didn't like being in Britain at all. He wanted to be in Hanover instead. The Queen had to use all her intelligence to keep him happy. Lady Suffolk had to be completely devoted to him. Even the minister Walpole had to argue hard just to keep the King at St. James's Palace or content.

### Original:
WOMAN WITH A STONE HEART INTRODUCTION To those whose love of adventure would cause them to plunge head-long into an abyss of vain glory, hoping at life's sunset to reap a harvest contrary to the seed that were sown, let me suggest that you pause first to read the story of "The Woman With a Stone Heart," Marie Sampalit, dare-devil of the Philippines. Perhaps we might profitably meditate for a few moments on the musings of Whittier: "The tissue of the life to be We weave in colors all our own, And in the field of destiny We'll reap as we have sown." --The Author. DEDICATION To Her, who, as a bride of only eighteen months, stood broken-hearted on the depot platform and bade me a tearful farewell as our train of soldier boys started to war; who later, while I was Ten Thousand miles away from home on soldier duty in the Philippine Islands, became a Mother; and who, unfortunately, three months thereafter, was called upon to lay our first-born, Oliver D. Coursey, into his snow-lined baby tomb amid the bleak silence of a cold winter's night, with no strong arm to bear her up in those awful hours

### Plain English:
WOMAN WITH A STONE HEART INTRODUCTION

If you love adventure and are thinking about chasing glory without caring about the consequences, stop and read this story first. It's about "The Woman With a Stone Heart," Marie Sampalit, a daredevil from the Philippines. Before you read, think about these words by Whittier: "The life we will live We create with our own choices, And what we get in the end Comes from what we put in." --The Author.

DEDICATION

This is for the woman who, just eighteen months after we got married, stood crying on the train platform as I left to go to war. While I was ten thousand miles away fighting in the Philippine Islands, she became a mother. Three months later, she had to bury our first baby, Oliver D. Coursey, in the cold ground during winter. She had to do this alone, with no one strong beside her to help her through those terrible hours.

I do not propose here to enter into a comparison of the Letters with those of Heloïse, as many writers have done, but shall content myself with referring the curious to the excellent work of Senhor Cordeiro, ‘Soror Marianna. A Freira Portugueza,’ Lisbon, 1888; 2nd edition, 1891. It is from him that I have learnt nearly all that I know about Marianna, and

In the stories are more than 75 historical characters, men, women, and children. The arrangement follows the school-year, beginning in October with Columbus. The book-cover is dressed in George Washington’s colours, scarlet and white. TREATMENT OF HISTORY FOR CHILDREN These tales are not packed full of dry facts and dates, boring to children. Instead, they treat history in a manner appealing to boys and girls. For it is the strong personalities that moved in the big events of the world, it is the forceful lives of the men themselves, their preparation in boyhood for successful careers, their struggles for right, their heroism, devotion, and high adventure, as well as the why and wherefore of things, which make history an intense reality to children and young folk. American history treated after such

### Plain English:
This book is dedicated to Frances Mary Jenkins Olcott on January 25. She was someone whose kindness made people smile. When she thought about others' suffering, her smile would fade and sadness would take its place. This quote is by William Cullen Bryant.

FOREWORD

This book has over 200 stories about 23 important birthdays of people who founded and built the countries of North and South America. The stories include more than 75 historical figures—men, women, and children. The stories are arranged by the school year, starting in October with Columbus. The cover is decorated in George Washington's colors: red and white.

HOW TO TEACH HISTORY TO CHILDREN

These stories don't just list boring facts and dates that kids hate. Instead, they tell history in a way that interests boys and girls. The strong personalities who did important things in history are what matter. It's the powerful lives of these people that make the difference. Their childhoods shaped who they became. Their fights for what was right, their bravery, their loyalty, and their adventures—plus understanding why things happened—all make history feel real and exciting to kids and young people. American history taught this way is much more interesting.

Published August, 1912 W. F. HAL PRINTING COMPANY, CHICAGO This Volume is Dedicated to NELLY KINZIE GORDON _PREFACE_ THERE were two Fort Dearborns, the first one having been built in 1803. This was occupied by a garrison of United States troops until 1812, when it was destroyed by the Indians immediately after the bloody massacre of that year. The second Fort Dearborn was built on the site of the former one in 1816, and continued in use as a military post, though at several intervals during periods of peaceful relations with the surrounding tribes the garrisons were withdrawn for a time. In 1836 the fort was finally evacuated by the military forces. The events narrated in the succeeding pages of this volume concern the first or Old Fort Dearborn. The name "Chicago," as descriptive of the river and its neighborhood, was in use for more than a century before the first Fort Dearborn was built; it appears on Franquelin's map printed in 1684 as "Chekagou," and is mentioned in various forms of spelling in the written and printed records of that and succeeding periods. It has been said that Chicago is the oldest Indian town in the West of which

### Plain English:
Published in August 1912 by W. F. Hal Printing Company in Chicago. This book is dedicated to Nelly Kinzie Gordon.

PREFACE

There were two Fort Dearborns. The first one was built in 1803. American soldiers lived there until 1812, when Native Americans destroyed it right after a terrible massacre that year. The second Fort Dearborn was built in the same place in 1816. It was used as a military post. Sometimes, when there was peace with the nearby tribes, the soldiers would leave for a while. In 1836, the military finally left the fort for good. This book tells stories about the first Fort Dearborn, the old one. 

The name "Chicago" was used to describe the river and the area around it for more than one hundred years before the first Fort Dearborn was built. The name appears on a map printed by Franquelin in 1684 as "Chekagou." It also shows up in different spellings in written and printed records from that time and later. Some people have said that Chicago is the oldest Native American town in the Western part of the country that we know about.

### Plain English:
Captain Nicholas Joel was standing on the bridge of his ship. He looked out through the thick windows and saw them coming. A jeep was bouncing across the yellow sand of the desert. It was heading straight for the ship that had brought them here three weeks ago. The two men inside were driving fast. They were kicking up clouds of dust behind them. Captain Joel could see how excited they were. He didn't need to use his communicator to know what they would say. "We found it again!" they would tell him. He turned away from the windows. He started to figure out how long it would take them to get there.

Nobody knew what would happen until they heard the ship made it safely to Jupiter Inlet. Then near Santiago, after waiting for a month, Cervera's squadron came out. This great ship, already burning hot and ready to go, shot forward ahead of all the other ships in the fleet like a dog let off its leash. It joined the famous ships in American naval history—the Bon Homme Richard, the Constitution, and the Hartford. From the start at the Golden Gate all the way to when the Colon was beached, there was one exciting event after another. Official fighting and jealousy couldn't change or hide how important these events were. The story has been told by officers, in court, and by government officials. goodly literature of disappointment. All the ungifted young people came back from the war to tell us that they were "fed up." That was their ailment, in outline. The gifted ones are now coming down to detail. They say that a web has been woven over the sky, or that something or other has made a goblin of the sun--about as full details of a pain as you can fairly expect a gifted person to give, although he really may feel it. No doubt disenchantment has flourished before. About the year 1880 nearly all the best art was wan and querulous; that of Burne-Jones was always in trouble; Matthew Arnold's verse was a well-bred, melodious whine; Rossetti was all disenamourment and displacement. Yet you could feel that their broken-toy view of the world was only their nice little way with the public. Burne-Jones in his home was a red, jovial man; Arnold a diner-out of the first lustre; Rossetti a sworn friend to bacon and eggs and other plain pleasures. The young melancholiasts of to-day are less good at their craft, and yet they do give you a notion that some sort of silver cord really seems to them to

### Plain English:
People are writing a lot of sad stories these days. Young people who didn't have special talents came back from the war saying they were "fed up." That was basically their problem. The talented young people are now explaining it more. They say something bad has covered the sky, or the sun has turned into something evil. That's about as much detail as you can expect a talented person to give about their pain, even if they really do feel it. People have been disappointed before. Around 1880, most of the best art was sad and complaining. Burne-Jones's work was always troubled. Matthew Arnold's poems were like a well-mannered, musical complaint. Rossetti was all about being disappointed in love and feeling out of place. But you could tell that their sad view of the world was just how they acted in public. At home, Burne-Jones was a cheerful, red-faced man. Arnold loved going out to dinner. Rossetti loved eating bacon and eggs and other simple foods. Today's sad young writers are not as good at what they do, and yet they do make you believe that something really important actually seems broken to them.

Lyrics and Labels: words that go with the notes start with [W: ...]. Breves and macrons, which show short and long stresses in poetry, are shown as ['] and [-].

The 3.20 train from Boston slowed up as it drew into a way station, and Malcolm McCann, grim and sullen from his weary ride in the dirt and cinders, the coal-smoke and the foetid air, the fretting babies and hot, worrying men, that characterise a railway journey in August, hurried out with a grunt of relief. It was not a pretty station where he found himself, and he glared ill-naturedly around with restless, aggressive eyes. The brick walls, the cheaply grained doors bearing their tarnished legends, "Gents," "Ladies," "Refreshment Saloon," the rough raftered roof over the tracks,--everything was black and grimy with years of smoke, belching even now from the big locomotive, and gathering like an ill-conditioned thunder-cloud over the mob of scurrying, pushing men and women, a mob that swelled and scattered constantly in fretful confusion. A hustling business-man with a fat, pink face and long sandy whiskers, his silk hat cocked on one side in grotesque assumption of jauntiness, tripped over the clay-covered pick of a surly labourer, red of face and sweaty, blue of overalls and mud-coloured of shirt, and as he

### Plain English:
# THE DECADENT

The 3:20 train from Boston slowed down as it pulled into a small station. Malcolm McCann got off quickly. He was tired and annoyed from his trip. The train car had been dirty and hot. Babies had been crying the whole time. Men had been sweating and complaining. It was August, so everything felt worse.

The station was not nice. Malcolm looked around angrily. He had a restless, aggressive look in his eyes. The brick walls were black and dirty from years of smoke. The doors said "Gents," "Ladies," and "Refreshment Saloon," but they looked cheap and worn out. The roof over the tracks was made of rough wood. Black smoke was still pouring out of the big locomotive. The smoke hung over the crowd of people like a dark cloud. Men and women were pushing and shoving each other. People kept moving in and out of the station in confused groups.

A businessman with a fat, pink face and long sandy whiskers walked by. His silk hat was pushed to one side in a silly way. He tripped over a pick-axe that belonged to a tired worker. The worker had a red face, was sweating, and wore blue overalls and a dirty brown shirt.

these went a river, slow and almost noiseless, wandering in the valley as if there were no necessity of arriving anywhere at appointed times. “What is the necessity?” it said softly to any that would listen. And there was none; so that for many days the white tent stood among the trees, overlooking the haycocks in the meadows. It was enough business in hand to study the philosophy and the subtle rhetoric of Still River. Opposite rose a strangely ruined mountain-side. There was a nobly-poised head and plenteous chest, the head three thousand feet nearer the stars--which was little enough from their point of view, no doubt, but to us it seemed a symbol of something higher than the stars, something beyond them forever waiting and watching. From its feet upward half a mile the mountain was one raw wound. The shivered roots and tree-trunks stuck out helplessly from reddish soil, boulders were crushed and piled in angry heaps, veins of granite ripped open--the skin and flesh of the mountain tom off with a curse, and the bones made a mockery. ### Plain English:
There was a river that ran slowly and quietly through the valley. It moved like it had no reason to hurry or get anywhere on time. "Why rush?" it seemed to ask anyone listening. And the answer was: there wasn't a reason. So for many days, a white tent sat among the trees, looking out over the haystacks in the fields below. There was plenty to do just studying how Still River worked and understanding its quiet ways. On the other side, a strange broken mountain rose up. It had a proud peak and a wide base. The peak was three thousand feet closer to the stars—which might not sound like much to the stars, but to us it meant something much greater than they were, something that would outlast them forever. Halfway up the mountain from its base, everything was torn apart and raw. Broken tree roots and trunks stuck out from reddish dirt. Rocks were smashed and piled in angry mounds. Granite was split open. The skin and flesh of the mountain were ripped off, and the bones inside were exposed like a cruel joke. Above all this destruction, a tall cliff wall rose up. Beyond that, hazy forests stretched upward for a mile.

This wonderful enterprise played an important part in history, and demonstrated what American spirit can accomplish. I have sought to bring to light and make accessible to all readers the more important facts of the Pony Express--its inception, organization and development, its importance to history, its historical background, and some of the anecdotes incidental to its operation.
