### Plain English:
PONY EXPRESS *** Made by David A. Schwan. HTML version by Al Haines. The Story of the Pony Express A true account of the best mail service that ever existed, and how it mattered in history. By Glenn D. Bradley Author of Winning the Southwest To My Parents Preface This book has one goal. It tells the true, useful, and easy-to-read story of the Pony Express. This amazing business was important to history. It showed what Americans could do. It proved that the "heroes of sixty-one" were not all fighting each other south of the Mason-Dixon line. It's strange, but not much has been written about it in an official way. I want to find the most important facts about the Pony Express and share them with everyone. I cover how it started, how it was run and grew, why it mattered to history, what happened before it, and some interesting stories from when it worked. The subject has a lot of cool information. It's all interesting, but some of it doesn't really matter. By itself this information is broken up and doesn't fit together well. It would be easy to write many pages about western adventures that don't really have anything to do with it.

I was sorry to lose the beautiful lake view, but I found it necessary to remove to the northern quarter of the city, into a very quiet Street behind the mouldering castle. My new home is a katchiu-yashiki, the ancient residence of some samurai of high rank. It is shut off from the street, or rather roadway, skirting the castle moat by a long, high wall coped with tiles. One ascends to the gateway, which is almost as large as that of a temple court, by a low broad flight of stone steps; and projecting from the wall, to the right of the gate, is a look-out window, heavily barred, like a big wooden cage. Thence, in feudal days, armed retainers kept keen watch on all who passed by--invisible watch, for the bars are set so closely that a face behind them cannot be seen from the roadway. ### Plain English:
The rooms were barely taller than cabins on a ship. They were so narrow that you couldn't hang a regular mosquito net in them. I was sad to leave the beautiful lake view. But I had to move to the north part of the city. It was on a quiet street behind an old, falling-apart castle. My new home is a katchiu-yashiki. It's an ancient house that used to belong to a samurai of high rank. A long, high wall with tiles on top separates it from the street, or the road, that runs along the castle moat. You climb up to the gate by walking up a low, wide set of stone steps. The gate is almost as big as the entrance to a temple. Sticking out from the wall on the right side of the gate is a watch window. It has heavy bars on it, like a big wooden cage. Long ago, armed guards would stand there and watch everyone who passed by. No one could see them though. The bars were so close together that you couldn't see a face behind them from the road. Once you go through the gate, the path to the house is also walled in on both sides. So unless you were allowed to be there, you could only see the house entrance ahead of you. And it was always closed.

Produced by David Widger OUR SQUARE AND THE PEOPLE IN IT By Samuel Hopkins Adams Illustrated by Scott Williams Boston and New York Houghton Mifflin Company 1917 _WALLED in by slums stands Our Square, a valiant green space, far on the flank of the Great City. Ours is an inglorious little world Sociologists have-not yet remarked and classified us. The Washington Square romancers who bold sentimental revel at the foot of Fifth Avenue reck nothing of their sister park, many blocks to the east. But we are patient of our obscurity. Close-knit, keeping our own counsel, jealous of our own concerns, and not without our own pride of place, we live our quiet lives, a community sufficient unto itself. So far as may be for mortals under the sway of death and love and fate, we maintain ourselves with little change amid the kaleidoscopic shiftings of the surrounding metropolis. Few come into Our Square except of necessity. Few go out but under the same stem impulsion. Some of us are held by tradition, some by poverty, some by affection, and some through loyalty to what once was and is no more. Here we live, and here hope to die, "the

### Plain English:
# Our Square and the People in It

Our Square is surrounded by poor neighborhoods. It's a green space that's far from the center of the Great City. Our neighborhood is not very famous. Sociologists haven't studied us yet or put us in any category. People who go to Washington Square, which is at the bottom of Fifth Avenue, don't know about our park. It's many blocks to the east of theirs. But we don't mind being unknown. We stick together. We keep our business to ourselves. We care about our own problems. And we're proud of our neighborhood. We live our quiet lives. We're a community that takes care of itself. As much as people can when they have to deal with death, love, and fate, we stay the same. The city around us keeps changing, but we don't change much. Not many people come to Our Square unless they have to. Not many people leave unless they have to. Some of us stay because of tradition. Some stay because we're poor. Some stay because we love the people here. Some stay because we're loyal to what this place used to be. This is where we live. And this is where we hope to die.

### Plain English:
"This is the famous Bois de Boulogne, Sam."

"The what?"

"Bois de Boulogne. It's one of the most popular places to drive in Paris."

"Huh!" grunted Sam Hickey. "That sounds like some kind of sausage to me. Why do they name their streets like that in Paris?"

"All the names in this great, fun city mean something," answered Dan Davis. "This park has the same name. Back in the fourteen hundreds, it was full of dangerous robbers."

"Robbers!" said the red-haired boy. "Really?"

"Yes."

"Are they still here?"

"No. Napoleon got rid of them. We'll be at the Arch soon. The French call it Arc de Triomphe."

"They do?"

"Yes."

"Just like that?"

"Of course."

"I bet there isn't a French person in all of France who would know what you were talking about if you said that name. I don't know French, but if that's what French sounds like, give me plain American English. Are you sure there are no robbers left in the sausage place?"

"Bois de Boulogne, Sam," said Dan, correcting him. "No. There are no robbers here. You don't need to be scared."

"Scared! What do you think I am, Dan?"

Chapter I
The Germans Are Coming!

The Germans had already entered Brussels. Their soldiers were spotted near Ghent. A little bit farther away, past the windmills on the horizon, and we could run into them at any time. For more than two weeks we had been moving east fast. We heard through cables and radio about that huge gray army moving down toward France. The first news had come from four thousand miles away.

### Original:
system of mental philosophy._ In a previous number of this Journal (see Art. I, page 161), we entered somewhat at length upon the discussion of the above proposition. We there contrasted the merits of phrenology, as the _true science of the mind_, with other systems of mental philosophy, particularly Dugald Stewart’s. We pointed out several important defects which have hitherto existed in every system of mental science. We stated that all writers on the philosophy of the mind had committed the following mistakes. First, _they leave out of view almost entirely all connection of the mind with the brain_; and, secondly, _they make their own individual consciousness the chief and principal source of information_. And that, from these two radical defects in their premises, they have fallen into numerous errors. Among others, _they do not recognise all the primitive faculties of the mind_; and throughout all their writings, _they confound primitive faculties of the mind with modes of activity_. Another radical defect in the systems of previous writers on mental science is, _When they admit and treat of the elementary faculties of the mind, they consider them merely as existing by themselves, and disregard almost entirely the influence of combination_.

### Plain English:
We talked about this idea before in this journal. We compared phrenology, which we said is the real science of the mind, with other ways of thinking about the mind. We especially compared it with Dugald Stewart's ideas. We pointed out some big problems that have been in every system of mental science. We said that all the writers about how the mind works made these mistakes. First, they almost completely ignored how the mind connects to the brain. Second, they only looked at their own personal thoughts and feelings to figure out how the mind works. Because they started with these two big mistakes, they made lots of errors. For example, they didn't recognize all the basic abilities of the mind. And in everything they wrote, they mixed up basic abilities of the mind with the different ways the mind can act. Another big problem with what other writers said about mental science is this: When they do talk about the basic parts of the mind, they only look at each part by itself. They almost completely ignore what happens when these parts work together.

Among the many wonderful achievements of the present century there is none more wonderful than the recovery and decipherment of the monuments of ancient Nineveh. For generations the great oppressing city had slept buried beneath the fragments of its own ruins, its history lost, its very site forgotten. Its name had passed into the region of myth even in the age of the classical writers of Greece and Rome; Ninos or Nineveh had become a hero-king about whom strange legends were told, and whose conquests were fabled to have extended from the Mediterranean to India.

One of the most amazing things that happened in this century is finding and understanding the old monuments from the ancient city of Nineveh. For a very long time, this powerful city was buried under its own broken ruins. Nobody knew its history. People even forgot where it was. By the time of the ancient Greek and Roman writers, the city was just a myth. A king named Ninos or Nineveh had become a legend. Stories said he was incredibly powerful. People claimed he conquered everything from the Mediterranean Sea all the way to India.

Whether he has sustained a loss or an addition to his family, whether he wants you to dine with him at the club or to lend him ten pounds, his handwriting at least will be the same, unless, indeed, he be offended, when he will generally indite your name with a studious precision and a distant grace quite foreign to his ordinary caligraphy. ### Plain English:
A person might run after you on the street, and you can tell right away if they have good news, bad news, or nothing important. But when someone writes you a letter and leaves it on the breakfast table, you can't tell what it's about just by looking at the handwriting. It doesn't matter if he's lost someone or gained someone in his family. It doesn't matter if he wants you to have dinner with him at his club or if he wants to borrow ten pounds from you. His handwriting will always look the same. The only time it changes is if he's mad at you. Then he'll write your name very carefully and formally, in a way that's nothing like his normal writing. I know these thoughts are pretty obvious, but they're the kind of thing you have to think about when you're starting a story in the modern way—starting as late as possible. That moment came when a waiter brought me a letter from Catherine Evers that would change everything. Apart from what happened because of it, the letter was interesting in an unusual way. It was the first letter I'd gotten from her in years, even though we used to write all the time. So I sat there looking at the envelope, really curious about it. What could someone I hadn't heard from in so long possibly want?

This, however, was not the case in this instance: the Counts von Steinrück carefully preserved the cradle of their race from decay, although otherwise they cared very little for the old pile, secluded as it was from the world in the depths of the mountains. ### Plain English:
The winds woke up the earth. They roared over fields and plains. They moved their wings around the tall mountain peaks. They stirred up the sea from the bottom. The air was full of wild fighting and chaos. But from all this came a sound of victory. These were spring winds. They brought life with them. They announced a new beginning. The mountains still had snow on them. An old castle sat on top of the mountains. It looked down at the valley below. Pine trees around it were covered in snow. This was one of those gray stone castles that used to scare people in the area. Most of them are now empty and forgotten. Nothing is left but ruins that show they were once great. But this castle was different. The Counts von Steinrück took good care of their family's castle. They kept it from falling apart. Still, they didn't really care much about the old building. It was hidden away in the mountains, far from everything. Life only came to the castle during hunting season. That's when guests usually showed up. Then the old walls would echo with noise and activity. This year was different though. Guests did come, but...

Antone, I've told you many times, no, you can't sell it until I'm dead." "But I need money. What good is that old fiddle to you? Even the crows laugh at you when you try to play it. Five years ago, Peter Sadelack had come here with his wife, his oldest son Antone, and a bunch of smaller kids. They came to the most depressing part.

The older men, indeed, were during much of their careers our fellow countrymen in the colonial period, and fought, some side by side with our own people in this new world, others in distant scenes of the widespread strife that characterized the middle of the eighteenth century, the beginnings of "world politics;" when, in a quarrel purely European in its origin, "black men," to use Macaulay's words, "fought on the coast of Coromandel, and red men scalped each other by the great lakes of North America." All, without exception, were actors in the prolonged conflict that began in 1739 concerning the right of the ships of Great Britain and her colonies to frequent the seas bordering the American dominions of Spain; a conflict which, by gradual expansion, drew in the continent of Europe, from Russia to France, spread thence to the French possessions in India and North America, involved Spanish Havana in the western hemisphere and Manila in the eastern, and finally entailed the expulsion of France from our continent. Thence, by inevitable sequence, issued the independence of the United States. The contest, thus completed, covered forty-three years. The four seniors of our series, Hawke, Rodney, Howe, and Jervis, witnessed

### Plain English:
The older men were our countrymen during colonial times. Some fought next to our people in this new world. Others fought in distant places during the middle of the 1700s, when world politics was just beginning. In wars that started in Europe, "black men" fought on the coast of Coromandel, and "red men" scalped each other by the great lakes of North America. All of them fought in a long war that started in 1739. It was about whether British ships and colonial ships could sail in the seas near Spain's territory in America. This conflict grew bigger and bigger. It pulled in countries from Russia to France. It spread to French territories in India and North America. It involved Havana in Cuba and Manila in the Philippines. In the end, France was forced out of America. Because of this, the United States became independent. The whole contest lasted forty-three years. Four senior men in our group—Hawke, Rodney, Howe, and Jervis—saw all of this happen.

THE BELGIAN ARMY'S ACTIVITIES SINCE THE BATTLE OF THE YSER. Everyone knows how severely the Belgian Army was tested in the initial stages of the campaign. Caught unawares by the war while in the midst of re-organisation, it had to struggle alone, for long weeks on end, against forces greatly superior in both numbers and equipment, suddenly hurled against it in accordance with a deliberate and carefully planned scheme of attack. Yet the Belgian Army bravely faced the enemy, grimly determined to fulfil its duty to the last, and at once aroused enthusiasm by its heroic resistance at Liège, from August 8 onwards, to the onset of several army corps. On the 12th the troops emerged victoriously from the bloody engagements at Haelen; and not till the 18th, and then only to escape being overwhelmed by the ever-rising flood of invasion, did the Belgian Army abandon its positions at La Gette and fall back on Antwerp, the national stronghold in which would be concentrated the whole of the country's powers of opposition. Its retreat was covered by rearguards which

### Plain English:
The pictures come from photos taken by the Belgian Army's photography team. THE BELGIAN FRONT AND WHAT MADE IT SPECIAL. WHAT THE BELGIAN ARMY DID AFTER THE BATTLE OF THE YSER. Everyone knows the Belgian Army had a really hard time at the start of the war. The war caught them by surprise while they were reorganizing. They had to fight alone for weeks against much larger enemy forces with better weapons. The enemy attacked them with a plan they had prepared ahead of time. But the Belgian Army fought back bravely. They were determined to do their duty no matter what. They got people excited by fighting hard at Liège starting on August 8. Several enemy groups attacked them there. On the 12th, the troops won the tough fighting at Haelen. But by the 18th, there were too many enemy soldiers coming in. The Belgian Army had to leave their positions at La Gette and go back to Antwerp. Antwerp was the country's main stronghold. That's where they would put all their fighting power. When they moved back, soldiers stayed behind to protect them while they left.

They've done this in Britain, the Colonies, and America. They tried to turn my readers against me on purpose. So my publishers think it's a good idea to show a real picture of what I actually look like now. This will show people the truth about me.

Sunlight suddenly appeared and then disappeared behind the tall buildings and chimneys of the city. The city looked like a dream that wasn't real. Stephen Culpeper stopped in the middle of the road and looked back at the hazy streets. In the distance, the sky was turning purple and silver as it got darker. It seemed to him that the city had created a new and strange force in the last few months. This force was made up of new ideas. It had taken over the Square and changed everything the Square meant to him. Even though he was only twenty-six years old, he felt like he had watched a hundred years go by. Nothing from the past was the same anymore. Not the old buildings. Not the old trees. Not even his old memories. Old traditions had disappeared because of bright electric lights. The brick streets that used to look nice in the sun were being covered with ugly concrete. Everything was different from when his father and grandfather lived here. Everything seemed cheap and ugly now. It looked naked and plain. But these new ideas kept spreading. Democracy was strong, messy, and all over the place.

### Plain English:
# FOR WHAT HE DID FOR THE WORLD

He did great things for the world. Even greater is how he calmly waits to do more. He pushed for education. He made justice faster. He freed people from slavery. And he always spoke against war. This is the rarest quality in a politician.

These pages are written for him.

Written by his faithful servant, January 30, 1835. Leigh Hunt.

# ADVERTISEMENT

This poem came from a sense of duty. It pulled the author away from his normal, quieter work during a major crisis. He was happy to write it as a poem. But it caused him more pain than he wants to talk about. Still, he did what he felt he had to do. If every person who cares about humanity expressed their feelings this way, as best they could, then all these individual opinions would add up to something powerful. It would speed up the day when reason and kindness win.

### Plain English:
We are citizens from Missouri, mostly from the part of the state next to Kansas. We want to tell you some things about a problem that really matters to our state. We think it could also affect the whole country in a big way. We will do this by explaining the facts clearly and honestly. We will talk about how things are where we live and how it connects to Kansas. Then we will share what we think about these facts.

The part of Missouri that borders Kansas has about fifty thousand slaves. We think they are worth about twenty-five million dollars based on prices here. Missouri has about one hundred thousand slaves total. So half of all the slaves in Missouri live in the eighteen counties next to Kansas. Most of these counties don't have any natural boundary between them and Kansas. You can ride there in a day from the border.

This part of our state has really good soil. The weather is mild and healthy. The people here are growing and doing well in many ways.

PREFACE Jean Henri Fabre, author of the long series of "Souvenirs Entomologiques" from which these studies are taken, was a French school-teacher and scientist whose peculiar gift for the observation and description of insect life won for him the title of the "insects' Homer." A distinguished English critic says of him, "Fabre is the wisest man, and the best read in the book of nature, of whom the centuries have left us any record." The fact that he was mainly self-taught, and that his life was an unending struggle with poverty and disappointment, increases our admiration for his wonderful achievements in natural science. A very interesting account of his early years, given by himself, will be found in Chapter XVII of this volume. The salaries of rural teachers and professors were extremely small in France during the last century, and Fabre, who married young, could barely support his large family. Nature study was not in the school curriculum,

PREFACE

Jean Henri Fabre was a French teacher and scientist who wrote a long series of books called "Souvenirs Entomologiques." These studies come from those books. Fabre was really good at watching insects and writing about them. People gave him the nickname "the insects' Homer" because of this. A well-known English critic said about him, "Fabre is the smartest person we know about who has studied nature." What makes his success even more amazing is that he mostly taught himself. His life was hard. He struggled with money problems and disappointment, but he still did great work in science. He wrote about his early life himself. You can read about it in Chapter XVII of this book. In France during the last century, teachers and professors didn't make much money. Fabre got married young and had a big family. It was hard to support them all. Schools didn't teach nature study back then.

idea of the profound and simple-hearted emotion of this populace, which has enough poetry in its soul to believe in its own happiness. The whole town adorns herself and attires herself like a bride for her wedding; the dark facades of marble and granite disappear beneath hangings of silk and festoons of flowers; the wealthy display their dazzling luxury, the poor drape themselves proudly in their rags. Everything is light, harmony, and perfume; the sound is like the hum of an immense hive, interrupted by a thousandfold outcry of joy impossible to describe. The bells repeat their sonorous sequences in every key; the arcades echo afar with the triumphal marches of military bands; the sellers of sherbet and water-melons sing out their deafening flourish from throats of copper. People form into groups; they meet, question, gesticulate; there are gleaming looks, eloquent gestures, picturesque attitudes; there is a general animation, an unknown charm, an indefinable intoxication. Earth is very near to heaven, and it is easy to understand that, if God were to banish death from this delightful spot, the Neapolitans would desire no other paradise. The story that we are about to tell opens with one of these magical pictures.

### Plain English:
The people here have simple, deep feelings. They believe in their own happiness so much that it's like poetry. The whole town gets dressed up like a bride on her wedding day. The dark marble and granite buildings disappear under silk decorations and flowers. Rich people show off their fancy things. Poor people wear their old clothes with pride. Everything is bright, balanced, and smells good. The sound is like a giant beehive. There are thousands of joyful shouts you can't even describe. Bells ring out loud songs in different pitches. The covered walkways echo with military bands playing victory songs. People selling drinks and watermelons shout loudly to sell their stuff. People gather in groups. They meet, ask questions, move their hands around. There are bright smiles and expressive movements. Everything feels alive. It's exciting and magical in a way you can't quite explain. It feels like heaven is right here on Earth. You can understand why Neapolitans would never want anything else if God took away death from this wonderful place. The story we're about to tell starts with one of these amazing moments.

We will, however, treat more especially of the last-named in connection with the first. The true proof-reader should not only be a practical printer, but he should be a lover of literature, familiar with the classics of all languages, with the results accomplished by science, and indeed with every subject that concerns his fellow-men. When an author prepares a work for the press, he often uses many abbreviations, his capitalization is frequently incorrect, his spelling occasionally not in accordance either with Worcester or Webster, his punctuation inaccurate, his historical and biographical statements careless, and his chirography frequently very bad. In such cases the proof-reader is sorely tried; and unless he is a man of much patience, well versed in

### Plain English:
In 1922, John Wilson wrote this paper. He used to own The University Press and died in 1903. His successors are now reprinting it. They think the subject is just as interesting today as it was twenty years ago.

THE IMPORTANCE OF THE PROOF-READER

When you get a book ready to print, three people are involved: the author, the person who sets the type, and the proof-reader. We're going to focus mainly on the proof-reader and the author. A real proof-reader should be more than just someone who works in printing. He should love reading. He should know famous books from all languages. He should understand what science has discovered. He should basically know about everything that matters to people.

When an author writes something to be printed, he often makes mistakes. He uses a lot of abbreviations. His capital letters are often wrong. His spelling doesn't always match standard dictionaries. His punctuation is messy. His facts about history and people are careless. His handwriting can be really hard to read. In these situations, the proof-reader has a tough job. Unless he is very patient and knows a lot about many different subjects, the work won't turn out right.

This book is about how the United States military became integrated between 1940 and 1965. It focuses on Black soldiers and race relations in America.

The book was written by Morris J. MacGregor. Several military leaders and historians helped advise on the project. They included people from the Army, Navy, Marine Corps, and other government offices.

The people who helped with this project worked at different places. Some worked at universities like the University of Kentucky and Ohio State University. Others worked at military schools like the Army War College. Some worked at the Library of Congress and National Archives.

The book includes a list of sources and an index to help readers find information. It was published by the Department of the Army.

In the meantime several of his works had been issued, the first being “A Man from the North” (1898) and a handbook, “Journalism for Women,” followed in the next year by the publication of a volume of plays, “Polite Farces,” his first experiments in drama. Afterwards appeared in rapid succession nine other novels, two volumes of short stories, seven volumes of belles-lettres, and seven fantasias. Besides these he wrote two plays, “Cupid and Common-Sense,” produced by the Stage Society in 1908, and “What the Public Wants,” also produced by the Stage Society in 1909, and afterwards by Mr. Hawtrey at the New Royalty Theatre. Both these plays were subsequently staged in Glasgow, and

The men in their blue blouses were mostly lean, dark, and taciturn; the women, small, black-eyed, and vivacious, with bright-colored petticoats, long earrings, and the quaintest of round white caps. The silvery whiteness of the lake, flashing back an answer to the sunlight, gave a peculiarly joyous radiance to the scene. ### Original:
Adults may draw from a book what of good there is in it, and close it before reaching the chapter usually devoted to fine writing. But the case of Haydn is extraordinary. One can only sustain interest in a biography of the man by an ever-present sense that he is scarcely to be written about. All an author can do is, in few or many words, to put a conundrum to the reader--a conundrum that cannot even be stated in exciting terms. This apparition and wonder-worker of the eighteenth century, Franz Joseph Haydn, is compact of paradoxes and contradictions. Born a peasant, and remaining in thought and speech a peasant all his days, he became the friend of princes, dukes, and, generally speaking, very high society indeed--and this in days when class distinctions had to be observed. He effected a revolution in music, and revolutionists must have daring; and save in music he showed no sign of unusual daring. His shaping and handling of new forms called for high intellect, and he displayed no intellect whatever in any other way--nothing beyond a canny, cunning shrewdness. Until he was sixty his life was a plodding one of dull regularity and routine;

### Plain English:
Grown-ups can read the good parts of a book and stop before it gets to the chapters full of fancy writing. But Haydn is different. You can only stay interested in his biography if you remember that he's almost impossible to write about. All a writer can do is ask the reader a puzzle—a puzzle that's hard to even explain in an exciting way. Franz Joseph Haydn was an amazing musician in the 1700s, but he was full of contradictions. He was born poor and stayed simple and plain his whole life. But he became friends with princes and rich, powerful people—back when rich and poor people didn't mix. He changed music completely. People who change things are usually brave. But Haydn wasn't brave about anything except music. He invented new kinds of music and was very smart about it. But in everything else, he wasn't smart at all—just practical and shrewd. Until he was sixty, his life was boring and the same day after day.

I always have, since I was young. But then the war in the East happened two years ago and shocked everyone like lightning. What a time that was! The real stories in the newspapers were so exciting that my old made-up stories seemed totally fake. The present was so sad and terrible and awful. It made the past seem boring and weak! Nobody read history books or novels or poetry back then. Everyone just read the scary newspapers. And now it's all over. We have peace.

DOROTHY'S DOUBLE

PROLOGUE

It was a dark night by the Thames River. The wind was blowing hard from the southwest and had sleet in it. The wind made small waves that hit the side of a boat tied to the bank. A rope held the boat in place around a willow tree that had lost most of its leaves. The tree's branches moved back and forth in the wind, and sometimes they dropped heavy raindrops on a man standing against the tree trunk. Beyond the tree was a large open lawn with groups of bushes. Behind that was the dark shape of a big house, but it was so dark you could barely see it. The only thing that showed it was there were lights in the upper windows. The man kept moving around because water was running down his neck and soaking his clothes. He muttered in an annoyed way.

### Plain English:
Norwood said calmly, "I can only do my best. But your friend has disappeared, sir. You're alone here."

The other man replied, "Maybe that's why you dared to insult me. That must be the French way of doing things."

An old cavalry officer who was helping Guilmard stepped in. "Come on, gentlemen, you both need to understand that this kind of talking isn't right or proper. We came here this morning for one reason. We want to get paid back for a serious injury. The man who should have apologized to us just ran away. I'm not saying anything about the fact that he only brought one friend with him. Maybe we could have fixed that problem. But we can't do anything about him being gone. So what can you offer us now?"

"Just give us a little time. That's all. My friend must have gotten lost. Something must have stopped him, and I expect him to show up any second."

"Should we wait another half hour, my Lord?" the other man asked. He pulled out his watch. "That will make it eight o'clock."

# Introduction

The writer wants to show people the best parts of Welsh scenery. He believes everyone who wants to see beautiful Welsh landscapes should have the chance to visit the most interesting places on a tour that takes about a month. This book describes a route that has amazing views. You probably won't find better scenery anywhere else in the world. Yes, there are higher mountains than the ones in Snowdonia, and bigger valleys somewhere else. But when you just hear about how huge those places are, you're amazed. However, when you see the beauty of Welsh scenery, you feel something even stronger.

But even more awful than the blackness was the loneliness of the six men who inhabited the silver rocket. They moved in loneliness as fish move in water. Their lives revolved in loneliness as planets revolve in space and time. They bore their loneliness like a shroud, and it was as much a part of them as sight in their eyes. They stared at the image in the _Wanderer's_ forward visi-screen, at the great, shining gray ball. The specimen was removed from the marl immediately below the fossil leaf zone (see Chaney and Elias, 1936; Frye and Hibbard, 1940). _Diagnosis._--Size large (see measurements) and differing from other species of _Chrysemys_ in having: The anterior end of the carapace broadly concave, the posterolateral marginals not greatly flared, the posterior end of the plastron broadly indented, the carapace more sculptured and relatively wider. _Description of type._--The specimen had been badly damaged before preservation, and had suffered further damage from exposure before discovery. ### Plain English:
Scientists found a new turtle species from the genus _Chrysemys_. Here is a description of it.

Chrysemys limnodytes is a new species. The main specimen is stored at the University of Kansas Museum of Natural History under number 7676. It is in the paleontology collection. The specimen includes a broken front part of the shell, the left side of the belly plate, and several edge pieces. Scientists from the University of Kansas Museum of Natural History collected it in 1946.

The fossil was found in marl beds from the Laverne formation. It dates back to the early Pliocene time period. The location was in southwest Oklahoma, in Beaver County. The specimen came from marl that was right below where fossil leaves were found.

This new species is large. It is different from other _Chrysemys_ species in several ways. The front of the shell is broadly curved inward. The back side edge pieces are not greatly spread outward. The back of the belly plate is broadly notched. The shell has more texture and is relatively wider.

The specimen was badly damaged before it was preserved. It suffered more damage while it was exposed before anyone found it. The front and back parts of the belly plate had folded over the bridge. This created three layers of bone stacked together.

If a right selection is made and properly planted, the plants may be relied upon to appear with perennial vigour and produce flowers more or less throughout the year. It does not seem to be generally understood that warm nooks and corners, under trees or walls, serve to produce in winter flowers which usually appear in spring when otherwise placed. ### Plain English:
Beautiful old-fashioned flowers are worth growing for good reasons. They come in many different shapes, sizes, and leaf types. They also need much less work than annual flowers and plants that can't survive winter. If you pick the right ones and plant them correctly, they will come back every year and produce flowers all year long. You might not get bouquets in the middle of winter, but you can still find flowers blooming, like Bluets, Violets, Primroses, Christmas Roses, Crocuses, Hepaticas, Squills, Snowdrops, and other winter flowers that aren't as well known. People don't always realize that warm, sheltered spots under trees or walls can produce winter flowers that normally bloom in spring. Some plants look nice even when they're not flowering because of their shape and leaves. Many people have small gardens, but if you take care of them properly, they have real advantages. The smaller your garden is, the more carefully you should choose what to plant. Everything you grow should be the best quality possible.

Regent Street looked closed off. Bond Street seemed weird and not human. The ghosts of old fashions were all around that lonely Woman. Some fashions were made by British people. Some came from other countries. Some were just a mix of random things. Pall-Mall had no sounds or footsteps. Park Lane was as quiet as death. Belgravia made no noise at all.

But that one Woman stood there like a lighthouse. Or she moved her skirts around. Like a wild wind that sweeps through the woods and blows leaves everywhere. She was singing, "Our time has come, Sun of Fashion! We won't have any more fun. Being alone is too slow! You've worn ten thousand different shapes. Even though men have made fun of you and joked about you. But now the thing doesn't work anymore.

"Even though Man puts on his big powerful acts and uses his skills! He could not make Woman and Worth bow down to what he wants. Fashion, I'm sad that your power is gone. You're a dim queen who lost her crown! We used to perform for full theaters and halls. But now everything is empty."

NEW YORK: HARCOURT, BRACE AND CO. 1922 PREFACE When my publishers were good enough to propose that I should undertake this book, they were also good enough to suggest that the Introduction should be of a character somewhat different from that of a school-anthology, and should attempt to deal with the Art of Letter-writing, and the nature of the Letter, as such. I formed a plan accordingly, by which the letters, and their separate Prefatory Notes, might be as it were illustrations to the Introduction, which was intended in turn to be a guide to them. Having done this with a proper _Pourvu que Dieu lui prête vie_ referring to both book and author, I thought it well to look up next what had been done in the way before me, at least to the extent of what the London Library could provide me in circumstances of enforced abstinence from the Museum and from "Bodley." From its catalogue I selected a curious eighteenth-century _Art of Letter Writing_, and four nineteenth and earliest twentieth century books--Roberts's _History of Letter Writing_ (1843) with Pickering's ever-beloved title-page and his beautiful clear print; the _Littérature Epistolaire_

### Plain English:
GEORGE SAINTSBURY LONDON G. BELL AND SONS, LTD. NEW YORK: HARCOURT, BRACE AND CO. 1922 PREFACE My publishers asked me to write this book. They also asked me to write an introduction that was different from what you'd find in a school book. They wanted me to talk about how to write letters and what letters really are. I made a plan for this. The letters and their notes would show examples of the ideas in the introduction. The introduction would then help explain the letters. After I did this, I hoped things would go well for both the book and me. Then I decided to look at what other people had done before me. I looked at what the London Library had available. At that time, I couldn't go to the Museum or to "Bodley" because of the situation. From the library's list, I picked out an interesting book from the 1700s about how to write letters. I also found four books from the 1800s and early 1900s. One was Roberts's History of Letter Writing from 1843. It had a title page printed by Pickering, who was very respected. The print was beautiful and easy to read. Another was called Littérature Epistolaire.

### Plain English:
# Team The Philippine Islands, 1493-1898

Early explorers wrote about the Philippine Islands and the people who lived there. They described the islands' history and records of Catholic missions. These writings show what life was like politically, economically, and religiously in the islands from when Europeans first arrived until the end of the 1800s.

This is Volume IX, covering the years 1593-1597. It was edited and annotated by Emma Helen Blair and James Alexander Robertson. Edward Gaylord Bourne wrote the historical introduction and added extra notes.

### Plain English:
He was one of the discoveries of the war. For years, he has been writing poetry. But only recently did people start to notice. Now he is taking his place among our best poets. What William Barnes was to Dorset, what T. E. Brown was to the Manx people—this is what Mr. Gilbert is to his home county of Lincoln. He has written about the lives of the people there. He has captured their sadness and their hopes. He does this really well. Mr. Gilbert keeps his feet on the ground. You can tell he knows the men he writes about really well. He knows their bad sides too. He is not afraid to write about their faults. The people of Lincolnshire are not dreamers like people in the South. A respectable chapel-goer who thinks "Why shouldn't I have a good time? Why should the Devil get all the beer?" is a true picture of these people. But they also have softer sides. They are loyal friends. They are brave and clever and never give up. These are the real qualities of the people of the Fens.

### Plain English:
The plays that were performed on Thespis's stage, or really on his cart, were definitely meant to make the crowd laugh. As I've already explained, the word originally had nothing to do with the feelings and subjects that we connect it to now. It simply meant a play performed at a festival for the goat god, to honor Bacchus. Back then, all the different types of drama were mixed together under one name. This one name became the main source that later split into two branches: tragedy and comedy. Tragedy came first and took the original name, and kept it forever after. People have wondered why we enjoy watching fake stories about other people's sadness and bad luck in tragedies, and about their foolishness and weaknesses in comedies. Many people have asked this question, but not many have been able to answer it. Everyone agrees that this happens. Because of this family connection, I'm a descendant of General Delcambre, who was a hero in the retreat from Russia. The young, small, weak girl had to get a job to survive. ### Plain English:
# The Bottom of the Sea

At the bottom of the sea, mermaids live in bright caves. Unknown jewels flash their light. Hidden gold is everywhere. At the bottom of the sea, huge anchors lie buried in golden sand. Old ships from China are down there. Their broken timbers are still standing. Destruction is all around. At the bottom of the sea, blind fish swim around bleaching bones. They swim in and out of human skulls. These were once brave men. At the bottom of the sea, countless thousands are sleeping. Sailors, lovers, and beautiful young women are there. They wear jewels in the depths. Now they rest in peace without worries. At the bottom of the sea, some bodies are wrapped in sails. Old warriors wear armor. But only one person comes back to tell about the bottom of the sea. The diver is a human being, just like those who sleep at the bottom of the sea. She is a humble hero of the deep. She crawls into sunken ships' hulls. She takes the golden treasure from the bottom of the sea. She wears armor, a helmet, and lead shoes. She bravely goes into those terrible deep waters.

He watched things and investigated them carefully. This is unusual for a French writer, if you don't mind me saying so. French people are usually so French that they can't stop thinking like French people, even for a short time. They have their own preferences and biases about their country and people. But when you find a French person who has learned to think fairly about all countries through study and travel, they think more clearly and openly than anyone else. This book is a good example of that. If there was no name on the cover and the author didn't use the word "we" about French people, you wouldn't be able to tell he was French from his writing. For example, he accepts a made-up story about a British admiral saluting a Japanese admiral's flag before sunrise outside Wei-hai-wei so that the guns would wake up sleeping Chinese sailors and warn them of danger. He also too easily accepts another story as proof that the British public always wants more and more things.

IN NEW GUINEA, I DEDICATE THIS BOOK. PREFACE. As every Bill has a preamble, so, I suppose, every book should have a preface. At any rate, such appears to be the universal custom, and I, being of an orthodox turn of mind, am not going to depart from the general rule. Some months ago I mentioned to an acquaintance of mine, a gentleman of considerable literary attainments, that I purposed writing a book on New Guinea, describing my experiences, etc. He replied "Why, the subject is thrashed out, and writings on New Guinea are overdone." I may state that, as the said gentleman had never been in that country, I was not biassed by his opinion. Had he said that the few writers on New Guinea were thrashed out, he would have been nearer to the mark, as the subject of a country so vast, and the civilised settlement of which is in its infancy, will not be thrashed out for many years to come. We do not all wear the same pair of spectacles. I have endeavoured to describe places and people as they appeared to me. Whether the results as set forth in this work will be found

### Plain English:
This book is dedicated to New Guinea. Preface. Every Bill has an introduction, so I think every book should too. At least, that seems to be what everyone does, and I follow the normal way of doing things, so I'm going to do it too. A few months ago, I told a friend—a man who knows a lot about writing—that I wanted to write a book about New Guinea. I wanted to describe what I experienced there. He said, "Why? People have already written a lot about New Guinea, and there are too many books on it." I should say that this man had never actually been to New Guinea, so I didn't let his opinion change my mind. If he had said that the few people who have written about New Guinea had said everything there is to say, he would have been more right. But New Guinea is such a huge country, and people are just starting to build civilized settlements there, so there's still plenty more to write about for many years. Not everyone sees things the same way. I tried to describe the places and people the way they looked to me. Whether what I wrote in this book will be good or not—

### Plain English:
She nodded to say thanks. Then she slowly went through the list. She counted with the tip of her pencil and wrote down the numbers. She looked confused. The two people were brother and sister. Their names were Nell and Dick Lorton. They looked really nice in the sunny room. The boy had very light hair. The girl had dark hair that was shiny like silk. She had dark eyebrows. Her eyes were light gray and bright like stars. Right now her eyes looked almost purple because she was thinking so hard. She looked up and her eyes showed she was annoyed and upset. "Dick, if you don't put that banjo down, I'll come over and make you. It's bad enough most of the time. But playing 'Old Folks at Home' on one string while I'm trying to check this awful book is too much. I can't stand it. Put it down, Dick, or I'll smash both of you!" He played a chord that was annoying. Then he started playing that same annoying thing again. Peck, peck.

### Plain English:
The sun beat down all day long, shining brightly on the white streets and burning the dry ground. People only came outside when the sun was about to set. Groups of people were heading to the Bishop's Garden. This wasn't the private garden of the bishop who ran the church in Westerbury. It was a semi-public garden that people could walk through, and it had that name.

A long time ago, a bishop of Westerbury took a piece of unused land near his palace and turned it into a garden. He made wide sunny paths for winter, shady winding paths for summer, bushes and trees, flower beds and grass areas, small rocks, and a fountain. Then he opened it to the public, and it's been called the Bishop's Garden ever since. But not everyone could go there—only rich and important people. Back then, there wasn't a thing like public spaces for poor people. The garden was mainly for the people who lived near it.

### Plain English:
The stories in this small book are just a few examples from a much bigger collection of Indian folk-tales. Mr. Crooke gathered them while doing an Ethnological Survey of the North-West Provinces and Oudh. Some stories came from people who lived in the jungle near Mirzápur. The Director of Public Instruction, Mr. J. C. Nesfield, sent out a request to all village school teachers asking them to send in stories. This book only includes Beast Stories. These are interesting because they are mostly from India itself and haven't been changed much by outside influences. Most of these stories are new. Some have only been printed before in the _North Indian Notes and Queries_ (called _N.I.N.Q._). Mr. Rouse rewrote the stories. He made some changes. The book is written for children. So the main goal was to make the stories fun and interesting to read. People who study folk-tales for serious reasons will find notes explaining all the changes. If a change is big, the notes will summarize what the original version said.

But because every man is an orator, how long soever he may have been a mute, an assembly of men is so much more susceptible. The eloquence of one stimulates all the rest, some up to the speaking point, and all others to a degree that makes them good receivers and conductors, and they avenge themselves for their enforced silence by increased loquacity on their return to the fireside. The plight of these phlegmatic brains is better than that of those who prematurely boil, and who impatiently break the silence before their time. Our county conventions often exhibit a small-pot-soon-hot style of eloquence. We are too much reminded of a medical experiment, where a series of patients are taking nitrous-oxide gas. Each patient, in turn, exhibits similar symptoms,--redness in the face, volubility, violent gesticulation, delirious attitudes, occasional stamping, an alarming loss of perception of the passage of time, a selfish enjoyment of his sensations, and loss

### Plain English:
One person needs extra energy and a public discussion. Another person needs someone to argue against, or strong anger. A third person needs a revolution. A fourth person needs nothing less than big, important ideas—the beauty and darkness of Heaven and Hell. But because every man can be a speaker, even if he's been quiet for a long time, a group of men is much more ready to listen. When one person talks well, it gets everyone else going. Some people start talking too, and all the others become good listeners and pass along what they hear. They make up for staying quiet by talking a lot when they get home. People who stay calm are better off than people who get too excited and start talking before they're ready. Our local meetings often have people jumping in too fast to talk. It reminds us of a medical test where patients breathe in a special gas. Each patient shows the same signs—red face, lots of talking, wild hand movements, crazy behavior, sometimes stomping, losing track of time, caring only about how they feel, and not paying attention to anything else.

INCA OF PERUSALEM: AN ALMOST HISTORICAL COMEDIETTA By George Bernard Shaw I must remind the reader that this playlet was written when its principal character, far from being a fallen foe and virtually a prisoner in our victorious hands, was still the Caesar whose legions we were resisting with our hearts in our mouths. Many were so horribly afraid of him that they could not forgive me for not being afraid of him: I seemed to be trifling heartlessly with a deadly peril. I knew better; and I have represented Caesar as knowing better himself. But it was one of the quaintnesses of popular feeling during the war that anyone who breathed the slightest doubt of the absolute perfection of German organization, the Machiavellian depth of German diplomacy, the omniscience of German science, the equipment of every German with a complete philosophy of history, and the consequent hopelessness of overcoming so magnificently accomplished an enemy except by the sacrifice of every recreative activity to incessant and vehement war work, including a heartbreaking mass of fussing and cadging and bluffing that did nothing but waste our energies and tire our resolution, was called a pro-German. Now that this is all over,

### Plain English:
# INCA OF PERUSALEM: AN ALMOST HISTORICAL COMEDIETTA By George Bernard Shaw

I need to remind readers that I wrote this play when its main character was still powerful. We were fighting against him, and we were very scared. He wasn't a defeated enemy locked up yet. He was still a threat to us.

Many people were so terrified of him that they got angry at me for not being scared too. They thought I was joking around about a serious danger. But I knew better. And I showed that the main character knew better too.

During the war, people had strange ideas about what you could say. If you said anything bad about Germany's organization, or questioned how smart German diplomacy was, or doubted German science, or suggested that not every German had perfect knowledge of history, people called you pro-German. People thought the only way to beat such a powerful enemy was to stop having fun and work constantly on the war effort. We did tons of pointless arguing and begging and lying. It just wasted our energy and made us tired.

Number Seven last week, a curious state of affairs was found: the prisoners below mining the ore as usual, the overseer dead, and every indication of some stark drama having taken place. In the study of the overseer's house one man was found dead, apparently by his own hand, and beside him on the desk was a hastily scribbled document which is herewith published._ * * * * * We hated Marnick. Because he was an Earthman and because he laughed, we hated him. Awake and asleep, at our daily drudge of labor and in the throes of sluggish nightmare, with a fierce tenacity from the very depths of our souls--those of us who still had souls--we hated him. And there was not a man among us who had not sworn to kill him if given the chance, who did not dream of being _the one_. For we knew that some day it was going to happen. But when? It seemed impossible. Daily that is what I thought as I trudged wearily to my place in B-Tunnel two miles below. We were forty men against him, Martians and Earthmen alike. Once there had been Venusians here, too, but they died

### Plain English:
# Plain English Version

Last week at Number Seven, something strange happened. The prisoners were mining ore like normal. But the overseer was dead. It looked like something terrible had gone on. In the overseer's house, in his study, they found another dead man. It looked like he killed himself. Next to him on his desk was a note written quickly. Here's what it said.

We hated Marnick. We hated him because he was from Earth. We hated him because he laughed. Whether we were awake or asleep, whether we were working or having bad dreams, we hated him. We hated him from deep inside, those of us who still had feelings. Every single man here had promised himself he would kill Marnick if he got the chance. We all dreamed about being the one to do it. We knew it was going to happen someday. But when? It seemed impossible. That's what I thought every day as I walked down to my place in B-Tunnel two miles underground. There were forty of us against him—men from Mars and men from Earth. There used to be men from Venus here too, but they all died.

the clerk when she saw that the bulky American who was standing there helplessly dangling two flaming red silk stockings which a copiously coiffured young woman assured him were _bien chic_ was edging nearer her. She was never so conscious of the truly American quality of her French as when a countryman was at hand. The French themselves had an air of “How marvellously you speak!” but fellow Americans listened superciliously in an “I can do better than that myself” manner which quite untied the Gallic twist in one's tongue. And so, feeling her French was being compared, not with mere French itself, but with an arrogant new American brand thereof, she moved a little around the corner of the counter and began again in lower voice: “_Mais, n'avez_--” “Say, Young Lady,” a voice which adequately represented the figure broke in, “_you_, aren't French, are you?” She looked up with what was designed for a haughty stare. But what is a haughty stare to do in the face of a broad grin? And because it was such a long time since a grin like that had been grinned at her it happened that the stare gave way to a dimple,

### Plain English:
The clerk saw the big American guy standing there looking confused. He was holding two bright red silk stockings. A girl with lots of hair told him they were really stylish. He was moving closer to the clerk. She felt nervous about her French accent whenever another American was around. French people would say "Wow, you speak French so well!" But other Americans would listen like they thought "I can do that better." It made her lose her French accent. She felt like her French was being judged against some new American way of speaking French, not against real French. So she moved around the corner of the counter a bit and started talking again in a quieter voice. "But, don't you—" "Hey, Young Lady," the man's voice interrupted, "you're not French, are you?" She looked up trying to give him an angry stare. But what good is an angry stare when someone is grinning at you? And because it had been so long since someone had grinned at her like that, her angry stare turned into a smile instead.

Far abler writers than I have frequently dilated on the charms attending a study of the reports of State Trials, as they are best known to the world; namely, in one-and-twenty stately volumes compiled by the industrious Howells, father and son, and published, a year after the battle of Waterloo, by the combined efforts of on a few of

### Plain English:
The pictures of Sir Walter Raleigh and Lord Russell come from photos of paintings in the National Portrait Gallery. They're used with permission from Messrs. Walker and Boutall.

To G. De L'É. D.

Dear Gerald,

Since you came up with the idea for this book, and since I know you'll support me whether it works or not, I'm writing this as a letter to you instead of a normal preface. I have some things to admit that are almost like saying sorry. I'd rather tell these things to you, because I know you'll say the nicest things about my writing. It's better than writing to some imaginary reader. I don't really know what that reader would like, even though I'm trying my best to think well of them.

Many writers who are much better than me have talked about how interesting it is to read State Trial reports. These reports are most famous in twenty-one big books put together by Howells and his son. They published these books one year after the Battle of Waterloo. They worked together with a few other people to make it happen.

They are the relics of a happily past age, one which can never return, and we, in this our day of cheap, plentiful, and good literature, can hardly conceive a time when in the major part of this country, and to the larger portion of its population, these little Chap-books were nearly the only mental pabulum offered. Away from the towns, newspapers were rare indeed, and not worth much when obtainable--poor little flimsy sheets such as nowadays we should not dream of either reading or publishing, with very little news in them, and that consisting principally of war items, and foreign news, whilst these latter books were carried in the packs of the pedlars, or Chapmen, to every village, and to every home. Previous to the eighteenth century, these men generally carried ballads, as is so well exemplified in the "Winter's Tale," in Shakespeare's inimitable conception, Autolycus. ### Plain English:
Many accounts are interesting. But no one has tried yet to collect them all and show them to the public. They should come with pictures, because without the pictures they wouldn't be worth much. These books are leftovers from an old time that is gone forever. We live now with cheap books and good literature everywhere. We can't really imagine a time when most people in this country, especially those living outside of towns, had almost nothing else to read. Newspapers were rare back then. When you could get them, they weren't very good. They were thin, flimsy sheets that we wouldn't even bother reading or printing today. They had very little news in them. Most of what was in them was about wars and foreign news. But these little books were different. The peddlers and chapmen carried them in their packs to every village and every home. Before the 1700s, these men usually sold ballads. You can see this in Shakespeare's play "The Winter's Tale." He created a character named Autolycus who is a perfect example. In Act 4, scene 3, a servant describes what Autolycus sells. He says: "He has songs for men and women of all kinds."

### Original:
Adults may draw from a book what of good there is in it, and close it before reaching the chapter usually devoted to fine writing. But the case of Haydn is extraordinary. One can only sustain interest in a biography of the man by an ever-present sense that he is scarcely to be written about. All an author can do is, in few or many words, to put a conundrum to the reader--a conundrum that cannot even be stated in exciting terms. This apparition and wonder-worker of the eighteenth century, Franz Joseph Haydn, is compact of paradoxes and contradictions. Born a peasant, and remaining in thought and speech a peasant all his days, he became the friend of princes, dukes, and, generally speaking, very high society indeed--and this in days when class distinctions had to be observed. He effected a revolution in music, and revolutionists must have daring; and save in music he showed no sign of unusual daring. His shaping and handling of new forms called for high intellect, and he displayed no intellect whatever in any other way--nothing beyond a canny, cunning shrewdness. Until he was sixty his life was a plodding one of dull regularity and routine;

### Plain English:
Grown-ups can read a book, take the good parts, and stop before the boring writing sections. But Haydn is different. You can only stay interested in a book about Haydn if you remember that he's almost impossible to write about. All a writer can do is ask the reader a puzzle—a puzzle that's hard to even explain in an interesting way. Franz Joseph Haydn was an amazing musician in the 1700s. But he was full of contradictions. He was born poor, and he stayed simple in how he thought and talked his whole life. Yet he became friends with princes and dukes and really powerful people—at a time when people cared a lot about social class. He changed music forever, and people who make big changes need to be brave. But he wasn't brave in anything except music. Creating new types of music needed a smart mind, but he didn't show intelligence in any other way. He was just clever and tricky in a practical sense. Until he was sixty, his life was boring and the same every day.

For the first time also in any campaign the labours of the surgeon and the physician have had the aid of the bacteriologist, the pathologist, the physiologist and indeed of every form of scientific assistance in the solution of their respective problems. Old medical books show that doctors today don't see new types of injuries. Instead, what matters is the huge amount of patient information doctors collected. This information was enough to prove important medical ideas and remove mistakes.

For the first time in any war, surgeons and doctors got help from bacteria experts, disease experts, scientists who study how bodies work, and many other types of scientists. All these experts helped solve medical problems together. Doctors who treated patients in the great war had all the help from science.

BY DANIEL G. BRINTON, M. D. The first battle on the American continent in which horses were used was that of Cintla in Tabasco, March, 1519, the European troops being under the leadership of Hernando Cortes. This fact attaches something more than an ordinary historic interest to the engagement, at least enough to make it desirable to ascertain its precise locality and its proper name. Both of these are in doubt, as well as the ethnic stock to which the native tribe belonged which opposed the Spanish soldiery on the occasion. I propose to submit these questions to a re-examination, and also to describe from unpublished material the ruins which,--as I believe--, mark the spot of this first important encounter of the two races on American soil. The engagement itself has been described by all the historians of Cortes' famous conquest of Mexico, as it was the first brilliant incident of that adventure. We have at least four accounts of it from participants. One prepared under the eye of Cortes himself, one

### Plain English:
# The Battle and the Ruins of Cintla

By Daniel G. Brinton, M.D.

The first battle fought on the American continent using horses happened at Cintla in Tabasco in March 1519. Hernando Cortes led the European troops. This fact makes the battle more interesting than just any other historical event. It's worth figuring out exactly where the battle took place and what it was really called. We're not sure about both of these things. We also don't know what group of Native people fought against the Spanish soldiers that day. I want to look at these questions again. I also want to describe some ruins that I believe mark where this first big meeting between the two groups happened on American land. I'm using materials that haven't been published before.

All the historians who wrote about Cortes' famous conquest of Mexico described this battle. It was the first exciting event of that whole adventure. We have at least four accounts written by people who were there. One was prepared with Cortes watching over it.

### Plain English:
Writing an article gives you money for sure. A novelist can be pretty sure their book will get published, even if they're not famous. But a play? There's no guarantee it will ever be performed. When I was a journalist, writing a play seemed like a waste of time and money. It seemed almost as stupid as going to watch cricket in the morning. I thought I could write one (everyone thinks they can), but I couldn't risk it. I needed the money from my job.

Then I joined the Army. Everything changed. I wasn't a journalist anymore. My job was being a soldier, and my free time was my own. Other soldiers played cards and golf in their spare time. So why not write plays instead? That's how we started writing Wurzel-Flummery. I say "we" because someone else was involved—even more than the Kaiser was. She wrote the words, and I told her what to write. And on nice evenings, we would go for walks together.

THE LAND OF CONTENT

It was early spring, and almost the end of a day. The sunshine and warmth had made many shy roadside flowers bloom. White cherry blossoms from farms were falling to the ground like tiny, late snowfalls. The setting sun was already lighting up the open space on top of the mountain where the ridge-road and turnpike met. The ridge-road was just one of the small mountain paths that wound through woods and went up and down valleys. The mountain people had worn these paths into existence. The turnpike was an old road that connected North and South. It ran through cities, villages, and farms like trophies on a chain. The shy mountain road had nothing more modern than the doctor's car pulled by a horse named White Rosy. The most exciting things on it were small groups of armed, silent men who walked over it at night or crossed it secretly during the day. But on the turnpike, coaches and cars pounded and rolled. A generation or so earlier, an army had swung north over it.

First, that they range over the whole animal kingdom, without completing the history of any one tribe. Secondly, that their authors, while professing to illustrate only what is new or little known, intrude a large proportion of subjects to be found in all the common natural histories. **PREFACE**

As I finish the last volume of these Illustrations, I want to say that I'm happy with how well this work has been received. People in this country and across Europe have liked it. However, some smart writers have complained about books like this one that cover many different animals. Here are their main complaints:

First, these books talk about lots of different types of animals without finishing the complete story of any single group.

Second, the authors say they only want to show new or unknown animals. But they actually include tons of stuff that's already in regular animal books.

Third, putting out new discoveries so quickly is unfair. It takes away from the hard work of scientists who spend their time studying just one area of animals.

These complaints have answers, though. For one thing, these mixed animal books should really be thought of as picture-based

The original lecture was written as a scholar's contribution to a modern movement. It looked on town-planning as one of those new methods of social reform, which stand in somewhat sharp contrast with the usual aims of political parties and parliaments. The latter concern mainly the outward and public life of men as fellow-citizens in a state; they involve such problems as Home Rule, Disestablishment, Protection. The newer ideals centre round the daily life of human beings in their domestic environment. Men and women--or rather, women and men--have begun to demand that the health and housing and food and comfort of mankind, and much else that not long ago seemed to lie outside the scope of legislation, should be treated with as close attention and logic and intelligence as any of the older and more conventional problems of politicians.

ANCIENT TOWN-PLANNING By F. HAVERFIELD Oxford at The Clarendon Press 1913 OXFORD UNIVERSITY PRESS London::Edinburgh::Glasgow::New York Toronto::Melbourne::Bombay HUMPHREY MILFORD Publisher to the University PREFACE This book is based on a paper I gave at the University of London as the Creighton Lecture in 1910. I also shared parts of it at the London Conference on Town-planning that same year. I wrote the original lecture as a scholar wanting to help with a modern movement. I saw town-planning as a new way to improve society. It's quite different from what traditional political parties and parliaments focus on. Political parties mainly care about how people act as citizens in government. They deal with issues like Home Rule, Disestablishment, and Protection. The newer ideas focus on how people live day to day at home. Men and women—or rather, women and men—have started to demand that health, housing, food, and comfort for people be given as much serious thought and attention as old-fashioned political problems. These things were not considered government concerns not long ago.

outset remark that among the many sciences that are occupied with the study of the living world there is no one that may properly lay exclusive claim to the name of Biology. The word does not, in fact, denote any particular science but is a generic term applied to a large group of biological sciences all of which alike are concerned with the phenomena of life. To present in a single address, even in rudimentary outline, the specific results of these sciences is obviously an impossible task, and one that I have no intention of attempting. I shall offer no more than a kind of preface or introduction to those who will speak after me on the biological sciences of physiology, botany and zoology; and I shall confine it to what seem to me the most essential and characteristic of the general problems towards which all lines of biological inquiry must sooner or later converge. It is the general aim of the biological sciences to learn something of the order of nature in the living world. Perhaps it is not amiss to remark that the biologist may not hope to solve the ultimate problems of life any more than the

It's a general term for a whole group of sciences. All of them study how living things work. I can't explain everything these sciences have discovered in one talk. I'm not even going to try. I'm just going to give a short introduction before the other speakers talk about physiology, botany, and zoology. I'll focus on the most important problems that all biology scientists work on. The main goal of biological sciences is to understand how nature works in the living world. It's worth noting that biologists can't answer all the deepest questions about life any more than other scientists can.

fought again. Money? No credit even. Trade was at a standstill, yet the court was uproariously gay. Money and credit--sinews of pleasure as well as business--having flitted from lively Paris, you might suppose that the wheels of Society would cease to turn--that the flower-decked car of gilded Juggernaut would come creeping to a standstill. Not yet. Impelled by the impulse of its own velocity, it continued to crush on awhile. Those who knew were numbed by the chill shadow of the inevitable, or rendered callous by the knowledge of their helplessness. Those who were deaf and blind groped blissfully on in their lighthearted ignorance. Selfish all, depraved most, the blue-blooded sang in merry chorus, "Let us eat and drink that the worms may grow fat on us." Not so the gaunt crowds whose blood was but mud and water. As their long-suffering ancestors had monotonously done, they groaned in unison, crying to God for death, as the only release from misery. What if whole villages were decimated by famine? What if plague and starvation stalked through the towns? My lords and my ladies cared not, for they were poised too high to see. Were the grovelling creatures slaves or insects?

### Plain English:
They fought again. Money? No credit at all. Trade stopped completely, but the royal court was wild and happy. Money and credit—the things you need for fun and business—had left Paris. You might think that society would fall apart. You might think the fancy parade would stop. But it didn't yet. It kept going because of its own momentum. It kept crushing forward. People who understood what was happening felt numb and scared. They knew things were going to get worse. Other people didn't want to know. They just kept partying and having fun without caring. The rich nobles were selfish and corrupt. They sang together saying, "Let's eat and drink while we can." But the poor people were different. They were starving and sick. Like their ancestors before them, they cried out together for death. Death was the only way to escape their suffering. Whole villages were dying from hunger. Disease and starvation were killing people in the cities. But the rich lords and ladies didn't care. They lived too high up to see what was happening below. To them, poor people were like slaves or bugs.

J. SYMES. INTRODUCTION. George Payne Rainsford James, Historiographer Royal to King William IV., was born in London in the first year of the nineteenth century, and died at Venice in 1860. His comparatively short life was exceptionally full and active. He was historian, politician and traveller, the reputed author of upwards of a hundred novels, the compiler and editor of nearly half as many volumes of letters, memoirs, and biographies, a poet and a pamphleteer, and, during the last ten years of his life, British Consul successively in Massachusetts, Norfolk (Virginia), and Venice. He was on terms of friendship with most of the eminent men of his day. Scott, on whose style he founded his own, encouraged him to persevere in his career as a novelist; Washington Irving admired him, and Walter Savage Landor composed an epitaph to his memory. He achieved the distinction of being twice burlesqued by Thackeray, and two columns are devoted to an account of him in the new "Dictionary of National Biography." ### Plain English:
J. SYMES. INTRODUCTION. George Payne Rainsford James was the official historian for King William IV. He was born in London in 1800 and died in Venice in 1860. His life was short but very busy and full of action. He was a historian, a politician, and a traveler. People say he wrote over a hundred novels. He also put together and edited almost fifty books of letters, memoirs, and biographies. He was a poet and wrote pamphlets too. For the last ten years of his life, he worked as a British Consul in Massachusetts, Norfolk in Virginia, and Venice. He was friends with most of the important people of his time. Scott liked his writing style and encouraged him to keep writing novels. Washington Irving admired him. Walter Savage Landor even wrote a poem to honor him after he died. Thackeray made fun of him twice in his writing. Two whole columns in the new "Dictionary of National Biography" are about him. Every generation has its own favorite writers. G. P. R. James probably wrote too many books to stay as popular as he once was. He was one of the most successful novelists of his time.
