PREFACE There are few campaigns that, either in point of the immense scale upon which it was undertaken, the completeness of its failure, or the enormous loss of life entailed, appeal to the imagination in so great a degree as that of Napoleon against Russia. Fortunately, we have in the narratives of Sir Robert Wilson, British commissioner with the Russian army, and of Count Segur, who was upon Napoleon's staff, minute descriptions of the events as seen by eye-witnesses, and besides these the campaign has been treated fully by various military writers. I have as usual avoided going into details of horrors and of acts of cruelty and ferocity on both sides, surpassing anything in modern warfare, and have given a mere outline of the operations, with a full account of the stern fight at Smolensk and the terrible struggle at Borodino. I would warn those of my readers who may turn to any of the military works for a further history of the campaign, that the spelling of Russian places and names varies so greatly in the accounts of different writers, that sometimes it is difficult to believe that the same person or

### Plain English:
SONS THE CAXTON PRESS NEW YORK. PREFACE

Few military campaigns are as interesting as Napoleon's attack on Russia. It was huge in size, it completely failed, and many people died. Luckily, we have detailed eyewitness accounts from Sir Robert Wilson, a British officer with the Russian army, and Count Segur, who worked for Napoleon. Military writers have also written a lot about this campaign. I have chosen not to describe the horrible things and cruel acts that happened on both sides. Instead, I give a simple outline of what happened in the war. I do describe the hard fighting at Smolensk and the terrible battle at Borodino in detail. I want to warn readers who look at other military books about this campaign. The spelling of Russian place names and people's names is very different in different books. Sometimes it's hard to tell if writers are talking about the same person or place.

## Chapter I: The Germans Are Coming!

The Germans had already taken Brussels. Their soldiers were spotted near Ghent. A little farther ahead, past the windmills on the horizon, and we could run into them at any second. For over two weeks we had been driving east. We heard reports on the radio and by cable about the huge German army moving toward France like distant thunder. The first news had come from four thousand miles away.

### Plain English:
February 13, 1883. His father was a city official in Leipzig who sent his son to the famous Thomas School. This was supposed to prepare him for university. But his son, young Wagner, didn't really benefit from it. Instead, he mostly focused on music. He led orchestras in different theaters. First in Magdeburg, then in Königsberg, then in Riga. In 1839, he went to Paris. There he finished two operas: "Rienzi" and "The Flying Dutchman" in 1841. "The Flying Dutchman" was inspired by a bad storm Wagner experienced on a short boat trip. "Rienzi" first showed in Dresden in 1842. This led to Wagner getting a job leading the orchestra in Dresden. He put on "Tannhäuser" there in 1845. In 1849, Wagner had to leave Germany for political reasons and went to Switzerland. There he finished "Lohengrin" and started working on a big group of four operas called the "Nibelung" tetralogy. Later, Wagner lived in Italy, Vienna, and Paris. In Paris, "Tannhäuser" was performed in 1861 and it was a disaster. This happened by chance and led to the following pages. In 1864, Wagner became close friends with Louis II, the young King of Bavaria. With the king's strong support, he put on "Tristan" in 1857, "The Mastersingers" in 1768, and "Rhinegold" in 1853.

### Plain English:
In the late 1980s, the FBI and DEA created fake companies to deal in drugs. They put money from drug seizures into these fake businesses. The goal was to catch global crime networks. They usually involve corrupt politicians, shady bankers, and greedy businessmen. Not glamorous hackers and James Bond-type spies. The prime minister of Kazakhstan, Imanghaliy Tasmaghambetov, admitted on April 4 to his country's parliament that there was a $1 billion secret fund. The money apparently came from the sale of the Tengiz oilfield. He said sending it back to Kazakhstan would have caused inflation. So the country's president, Nazarbaev, kept the money outside the country "in case of an economic crisis or a threat."

and his appearance was that of the deepest dejection. In fact upon his face I read the most melancholy despair. He was not weeping, his eyes were dry and almost expressionless and received the impression of exterior objects like motionless ice. He had placed upon his knees a small oaken chest, ornamented with ironwork. His hands were crossed over this object and hung down, accentuating his dejected appearance. An attendant told me that he had been awaiting my arrival there three long hours without a movement, without so much as a sigh. I went towards him, and announcing myself, I invited him to enter my office. I stopped him, however, and said: “Why, do not go, I cannot receive this box without a knowledge of its contents.”

### Plain English:
He looked extremely sad. His face showed deep despair. He wasn't crying, but his eyes were dry and empty. They just stared ahead like ice. He held a small wooden chest with metal decorations on his knees. His hands were crossed over it and hanging down, which made him look even sadder. An attendant told me he had been waiting for me in that same spot for three hours without moving or even sighing. I walked over to him and told him who I was. I asked him to come into my office. I showed him a chair, but he didn't sit down. Instead, he went straight to my desk and put the wooden chest on it. "Sir, this chest is yours," he said. His voice sounded strange and distant. "My friend M. Théophraste Longuet asked me to bring it to you. Take it, sir, and know that I am your servant." He bowed and started to walk toward the door. But I stopped him. I said, "Wait, don't go. I can't take this box without knowing what's inside it."

The design of it is to be a useful assistant to those who are fond of a rational and scientific amusement, and the occasion of it arises from the great scarcity and general difficulty of procuring any work on the subject; none having appeared worthy of notice since that published by Lieutenant Robert Jones, in 1760, and those by the French Artists mentioned in our Introduction. In didactic particulars the Author has occasionally availed himself of the language of the best writers, where such has been corroborated by subsequent experience. Perspicuity has been a particular object through the work, and when technical terms have been used they are generally followed by familiar explications, and the Author feels assured that the whole will be found perfectly intelligible to every reader. To experienced Pyrotechnists this little work cannot be expected to afford much additional information, yet to them it may contain some little particulars not known to them before, which from their practical utility it is hoped will prove acceptable. The Author publishes this little work, with the desire that it may prove a useful assistant to those who are unacquainted with the principles of the art on which it treats.

### Plain English:
This book is made to help people who enjoy learning about fireworks in a scientific way. The author wrote it because there aren't many good books on this subject. The last important book was written by Lieutenant Robert Jones in 1760. The author used language from the best writers when it was backed up by real experience. The author tried to make the book easy to understand. When technical words are used, they are explained in simple language. The author believes anyone can understand this book. People who already work with fireworks probably won't learn much new information. But they might find a few useful details they didn't know before. The author hopes this book will be helpful to people who are just starting to learn about fireworks.

# San Francisco to Portland: A Journey of Over 1,300 Miles

This book shows and tells you about this area and what makes it special. You'll read about the ocean and sky, mountains and valleys, fields and forests. The weather here is nice all year long. There are lots of places to visit if you want to relax and be healthy. You can do all kinds of outdoor activities.

**Passenger Department, Southern Pacific Company, San Francisco, California, 1907**

Sunset Magazine shows pictures of the West and gets people interested in everything beyond the Rocky Mountains. This magazine wants to tell you about a great trip to see—the amazing railroad that goes along the Pacific Ocean. It connects California and Oregon. The train passes by Mount Shasta, goes over the Siskiyou Mountains, and travels to the Columbia River. Along the way, you'll see thousands of acres of roses and sweet peas, oranges and walnuts, wheat and hops, apples and apricots. There's something new to see around every corner. You'll be comfortable the whole trip.

Don't forget—you can read more of this story in Sunset Magazine every month, every year. You'll find other stories and pictures that are just as interesting.

Now go! Say goodbye and head to the sea!

We don't know for sure where the Steel Flea legend started. It could have come from Tula, Izhma, or Sestroryetzk. But it definitely came from one of those places. The Steel Flea story belongs to the gunsmiths' guild. It shows how proud Russian gun makers were. The legend tells about a contest between Russian workers and English workers. He had moved there from Tula during the reign of Alexander the First. Two years ago, he was still healthy and had a good memory. He liked to talk about the old days. He really respected Emperor Nicholas the First. He followed the old faith, read religious books, and raised canary birds. People in town respected him a lot.

The 3:20 train from Boston slowed down as it pulled into a small station. Malcolm McCann got off quickly, looking angry and tired. The train ride had been awful—dirty, smoky, and full of crying babies and hot, frustrated people. It was August, which made everything worse. He jumped out with a relieved grunt.

The station was ugly. Malcolm looked around with angry, restless eyes. The brick walls were black and dirty from years of smoke. The wooden doors said "Gents," "Ladies," and "Refreshment Saloon," but they looked cheap and worn. The roof over the tracks was rough and dark. Smoke was still coming from the big train engine, hanging over the crowd like a dark cloud. Men and women were rushing around, pushing past each other, looking stressed and confused.

REMARKS The position of Ancient Egypt was unique, not in one, but in every sense. To begin at the very foundation of life in that country, we find that the soil was unlike any other on earth in its origin. Every acre of fruitful land between the first cataract and the sea had been brought from Inner Africa, and each year additions were made to it. Out of this mud, borne down thousands of miles from the great fertile uplands of Abyssinia by rivers, grew everything needed to feed and clothe man and nourish animals. Out of it also was made the brick from which walls, houses, and buildings of various uses and kinds were constructed. Though this soil of the country was rich, it could be utilized only by the unceasing co-ordinate efforts of a whole population constrained and directed. To direct and constrain was the task of the priests and the pharaohs. Never have men worked in company so long and successfully at tilling the earth as the Egyptians, and never has the return been so continuous and abundant from land as in their case. The Nile valley furnished grain to all markets accessible by water; hence Rome,

### Plain English:
Ancient Egypt was special in every way. Let's start with the most basic thing—the land itself. The soil was different from anywhere else in the world. All the good farmland between the first cataract and the sea came from inner Africa. Every year, more soil was added. Rivers brought this mud down thousands of miles from the fertile highlands of Abyssinia. Everything people needed to eat and wear came from this soil. Animals were fed from it too. The mud was also used to make bricks. These bricks built walls, houses, and all kinds of other structures. The soil was rich, but people couldn't use it alone. The whole population had to work together in an organized way. The priests and pharaohs were in charge of organizing and controlling this work. No group of people has ever farmed the land together as long and as successfully as the Egyptians did. And no land has ever given back as much food year after year as theirs did. The Nile valley produced so much grain that it could supply all the markets you could reach by boat. That's why Rome,

The baked clay tablets and portions of tablets which describe the views and beliefs of the Babylonians and Assyrians about the Creation were discovered by Mr. (later Sir) A.H. Layard, Mormuzd Rassam and George Smith, Assistant in the Department of Oriental Antiquities in the British Museum. They were found among the ruins of the Palace and Library of Ashur-bani-pal (B.C. 668-626) at Kuyûnjik (Nineveh), between the years 1848 and 1876. Between 1866 and 1870, the great "find" of tablets and fragments, some 20,000 in number, which Rassam made in 1852, was worked through by George Smith, who identified many of the historical inscriptions of Shalmaneser II, Tiglath-Pileser III, Sargon II, Sennacherib, Esarhaddon, and other kings mentioned in the Bible, and several literary compositions of a legendary character, fables, etc. In the course of this work he discovered fragments of various versions of the Babylonian Legend of the Deluge, and portions of several texts belonging to a work which treated of the beginning of things, and

### Plain English:
# CREATION STORIES FROM BABYLON

People found old clay tablets that tell what the Babylonians and Assyrians believed about how the world was created. A.H. Layard, Mormuzd Rassam, and George Smith found these tablets. Smith worked at the British Museum. They discovered the tablets in the ruins of a palace and library that belonged to a king named Ashur-bani-pal. This happened between 1848 and 1876.

Rassam found about 20,000 tablets and pieces of tablets in 1852. Between 1866 and 1870, George Smith studied all of these. He found writings about several kings who are mentioned in the Bible. These kings included Shalmaneser II, Tiglath-Pileser III, Sargon II, Sennacherib, and Esarhaddon. He also found stories and fables.

While he was working, Smith found pieces of different versions of an old Babylonian flood story. He also found parts of texts about how things began in the world.

The first battle on the American continent in which horses were used was that of Cintla in Tabasco, March, 1519, the European troops being under the leadership of Hernando Cortes. This fact attaches something more than an ordinary historic interest to the engagement, at least enough to make it desirable to ascertain its precise locality and its proper name. I propose to submit these questions to a re-examination, and also to describe from unpublished material the ruins which,--as I believe--, mark the spot of this first important encounter of the two races on American soil. The engagement itself has been described by all the historians of Cortes' famous conquest of Mexico, as it was the first brilliant incident of that adventure. PREFACE The following was at first intended to be no more than an attempt to foresee the probable trend of mechanical invention and scientific discovery during the present century. But as the work took shape it was seen to involve a certain amount of what may be called moral conjecture, since the material progress of the new age could not very well be imagined without taking into account its mental characteristics. In these expectations of an optimist, a great ethical improvement of the civilised human race has been anticipated, and a rate of progress foreseen which perhaps no previous writers have looked for. ### Plain English:
# Plain English Version

If you understand what times are like now, you can make a pretty good guess about what will happen next. Things that haven't happened yet are already starting to form. — Shakespeare, Henry IV Part 2

People who think about how things will turn out before they act can avoid big problems. — Webster, Duchess of Malfi

PREFACE

At first, this book was just supposed to guess what inventions and scientific discoveries might happen in this century. But as I wrote it, I realized I also had to think about moral and ethical stuff. I couldn't imagine how technology would change without also thinking about how people would change mentally.

I'm an optimist. So I predict that human civilization will get much better morally and make faster progress than people have ever expected before. For both how we act as people and how our technology develops, I've only predicted things that seem like they're already starting to happen based on what we see going on now.

these Volumes~ IN MEMORY OF COMMON INTERESTS, JOINT STUDIES, AND SIXTEEN YEARS OF FRIENDSHIP PREFACE TO THE AMERICAN EDITION. The studies of Greek Poets, now reprinted, appeared in England in two series, published at an interval of three or four years. In preparing this edition, I have rearranged the chapters of both series in their proper order, and have made certain additions, with the view of rendering the book more complete as a survey of Greek Poetry. Thus I have inserted several new translations in the chapters on the Lyric Poets and the Anthology. The criticism of Euripides has been enlarged, and the concluding chapter has been, in a great measure, rewritten. Each chapter has undergone such revision and alteration in minor details as might remove unnecessary repetitions and bring the whole series of essays into harmony. At the same time I have judged it inexpedient to introduce radical changes into a book which professes to be the reprint of volumes already known to the English public. For this reason the chapters which deal with the Greek Tragedians have been left substantially in their original form, and bear upon their face the record of their composition as almost independent essays.

### Plain English:
These books are written in memory of shared interests, studying together, and sixteen years of friendship.

PREFACE TO THE AMERICAN EDITION

The studies of Greek poets that are being reprinted here first came out in England. They were published in two separate groups, a few years apart. While preparing this new edition, I organized the chapters from both groups in the right order. I also added new material to make the book a better overall look at Greek poetry. I added several new translations in the sections about lyric poets and ancient poetry collections. I expanded the section about Euripides and mostly rewrote the final chapter. Every chapter was reviewed and cleaned up. I removed parts that repeated themselves and made all the essays fit together better. But I decided not to make big changes to the book. It's supposed to be a reprint of books that English readers already knew. Because of this, I left the chapters about Greek tragedy mostly the way they were. You can tell they were written separately as individual pieces.

I also like that Dante stressed how important these beings are—he calls them "people of great worth." This matters because we care about the high quality of the things I mean by Limbo.

The first feature determines the worth of the several philosophical systems in the history of mankind, being the gist of that which will last, and giving them strength and backbone. Truth is not something that we can fashion according to our pleasure; it is not subjective; its very nature is objectivity. To THE GOVERNORS of the NEW YORK SKIN AND CANCER HOSPITAL whose kind appreciation of and assistance to the author in his clinical work in their institution have done much to encourage him and to promote the interest of the profession in the branch of DERMATOLOGY this little book is inscribed PREFACE Cancer has hitherto been regarded almost wholly from its histological and surgical aspects. The investigators and practitioners along these lines have been innumerable, the work done prodigious, and the literature relating thereto enormous. But relatively little attention has been paid to the medical aspects of this most threatening malady, although voices have been raised from time to time, with more or less force, all joining in the same plea that the basic cause of the disease lies in some derangement in the vital forces of the organism, as influenced largely by diet and mode of life. It has, therefore, seemed to the writer that it was time to stop and look at cancer from a broader aspect, and by synthesis and deduction to seek to understand some of the underlying causes of malignant disease; also to see if some general principles could

### Plain English:
1915_ Printed in the U.S.A. To the GOVERNORS of the NEW YORK SKIN AND CANCER HOSPITAL. You have helped the author and supported his work at your hospital. You have encouraged him and helped the medical field learn more about DERMATOLOGY. This book is dedicated to you.

PREFACE

Until now, cancer has been studied mostly from two angles. People have looked at what cancer cells look like under a microscope. And people have studied how to remove cancer through surgery. Many doctors and scientists have done this work. They have written a lot about it. But not many people have studied the medical side of cancer. Even though some doctors have tried to say the same thing over and over. They say that cancer comes from something wrong with the body's natural systems. They think diet and how people live their lives cause this problem. The writer thinks we need to stop and look at cancer in a new way. We should use logic and reasoning to understand what really causes cancer. We should also try to find some basic rules about cancer that apply to everyone.

Sing the sad song, sing it. Let good win.

It's good for someone who lives an easy life with lots of money and land. He doesn't care about the rain falling or trees crashing down. It's good for someone who has never known hard times and hunger. He has never seen his father grow old from sadness and tears. He has never seen his mother cry alone. But it's good for someone whose feet have walked the hard road of work and struggle. Even though his life has been full of pain, he uses his suffering to build a way to get closer to God. 

Oscar F. O'F. Wills Wilde. _S. M. Magdalen College,_ _Oxford._

I do not desire to make any capital out of whatever public interest may attach to my own person. But I believe the clear and simple narrative of an artist's life may often convey useful information, hidden under a word or fact of no apparent importance, but which tallies exactly with the humour or the need of some particular moment. An everyday occurrence, a hastily spoken word, often holds its own opportunity. Experience teaches; and that which has been useful and salutary to me may perchance serve others too. The Author of his own Memoirs must perforce speak frequently, nay constantly, about himself. It has been my endeavour in this book to do so with absolute impartiality. I can lay claim to scrupulous exactness both in detailing facts and in reporting the remarks of

### Plain English:
Saint-Saëns and His Opera "Henri VIII" 209
Nature and Art 225
The Academy of France at Rome 239

Introduction

The pages that follow tell the story of the most important moments in my life as an artist. They describe how these moments changed me personally and affected my career. They also explain what I learned from these experiences. I don't want to use my life story just to get attention from the public. But I think that when an artist tells their life story in a straightforward way, it can teach readers useful lessons. Sometimes these lessons are hidden in small details that don't seem important at first. But they fit perfectly with what someone needs to learn at just the right moment. Everyday events and quick comments can teach us a lot. What I have learned may help other people too. When you write your own memoirs, you have to talk about yourself a lot. In this book, I have tried to do that fairly. I can promise that I have been very careful to get the facts right and to report accurately what people said to me.

### Plain English:
All people were foolish by nature because they didn't know God. They looked at the good things they could see in the world, but they couldn't figure out who made them. They didn't pay attention to what God had created and recognize the creator. Instead, they thought that fire, wind, fast air, stars, water, and the lights in the sky were gods that controlled the world. If they thought these things were gods because they were beautiful, they should know that God, who made all these beautiful things, is much greater. If they were amazed by the power of these things, they should understand that God, who created them, is much more powerful. When people see how beautiful and great created things are, they should be able to understand how great their creator is. But these people never learned this lesson.

WHILE engaged in editing my Handbook of English Proverbs, it occurred to me that a Collection of Foreign Proverbs, arranged in monographs, and brought as far as possible into juxta-position by a General Index, would be an interesting volume, as well to the ordinary reader as to the linguist. And it happened that more than one public writer, in reviewing my Handbook, suggested nearly the same idea, which determined me to realise it as speedily as other engagements would permit. After a lapse of more than two years, and much patient labour, during intervals of business or hours snatched from repose, I am enabled to present my gleanings to the public, and hope they will not be found deficient of grain. It will be seen that many of the proverbs are quite new to the English reader, and that others, hitherto supposed to be essentially, if not exclusively, English, are common to several other languages. A task so various and complicated could not well be executed without aid, nor do I pretend to be master of all the languages included. Accordingly, I

AND A GENERAL INDEX. BY HENRY G. BOHN. LONDON: HENRY G. BOHN, YORK STREET, COVENT GARDEN. 1857. PREFACE. While I was editing my Handbook of English Proverbs, I thought that a book of foreign proverbs would be interesting. I wanted to organize them by topic and use a general index to connect them. I thought regular readers and language experts would both find it useful. Some book reviewers actually suggested the same idea when they reviewed my Handbook. That made me decide to do it. After more than two years of hard work, doing this project in my spare time, I can now show my collection to the public. I hope people will find it worthwhile. Many of these proverbs will be new to English readers. Some proverbs that people thought were only English are actually used in many other languages too. A project this big and difficult would be impossible to do alone. I also don't speak all the languages in this book. So I needed help, and I want to thank the people who gave it to me.

PREFACE Mythology, since it began to receive a scientific handling at all, has been treated as a subordinate branch of history or of ethnology. The "science of religion," as we know it in the works of Burnouf, Müller, and others, is a comparison of systems of worship in their historic development. The deeper inquiry as to what in the mind of man gave birth to religion in any of its forms, what spirit breathed and is ever breathing life into these dry bones, this, the final and highest question of all, has had but passing or prejudiced attention. To its investigation this book is devoted. The analysis of the religious sentiment I offer is an inductive one, whose outlines were furnished by a preliminary study of the religions of the native race of America, a field selected as most favorable by reason of the simplicity of many of its cults, and the absence of theories respecting them. This study was embodied in "The Myths of the New World; a Treatise on the Symbolism and Mythology of the Red Race of America" (second

Until recently, mythology has been studied as a small part of history or the study of cultures. The "science of religion," as we see in the work of Burnouf, Müller, and others, compares different religions and how they developed over time. But there's a bigger question that hasn't gotten much attention. What makes people create religion in the first place? What force keeps religions alive? This is the most important question of all. This book tries to answer it.

I studied religion by looking at facts and examples. I started by studying Native American religions. I chose this area because many Native American beliefs are simple and straightforward. People haven't written complicated theories about them yet.

## Chapter 1

Not many men see their dream job right in front of them when they are thirty-eight years old. But Sir Percy Carlyon did. He got a job as First Secretary at the British Embassy in Washington. His uncle, Lord Baudesert, was the Ambassador. The plan was that after four years, Sir Percy would take over his uncle's job. This dream came true for Sir Percy on a cold, dark December afternoon.

And one can only surmise how long ago it must have been, in the dim unchronicled past, when the idea of human flight first suggested itself to our ancestors. ¶ The ancient mythology of the Greeks reveals an interest in this problem. The philosophers who lived when Rome was ruler of the world, and the alchemists of the middle ages have left time-stained parchments of their thoughts and theories about artificial flight. ¶ And even today, after centuries of trial and error have brought an intangible dream to a streamlined actuality, the glamour and marvel of aviation still grips the minds of men. Hardly any device which humanity has fashioned has taken so lengthy a time to accomplish and, after being achieved, has been able to hold its place in the sun—in war and in peace. ¶ For it was war that brought the rapid development of the airplane into a machine that is serving man so capably as a peace-time vehicle. ### Plain English:
We can only guess how long ago people first thought about flying. It probably happened way back in history, before anyone wrote it down. ¶ The ancient Greeks were interested in flight. And hardly anything has stayed important for both war and peace like the airplane has. ¶ War actually helped the airplane develop fast. It became a machine that could help people during peacetime too. Airplanes bring medicine to sick people. They deliver food to areas that are flooded or snowed in. They carry people thousands of miles in one day. They speed up business by delivering mail. These things show how useful airplanes have become.

Keeping a diary always seemed like just another bad thing in life. Yet now I have a hidden notebook like everyone else.

I know you'll let me give you these essays. They come more from what you know than from what I feel strongly about. You know why I'm publishing them. We have tons of travel writing that's better than other countries have. So we were shocked that France especially was so unknown to us. When we were younger, we tried to explain this strange situation with simple answers. I think both of us have come to understand it better now.

### Original:
WRITER TO THE READER: "Those who know will not tell; those who tell do not know." The old saying applies to woman in to-day's literature. Women writers when they write of women, evade and conceal and palliate. Ancestral reticences, sex loyalties, dissuade the pen. Men writers when they write of women, do so without comprehension. Men understand women only as women choose to have them, with one exception, the family physician. He knows. He sees through the body to the soul. But he may not tell what he sees. Professional honour binds him. Only through the unaccustomed medium of fiction and out of the vatic incense-cloud of pseudonymity may he speak the truth. Being a physician, I must conceal my identity, and, not less securely, the identity of those whom I picture. There is no such suburb as Dorrisdale ... and there are a score of Dorrisdales. There is no such family as the Fentrisses ... and there are a thousand Fentriss families. For the delineation which I have striven to present, honestly and unreservedly, of the twentieth century woman of the luxury-class I beg only the indulgence permissible to a neophyte's pen. I have no other apologia to offer.

### Plain English:
# Rewritten Passage

To the reader: "People who really know something won't talk about it. People who talk about it don't really know." This old saying is true about how women are written about in today's books. Women writers hide the truth when they write about women. Old traditions and loyalty to their own sex stop them from writing honestly. Male writers write about women without actually understanding them. Men only know what women let them know—except for one person: the family doctor. He really knows. He can see past the body to the soul. But he can't tell people what he sees. Professional rules stop him. He can only tell the truth by writing fiction and hiding who he really is. I'm a doctor, so I have to hide who I am. I also have to hide who the people in my book really are. There is no real town called Dorrisdale, but there are dozens of towns just like it. There is no real family called the Fentrisses, but there are thousands of families like them. I tried to write honestly about the women of the wealthy class in the twentieth century. I'm asking only for patience, since this is my first time writing. I have nothing else to say.

But more than most old masters of a type, strong, albeit coarse, Rabelais--from the distinctly marked physical attributes of his chief personages--may claim certain good points which, drawn out and grouped together, ought to fall within the circle of those tales which interest children. I have read Rabelais twice in my life. Each time, I have read him in that old French, which has no master quite so great as he; and each time in Auguste Desrez's edition, which, in its careful _Table des Matières_, learned glossary, quaint notes, Gallicized Latin and Greek words, and a complete _Rabelaisiana_, shows the devotion of the rare editor, who does not distort, because he understands, the Master whom he edits. When I first peeped into his pages I was a lad, altogether too young to be tainted by profanity, while I skipped, true boy-fashion, whole pages to pick out the wondrous story of his Giants. ### Plain English:
# Plain English version:

A person who is happy just getting dirty and crawling around doesn't deserve anything good. People should give him bad looks and close their doors on him. But Rabelais, who was one of the great old writers, was strong even if he was rough. Looking at how he described his main characters, you can actually find some good things about his work. If you put these good things together, his stories could actually be interesting to kids. I have read Rabelais twice in my life. Both times, I read him in old French, and nobody wrote in that language better than he did. Both times I used Auguste Desrez's edition. This edition has a careful table of contents, a learned dictionary, interesting notes, and a complete collection of Rabelais information. You can tell the editor really cared about his work. He didn't mess with the original because he actually understood what the author meant. When I first looked at his book, I was just a kid. I was too young to be bothered by the bad language. Like most boys do, I skipped a lot of pages to get to the cool story about his giants. When I came back to Rabelais many years later, I was older and hopefully smarter. Because I was older, I could understand him better. I could see what was strong about his writing and what was weak.

It was originally edited by Pandit Ísvarachandra Vidyáságara, but a subsequent edition, with no important alterations, was published in 1872 by Pandit Táránátha Tarkaváchaspati. The work had been used by Wilson in his "Sketch of the Religious Sects of the Hindus" (first published in the Asiatic Researches, vol. xvi., Calcutta, 1828); but it does not appear to have been ever much known in India. MS. copies of it are very scarce; and those found in the North of India, as far as I have had an opportunity of examining them, seem to be all derived from one copy, brought originally from the South, and therefore written in the Telugu character. Certain mistakes are found in all alike, and probably arose from some illegible readings in the old Telugu original. As I was at that time the Oriental Secretary of the Bengal Asiatic Society, I was naturally attracted

### Plain English:
I remember well how excited the educated Hindus in Calcutta were when the Sarva-darsana-samgraha by Mádhava Áchárya was published in the Bibliotheca Indica in 1858. Pandit Ísvarachandra Vidyáságara edited it first, but then Pandit Táránátha Tarkaváchaspati published another edition in 1872 that didn't have any major changes. Wilson had used this work in his "Sketch of the Religious Sects of the Hindus" (first published in the Asiatic Researches, volume 16, Calcutta, 1828), but it doesn't seem like the book was ever very well known in India. There are very few handwritten copies of it. The ones I found in North India all seemed to come from one copy that was originally brought from the South and written in Telugu characters. All the copies have the same mistakes in them. These mistakes probably came from parts of the old Telugu original that were hard to read. I noticed the same problem in the Nágarí copies of Mádhava's Commentary on the Black Yajur Veda that people use in North India. I was the Oriental Secretary of the Bengal Asiatic Society at that time, so I was naturally interested.

### Plain English:
But if you ask me what I want for my next birthday, Grace, couldn't you suggest something that shows I'm not a girl anymore? "Just try it on, Rachel. She'll be happy to see you wearing it." "Oh, fine, I'll dress up in it to make her happy. I'm not ungrateful, I promise. It's beautiful and it shows how well nature can be copied. But it's made for a little girl, and today is the day I decided to stop being young." "Oh, Rachel." "Ha! If Rachel is an old maid, what does that make you, Grace? Come on, accept it! There's nothing worse than not realizing when you stop being a young girl." "Of course I know we're not really young girls anymore," said Grace, confused and annoyed. "Exactly. From now on, we're the unmarried sisters of Avonmouth. We belong to each other, like unmarried pairs always do." "Then let me do this to celebrate our marriage," said Grace. She put a wreath of white roses on her sister's head. "You traitor!" cried Rachel. She put her hands up and shook her head, but her sister held her.

# Plain English Version

Lobenstine Printed Privately 1920 COPYRIGHT 1920 BY BELLE W. LOBENSTINE In Loving Memory of My Father WILLIAM C. LOBENSTINE So that those of us who come after can honor and love his memory and live in a way that makes his name proud FOREWORD This book is not really a biography. When Father was alive, during our long walks together or quiet evenings at home, he would tell stories about his early life. He told the same stories over and over again because they meant a lot to him. After he died, we found two diaries in his papers. They were bound in calf leather and told about his trip to California and his trip back home. It seemed like the right thing to do. We worked to read these diaries, which were written in pencil and hard to read. Then we published them with very few changes. We put them together with a short story about his early life and a few notes to explain things. We did this for his family and friends and anyone else who might be interested in reading about this man who came to America looking for the best things in life. We kept the way he wrote the same way it was. It sounds a lot like German writing. We also kept the way he used words the same way.

OTHER STUDIES BY ADMIRAL SIR CYPRIAN BRIDGE, G.C.B. PREFACE The essays collected in this volume are republished in the hope that they may be of some use to those who are interested in naval history. The aim has been to direct attention to certain historical occurrences and conditions which the author ventures to think have been often misunderstood. An endeavour has been made to show the continuity of the operation of sea-power throughout history, and the importance of recognising this at the present day. In some cases specially relating to our navy at different periods a revision of the more commonly accepted conclusions--formed, it is believed, on imperfect knowledge--is asked for. It is also hoped that the intimate connection between naval history in the strict sense and military history in the strict sense has been made apparent, and likewise the fact that both are in reality branches of the general history of a nation and not something altogether distinct from and outside it. In a collection of essays on kindred subjects some repetitions are inevitable, but it is believed that they will be found present only to a moderate extent in the following pages. My nephew, Mr. J. S. C.

### Plain English:
OTHER STUDIES BY ADMIRAL SIR CYPRIAN BRIDGE, G.C.B. PREFACE

The essays in this book are being published again. I hope they will help people who are interested in naval history. I want to draw attention to certain historical events and situations that I believe people have often gotten wrong. I tried to show how sea power has stayed important throughout history. I also tried to show why we need to understand this today.

In some cases, I'm asking people to reconsider what they thought about our navy at different times in the past. I believe these common ideas were based on incomplete information. I also hope I've shown how naval history and military history are closely connected. Both of them are actually parts of a nation's overall history. They are not separate from everything else.

When you write a group of essays about similar topics, there will be some repetition. But I tried to keep it to a reasonable amount in these essays.

My nephew, Mr. J. S. C.

OF RETRACTED LEAVES PREFACE. The following addresses, though spoken at different times, are intentionally connected in subject; their aim being to set one or two main principles of art in simple light before the general student, and to indicate their practical bearing on modern design. The law which it has been my effort chiefly to illustrate is the dependence of all noble design, in any kind, on the sculpture or painting of Organic Form. This is the vital law; lying at the root of all that I have ever tried to teach respecting architecture or any other art. It is also the law most generally disallowed. I believe this must be so in every subject. We are all of us willing enough to accept dead truths or blunt ones; which can be fitted harmlessly into spare niches, or shrouded and coffined at once out of the way, we holding complacently the cemetery keys, and supposing we have learned something. But a sapling truth, with earth at its root and blossom on its branches; or a trenchant truth, that can cut its way through bars and sods; most men, it seems to me, dislike the sight or entertainment of, if by

### Plain English:
# Plain English Version

This book has several talks in it. Even though they were given at different times, they're all about the same topics. The goal is to explain one or two basic rules about art in a way that's easy to understand. I also want to show how these rules matter for modern design. The main rule I've tried to teach is this: all great art, no matter what kind, depends on drawing or painting real living things. This is the most important rule. It's the foundation of everything I've tried to teach about buildings and other kinds of art. But most people don't agree with this rule. I think that's normal. We're all okay with accepting ideas that are dead or boring. We can stick them in empty corners or just bury them and forget about them. We feel good about keeping the keys to that cemetery and pretending we've learned something. But when you have a fresh, living truth — something with roots and flowers — or a sharp truth that can cut through walls and barriers, most people don't want to see it or think about it.

TO THE RIGHT HON. SIR JOHN CHARLES BELL, BART. LORD MAYOR OF LONDON

These memories of the old city that he runs are dedicated to him. This is done with his permission, by the editor.

PREFACE

In 1909, the people of London are putting on a big show called the Pageant. It shows important and grand scenes from London's past. These scenes happened from the time of King Alfred all the way to the 1800s.

These books about Old London are coming out at a good time. People in England are interested in this amazing look at the history of London, which is the most important city in the Empire.

The Pageant will be beautiful, grand, and interesting. It will teach people a lot. But really, London doesn't need a Pageant to show how great it is. London is already like a Pageant itself.

The street names, the buildings, the cathedral, churches, prisons, theaters, the river with its bridges, and many other things all show London's story.

### Plain English:
There are Students' Days on Tuesdays and Wednesdays instead of the days mentioned above. This is a popular handbook about the National Gallery. It includes special notes taken from the writings of John Ruskin. This is volume one, covering foreign schools. It was put together by E. T. Cook with a preface by John Ruskin. A picture that is worth buying is also worth looking at. Every great picture is like a unique manuscript book. Only one copy exists or can ever exist. A National Gallery is like a great library. The books in it must be read while they are on the shelves.

In the long, dim galleries, artists can see amazing things. The paintings show us records from long ago. Gods and goddesses appear in the paintings. They seem to walk around under the fiery forests in Titian's paintings and the yellow skies in Claude's paintings. Rubens painted strong women warriors lifting their arms to fight. A pale light falls on the horses in Vandyke's paintings. In Berghem's paintings, you can see cattle reflected in pools. Murillo painted soft-looking young boys laughing among grape vines from Seville. The purest and most beautiful ideas that poets have imagined appear in these paintings.

MY FIRST JOB FOR THE FOREIGN OFFICE I was walking across Green Park one day feeling really sad. My hat was pulled down over my eyes. I wasn't looking around. ### Plain English:
Instructions came in saying that all reports about flying discs need to be looked into by local FBI offices. Before we got these instructions, two flying disc reports came to our office. The first report came in on July 7, 1947. We have the details in a letter sent to the main FBI office on July 8, 1947. It was called "Flying Discs or Saucers, Miscellaneous." Mr. Fletcher from the main office called us at 8:30 a.m. on July 7, 1947. We didn't investigate this report. The second report came in at 1:20 p.m. on July 11, 1947. It was from someone who runs the Civil Air Patrol in Wisconsin. This group helps the Army Air Forces. He called from Black River Falls, Wisconsin. He told us that a disc-shaped object was found on July 10, 1947. It was about nineteen inches across. A city electrician found it at the Jackson County fairgrounds near Black River Falls, Wisconsin. It was around 3:30 p.m. The disc might have been made of cardboard covered with silver airplane paint. The thing had a small wooden tail like a rudder on the back.

But the basis upon which literature shall be selected and arranged is still a matter of discussion. Chronology, race-correspondence, correlation, and ethical training should all be recognized incidentally; but the main purpose of the teacher of literature is to send children on into life with a genuine love for good reading. To accomplish this, three things should be true of the reading offered: first, it should be _literature_; second, it should be literature of some scope, not merely some small phase of literature, such as the fables, or the poetry of one of the less eminent poets; and third, it should appeal to children's natural interests. Children's interests, varied as they seem, center in the marvelous and the preternatural; in the natural world; and in human life, especially child life and the romantic and heroic aspects of mature life. In the selections made for each grade, we have recognized these different interests. To grade poetry perfectly for different ages is an impossibility; much of the greatest verse is for all ages--that is one reason why it _is_

Everyone who really thinks about education now agrees that literature helps shape how people think and feel. But people still argue about which books should be taught and in what order. Teachers should think about when things happened, different cultures, how ideas connect, and right and wrong. But the real job of a literature teacher is to help kids love reading good books. To do this well, three things need to be true about the books kids read. First, they need to be real literature. Second, they need to cover a wide range of literature, not just one small part like fables or poems by unknown poets. Third, they need to be interesting to kids. Kids care about magical and strange things. They also care about nature and people—especially other kids and exciting parts of adult life. When we pick books for each grade, we think about what kids like. It's impossible to pick poetry perfectly for each age group. The best poems work for everyone—that's one reason why they are great.

His Majesty PREFACE This book has been prepared in accordance with a wish expressed by many known and unknown admirers of my husband's writings, who desire to possess in a portable form the passages that have specially appealed to them in his different works, and in the _Life and Letters_. I have taken this opportunity of adding extracts from private letters, and from the writings he left unfinished, which would not otherwise have become known to any but his own family or a few intimate friends. Those who have read the _Life and Letters_, do not need to be told that Max Müller lived from his earliest years in the firm conviction that all is wisely ordered in this life, and 'all for our real good, though we do not always see it, and though we cannot venture to fathom the wisdom guiding our steps through life.' To others his unswerving trust and faith as shown in these extracts may be a revelation, for he seldom talked on such subjects. This trust and faith gave him strength through the bitter struggles of his early life, taught him resignation during the years when the dearest wish of his heart seemed unattainable,

### Plain English:
This book was made because many people who loved my husband's writings wanted a small book they could carry around. They wanted it to have the parts of his work that meant the most to them. I included extracts from private letters and writings he never finished. Without this book, only his family and close friends would have known about these pieces.

If you read the book about his life and letters, you already know that Max Müller believed from childhood that everything happens for a reason. He thought that everything is good for us, even if we can't see how or understand why. People who haven't read that book might be surprised by how much he trusted in this idea. He didn't talk about it much with other people. This trust gave him strength when life was hard. It helped him accept things when what he wanted most seemed impossible to get.

THE BOSTON STEREOTYPE FOUNDRY. Printed by G. C. Rand & Co. No. 3 Cornhill. DEDICATION. TO RODERICK IMPEY MURCHISON, Esq., F. R. S., Etc., PRESIDENT OF THE GEOLOGICAL SOCIETY. In the autumn of last year, I sat down to write a few geological sketches for a newspaper; the accumulated facts of twenty years crowded upon me as I wrote, and the few sketches have expanded into a volume. Permit me, honored Sir, to dedicate this volume to you. Its imperfections are doubtless many, for it has been produced under many disadvantages; but it is not the men best qualified to decide regarding it whose criticisms I fear most; and I am especially desirous to bring it under your notice, as of all geologists the most thoroughly acquainted with those ancient formations which it professes partially to describe. I am, besides, desirous it should be known, and this, I trust, from other motives than those of vanity, that, when prosecuting my humble researches in obscurity and solitude, the present President of the Geological Society did not deem it beneath him to evince an interest in the results to which they led, and to encourage and assist the inquirer with his advice.

### Plain English:
THE BOSTON STEREOTYPE FOUNDRY. Printed by G. C. Rand & Co. No. 3 Cornhill. DEDICATION. TO RODERICK IMPEY MURCHISON, Esq., F. R. S., Etc., PRESIDENT OF THE GEOLOGICAL SOCIETY. Last autumn, I started writing some short geology pieces for a newspaper. As I wrote, I realized I had twenty years of facts to share. What I thought would be a few short pieces turned into a whole book. I would like to dedicate this book to you, sir. The book has problems in it, because I had to work under difficult conditions. But I'm not worried about what the best experts will say. I really want you to see this book because you know more about ancient rock formations than any other geologist. I also want people to know that when I was doing my geology work alone, you were kind enough to care about what I was learning. You gave me advice and helped me as I studied. I'm grateful for that.

IN NEW GUINEA, I DEDICATE THIS BOOK. PREFACE. As every Bill has a preamble, so, I suppose, every book should have a preface. At any rate, such appears to be the universal custom, and I, being of an orthodox turn of mind, am not going to depart from the general rule. Some months ago I mentioned to an acquaintance of mine, a gentleman of considerable literary attainments, that I purposed writing a book on New Guinea, describing my experiences, etc. He replied "Why, the subject is thrashed out, and writings on New Guinea are overdone." I may state that, as the said gentleman had never been in that country, I was not biassed by his opinion. Had he said that the few writers on New Guinea were thrashed out, he would have been nearer to the mark, as the subject of a country so vast, and the civilised settlement of which is in its infancy, will not be thrashed out for many years to come. We do not all wear the same pair of spectacles. I have endeavoured to describe places and people as they appeared to me. Whether the results as set forth in this work will be found

### Plain English:
I'm dedicating this book to New Guinea. A preface. Every bill has an introduction at the beginning, so I guess every book should too. Anyway, that's what everyone does, and I like to follow the rules. A few months ago I told a friend who knows a lot about writing that I wanted to write a book about New Guinea and describe what happened to me there. He said "Why? People have already written about New Guinea and talked about it to death." I should say that since this guy had never actually been to New Guinea, I didn't really care what he thought. If he had said that the few writers who have written about New Guinea have already covered it, he would have been more right. But New Guinea is huge, and people are just starting to settle there and build cities. So there's still a lot more to say about it for many years. People don't all see things the same way. I tried to describe the places and people the way they looked to me. Whether what I wrote in this book will seem good to people, I don't know.

Lincoln. Among them there surely must be reckoned his capacity for companionship. None more catholic in his selections ever lived. All men were his fellows. He went unerringly and unconsciously for the most part, to the meeting place that awaited him in each man’s nature. There might be a wall, often there was; but he knew, no one better, that there is always a secret door in human walls. Sooner or later he discovered it, put his finger on its spring, passed through and settled into the place behind that was his. His life was rich in companionships with unlikely people, often people who began by contempt or semi-contempt of him. ### Original:
it happens that those natural productions with which we are to a certain extent superficially familiar, are to a great many not only uninteresting, but are regarded as subjects more or less beneath their notice; and by others as deleterious to the human race, and therefore to be cautiously used or scrupulously avoided. Another peculiarity is, that the more we are accustomed to them, the more our interest wanes, and probably at last degenerates into apathetic indifference. We can only attribute these ignorant conceits and apparently unaccountable obliquity of judgment to two causes: an assumption of wisdom, and an unenlightened mind, unwilling to learn and loath to improve. Another hindrance which to a considerable extent precludes the study of what one may truthfully designate every-day subjects, is the restless _furor_ for artful counterfeits of science, which are nothing else than the emanations of vain and visionary minds mixing together, as it were, an amalgam of truth and error. The present age is wonderfully productive of these eccentric ideas, while at the same time it is unhappily pregnant with the most unnatural and anti-healthful habits. The mystified authors take good care to run into the wildest extremes, so that their marvellous

### Plain English:
A lot of people don't care about the natural things around us. Some think these things are boring or not worth thinking about. Others believe they're actually bad for people and should be avoided. Here's something strange: the more we see these things, the less interested we become. Eventually, we just stop caring about them at all. We can blame this on two things. First, people think they're already smart enough and don't need to learn. Second, people have closed minds and don't want to improve. Another problem is that people are obsessed with fake science. It's not real science—it's just made-up ideas from people who let their imaginations run wild. They mix together things that are true and things that are false. Today's world creates a lot of these weird ideas. At the same time, people are developing really unhealthy habits. The people who write these fake ideas always go to crazy extremes. They come up with amazing sounding claims.

EVIDENCE Before we proceed to examine the evidence for miracles and the reality of Divine Revelation which is furnished by the last historical book of the New Testament, entitled the "Acts of the Apostles," it is well that we should briefly recall to mind some characteristics of the document, which most materially affect the value of any testimony emanating from it. Whilst generally asserting the resurrection of Jesus, and his bodily ascension, regarding which indeed it adds fresh details, this work presents to us a new cycle of miracles, and so profusely introduces supernatural agency into the history of the early church that, in comparison with it, the Gospels seem almost sober narratives. The Apostles are instructed and comforted by visions and revelations, and they, and all who believe, are filled with the Holy Spirit and speak with other tongues. The Apostles are delivered from {2} prison and from bonds by angels or by an earthquake. Men fall dead or are smitten with blindness at their rebuke. They heal the sick, raise the dead, and handkerchiefs brought from their bodies cure diseases and expel evil spirits. As a general rule, any document so full of miraculous episodes and supernatural occurrences

The Apostles learn and feel better through visions and revelations. They and all believers are filled with the Holy Spirit and speak in languages they don't know. Angels or earthquakes free the Apostles from prison and chains. People fall dead or go blind when the Apostles tell them to. HOWARD PUGH, DEAR SIR: Having listened, with so much pleasure and profit, to the appropriate and impressive address with which you favored us upon the occasion of the recent celebration of “Independence Day,” we feel that we would be failing in duty to those of our fellow-citizens who were deprived of that gratification, were we to allow the occasion to go by and be forgotten, without taking measures to have your remarks placed upon record, and to secure their dissemination among the reading and thinking members of the community. Our own sentiments are so ably and admirably expressed therein, that we wish to have the privilege of presenting them in that shape to all our friends, not only in our own community, but wherever we can reach them――for even by those who assisted at the original delivery, they will bear perusing often and pondering well. That was a narrative of events reaching over a considerable time. This is the portrait of a man in his personal character, a man whose life takes up only a part of the time treated of in the other work. We have now to look on William as one who, though stranger and conqueror, is yet worthily entitled to a place on the list of English statesmen. There is perhaps no man before or after him whose personal character and personal will have had so direct an effect on the course which the laws and constitution of England have taken since his time. ### Plain English:
William the Conqueror
By Edward A. Freeman

Published by Macmillan and Co., Limited
London, 1913

PREFACE

This small book is the first in a series. It is different from my other book called "Short History of the Norman Conquest." That book told the story of events that happened over a long time. This book is about one man and who he was as a person. His life only covers part of the time I wrote about in the other book.

Now we look at William. He was a foreigner and a conqueror. But he deserves to be called an English leader. Maybe no one before or after him had such a big effect on England's laws and government just by being who he was and what he wanted to do. He was Norman.

For I had been a lifelong student of nautical matters. A side glance showed me the speaker, a white-haired, sunburned old fellow in immaculate evening dress. With him at the table in the restaurant were other similarly clad men, evidently of good station in life, and in their answers and comments these men addressed the white-haired man as Commodore. A navy captain, I thought, promoted on retirement. His talk bore it out. "Yes, sirree," he said, as he thumped the table mildly. "A good, tight merchant ship, with nothing wrong except what might be ascribed to neglect such as light canvas blown away and ropes cast off the pins, with no signs of fire, leak, or conflict to drive the crew out, with plenty of grub in the stores and plenty of water in the tanks. Yet, there she was, under topsails and topgallant-sails, rolling along before a Biscay sea,

### Plain English:
I couldn't help but listen to the conversation at the table next to mine. The orchestra had stopped playing and the people were talking loudly. Also, I noticed they were using nautical words, which caught my attention. I had always been interested in ships and sailing. I glanced over and saw the main speaker. He was an old man with white hair and a sunburned face. He was wearing a fancy evening suit. The other men at his table were dressed the same way. They all seemed to be important people. When they talked to the white-haired man, they called him Commodore. I figured he must be a navy captain who had retired and been promoted. His story supported this idea. "Yes, that's right," he said, tapping the table lightly. "A strong, well-built cargo ship with nothing really wrong with it except some damage from neglect—some sails blown away and ropes that had come loose from the posts. There were no signs of fire, no leaks, and no fighting that would have made the crew leave. There was plenty of food in storage and plenty of water in the tanks. Yet there it was, sailing along with just a few sails up, rolling in the waves of the Bay of Biscay."

How sound his judgment has proved to be may be discovered from the dispatches--carefully worded--in which he describes how Smith-Dorrien conducted the most successful retreat since that of Sir John Moore to Corunna, 1808-9, and how Douglas Haig carried his Army across the Aisne river in the face of the enemy's fire opposition. He includes descriptions of what the area looks like and information from its history that relates to his subject. He could only use information that was available to him.

There are many interesting things about Lancashire that writers could use if they knew how to do it well. But he thinks that what he has included is good, at least as far as it goes.

### Plain English:
# The History of Mary I, Queen of England

[From the Gallery of the University, Oxford]

**THE HISTORY OF MARY I, QUEEN OF ENGLAND**

Based on government records, letters from ambassadors, private letters, and other documents from that time. Written by J. M. Stone. Published in London by Sands & Co., 12 Burleigh Street, Strand, W.C. 1901

**PREFACE**

Right now, historians are fixing old, biased opinions about history. Everyone agrees that we need to study history more fairly and carefully than we used to. So it makes sense to tell the true story of our first female queen. It doesn't need an excuse.

Two books helped me understand Mary as a real person. One was about the Princess Mary's spending money. It had an introduction by Sir Frederick Madden, who worked at the British Museum taking care of old papers. The other book was about Jane Dormer, the Duchess of Feria. A priest named Joseph Stevenson edited it. He used the original manuscript that Lord Dormer owned. These books showed me that Mary was actually a likeable and kind person.

After that, I looked carefully at many papers about her life and time as queen. These papers were spread across different archives all over Europe. They proved that my first impression was right. They also showed that her hard fight against all her problems was more interesting than people thought.

Produced by David Widger OUR SQUARE AND THE PEOPLE IN IT By Samuel Hopkins Adams Illustrated by Scott Williams Boston and New York Houghton Mifflin Company 1917 _WALLED in by slums stands Our Square, a valiant green space, far on the flank of the Great City. Ours is an inglorious little world Sociologists have-not yet remarked and classified us. The Washington Square romancers who bold sentimental revel at the foot of Fifth Avenue reck nothing of their sister park, many blocks to the east. But we are patient of our obscurity. Close-knit, keeping our own counsel, jealous of our own concerns, and not without our own pride of place, we live our quiet lives, a community sufficient unto itself. So far as may be for mortals under the sway of death and love and fate, we maintain ourselves with little change amid the kaleidoscopic shiftings of the surrounding metropolis. Few come into Our Square except of necessity. Few go out but under the same stem impulsion. Some of us are held by tradition, some by poverty, some by affection, and some through loyalty to what once was and is no more. Here we live, and here hope to die, "the

### Plain English:
# Our Square and the People in It

Our Square is a small green space surrounded by poor neighborhoods. It's far out on the edge of the big city. Our world isn't fancy or important. Scientists who study society haven't even bothered to study us yet. People who visit Washington Square, the famous park near Fifth Avenue, don't care about our park. It's many blocks to the east. But we don't mind being ignored. We stick together. We don't tell outsiders our business. We care about ourselves. We're proud of our place. We live quiet lives. We're a community that takes care of itself. As much as people can when they have to deal with death, love, and fate, we stay the same. The city around us keeps changing, but we don't. Not many people come to Our Square unless they have to. Not many people leave unless they have to. Some of us stay because of tradition. Some stay because we're poor. Some stay because of love. Some stay because we're loyal to how things used to be. Here we live. Here we hope to die.

I haven't changed anything on purpose. But I must have changed lots of things without meaning to. I'm writing this after so many years. I haven't looked at old letters or newspapers or asked friends about what happened. I'm just writing down what I remember best. I'm saying all this because some of my old friends might remember things differently. They might get upset with my book because of that.

Everything that makes your spirit grow also scares you. It all gathers at these mountaintops to show how the earth can reach toward Heaven. But it leaves ordinary people down below." BYRON. ### Plain English:

The Town Hall and Cathedral are definitely beautiful, but the Market Hall is even more special because it is one of a kind. Its plain design, its length, its balanced lines, and its roofs that look like huge wings covered with slate tiles all make it look like a giant victory arch. It is so huge that the whole town could fit inside it if they needed to hide there during danger. The Market Hall of Ypres has always been a building for the whole community. In the Middle Ages, cloth makers and weavers used it as their business center. It has seen people riot and fight. It has gone through hard times and happy times, sadness and pride. For hundreds of years it has stood there, amazing everyone in Ypres. ÉMILE VERHAEREN was born in St. Amand, Belgium, on the River Scheldt in May 1855. He died in Rouen, France in November 1916. Verhaeren was a patriotic writer with great ideas, and he was one of the best poets of his time. He "made poetry show the modern world." "At his best, he speaks for the city, the train, the factory, the power plant, and the spirit of crowds and groups of people."

Cæsar In person Cæsar was tall and slight. His features were more refined than was usual in Roman faces; the forehead was wide and high, the nose large and thin, the lips full, the eyes dark gray like an eagle’s, the neck extremely thick and sinewy. His complexion was pale. His hair was short and naturally scanty, falling off toward the end of his life and leaving him partially bald. His voice, especially when he spoke in public, was high and shrill. * * * * * His health was uniformly strong until his last year, when he became subject to epileptic fits. He was scrupulously clean in all his habits, abstemious in his food, rarely or never touching wine, and noting sobriety as the highest of qualities when describing any new people. He was an athlete in early life, admirable in all manly exercises, and especially in riding. From his boyhood it was observed of him that he was the truest of friends, that he avoided quarrels, and was most easily appeased when offended. In manner he was quiet and gentlemanlike, with the natural courtesy of high-breeding. * * * * * He was singularly careful of his soldiers.

### Plain English:
Caesar was tall and thin. His face looked more refined than most Roman faces. He had a wide, high forehead, a large thin nose, full lips, and dark gray eyes like an eagle's. His neck was very thick and muscular. His skin was pale. His hair was short and thin, and as he got older it fell out, leaving him partially bald. When he spoke in public, his voice was high and shrill.

His health was very good until his last year, when he started having epileptic fits. He was very clean in his habits. He ate little and rarely or never drank wine. He thought being sober was the best quality and praised it when he described new people. When he was young, he was an athlete and great at all physical activities, especially horseback riding. People noticed from his childhood that he was a loyal friend. He avoided fights and was quick to forgive when someone upset him. He was quiet and well-mannered, with the natural politeness that comes from a good family. He took very good care of his soldiers.

I have in the present volume dealt with the intricate phenomena of different tropisms. In this volume I have looked at the complicated ways plants move and respond to their environment. Plants move in response to things around them. Vines curl and twist. Temperature affects them. Light makes them move toward it or away from it. Shoots and roots respond to gravity in completely opposite ways. Plants change the position of their leaves during the day and night. There are many other examples. All these different movements seemed so varied that it looked impossible to find one basic rule that explained all of them. So people got used to saying that plants had different abilities, each one helping the plant survive. Introduction by Frederic Harrison:

People have been talking about Positivism a lot lately. Philosophers, religious people, regular readers, and newspapers have all discussed it. But probably not many of them really understand what it means. Positivism is a system for both how to think and how to live.

UNIVERSITY PREFACE It has long been held that the laws of the Israelites, as revealed by God to Moses, by him embodied in the books of the Pentateuch and since preserved by the zealous care of the Jewish people, are incomparable. Accordingly they have been adopted professedly by most Christian nations and were early accepted by our own king Alfred[1] as the basis of the law system of this our land. We live in an age of devotion to comparative methods, when it is an article of faith to hold that the most fruitful means to attain a clear understanding of the exact nature of anything is to compare it with its like. This comparative method forms a large part of modern scientific research and, with proper safeguards and reserves, has become a favourite weapon of literary research into the history of human institutions. Long ago, as it seems to us, SIR HENRY MAINE used it[2] when he wrote his _History of Early Law_. As a consequence of his investigations and those of many who have followed in his footsteps, the Science of Comparative Law has grown up. All the great law systems of the world have been classified and

### Plain English:
People have always believed that the laws of the Israelites were the best. God gave these laws to Moses, and Moses wrote them down in the first five books of the Bible. Jewish people have kept these laws safe for thousands of years. Most Christian countries have used these laws as their own. Even King Alfred of England used them as the basis for English law. Today, scientists and researchers believe that the best way to understand something is to compare it with similar things. This method of comparing things is a big part of modern science and research. It helps us understand the history of how people made their laws and institutions. A long time ago, Sir Henry Maine used this method when he wrote his book called "History of Early Law." Because of his work and the work of many others after him, a new field of study was created. It's called Comparative Law. Experts studied all the major legal systems in the world and organized them into groups.

### Plain English:
The past twenty years have let me watch these research projects at different stages. I found them so interesting that I thought doctors and other readers might want to read about them. Some people might care about it because of the religion side of things, not just because they study old Greek buildings. That's why I tried to show what the old buildings probably looked like and what people did in them. Someone like me who studies how a religion developed can probably guess about things more freely than someone who studies archaeology scientifically. I'm not saying I came up with the basic facts. Some of my drawings and reconstructions are my own ideas. So are some of my theories and explanations. For example, I have ideas about what the Tholos and the Circular Pit at the Asklepieion were used for. I think the Square Building was a Prytaneion, where people held ritual meals and kept a fire burning all the time. I think some buildings were places where visitors stayed. I also have a theory about why the ancient Greeks picked certain spots to build theaters. Whether these ideas are correct or not remains to be seen.

### Plain English:
A young man was riding his horse as fast as he could down the Wyoming Valley toward a settlement. He could see smoke rising into the air and hear gunshots and people yelling. "I hope I'm not too late," he thought to himself several times. He pushed his horse to go even faster. The road felt like it was taking forever. He had good reason to worry. His family lived in the Wyoming Valley, and they meant more to him than anything. Whatever happened to the settlers would happen to him too. His horse was sweating and covered in foam from running so hard. Even though he knew every turn in the road, it seemed twice as long because he was so anxious to get there. Fred Godfrey's father had promised him that he could join the army fighting for independence as soon as he turned seventeen. On the day Fred turned seventeen, he put on his uniform.

The house was small—it only had three windows in the front. This is where his two kids grew up. He rented the first-floor rooms to an old couple who didn't have kids. He would never kick them out. He respected the old man because he used to be a famous singer. Back when he was young, he had heard the man sing and loved it.

They made up this shared pen name from their middle names (Philip and Mark). They came up with it right after they first met at a science fiction convention. Both guys were drunk, which explains a lot. Only one of them has ever gotten sober. The two writers argue about which one all the time. They've been working together for a while now. They came up with an interesting way to write: Mr. Garrett writes the verbs, adverbs, and interjections. Mr. Janifer writes the nouns, pronouns, and adjectives. They decide on conjunctions together. If they tie, they replay the whole thing at Fenway Park in Boston early the next year. BRAIN TWISTER took fifteen years to make. Only three days of that time was spent actually writing it. When the book was done, both writers felt good about what they had done together.
