OF RETRACTED LEAVES PREFACE. The following addresses, though spoken at different times, are intentionally connected in subject; their aim being to set one or two main principles of art in simple light before the general student, and to indicate their practical bearing on modern design. The law which it has been my effort chiefly to illustrate is the dependence of all noble design, in any kind, on the sculpture or painting of Organic Form. This is the vital law; lying at the root of all that I have ever tried to teach respecting architecture or any other art. It is also the law most generally disallowed. I believe this must be so in every subject. We are all of us willing enough to accept dead truths or blunt ones; which can be fitted harmlessly into spare niches, or shrouded and coffined at once out of the way, we holding complacently the cemetery keys, and supposing we have learned something. But a sapling truth, with earth at its root and blossom on its branches; or a trenchant truth, that can cut its way through bars and sods; most men, it seems to me, dislike the sight or entertainment of, if by

### Plain English:
# RETRACTED LEAVES PREFACE

The talks in this book were given at different times, but they connect to each other on purpose. They try to explain one or two basic ideas about art in a simple way for students. They also show how these ideas actually matter for design today.

I mainly want to show one important rule. All great design—any kind—depends on studying the shapes of living things through sculpture or painting. This is the most important rule. It's the foundation of everything I've ever tried to teach about architecture or any other art.

But this is also the rule that most people reject. I think this happens with every subject. We're all happy to accept ideas that are dead or boring. We can stick them in empty spaces and forget about them. We act like we learned something and move on.

But people don't like living ideas. These are ideas with real roots and flowers. Or sharp ideas that can break through obstacles. Most people don't want to see or deal with them if they can help it.

### Plain English:
# Plain English Version

This book is respectfully dedicated to those friends who have been part of many of these scenes.

**PREFACE**

I wrote this book because I wanted to help spread a pure and beautiful art form. I wanted to improve and expand a real system for creating beautiful arrangements and displays. I hoped to help more people notice and appreciate the grace and elegance in everyday objects around them. I also wanted to encourage everyone to develop a love for beautiful things.

Art shouldn't only exist in an artist's studio. Beauty should make every home better. Art should inspire everyone's mind. Art is powerful and doesn't belong in just one place or one type of location. Beauty is still beautiful whether it's in a small cottage or a huge palace. A great picture will be recognized and loved no matter where you see it or who made it. In the end, nature is still nature. And the feeling of poetry is the same wherever you find it, no matter where that happens to be.

I do not really care how the naturalists maltreat language, but I do strenuously object to the earthiness of their ideas. They have made our literature the incarnation of materialism--and they glorify the democracy of art! "Say what you will, their theory is pitiful, and their tight little method squeezes all the life out of them. Filth and the flesh are their all in all. They deny wonder and reject the extra-sensual. I don't believe they would know what you meant if you told them that artistic curiosity begins at the very point where the senses leave off. "You shrug your shoulders, but tell me, how much has naturalism done to clear up life's really troublesome mysteries? When an ulcer of the soul--or indeed the most benign little pimple--is to be probed, naturalism can do nothing. 'Appetite and instinct' seem to be its sole motivation and rut and brainstorm its chronic states. The field of naturalism is the region below the umbilicus. Oh, it's a hernia clinic and it offers the soul a truss! "I tell you, Durtal, it's superficial quackery, and that isn't all. This fetid naturalism eulogizes the atrocities of modern life and flatters our positively American ways.

### Plain English:
I don't really care how the naturalists mess up language, but I do strongly object to how earthly and basic their ideas are. They've made our literature all about materialism—and they celebrate the democracy of art! Say what you want, but their theory is weak, and their tight little method removes all the life from their work. Filth and the body are all they care about. They ignore wonder and reject anything beyond the senses. I don't think they would understand what you meant if you told them that real artistic curiosity starts exactly where the senses stop. You shrug your shoulders, but tell me, how much has naturalism actually done to explain life's real difficult problems? When you need to deal with a deep wound of the soul—or even just a small pimple—naturalism can't help. "Appetite and instinct" seem to be all that drives it, and being stuck in a rut and having crazy thoughts are its normal states. Naturalism only deals with the region below the belly button. Oh, it's like a hernia clinic and it offers the soul a medical support belt! I'm telling you, Durtal, it's shallow fake medicine, and that's not even all of it. This disgusting naturalism praises the horrible parts of modern life and flatters our very American ways.

Baby Harriett laughed. The sound of her laugh was so funny that she laughed again at that; she kept on laughing, with shriller and shriller squeals. “I wonder why she thinks it’s funny,” her mother said. Her father considered it. “I don’t know. The cat perhaps. The cat and the Queen. But no; that isn’t funny.” “She sees something in it we don’t see, bless her,” said her mother. Each kissed her in turn, and the Baby Harriett stopped laughing suddenly. “Mamma, _did_ Pussycat see the Queen?” “No,” said Mamma. “Just when the Queen was passing the little mouse came out of its hole and ran under the chair. That’s what Pussycat saw.” Every evening before bedtime she said the same rhyme, and Harriett asked the same question. When Nurse had gone she would lie still in her cot, waiting. The door would open, the big pointed shadow would move over the ceiling, the lattice shadow of the fireguard would fade and go away, and Mamma would come in carrying the lighted candle. Her face shone white between her long, hanging curls. She would stoop over the cot and lift Harriett up, and her face would be hidden in curls.

### Plain English:
Baby Harriett laughed. Her laugh was so funny that she laughed again just from hearing it. She kept laughing, getting louder and louder with high-pitched squeals. "I wonder why she thinks it's funny," her mother said. Her father thought about it. "I don't know. Maybe the cat. The cat and the Queen. But no, that's not funny." "She sees something we don't see, that's for sure," said her mother. They each kissed her, and Baby Harriett stopped laughing suddenly. "Mommy, did the cat see the Queen?" "No," said Mommy. "When the Queen was walking by, a little mouse came out of its hole and ran under the chair. That's what the cat saw." Every night before bed she told the same story, and Harriett asked the same question. After the nurse left, she would lie still in her crib and wait. The door would open. A big pointed shadow would move across the ceiling. The shadow from the fireplace screen would fade and disappear. Then Mommy would come in carrying a lit candle. Her face was white between her long curls hanging down. She would lean over the crib and pick Harriett up. Then her face would disappear into her curls.

### Plain English:
I had many quiet nights where I slept deeply and sweetly. Fairy people would visit me in my dreams. I saw them flying east and west in my dreams. They brought wonderful fairy gifts to bless the newborn baby. One brought a jewel. One brought a gold crown. One brought a curse, but she was wrinkled and old. The gentle queen turned pale when she heard these evil words. But the king just laughed and told everyone to start dancing. The baby grew up and became the most beautiful person in the land. She rode through the green forest with a hawk on her hand. She had a white horse, a gold robe, and a crown. I saw her riding back and forth in my dreams. I heard an ogre laugh as she fell into his trap. She was a small, tender creature who cried and pulled at her hair. But whenever things seemed really bad for her, a prince in shining armor came riding through the forest on a horse. He had a waving feather on his helmet and a bright shield. I saw him in my dreams. He was a great knight. His lips were coral red under a dark mustache. You could see him wave his hand and see his blue eyes flash. "Come out, you pagan knight!" he shouted clearly. The giant and the girl both trembled.

The poem covers a huge range; Paul is a majestic main character; the other characters are fascinating; the characters are drawn carefully; the interviews are sad and moving; the plot is complex but makes sense; the poem uses the Bible carefully and accurately; the images are beautiful; the moral lessons are noble; and the style is grand and formal."

in cities and towns, and at work which keeps them nearly all day in-doors. Many hours are devoted for days and years, under careful teachers, and many millions of dollars are spent annually, in educating the mind and the moral nature. But the body is allowed to grow up all uneducated; indeed, often such a weak, shaky affair that it gets easily out of order, especially in middle and later life, and its owner is wholly unequal to tasks which would have proved easy to him, had he given it even a tithe of the education bestowed so generously in other directions. Not a few, to be sure, have the advantage in youth of years of active out-door life on a farm, and so lay up a store of vigor which stands them in good stead throughout a lifetime. But many, and especially those born and reared in towns and cities, have had no such training, or any equivalent, and so never have the developed lungs and muscles, the strong heart and vigorous digestion--in short, the improved tone and strength in all their vital organs--which any sensible plan of body-culture, followed up daily, would have secured. It does not matter

### Plain English:
People in cities and towns spend most of their day indoors at work. They spend many hours, days, and years learning from teachers. Millions of dollars are spent every year on education. But nobody teaches people how to take care of their bodies. The body grows up without any training. Often it becomes weak and fragile. It breaks down easily, especially when people get older. Then people can't do things that would have been easy if they had taken care of their bodies. Some people are lucky. When they're young, they grow up on farms doing outdoor work. This makes them strong, and they stay healthy their whole lives. But many people, especially those who grow up in cities and towns, never get this kind of training. They never develop strong lungs and muscles. They never build a strong heart or good digestion. In short, they never get the strength and health in all their organs that they would have gotten from daily exercise and body training. It doesn't matter.

### Plain English:

So sings the poet, and so agrees the world. Humiliating as it is to make the confession, it is undeniably true. "Men and Dress are all women think about," cry the lords of creation in their unbounded vanity. And again, we must submit--and agree--to the truth of the accusation; at any rate, in nine cases out of ten. Fortunately I am a tenth case; at least, I consider myself so. I don't dispute the "dress" imputation. I am very fond of dress. Nearly as fond of it as the twenty-year old youth, and saying that, I allow a good deal. But very few of my thoughts are given to the creature "man"! I do not think him worth it. As my old nurse used to say, "I never 'ad no opinion of the sex!" Do not conclude, however, that because of my statement that I am a disappointed, soured old maid, for I am nothing of the sort. I am on the right side of twenty-five, and I have never been crossed in love; indeed, I have never even experienced the tender passion, and only write from my observations of other people; thus taking a perfectly neutral ground in speaking

### Plain English:
The poet sings about this, and the whole world agrees. It's embarrassing to admit, but it's true. "All women think about are men and clothes," say the men, acting like they know everything. And honestly, we have to agree with them most of the time. In nine out of ten cases, they're right. Lucky for me, I'm that tenth case. At least, I think I am. I won't deny that I like clothes. I really like them. Almost as much as a twenty-year-old guy likes them, and that's saying a lot. But I don't spend much time thinking about men. I don't think they're worth it. My old nurse used to say, "I never had any opinion of men!" Don't think I'm some bitter old woman who never found love, because I'm not. I'm under twenty-five, and I've never had my heart broken. I've never even been in love. I'm just writing about what I've seen in other people's lives. So I'm not taking sides either way.

In other words, I had the good or ill luck, as you may choose to think it, to be the possessor of a Texas-Land-Scrip--that is to say, a certificate issued by the Galveston Bay and Texas Land Company, declaring and making known to all whom it might concern, that Mr Edward Morse had paid into the hands of the cashier of the said company the sum of one

I'm going to answer that question first. It's probably the best way to start telling you about my time in that country. A lot of people back home have been surprised—and they're being nice about it—that I, the son of Judge Morse from Maryland, would leave my home state for Texas. At that time, Texas didn't have a very good reputation. Most of the white people living there were criminals and bad guys who had run away from the rest of America. Here's what actually happened: I went to Texas because a group of businessmen from New York were interested in investing money there. I had invested money too. To be honest, I had what you could call good luck or bad luck—depending on how you look at it. I owned a Texas Land Certificate. This was a document from the Galveston Bay and Texas Land Company. It said that Edward Morse had paid a certain amount of money to the company's cashier. The document was supposed to prove this to anyone who needed to know.

### Plain English:
# SOUL OF THE INDIAN

Produced by Judith Boss

THE SOUL OF THE INDIAN
An Interpretation By Charles Alexander Eastman (Ohiyesa)

TO MY WIFE ELAINE GOODALE EASTMAN

I am dedicating this book to you. I want to thank you for always being there with me. You support me in my thinking, my work, and my love. You show the best Indian values and qualities.

I speak for each tree that has no voice. Every spring it grows stronger and better. It spreads out its big arms like it's praying. It sends down its blessings to everything below.
--SIDNEY LANIER

But there is a better, bigger dome. It is a temple for your faith. No one can stop it or ban it. Its space is heaven! Its roof is the sky and stars that nature made. The spirit feels amazed and moved by this. God shows himself to people through it. The spheres in space make music together. We can't hear the sound with our ears! But it's there.
--THOMAS CAMPBELL

God! Sing, you streams in the meadows! Be happy and joyful! You pine trees, make your soft and beautiful sounds! You eagles, you play with the storms on the mountains! You lightning bolts, you are the scary arrows shot from the clouds! You signs and wonders in nature, speak about God! Fill the hills with praise!... Earth, with all her thousand voices, praises GOD!
--COLERIDGE

FOREWORD

to me dearer Friends than you!_ The Humorous Ghost INTRODUCTION The humorous ghost is distinctly a modern character. In early literature wraiths took themselves very seriously, and insisted on a proper show of respectful fear on the part of those whom they honored by haunting. A mortal was expected to rise when a ghost entered the room, and in case he was slow about it, his spine gave notice of what etiquette demanded. In the event of outdoor apparition, if a man failed to bare his head in awe, the roots of his hair reminded him of his remissness. Woman has always had the advantage over man in such emergency, in that her locks, being long and pinned up, are less easily moved--which may explain the fact (if it be a fact!) that in fiction women have shown themselves more self-possessed in ghostly presence than men. Or possibly a woman knows that a masculine spook is, after all, only a man, and therefore may be charmed into helplessness, while the feminine can be seen through by another woman and thus disarmed. The majority of the comic apparitions, curiously enough, are masculine. You don't often find women wraithed in smiles--perhaps because

INTRODUCTION

The funny ghost is a modern invention. In old stories, ghosts were serious. They demanded that people be really scared of them when they showed up to haunt them. When a ghost walked into a room, you were supposed to stand up out of respect. If you didn't do it fast enough, fear made your body do it for you. If a ghost appeared outside, a man was expected to take off his hat and show respect. If he didn't, his hair would stand on end and remind him he messed up. Women have always had an easier time than men in these situations. Their long hair is pinned up, so it doesn't move as easily. This might be why women in stories seem calmer around ghosts than men do. Or maybe women know that a male ghost is just a man after all, so they can charm him into being harmless. Meanwhile, a woman can see through a female ghost and stop being scared. Funny ghosts are usually male. You don't see many female ghosts trying to be funny. Maybe it's because...

Aulus Gellius,[2] who gives a more circumstantial detail of this uncommon law, affirms the penalty to be "no less than confiscation of all the effects, and banishment of the delinquent." Cicero mentions the same law to his friend Atticus,[3] and even makes the punishment capital, though he resolves at the same time not to conform to it under his present circumstances, unless his friend should advise him to the contrary. Which of these relators has given us the real penalty annexed to this law by Solon, is scarce worth our inquiry. But I cannot help observing, that strange as this law may appear at first sight, yet if we reflect upon the reasons of it, as they are assigned by Plutarch and A. Gellius, it will not appear unworthy of that great legislator. The opinion of Plutarch is; "that Solon intended no citizen, as soon as ever he had provided for the security of his own private affairs, should be so unfeeling with respect to the publick welfare, as to affect a brutal insensibility,[4] and not to sympathize with the distress and calamities of his country: but that he should

He says the punishment was "at least losing all your stuff and being forced to leave the country." Cicero talks about the same law to his friend Atticus. He even says the punishment could be death. But at the same time, he says he won't follow this law in his situation right now. He'll only do it if his friend tells him to. We don't really need to figure out which of these people got the real punishment right. But I think it's worth noting that even though this law seems weird at first, if you think about why it existed, based on what Plutarch and Aulus Gellius said, it was actually a pretty smart law. Plutarch's idea was this: Solon didn't want any citizen to focus only on their own private life and then act like they don't care about their country. He didn't want them to be so cold and heartless that they didn't feel bad when their country was in trouble. Instead, he wanted them to

Mons. Corroyer writes, indeed, from a thoroughly French standpoint. He is apt to believe that everything admirable in Gothic architecture had a Gallic origin. Vexed questions of priority, such as that attaching to the choir of Lincoln, he dismisses with a phrase, while the larger question of French influence generally in these islands of ours, he solves by the simple process of referring every creation which takes his fancy either to a French master or a French example, here coming, be it said, into occasional collision with his own stock authority, the late Mons. Viollet-le-duc. The Chauvinistic tone thus given to his pages may be regretted, but, when all is said, it does not greatly affect their value as a picture of Gothic development. Mons. Corroyer confines himself in the main to broad principles. He travels along the line of evolution, pointing out how material conditions and discoveries, and their consequent social changes, brought about one development after another in the forms and methods of the architect. ### Plain English:
These pages have been translated by Miss Florence Simmonds under my supervision. They explain how Gothic Architecture started and developed. This should be enough information for a handbook. Mons. Corroyer writes from a French point of view. He tends to believe that everything good in Gothic architecture came from France. When there are disagreements about which building came first, like the Lincoln Cathedral choir, he just gives a quick answer. When it comes to French influence in Britain, he solves it by saying that every building he likes either came from a French architect or a French example. Sometimes this disagrees with what Viollet-le-duc, his main source, said. It's unfortunate that Corroyer's writing is so pro-French. But honestly, it doesn't really hurt how useful these pages are for understanding how Gothic architecture developed. Corroyer mostly sticks to the big ideas. He shows how practical things and new discoveries led to social changes. These changes then caused architects to develop new building styles and methods. He follows this line of development throughout.

The visit was always a pleasant one, and greatly enjoyed by the small visitor, but there was always a wrench at parting, for the Maynard family were affectionate and deeply devoted to one another. The night before the departure was always celebrated by a festival of farewell, and at this feast tokens were presented, and speeches made, and songs sung, all of which went far to dispel sad or gloomy feelings. She had her world in her time, but that world and time disappeared with the French Revolution [a]. Now even professed students of the novel shrink from reading many of her seventy odd volumes, nor can the infamous celebrity conferred by Pope's attack in "The Dunciad" save her name from oblivion. But the significance of Mrs. Haywood's contributions cannot safely be ignored. Her romances of palpitating passion written between 1720 and 1730 formed a necessary complement to Defoe's romances of adventure exactly as her Duncan Campbell pamphlets supplied the one element lacking in his. The domestic novels of her later life foreshadowed the work of Miss Burney and Miss Austen, while her career as a woman of letters helped to open a new profession to her sex. Since even the weakest link in the development of a literary form is important, I have endeavored to provide future historians of English fiction with a compact and accurate account of this pioneer "lady novelist." Hitherto the most complete summary of Mrs. Haywood's life and writings has been Sir

### Plain English:
Her novels had old-fashioned charm at first, but when fashion changed, nobody wanted to read them anymore. She was popular in her time, but that time ended with the French Revolution. Today even people who study novels don't want to read most of her seventy books. Even a famous attack on her by Pope in "The Dunciad" can't keep her name alive. But we shouldn't ignore what Mrs. Haywood did. Her exciting romance novels from 1720 to 1730 were important because they were different from Defoe's adventure stories. Her pamphlets about Duncan Campbell filled in gaps that Defoe left. Her later domestic novels showed what Miss Burney and Miss Austen would later write. Also, she helped open up writing as a job for women. Every part of how novels developed matters, even the weak parts. Preface

A long time ago, people in the South started singing religious songs using folk tunes. This does not mean they did not respect religion. Actually, it shows they loved and respected their traditional music even more. They thought this music was their most important treasure. They brought this great music and used it in their worship. This practice probably continued without stopping for at least thirteen hundred years. William of Malmsbury wrote about this in the twelfth century.

The studies of Greek Poets, now reprinted, appeared in England in two series, published at an interval of three or four years. In preparing this edition, I have rearranged the chapters of both series in their proper order, and have made certain additions, with the view of rendering the book more complete as a survey of Greek Poetry. Thus I have inserted several new translations in the chapters on the Lyric Poets and the Anthology. The criticism of Euripides has been enlarged, and the concluding chapter has been, in a great measure, rewritten. Each chapter has undergone such revision and alteration in minor details as might remove unnecessary repetitions and bring the whole series of essays into harmony. At the same time I have judged it inexpedient to introduce radical changes into a book which professes to be the reprint of volumes already known to the English public. For this reason the chapters which deal with the Greek Tragedians have been left substantially in their original form, and bear upon their face the record of their composition as almost independent essays.

These books are dedicated to shared interests, work we did together, and sixteen years of friendship.

PREFACE TO THE AMERICAN EDITION

These studies about Greek poets were first printed in England. They came out in two separate groups, a few years apart. For this new edition, I put the chapters from both groups in the right order. I also added new material to make the book a better overall look at Greek poetry. I added some new translations in the chapters about lyric poets and the anthology. I expanded the section on Euripides and mostly rewrote the last chapter. I went through each chapter and fixed small details to remove things that were repeated and make all the essays work better together. However, I didn't want to make big changes to a book that was already known to English readers. Because of this, the chapters about Greek tragedians stayed mostly the way they were. You can still see that they were written as separate pieces.

# THE JEALOUSY OF LE BARBOUILLÉ

Among the short funny plays that Molière supposedly wrote when he was traveling around the provinces, only two real ones have survived. They've been published with his comedies for thirty years. Molière himself played the part of Le Barbouillé.

Whilst generally asserting the resurrection of Jesus, and his bodily ascension, regarding which indeed it adds fresh details, this work presents to us a new cycle of miracles, and so profusely introduces supernatural agency into the history of the early church that, in comparison with it, the Gospels seem almost sober narratives. The Apostles are instructed and comforted by visions and revelations, and they, and all who believe, are filled with the Holy Spirit and speak with other tongues. The Apostles are delivered from {2} prison and from bonds by angels or by an earthquake. Men fall dead or are smitten with blindness at their rebuke. As a general rule, any document so full of miraculous episodes and supernatural occurrences

The old spiritual names for these changes were Purgation, Illumination, and Union. But even theologians tell us that the mysterious thing Catholics call God's Grace doesn't erase who someone naturally is. Instead, it makes their natural qualities even stronger and more beautiful. I think it was the same thing in Frank. The same basic part of him is what made him do those weird things with bread and butter.

* * * * * The First Chapter ABNER FELLOWS was born in the front bedroom of Number Eleven Hackett’s Cottages, a four-roomed house of old brickwork that stood in the middle of a row of twenty-one, set diagonally across a patch of waste land on the outskirts of Halesby. The terrace was fifty years old, and looked older, for the smoke and coal dust of the neighbouring pits had corroded the surface of the bricks, while the ‘crowning in’ of the earth’s crust above the gigantic burrowings of the Great Mawne Colliery had loosened the mortar between them and even produced a series of long cracks that clove the house-walls from top to bottom like conventional forked lightning. One of these lines of cleavage split the face of Number Eleven and ran through the middle of a plaster plaque on which the pious owner of the cottages had carved the words:— _ISAIAH HACKETT_: _GLORY BE TO GOD_, _1839_. This plaque, together with the metal medallion of a fire insurance corporation and two iron bosses connected with the system of stays by which Mr Hackett’s descendants had tried to save their property from collapsing, made the Fellows’s house the most

### Plain English:
Abner Fellows was born in the front bedroom of Number Eleven Hackett's Cottages. It was a four-room house made of old brick. It stood in the middle of a row of twenty-one identical houses. They were built diagonally across some empty land on the edge of Halesby. The row of houses was fifty years old. It looked even older than that. Smoke and coal dust from the nearby coal mines had damaged the surface of the bricks. The ground underneath had settled because of the huge coal mines beneath it. This had loosened the mortar between the bricks. Long cracks had appeared all over the house walls. They looked like lightning bolts. One of these cracks went through Number Eleven. It ran right through the middle of a plaque on the wall. The plaque had been put there by the original owner, Isaiah Hackett. It said: "ISAIAH HACKETT: GLORY BE TO GOD, 1839." This plaque, a metal insurance medallion, and two iron pieces that held up the walls made the Fellows's house stand out from the others.

I didn't want to leave the beautiful lake view, but I had to move to the northern part of the city. I found a very quiet street that was behind an old, crumbling castle. My new home is an old samurai house. It's blocked off from the street by a long, high wall topped with tiles. You walk up a low, wide set of stone steps to reach the gate, which is almost as big as the entrance to a temple. A lookout window sticks out from the wall to the right of the gate. It has heavy bars on it, like a big wooden cage. In the old days, armed guards would watch from this window to see everyone who walked by. They could see out, but people on the road couldn't see them because the bars were so close together. Once you go through the gate, the path to the house is also blocked off by walls on both sides. So if you weren't someone important, you could only see the house entrance in front of you, and it was always closed.

At the bottom of his prayer chair was always a small black wooden box chained up. It was tied with iron. Inside he kept a tiny yellow man. He was a demon that was very small and compact. He was hardly a foot long. This creature could fly through the air when he had to do something or when he was told to.

governess and had brought us all up, and my memories of her, like my love for her, went as far back as my memories of myself. Sonia was my younger sister. The winter had dragged by, sad and sombre, in our old country-house of Pokrovski. The weather had been cold, and so windy that the snow was often piled high above our windows; the panes were almost always cloudy with a coating of ice; and throughout the whole season we were shut in, rarely finding it possible to go out of the house. It was very seldom that any one came to see us, and our few visitors brought neither joy nor cheerfulness to our house. They all had mournful faces, spoke low, as if they were afraid of waking some one, were careful not to laugh, sighed and often shed tears when they looked at me, and above all at the sight of my poor Sonia in her little black frock. Everything in the house still savored of death; the affliction, the horror of the last agony yet reigned in the air. Mamma's chamber was shut up, and I felt a painful dread and yet an irresistible longing to

### Plain English:
The governess raised all of us, and I have loved her for as long as I can remember myself. Sonia was my younger sister. Winter had been long, sad, and dark in our old house called Pokrovski. It was freezing cold and very windy. The snow piled up so high that it covered our windows. Ice formed on the glass almost every day. We were stuck inside all winter and hardly ever went outside. Almost no one visited us. The few people who did come didn't make us feel happy or cheerful. They all looked sad. They spoke quietly, like they were afraid to wake someone up. They didn't laugh. They would sigh and cry when they looked at me. They cried even more when they saw my poor sister Sonia in her little black dress. Everything in the house still felt like death was there. Sadness and fear from the last terrible days still filled the air. Mamma's bedroom was locked shut. I felt scared and sad, but I also really wanted to go in there.

It's one of the biggest armies fighting with the Allies. We also have our own government in Paris. The Allies recognize it. All Czechs living in other countries recognize it. Even Czech leaders back home in Bohemia recognize it. We've worked together with them since the start of the war with complete agreement. Our new independent country is being set up quickly. Austria-Hungary is now broken and destroyed. It ran out of money and lost all its political power. The people living under their rule broke free on their own. They stopped following the Habsburg and Hohenzollern rulers before Austria even officially gave up to the Allies. Things have been happening very fast in Austria. This started especially after Britain made an important announcement.

### Plain English:
# Ten Girls from History

This book is like another book called Ten Boys from History. The goal is to tell you about girls who did important things when they were young. These girls all had courage as their most important character trait. Many sources were used to tell these stories accurately. We focused on getting the facts right rather than making the stories more exciting. This book is meant to introduce you to these girls before you read longer history books about them.

## Jeanne d'Arc: The Maid of France

Five hundred years ago, a girl was born in a small, peaceful village in France called Domrémy. This village is in a valley near the river Meuse, south of the area called Bar. People called her the "Maid of France." She became famous and is remembered as a hero in history. But when she was young, other girls her age were busy with games and household chores. Jeanne, however, was dreaming big. She was planning a huge military campaign that would eventually make her one of France's greatest heroes. The parish register recorded her birth.

But whenever things got really bad, a prince in shining armor came riding through the forest on his horse. He had a feather on his helmet and a shiny shield. I've seen him in my dreams. He was a brave and handsome knight. His lips were red under his dark mustache. You could see how he waved his hand and how his blue eyes shone! "Come out, you evil knight!" he shouted loudly and clearly. The giant and the girl both shook with fear.

It was noon on a perfect summer day. Paul Mario lay back in a white deck-chair on the terrace under green sun shades. He had finished lunch and was looking out at the view. He could see sloping grass, paths with roses, and old brick steps going down to some fir trees. Beyond that, a river wound through green banks toward a distant town. A lark sang beautifully in the sky, climbing higher and higher. Swallows that had recently come from southern Europe flew around Hatton Towers in patterns. Young birds chirped loudly from nests under the roof edges. Bees worked in the climbing roses. Ants swarmed near Mario's feet, crawling over something that was still moving.

### Plain English:
The idea of free land out west had attracted American farmers for a long time. The promise of hard work and adventure appealed to young people too. Nobody in England really understood how much work farming was in Canada and America. Back then, you could get land easily. The government gave away 160 acres for free if you met certain conditions. Many people didn't follow these rules anyway. I searched for land starting in Iowa and moved north. I found a nice area with rolling hills, small lakes, and trees. It led to a beautiful valley called the Little Saskatchewan. My driver told me the land I wanted was already taken. But I saw a Scottish man ploughing his field. We talked. He said the other farmers nearby were abandoning their grants to find new ones somewhere else. They hadn't done enough work on their current land to keep it. I checked and found this was true. So I bought two square miles of prairie land from the railway company. Their railway was going to go right through this land. My son never used it. Twenty years later, it was still just empty prairie when it was sold.

There was desperation in that tight clutch of the bag and the box. Suddenly Mr. O’Brien spoke; he recognised the futility of ignoring what was in everyone’s mind. "Well," he said, "a year soon passes, after all, and I hope we shall be back in about ten or eleven months." "But—but not—" came a watery stammer from Molly’s corner, but Nell broke in hurriedly. "I—I wonder will you look different, dad?" "Oh, yes," her mother laughed the pretty laugh that was just like Nell’s, "I shall be a horrid, stout old woman! Even Sheila Pat won’t acknowledge me then!" Sheila Pat said nothing. Mrs. O’Brien squeezed Nell’s fingers tightly. There was another silence. There was nothing to say. Everything had been said over and over again. The wind sent the rain beating angrily

### Plain English:
It was a strange ending. "Yes, it is!" she said. She was trying to sound happy, but her voice sounded surprised, like she had just noticed the storm outside. Sheila Pat sat in her corner without speaking. Her thin body was stiff and straight. She held a bag and a box tightly in her small arms. Later that night, she realized her arms hurt. She was gripping the bag and box with real desperation. Suddenly Mr. O'Brien spoke. He knew it was useless to pretend nobody was thinking about what was happening. "Well," he said, "a year goes by fast. I hope we'll be back in about ten or eleven months." "But—but not—" Molly started to say something in a shaky voice, but Nell cut her off quickly. "I—I wonder if you'll look different, dad?" "Oh, yes," her mother laughed. It was a pretty laugh, just like Nell's. "I'll be a fat old woman! Even Sheila Pat won't know me!" Sheila Pat said nothing. Mrs. O'Brien squeezed Nell's fingers hard. Nobody spoke again. There was nothing left to say. They had already said everything a hundred times. The wind pushed the rain down hard.

No less wonderful and varied are the inhabitants and the phenomena of the Philippines, and a new author, showing rare knowledge of the country and its strange peoples, now gives us a collection of simple yet powerful stories which bring them before us with dramatic vividness. Pirates, half naked natives, pearls, man-apes, towering volcanoes about whose summits clouds and unearthly traditions float together, strange animals and birds, and stranger men, pythons, bejuco ropes stained with human blood, feathering palm trees now fanned by soft breezes and now crushed to the ground by tornadoes;--on no mimic stage was ever a more wonderful scene set for such a company of actors. That the truly remarkable stories written by Sargent Kayme do not exaggerate the realities of this strange life can be easily seen by any one who has read the letters from press correspondents, our soldiers, or the more formal books of travel. Strangest, perhaps, of all these possibilities for fiction is the anting-anting, at once a mysterious power to protect its possessor and the outward symbol of the protection. No more curious fetich can be found in the history of folk-lore. A button, a coin, a bit of

### Plain English:
The Philippines has amazing people and strange things happening there. A new writer knows the country really well. He wrote simple but powerful stories that show us what it's like there in a very dramatic way. The stories have pirates, almost naked natives, pearls, man-apes, huge volcanoes with clouds and weird legends around them, strange animals and birds, and stranger people. There are pythons, jungle ropes with blood on them, and palm trees that get blown over by storms. No stage has ever had such an amazing scene with so many weird characters. The stories by Sargent Kayme are really good. They don't make things up more than they really are. You can tell this is true if you read letters from reporters, soldiers, or travel books. Maybe the strangest thing in these stories is the anting-anting. It's a mysterious charm that protects you and shows that you have that protection. You won't find a weirder charm like this in any folklore history. It could be a button, a coin, or a piece of something.

# The Captain of the Gray-Horse Troop

I. A Camp in the Snow

Winter in the high parts of the Bear Tooth Range is a bright, empty wasteland of snow. The sky above is a bright blue. Nothing moves. Nothing makes a sound. Only the rabbits at the mine shaft opening move. They go down into their dens deep in the rocks. The mountain peaks look like marble domes. During the day, the sun shines on them. At night, they reach up toward the stars. The pine trees look sad under all the heavy snow weighing them down. The streams are frozen and silent. Only the wind moves across the empty land. The wind never stops. It is cruel and harsh, like ungrateful people. It wants more and more, like fire. But it is still beautiful. It is especially beautiful at dawn. The shadows are a dark purple color. Each snowy peak glows with a soft, heavenly light. Each slope facing east shines like a path made of crushed diamonds. It is also beautiful at sunset. ### Plain English:
This was printed in the United States of America.

I work as the curator of birds at the American Museum of Natural History. My job has given me great chances to organize bird collections. I want to help students learn about local birds without confusing them with too much information. One way we do this is by having a special collection called 'Birds Found within 50 Miles of New York.' These birds are organized in their own hall. If you see a bird near New York and want to know what it is, you can come here and figure it out easily.

We have two main collections. The first one is called the 'General Systematic Collection.' It has examples of about 350 different types of birds that people have seen near New York City. The second collection is called the 'Seasonal Collection.' This one only shows birds that are here right now.

The Seasonal Collection starts with birds that live here all year long. Crows are a good example. They are always here. Then we add or remove birds that only visit at certain times of the year. We change this collection on the first day of each month. We add birds that are supposed to arrive this month. We remove birds that have already left.

SMYTH, M.A., B.Sc., Ph.D. When the early settlers began to pioneer the unbroken forests of North America, they considered the various Indian tribes to be the true Aborigines of this continent. But long before the red man, even long before the growth of the present forests, there lived an ancient race, whose origin and fate are surrounded with impenetrable darkness. The remains of their habitations, temples and tombs, are the only voices that tell us of their existence. Over broad areas, in the most fertile valleys, and along the numerous tributaries of the great rivers of the central and western portions of the United States, are to be found these wonderful remains, of the existence and origin of which, even the oldest red man could give no history. ## MOUND-BUILDERS
BY REV. WILLIAM J. SMYTH, M.A., B.Sc., Ph.D.

When the early settlers started to move into the thick forests of North America, they thought the different Native American tribes were the first people to live on this continent. But long before Native Americans, and even before today's forests grew, there was an ancient group of people. We don't know where they came from or what happened to them. The only way we know they existed is through the ruins they left behind — their homes, temples, and burial sites.

These amazing ruins can be found across large areas. They're in the most fertile valleys and along the many rivers in the central and western United States. Even the oldest Native Americans couldn't tell any stories about these ruins or where they came from. If we follow these ancient mounds that were built with some planning and intelligence, we can learn more about them.

It will require but few words to explain Sam Rossiter's presence in the north-bound Empire Express, but it would take volumes to express his feelings on the subject in general. Back in New York there lived Godfrey Wharton, millionaire and septuagenarian. For two years he had been husband to one of the prettiest, gayest young women in the city, and in the latter days of this responsibility he was not a happy man. His wife had fallen desperately, even conspicuously, in love with Everett Havens, the new leading man at one of the fashionable playhouses. The affair had been going on for weeks, and it had at last become the talk of the town. By "the town" is meant that vague, expansive thing known as the "Four Hundred."

### Plain English:
She's twenty-five and beautiful. He's seventy and not attractive. I'm thinking about giving up on this whole thing and going back to doing normal, honest work in the Tenderloin. This isn't for me. I was stupid for getting involved. My company played a dirty trick on me by sending me out here to work on this case for Wharton. It's like being a peeping tom, and I hate it.

It's easy to explain why Sam Rossiter is on this northbound train, but it would take forever to explain how he feels about it. Back in New York, there's a rich man named Godfrey Wharton. He's seventy years old. For two years he's been married to one of the most beautiful and fun young women in the city. But lately he's not happy about it. His wife has fallen hard for Everett Havens. He's the new lead actor at a fancy theater. They've been having an affair for weeks. Now everyone in the city is talking about it. By "everyone," I mean all the rich and important people in New York.

He'd just write whatever came into his head as a poem. He was a sensitive and thoughtful person.

### Original:
Paradise Of God. By Joseph Bates New Bedford: Press Of Benjamin Lindsey. 1846. PREFACE In presenting the following subject to the consideration of whom it may concern, I would here state that the two leading motives which have actuated and guided me through this absorbing subject has been--first, the truth of God to encourage and strengthen the true-believer. Second, to correct, or "rebuke" the spiritual views, (may I not say of almost all Christendom,) in respect to the appearing and kingdom of our Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ. Twenty-one years observation and experience, but more especially the last seven, in pursuit of this object, has taught me that truth is the only thing that can save the soul. But the great mass of the professed Christian world seem to pay no more regard to it than their great _Predecessor_, who said unto the Saviour "what is truth?" when he had just said to him that he "came into the world to bear witness unto the truth, and every one that is of the truth, heareth my voice." Jesus in his last prayer for his disciples asks the Father to sanctify them through the truth. "Thy word is truth." _St. John._

### Plain English:
Paradise Of God. By Joseph Bates. New Bedford: Press Of Benjamin Lindsey. 1846.

PREFACE

I want to tell you why I wrote this book. I had two main reasons. First, I wanted to share God's truth so that real believers would feel encouraged and strong. Second, I wanted to correct what almost all Christian churches are teaching about Jesus coming back and setting up his kingdom.

I have spent twenty-one years studying this subject. The last seven years have been especially important to me. What I learned is this: truth is the only thing that can save people's souls. But most people who call themselves Christians don't care much about truth. They act like a famous person in the Bible who asked Jesus, "What is truth?" This was strange because Jesus had just said that he came into the world to tell people the truth. Everyone who believes in the truth listens to what he says.

When Jesus prayed for his followers at the end of his life, he asked God to make them holy through the truth. The Bible says, "Your word is truth."

MANNING THE SERVICE PREFACE "I went in at the hawse-hole and came out at the cabin window." It was thus that a certain North Country shipowner once summarised his career while addressing his fellow-townsmen on some public occasion now long past, and the sentence, giving forth the exact truth with all a sailor's delight in hyperbole, may well be taken to describe the earlier life-stages gone through by the author of this book. The experiences acquired in a field of operations, that includes all the seas and continents where commerce may move, live, and have its being, have enhanced in value and completed what came to him in his forecastle and quarter-deck times. He learned in his youth, from the lips of a race now extinct, what the nature and traditions of seamanship were before he and his contemporaries lived. He has seen that nature and those traditions change and die, whilst he and his generation came gradually under a new order of things, whose practical working he and they have tested in actual practice both

STAFFING THE SERVICE PREFACE "I started at the bottom and worked my way up to the top." That's how a shipowner from northern England once described his life when he was talking to people in his town at some event a long time ago. This sentence tells the truth in the way sailors like to exaggerate things, and it describes the author's early life pretty well. The author gained experience working on ships all over the world wherever trade happened. This work added to and improved what he had already learned working on ships. When he was young, he learned from old sailors who don't exist anymore what sailing was really like before his time. He watched that old way of sailing disappear and change. He and people his age had to get used to new ways of doing things. He and they tested out these new ways by actually using them.

It is the character of this calm observer that the writer has imagined himself to be assuming in the dozen little sketches and stories garnered here into a volume. They are snapshots or flashlights of one or another of the shifting aspects of this huge and sprawling metropolis of ours. ### Plain English:
1908. CONTENTS: Destroying meadow moths. Picture from the shore of Juukujärvi. Känsä-Topias as a customs smuggler. Temptation. Old Iisak's place in Anttila. The old master's words. The red-faced freight worker's opinion. "Tösseli". A letter from America. Frans Tuira's Christmas story. From cottage to cottage. Smoothing over the argument. Poor Laura. Antti from Raitikoski. New justice. When Savinainen was arrested. Engaged. During subscription time, or Our people's wellbeing. DESTROYING MEADOW MOTHS Last summer, meadow moths appeared far up in the north. They filled the earth with what farmers valued most: the beautiful grain of grain fields. They also showed up in Yliranta village. No one in this generation had seen these pests before. People had read in newspapers how they caused big damage in the south. They had tried everything to get rid of them. But nothing had worked yet. Even farming experts had tried everything they knew. But the moths did not disappear. The newspapers said that when it rained really hard, the moths would die and rot in the grass roots. Then they would not reproduce that summer at least. The meadow moths first appeared in Niemelä's fields in Yliranta and along Lompalon shore. These fields had thin, short meadow grass. The moths appeared there first. No one knew if they came from the ground or fell from the sky. But the fields started to get destroyed in a scary way. Lompalon shore meadow was right by the road. Everyone passing by could see the damage the moths were doing to the fields. You could see the meadow clearly from the house too, if you looked south from the back window. Passersby could see it clearly too, whether they were walking or riding horses.

### Plain English:
The man wasn't someone who got emotional easily. He thought it would be wrong to leave the house without a good reason. After all, it had protected him and made him happy. He had lived in almost every room at some point. As a young apprentice, he slept in a small attic room where rain on the roof kept him awake at night. Later, when he became head journeyman, he brought his new wife to the best bedroom on the first floor. That's where they slept after he married his dead master's daughter. But he was too careful with money to keep using those fancy rooms. After six months, he moved to the second floor. It wasn't fancy—the house only had three windows in front. His two children grew up there. He rented the first-floor rooms to an old couple who had no kids. He would never ask them to leave. The old man had once been a famous singer, and when the owner was young, he had heard him perform and loved it.

INTRODUCTION This Supplement is designed to supply a double need: it furnishes an analytical index to the entire series of twenty volumes; and it affords a great deal of additional information, bearing on the subject-matter of these volumes, but which from its very nature it was impossible to incorporate in the text. This additional information includes biographical sketches of the characters mentioned in each volume; similar sketches of prominent Canadians who for one reason or another do not appear in any of the twenty volumes; and brief descriptions of wars, battles, treaties, and political and other events having a vital bearing on the history of Canada. References have been added, wherever necessary, to the principal sources which the student may consult for further information. The whole has been thrown into one alphabetical arrangement, and it constitutes, to a large extent, a dictionary of Canadian history. REMARKS The position of Ancient Egypt was unique, not in one, but in every sense. To begin at the very foundation of life in that country, we find that the soil was unlike any other on earth in its origin. Every acre of fruitful land between the first cataract and the sea had been brought from Inner Africa, and each year additions were made to it. Out of this mud, borne down thousands of miles from the great fertile uplands of Abyssinia by rivers, grew everything needed to feed and clothe man and nourish animals. Out of it also was made the brick from which walls, houses, and buildings of various uses and kinds were constructed. Though this soil of the country was rich, it could be utilized only by the unceasing co-ordinate efforts of a whole population constrained and directed. To direct and constrain was the task of the priests and the pharaohs. Never have men worked in company so long and successfully at tilling the earth as the Egyptians, and never has the return been so continuous and abundant from land as in their case. The Nile valley furnished grain to all markets accessible by water; hence Rome,

### Plain English:
Ancient Egypt was special in every way. Let's start with the basic facts of life there. The soil was different from anywhere else on Earth. Every piece of fertile land between the first cataract and the sea came from Inner Africa. Every year, more soil was added to it. This mud came down thousands of miles from the fertile highlands of Abyssinia through rivers. Everything people needed to eat and wear grew from this mud. Animals also got food from it. The mud was also used to make bricks for walls, houses, and other buildings. The soil was rich, but it could only be used if the whole population worked together in an organized way. The priests and pharaohs directed and controlled this work. No group of people has ever farmed the land together for so long and with so much success as the Egyptians. No land has ever given back so much food year after year as theirs did. The Nile valley produced grain for all markets that could be reached by water. That's why Rome and other places could get grain from Egypt.

If "he who causes two blades of grass to grow where but one grew before, is a benefactor of his race," he is not less so who imparts to millions a knowledge of the methods by which it is done. The last half century has been the era of experiments and writing on the cultivation of the soil. The result has been the acquisition of more knowledge on the subjects embraced, than the world had attained in all its previous history. That knowledge is scattered through many volumes of numerous periodicals and books, and interspersed with many theories, and much speculation, that can never be valuable in practice. In the form in which it is presented, it confuses, rather than aids, the great mass of cultivators. Hence the prejudice against "_book-farming_." Provided established facts only are presented, they are none the worse for being printed. The object of this volume is to condense, and present in an intelligible form,

Vandewater Street, N.Y. * * * * * TO THE PEOPLE WHO FARM THE LAND, The True Owners of the Manor, THIS BOOK IS DEDICATED, BY THEIR HONEST FRIEND, THE AUTHOR. * * * * * A NOTE TO YOU, THE READER. If "someone who makes two blades of grass grow where only one grew before is helping humanity," then someone who teaches millions how to do it is just as helpful. The last fifty years have been a time of testing and writing about how to farm. The result is that we now know more about farming than the whole world knew before this time. But that knowledge is spread across many books and magazines. It is mixed in with lots of theories and guesses that will never actually work in real farming. The way it is written confuses farmers instead of helping them. That's why farmers don't trust "book-farming." Real facts are just as good when they are printed as when they are not. The goal of this book is to take all that information and present it in a way that is easy to understand.

Physical measurements have supplied much of the foundation of knowledge and it is not a reflection upon their great usefulness to state that often they differ from the results of intellectual appraisal through the visual sense. In other words, there are numberless so-called visual illusions which must be taken into account. All are of interest; many can be utilized; and some must be suppressed. Scientific literature yields a great many valuable discussions from theoretical and experimental viewpoints but much of the material is controversial. The practical aspects of visual illusions have been quite generally passed by and, inasmuch as there does not appear to be a volume available which treats the subject in a condensed manner but with a broad scope, this small volume is contributed toward filling the gap. ### Plain English:
# Rewritten Passage

People have long believed that measuring light, color, and lighting with instruments is not enough. Measurements can't show us what we actually see. Physical measurements have helped us learn a lot. But that doesn't mean they're perfect. Often, what we measure is different from what our eyes actually tell us. There are many visual illusions we need to think about. Some are interesting. Some are useful. Some we need to ignore. Scientists have written about visual illusions from different angles, but they don't always agree. However, nobody has really looked at how visual illusions work in real life. There's no simple book that covers the whole subject. This book tries to fill that gap. The topic is very complicated. But this book tries to keep things simple. It focuses on illusions that don't move. It leaves out small details. It doesn't spend much time on theory. Instead, it tries to focus on what actually happens when we do experiments.

All this is not to say that the epic is the oldest form of literary expression, but it is the expression of the oldest literary ideas, for, even when the epic is not at all primitive in form, it deals essentially with elemental moods and ideals. Epical poetry is poetic not because it is metrical and conformative to rhythmical standards,--though it usually is both,--but it is poetry because of the high sweep of its emotional outlook, the bigness of its thought, the untamed passion of its language, and the musical flow of its utterance. Here, then, we have a veritable source book of the oldest ideas of the race; but not only that--we are also led into the penetralia of the earliest thought of many separate nations, for when the epic is national, it is true to the earliest genius of the people whose spirit it depicts. Books about sports, travel, and adventure in countries that most people don't know much about usually fall into two types. Neither type is usually very good. One type seems to come from the other.

For example, if a writer gets a normal permit to go to certain areas, he makes it sound like he got special permission. He talks about it like he went somewhere almost nobody else could go. ### Plain English:
A famous author whose name people nowadays doubt — wrote in his book Pilgrim's Progress that "if you're already down, you don't need to fear falling." And who can argue with that? It's obvious that if a man is sitting on the ground, he won't slip through his chair. And if he's already at the bottom of the water, he doesn't need to worry about falling from a rope. So I feel confident about Colin Clink. He was down in the world when it came to money. He was down in society. His own mother and father looked down on him. And he was down in what modern politicians call the "accident" of being born poor. So he really had no reason to worry about falling any lower. From the very start, he had only one thing to look forward to — rising above it.

I have a soul. I sing quietly to myself about him. His pride is greater than all other pride. He brings greatness down low and lifts low things up high. He treats everyone the same—the poor person and the king stand on equal ground." --_Wasson_ PREFACE

We have known the basic facts about Nathaniel Hawthorne's life for a long time. From 1835 onward, we can easily find them in his notebooks. Before that time, we learned about his life mainly from what his two close friends, Horatio Bridge and Elizabeth Peabody, remembered about him. George P. Lathrop organized these memories and published them in 1872. Later, Julian Hawthorne wrote a longer and more accurate biography twelve years after that. However, Julian did not share his thoughts about how good his father's writing was. He did not try to analyze his father's literary work. This book was written to fill that gap. At the same time, I want to give credit to the people who came before me for helping us learn about Hawthorne.

Since then, I found old handwritten journals from some of these voyages in Madrid. This gave me new facts. I was able to understand other facts better and fix some mistakes. I made arguments that were weak stronger, and I came up with new ideas. The subject seems pretty complete now. It would be hard to add anything more to all the letters that went back and forth between the two governments.

It will require but few words to explain Sam Rossiter's presence in the north-bound Empire Express, but it would take volumes to express his feelings on the subject in general. Back in New York there lived Godfrey Wharton, millionaire and septuagenarian. For two years he had been husband to one of the prettiest, gayest young women in the city, and in the latter days of this responsibility he was not a happy man. His wife had fallen desperately, even conspicuously, in love with Everett Havens, the new leading man at one of the fashionable playhouses. The affair had been going on for weeks, and it had at last become the talk of the town. By "the town" is meant that vague, expansive thing known as the "Four Hundred."

No one has written a complete and really good life of Erasmus of Rotterdam yet. This book focuses on Erasmus and how he helped cause the Protestant Reformation in the 1500s. It looks at Erasmus and how he fits into that big religious change. It doesn't try to explain everything about who Erasmus was as a person. That part of him is hard to pin down anyway.

Maida half-shut her eyes and the whole world seemed to flash by in ribbons. "May I get out for a moment, papa?" she asked suddenly in a thin little voice. "I'd like to watch the waves." "All right," her father answered quickly. To the chauffeur he said, "Stop here, Henri." To Maida, "Stay as long as you want, Posie." "Posie" was Mr. Westabrook's nickname for Maida. Billy Potter jumped out and helped Maida to the ground. The three men watched her walk slowly to the sea-wall. She was a child you would have noticed anywhere because of her bright, very quiet, gray eyes. And because of the delicate look on her face from her floating mass of pale-gold, curly hair. And yet I think you would have known that she was a sick little girl right away. When she moved, it was very slowly as if everything made her tired. She was so thin that her hands looked like claws.

In the same week, and within three days of the same date, I received from three Judges of the Supreme Court, of three States, the request that I would record my remembrances of the men and things I had known for fifty years. The gentlemen making this request were Joseph Henry Lumpkin, of Georgia; William L. Sharkey, of Mississippi, and James G. Taliaferro, of Louisiana. From Judge Sharkey the request was verbal; from the other two it came in long and, to me, cherished letters. All three have been my intimate friends--Lumpkin from boyhood; the others for nearly fifty years. Judge Lumpkin has finished his work in time, and gone to his reward. This book is dedicated to my nephew, the Honorable Ovid Garten Sparks, and Colonel Thomas Hardeman of Macon, Georgia. I am an old man dedicating this book to them. I hope they will value it after I die as a sign of how much I care about them.

PREFACE.

Within the same week, just three days apart, I got requests from three judges. All three were Supreme Court judges from three different states. They all asked me to write down what I remembered about the people and events I had known about for fifty years. The judges were Joseph Henry Lumpkin from Georgia, William L. Sharkey from Mississippi, and James G. Taliaferro from Louisiana. Judge Sharkey asked me in person. The other two sent me long letters that I really valued. All three have been my close friends. I have known Lumpkin since I was a boy. I have known the other two for almost fifty years. Judge Lumpkin finished his work and died. Judges Sharkey and Taliaferro are still alive. Both are over seventy years old now. Judge Sharkey stopped working as a judge and is respected, trusted, and loved. Judge Taliaferro still works as a judge on the Supreme Court.

YOUR MAJESTY’s particular Inquiries into the State of Your Military Hospitals, in every Quarter of the World, in the Time of the late glorious and successful War; Your Concern for every Officer and Soldier who suffered either by Sickness or by Wounds in the Cause of their King and Country; and Your Solicitude to procure them every possible Assistance and Relief, cannot fail to excite the highest Admiration of Your MAJESTY’s Goodness in the Breast of every Subject, and the warmest Gratitude in the Heart of every Soldier. The Knowledge of these Circumstances induced me to flatter myself, that a Work of this Kind would be agreeable to Your MAJESTY; and should this Attempt towards pointing out the Means of alleviating those Miseries, which necessarily attend a Military Life in the Time of Service, be acceptable, I shall obtain the

### Plain English:
This book was printed for A. Millar, D. Wilson, and T. Durham on the Strand. It was also printed for T. Payne at the Mews-Gate in 1764.

TO THE KING.

Your Majesty, I hope you will let me give you this book. It was written to help the people who will take care of your soldiers' health in the future.

Your Majesty has always asked questions about the military hospitals all over the world during the recent war. You have cared about every officer and soldier who got sick or was wounded while fighting for their king and country. You have tried hard to help them and make them feel better. This shows how good you are, and every person in your kingdom admires you for it. Every soldier is grateful to you.

Because I know how much you care about these things, I thought you would like this book. I wrote it to show ways to help soldiers suffer less during their time in the military. If you think this work is good and useful, then I will be very happy.

### Plain English:
I know about it. It has a lot of vividness, which R. L. Stevenson really liked. The plot is clever. The dramatic scenes are powerful. And the surprising ending is typical of the Hungarian writer's style. I don't know of a more exciting scene in modern fiction than the terrible fight between the strong goatherd Juon and the quick bandit Fatia Negra while two scared, helpless women watch. They can't do anything. But their lives depend on who wins the fight. We have to go back to Sheridan Le Fanu to find anything as scary as poor Henrietta's terrible night alone in an empty inn on an empty field. At the moment of danger, she discovers that her only companion, the brave old squire, is asleep and won't wake up because someone drugged him. There isn't as much room in this action-filled novel for Jókai's funny and nice style as there is in that other work.

About wanting to learn and knowing the truth.

# Book 3: About Right and Wrong

# Part 1: About Being Good or Bad

### Plain English:
When Commodore Dupont captured two forts at Hilton Head and Bay Point, South Carolina on November 7, 1861, the Sea Island region became Union territory. The plantation owners and their families had run away quickly. So the U.S. Government now owned almost everything they had left behind. The two most important things were a huge cotton crop that was almost ready to sell, and several hundred enslaved people who were poor and confused. More enslaved people kept arriving as refugees. The enslaved people were a difficult problem to deal with. The cotton was valuable property. The first step was clear. The cotton agents that Washington sent to collect and ship the cotton could also hire the enslaved people to work. This helped start to solve the problem. A month later, the next step happened. The Secretary of the Treasury sent Edward L. Pierce from Milton, Massachusetts as a special agent. His job was to figure out how to manage the enslaved people and start growing cotton for 1862. Mr. Pierce had been there the summer before.

George Wither (1588-1667) _The praises of poetry have been often sung in ancient and in modern times; strange powers have been ascribed to it of influence over animate and inanimate auditors; its force over fascinated crowds has been acknowledged; but before Wither, no one ever celebrated its power at home, the wealth and the strength which this divine gift confers upon its possessor. Fame, and that too after death, was all which hitherto the poets had promised themselves from this art. It seems to have been left to Wither to discover that poetry was a present possession, as well as a rich reversion, and that the Muse has a promise of both lives,--of this, and of that which was to come._--CHARLES LAMB. Stretched full length in a gaily striped hammock swung between two great trees, her gray eyes dreamily turned toward the distant mountain peaks, Jane Allen had not heard her father's noiseless approach over the closely clipped green lawn. At sound of his voice, she bobbed up from the hammock with an alacrity that left it swaying wildly. "Of course I was dreaming, Dad," she declared gaily, making an ineffectual grab at the hands he held behind him. "No fair using force," he warned, dexterously eluding her. "This is a guessing contest. Now which hand will you choose?" "Both hands, you mean thing!" laughed Jane. "I know what you have in one of them. It's a letter. Maybe two. Now stand and deliver." "Here you are." Obligingly obeying the imperative command, Mr. Allen handed Jane two letters. "Oh, joy! Here _you_ are!" Jane enveloped her father in a bear-like hug, planting a resounding kiss on his sun-burnt cheek. "Having played postman, I suppose my next duty is to take myself off and leave my girl to her letters," was his affectionately smiling comment.

### Plain English:
"Guess what I have for you." Henry Allen stood with his hands behind his back, looking down at his daughter with a smile. Jane Allen was lying stretched out in a colorful striped hammock between two big trees. She was staring dreamily at the distant mountains and hadn't heard her father walk across the lawn toward her. When he spoke, she jumped up so fast the hammock swung back and forth. "Of course I was daydreaming, Dad," she said happily. She reached for his hands, but he was too quick. "No fair using force," he said, moving away from her. "This is a guessing game. Which hand do you pick?" "Both hands, you mean!" Jane laughed. "I know what you have. It's a letter. Maybe two letters. Now give it to me." "Here you go." Mr. Allen gave Jane two letters. "Yes! Thank you!" Jane hugged her father really tight and kissed his sunburned cheek loudly. "I guess now that I've delivered the mail, I should go away and let you read your letters," he said with a smile.

### Plain English:
Less than one hundred years ago, a large sailing ship carrying oranges from Spain was heading to Liverpool. A storm pushed it off course into a bay called the Solway Firth. After being tossed around for a while, the ship finally made it to a small port called Workington on the coast of Cumberland. For several days before this, some of the crew had been feeling sick. They were tired, sad, and wanted to sleep all the time. But because of the storm and because they wanted to survive, they kept working on the deck. As soon as the ship was safely tied up in the harbor, everything changed. The sickness that had been building up inside them suddenly got worse. Three crew members went to bed and looked almost dead. The ship didn't have enough healthy workers, and the storm was still happening, so the captain decided to sell his cargo right there. He did that. But his men didn't get better. The captain got sick too. And within a few days, most of the crew was sick.

Silverton is centered in the rugged, majestic San Juan Mountains, and nowhere in the nation is there another 100,000 square miles of such spectacularly scenic grandeur. One evening, a group of men were in a saloon discussing the possibilities of the San Juan. One fellow remarked, “We have silver by the ton”, and thus Silverton got its name. It is the only incorporated town in the county, and boasts not an acre of farm land. Silverton was, at one time, named Baker’s Park after Charles Baker who led a group of prospectors to this country in 1860 or 1861. There is no record of any permanent locations made, or of any quantity of mineral taken out, though there were indications of prospecting being done. As you can imagine, Baker and his party endured many hardships. Relics, broken wagon parts, and some discarded camp equipment lead to the belief the party came by way of Durango, thence to Cascade Creek along the route of our present highway. From there they went to the West of Spud Mountain and kept fairly high, crossing Coal Bank Hill and following around the head of Lime Creek.

### Plain English:
Silverton sits 4,000 feet above the valley floor in the rugged and beautiful San Juan Mountains. There is no other place in the country with 100,000 square miles of scenery as stunning as this. One night, some men in a saloon were talking about what they could do in the San Juan area. One guy said, "We have silver by the ton," and that's how Silverton got its name. It's the only town in the county that is officially incorporated, and it has no farmland at all. Silverton used to be called Baker's Park after Charles Baker. He led a group of people looking for minerals to this area in 1860 or 1861. There's no record that they set up any permanent camps or took out any large amounts of minerals, but there are signs they did some exploring. Baker and his group probably had a really hard time getting there. Old broken wagon parts and abandoned camping equipment suggest they came through Durango first. Then they went to Cascade Creek along the road we use today. After that, they went west of Spud Mountain and stayed fairly high up. They crossed Coal Bank Hill and went around the top of Lime Creek.

### Plain English:
Her thoughts were pleasant and interesting. She wasn't exactly daydreaming like a young single girl would—she was a widow. And even though she was closer to thirty than twenty, she wasn't completely free to do whatever she wanted. Mrs. Lascelles loved her horse very much. She had been riding him boldly and with confidence because she really cared about him. She rode in a graceful, ladylike way, but she was also daring and rode fast. The horse had every reason to be tired. He had carried her safely right next to the hunting dogs for as long as the old fox could run. He had done a good job, and he deserved the affection she showed him now, petting his sweaty neck and shoulders while her hunting partner's horse got quieter in the distance. Mrs. Lascelles loved her horse very much. But for the first time ever, maybe she was starting to realize she could love something better than her horse. The light was fading quickly. Heavy clouds were moving in from the west, pushing steadily forward on the wind that moaned—the kind of wind that often comes in English winters when the sun sets. The day had been gloomy and cloudy. It was perfect for hunting, but it felt sad for anything else.
