Sometimes it moved along the brink of a torrent, and the long line of bayonets crept through the ravine like a huge serpent with glittering scales; sometimes it wound around a mountain, making it look like one of those triumphal columns about which curves an army of heroes in bronze. The soldiers marched with trailing weapons and cloaks dragging in the dust, looking surly and tired, for these noble fellows are averse to anything but battle or inaction. ### Plain English:
The musketeer regiment from Munkholm was walking through narrow mountain passes between Throndhjem and Skongen. Sometimes the soldiers walked along the edge of a rushing stream, and the long line of bayonets looked like a huge snake with shiny scales moving through the valley. Sometimes the line wrapped around a mountain, looking like those bronze statues of armies you see on victory columns. The soldiers marched slowly with their weapons dragging and their cloaks getting dusty. They looked angry and tired. These tough soldiers only really enjoyed fighting or doing nothing. The jokes that had made them laugh yesterday weren't funny anymore. The air was cold and the sky was cloudy. Nothing would make the soldiers laugh, unless one of the women selling supplies fell off her small horse, or a metal pan rolled down the cliff and bounced off rocks. To make the boring march less boring, Lieutenant Randmer, a young Danish nobleman, started talking to old Captain Lory, who had worked his way up from being a regular soldier. The captain was quiet and moody, and walked with heavy, sure steps. The lieutenant was light on his feet and quick.

To begin with the Business of Translation. Whoever sits down to translate a Poet, ought in the first place to consider his Author's peculiar _Stile_; for without this, tho' the Translation may be very good in all other respects, it will hardly deserve the Name of a Translation. The two great Men amongst the Antients differ from each other as much in this particular as in the Subjects they treat of. The Stile of _Homer_, who sings the Anger or Rage of _Achilles_, is _rapid_. The Stile of _Virgil_, who celebrates the Piety of _Æneas_, is _majestick_. But it may be proper to explain in what this Difference consists. The Stile is _rapid_, when several Relatives, each at the head of a separate Sentence, are governed by one Antecedent, or several Verbs by one Nominative Case, to the close of the Period. Thus in _Homer_: "Goddess, sing the pernicious Anger of _Achilles_, which brought infinite Woes to the _Grecians_, and sent many valiant Souls of Heroes to Hell, and gave their Bodies to

### Plain English:
I've talked to you about translating poetry. I also mentioned how Virgil and Milton write in similar ways, plus some related topics. Let me start by talking about translation itself. When someone decides to translate a poet, they should first look at how that poet writes. Without doing this, even if the translation is good in every other way, it won't really be a true translation. The two great ancient poets are different from each other in how they write, just like they're different in what they write about. Homer writes in a fast, exciting style. He tells the story of Achilles' anger. Virgil writes in a grand, impressive style. He tells the story of Aeneas' loyalty. But I should explain what this difference actually means. Writing is fast and exciting when you use several describing words or phrases. Each one starts a new sentence. They all connect back to one main word or idea. Or when several action words all connect to one subject all the way to the end of the sentence. Here's an example from Homer: "Goddess, sing about the terrible anger of Achilles. It caused endless suffering for the Greeks. It sent many brave hero souls down to the underworld. It left their bodies behind.

### Plain English:
The people connected to it were killed on orders from the Yekaterinburg soviet of workers' deputies. News of this crime got out of Bolshevik Russia and spread around the world. At the end of July 1918, the town of Yekaterinburg was taken from the Bolsheviks by the Siberian Government's forces. Soon after they took control of the area, they ordered an investigation into what happened during the murders. N. A. Sokoloff was an investigating judge for important cases at the Omsk court. He questioned witnesses who had known the imperial family at Czarskoe-Selo, Tobolsk, and Yekaterinburg. When the Kolchak government fell, M. George Gustav Telberg made copies of these witness statements. Telberg was a law professor at the University of Saratov and also served as Minister of Justice in Omsk. He took the copies with him when he fled with the other Omsk government ministers. These combined statements tell the story of the imperial family's life from when the emperor gave up his throne until he, his wife, his children (including the heir), and their few loyal servants were killed at Ipatieff's.
